MOTION  BY  SOUTHFIELD
PUBLIC  SCHOOLS  TO INTERVENE

Now comes  Southfield  Public  Schools by its attorneys, CON-
DIT  ‘and  McGARRY,  P.C.,  and  by  way  of  motion  to  intervene
shows  unto  the  court  as  follows:

1.  Southfield  Public  Schools  is a  School  District  and  a Pub-
lic Municipal  Corporation  of the  State  of Michigan,  Organized  as  a
third  class  district  pursuant  to  the School  Code  of 1955,  1955  PA
269,  being  340.1  et  seq.,  1948  CL.  The  School  District  of South-
field  Public  Schools  enrolls  in excess  of  16,000  students  and geo-
graphically  borders  the City of Detroit,  Michigan.

.

2.  Having  been  informed  on  or about  February  3, 1972  that
the  State  Board  of Education  of the  State  of Michigan  filed  with
this  Court  various  proposals  for desegration,  which  plans allegedly
in  part  concern  the  future  education  of Southfield  students,  the
Board  of  Education  of  Southfield  Public  Schools  at  its  next
regular  meeting  of February  8,  1972,  authorized  its counsel  to re-
quest  intervention  in  these  proceedings  and  thereby  protect  the
best  interests  of  said  School  District  and  the  welfare  of  its

Ia  190

——

students.  The  grounds  upon  which  this  motion  is  filed  are  as
follows:

nctigieh dhibie: OF RIGHT

(a)  The  School  District  of  Southfield  Public  Schools
through  its elected  Board  of Trustees  is charged  with  the  responsi-
bility  of establishing  and  maintaining  a program  of elementary  and
secondary  school  education  for the children  of School  District  res-
idents.  Since  its inception  the School  District  has consistently  pro-
vided  a  quality  education  for  all  its  students  regardless  of  race,
color,  religion  or  national  origin.  Successive  Boards  of Education
and  professional  educators  serving  as  administrators  have  tradi-
tionally  supported  the  neighborhood  school  concept  as  the  most
effective  means  of achieving  quality  education.  Historically,  the
School  District  residents  have  supported  their  School  District,  not
only  by citizen  participation  in  school  affairs,  but  also  by assum-
ing additional  tax  burdens,  often  at  considerable  sacrifice,  so  that
proper  financing  would  be  available  for school  operation  and  con-
struction.

(b)  On  information  and  belief,  the  subject  matter  of  the
above-entitled  litigation,  although  not.apparent  from  its inception,
now  directly  concerns  the  future  educational  program  of South-
field  Public  Schools  by reason  of the various  proposals  for desegre-
gation  providing  for drastic  revision  of local  school  district  respon-
sibilities  and  prerogatives  as  well  as  the  proposed  disruption  and
transportation  of school  children  from  their  neighborhood  schools
to  schools  outside  the  district.  The  mere  consideration  of said  de-
segregation  proposals  implies  that  Southfield  Public  Schools  may
be  included  in  a  future  judgment  of this Court  and  expected  to
comply  therewith.

(c)  Southfield  Public  Schools  ae to the Court  that  it
in any of
or indirectly  represented  by counsel

is not now  directly
the proceedings  to date.

PERMISSIVE  INTERVENTION

(a)  The  relief  proposed  by some  of the  litigants  in this law-
suit  raises  substantial  questions  of law  and  fact,  the  resolution  of
which  could  seriously  and  materially  interfere  with  presently  con-
stituted  rights  of the  School  District.  This  is particularly  so  since

Ia  191

Southfield  Public  Schools  was  not  a party  to the  proceedings  thus
far  which  have  formed  the  basis  for  the  Court’s  judicial  inter-
vention  and  prospective  equitable  relief.  Without  an  opportunity
to  be heard,  the Scffool  District  will  be unable  to advocate  its posi-
tion  concerning  the  common  questions  before  the  Court.  Such  a
result  will Only  serve  to  extend  the  controversy  and  further  frust-
rate  the  School  District  and  its patrons  who  are  presently  situated
on  the side  lines  of the  litigation.

(b)  The  proposed  intervention  of Southfield  Public  Schools
will  not  delay  or  prejudice  the  adjudication  of  the  rights  of the
original  parties, many  of whom  allegedly  would  in fact  support  the
requested  intervention.

CLAIM  OF INTERVENOR

3.  Based  upon  the  foregoing,  it is the  position  of Southfield
Public  Schools  that  the judicial  intervention  should  not  lawfully  or
properly  be extended  to this School  District  and that equity  neces-
sitates  the  opportunity  to be heard  on  the critical issues  before  the
Court.

4.  Based  upon  the rather  rapid  development  of events,  parti-
cularly,  the  publication  on  February  15,  1972,  of the  Court’s  in-
tention  to  schedule  intervention  hearings  for  February  22, 1972,
Southfield  Public  Schools  requests  leave  to  file  a  formal  pleading
on  or about  the  forthcoming  hearing  date.

This  motion  is based  upon  FR  Civ  P, 24(a)(2)  and  24(b)\(2).

WHEREFORE,  Southfield  Public  Schools  prays  that  this
Court  enter  its  order  permitting  interverition  as  a  party  to  this
Litigation  and  for  such  other  and  further  relief  as  the  Court  may
deem proper.

SOUTHFIELD  PUBLIC  SCHOOLS

CONDIT  and  McGARRY,  P.C.

RULE 24 MOTION TO INTERVENE

COMES.  NOW  Intervenor  applicant,  The  School  District  of
the  City  of  Royal  Oak,  a  Michigan  Municipal  Corporation  and
School  District  of  the  3rd  Class,  respectfully  moving  this  Honor-
able  Court  for  leave  to  intervene  as  a  Defendant  in  the  captioned
cause  for the  following  reasons:

1.  Said  applicant  may  have  a  substantial  interest  in the sub-
ject  matter  of  this  cause  depending,  of course,  upon  the  remedy
ultimately  fashioned  by this Court  in said  cause.

2.  Said  applicant’s  interest  is not  being directly  or  adequate-

ly represented  by any  of the existing  parties  to this  cause.

3.  Said  applicant’s  capacity  to perform  its [fidicuary]  duties
to  all  children,  parents  and  other  entitities  located  within  its dis-
be measureably  impaired  and im-
trict may, as a practical  matter,
peded  unless  it  is  allowed  to  present  all  material  facts  relevant
thereto  prior  to this Court’s  determination  of the ultimate  remedy
in this cause.

4.  Grant  of said  applicant’s  requested  intervention  will  not
unduly  delay  or  prejudice  final  adjudication  of the  pleaded  claims

'

la  197

and  defenses  containing  mainly  common  questions  of law  and  fact
upon  which  this  Court  must  pronounce  the  appropriate  remedy.

5.  Prior  to  the  scheduled  hearing  of this  Motion,  said  appli-
cant  will  request  concurrence  or  acquiescence  in the  relief  sought
from  other  parties  to this  cause  but success is not  anticipated.

6.  This Motion  is based upon the files and records of this

cause  and the  Affidavit  of Donald M. Currie annexed  hereto.

WHEREFORE,  Intervenor  applicant,  the  School  District of
the  City  of  Royal  Oak,  respectfully  prays  that  this  Honorable
Court  grant  its  Motion  for  leave  to  intervene  in  this  captioned
cause  in keeping  with  the  provisions  of  the above-mentioned rule.

UNITED  STATES  DISTRICT  COURT
FOR  THE  EASTERN  DISTRICT  OF  MICHIGAN
FLINT  48502

DATE:

March  6, 1972

SUBJECT:  Detroit  School  Case, Civil  Action  No.  35257,  Bradley

v.  Milliken,  et al.

RE:

Notice  to Counsel  for Parties  Seeking  Intervention.

It is expected  that  rulings  on  applications  for intervention  in
this case will be made  on or about  March  15,  1972. So that
counsel  may  be put on  notice a copy  of scheduling  sent  to counsel
for present parties  is addressed  to you.

You  may,  if you  wish, submit  your  views  on  “conditions”  of

intervention  not later than  March  14, 1972.

Re:  Bradley v.  Milliken

Civil  Action  No. 35257

Dear Judge  Roth:
Thank  you  for  your  letter  of  March  6, 1972,  addressed  to  us  as
counsel  for  The  Grosse  Pointe  Public  School  System,  one  of the
parties seeking  intervention  in the captioned  cause.
In  your  letter  you  granted  permission  to  ‘submit  our  views  on
“conditions”  of  intervention,  and  in this  regard,  we would  like to
state as follows:

it

’

:

1)  The  Grosse  Pointe  Public  Schoo!  System  has sought

intervention  for two  principal  reasons,  which  are:

a)  To participate  in the litigation  Of the question  of the
legal  propriety  of the implementation  of a metropolitan
plan for the desegregation  of  the School  System  of the
City  of Detroit  which  would  directly  involve  The Grosse
Pointe  Public  School  System,

-

Ia 202

b)  To offer  objections,  modifications  or  alternatives  to
metropolitan  plans  of  desegregation  presented  to  the
Court  by other  parties;  insofar as such  plans  affect  the
interests  of The Grosse  Pointe  Public  School  System.

2)  It  is not  the  intention  of The  Grosse  Pointe  Public
School  System  to  reopen  any  matters  previously  adjudi-
cated by the Court except  as the same  may directly af-
fect  the interests  of The Grosse  Pointe  Public  School
System.  Specifically,
the ques-
we do not wish  to litigate
tion of de jure  segregation  in  the Detroit  Public  School
System.  We  intend  to  approach  this  litigation  in a posi-
tive  and  constructive  manner  and  to  minimize  any  ad-
ministrative  problems  which  may  be creeted  by the ad-
dition  of a  number  of additional  counsel  to the proceed-
ings.

;

3)  Regarding  “conditions”  which  might be imposed  on
our  intervention,  it  is  our  position  that  The  Grosse
Pointe  Public  School  System  should  be afforded  the full
status of a party  litigant  to the proceedings  with  no  limi-
tation  as  to  its  participation,  as  has  been  heretofore
granted  by the Court  to  other  intervenors  in this  cause.
We  would  have  no  objection,  however,  if  the  Court
should  provide  in  its  Order  that  no  claims  or  defenses
previously.  adjudicated  will  be  reasserted,  with  the  ex-
ception  of  such  as  may  directly  affect  the  interests  of
The Grosse Pointe  Public School  System.

We also note that in your  notice  to  counsel  for present  parties  to
the litigation,  you have scheduled the receipt  of briefs on the pro-
remedy  on March  22, 1972.  Because  we
priety  of a metropolitan
will  not  be  informed  by  the  Court  until  on  or  about  March  15,
1972  as  to  whether  or  not  intervention  will  be  permitted,  The  -
Grosse  Pointe  Public  Schoo! System  requests  than an  extension of
time for this purpose  be granted.

Very truly  yours,

HILL,  LEWIS,  ADAMS,  GOODRICH  & TAIT

Douglas  H. West

RULING  AND  ORDER  ON PETITIONS  FOR  INTERVENTION

At  a session  of said Court held in the Federal  Building,  City of
Detroit,  County  of Wayne,  on  this  15th  day of MARCH,  A.D.
1972.

PRESENT:  HONORABLE  STEPHEN  J. ROTH,

United  States  District  Judge

The  motion  of intervening  defendants  Denise  Magdowski,  et
al.  to  add  parties  defendant  is continued  under  advisement,  to
await  further  developments  in this proceeding.

Ruling  on  the  motion  of  the  Jefferson-Chalmers  Citizens
District  Council  to  intervene  is  continued  in accordance  with  the
request  of the  movant.

The  motions  of Allen  Park  Public  Schools,  et  al.,  the  Grosse
Pointe  Public  Schools;  the  School  District  of  the  City  of Royal
Oak  and  the  Southfield  Public  Schools  are  GRANTED,  under

Ia  205

Federal  Rules  of Civil  Procedure,  Rule  24(a)(2)  and,  in the alterna-
tive,  under  Rule  24(a)(2),  under  conditions  hereinafter  specified.

The  motion  of  Kerry  Green,  ‘et  al.,  including  Tri-County
Citizens  for  Intervention  in  Federal  School  Action  No.  35257,  is
GRANTED  under  Rule  24(b)  (2),  under  conditions  hereinafter
specified.  The  intervention  of  the  Tri-County  Citizens  for  Inter-
vention  is granted  for  the  following  reasons:  (1) The  standing  of
the  National  Association for the  Advancement  of Colored  People
was  not  challenged  by the  original  parties  to  this  action;  (2)  For
practical  purposes  the  grant  of intervention  to  Kerry Green,  et al.
is a grant  of intervention  to said  organization.  The  court,  for  the
reasons  stated,  has  not  and  does  not  pass  on  the  procedural
propriety  of either  the standing  of the NAACP  or the  intervention
of the citizens’  group.

The  motion  of the City of Warren,  a municipal  corporation  of
the  State  of Michigan,  to  intervene,  is DENIED,  as  of right, under
Rule  24(a){2),  and  in  the  discretion  of  the court,  pommel Rule
24(b)(2).

The  motion  of  Nancy  Bird,  et  al.;  to  intervene,  is DENIED,
under  Rule  24(a)(2),  and under  Rule  24(b)(2),  in the discretion  of
the  court,  for the reason  that  their  interests  are  already  adequately
represented  by the  parties,  including  those  to  whom  iritervention
has been  granted  this a

The motion  of Professional Personnel  of Van  Dyke, to  inter-
vene,  is DENIED,  under  Rule  24(a)(2) and, in the  discretion  of the
court, under  Rule  24(b)(2).

‘The petitioners who  have  been  denied  intervention  shall  have

aright  to appear  as — curiae.  _

The  interventions  granted 1 this  day  shall  be  pk  to the  fol-

lowing conditions:

la  206

1.  No intervenor  will be permitted  to assert  any claim or

by the court.
defense  previously  adjudicated

2.  No intervenor  shall reopen  any question  or issue which  has

been decided  by the court.
previously

3. The  participation  of  the intervenors  considered  this day
shall be subordinated  to that of  the original  parties and previous
intervenors.

4.  The new intervenors  shall  not initiate  discovery  pro-
ceedings except by permission  of the court upon application in
writing, accompanied  by a showing  that no present  party plans to
or is willing to undertake  the particular  discovery  sought and that
the particular  matter to be discovered is relevant to the current
stage of the proceedings.

5.  No new intervenor  shall be permitted  to seek a  delay  of
any proceeding  in this cause;  and he shall  be bound  by the brief
and hearing  schedule  established  by the court’s Notice to Counsel,
issued  March’6,  1972.

6.  New intervenors  will not file counterclaims  or cross-
complaints;  nor will they be permitted  to seek the joinder of
additional  parties or the dismissal of present parties, except upon a
showing  that such action will not result in delay.

of this court.
orders

8.  New intervenors  shall  present  evidence,  if any they have,
through  witnesses to a  number  to  be set, and limited, if necessary,
by the court, following  conference.

eee

1a  207

9.  With regard to the examination  of  witnesses, all new inter-
yenors  shall  among  themselves  select  one  attorney  per  witness  to
act for them,  unless  one or more of the new intervenors  show
cause otherwise.

These  conditions  of  intervention  shall  remain  subject  to
change or modification  by  the court in the interest of timely dis-
position  of the case.

DATE:  March  15,  1972.

OBJECTIONS  TO  CONDITIONS  PLACED  ON  INTERVENORS
BY  THE  RULING  AND  ORDER  ON  PETITIONS  FOR
INTERVENTION  DATED  MARCH  15,  1972

NOW  COMES  Allen  Park  Public  Schools,  et  al,  Defendants-
Intervenors,  by  their  attorneys,  Butzel,  Long,  Gust,  Klein  & Van
Zile,  and  do hereby  make  objections  to  the  conditions  imposed  by
this  Court  in  connection  with  the  granting  of  said  Defendants-
Intervenors  Motion  for  Intervention  upon  the  following  grounds:

1.  “No  intervenor  shall  be  permitted  to  assert  any  claim  or

defense  perviously  adjudicated  by the Court.”

Defendants-Intervenors  object  to  the  foregoing  condition  to
the  extent  that  the  same  is  intended  to,  or  does,  preclude

Ia  210

Defendants-Intervenors  from  asserting  any  claim  or  defense  in
pursuance  of protecting  the  rights  and  interest  of said  Defendant-
s-Intervenors,  and  with  respect  to  which  Defendants-Intervenors
have not had the opportunity  to present  evidence  and argument.

In acknowledging  Defendants-Intervenors  right to intervene  in
this  action,  pursuant  to  Rule  24  (a2)  of the  Federal  Rules  of
Civil Procedure,  this Court necessarily  took cognizance  of the fact
that  Defendants-Intervenors  have  not  been,  and  are  not  now,
adequately  represented  by  the principal  parties  and  prior
Accordingly,  it  is  submitted  that  to  preclude
intervenors.
Defendants-Intervenors  from  asserting  claims  or  defenses,  limited
to  the  protection  of their  own interests,  because  said claim or
defense  may  have  been previously  adjudicated  by  the Court,  is
unreasonable  and  serves  to  effectively  preclude  Defendants-
Intervenors  from  adequately  presenting  a  case  in  protection  of
their  own  interests.

As  stated  in  Moore’s  Federal  Practice,  Second  Edition,

Volume  3-B, Section  24.  16[4].

“There  seems  ta  be  no  doubt  that  an  intervenor  having
an  absolute  right  to  intervene is able  to raise  any  one  of these
questions.  It  would be meaningless  to give him an absolute
right  to  intervene  in  order  to  protect  his  interest,  if once  in
the  proceeding  he  were  barred  from  raising  questions
necessary  for his own protection  .

.  .”

2.  “No  intervenor  shall  re-open  any  question  or  issue  which

has previously  been  decided  by the court,”

Defendants-Intervenors  object  to  the  foregoing  condition  for
the same  reasons  and  on the  same  grounds  as set forth
immediately  above in paragraph  1.  -

3.  “The  participation  of the  intervenors  considered  this day
shall  be subordinated  to  that of the original  parties  and previous
intervenors.””

The  subordination  requirement  that  was  contained  in  Equity

la2lil

Rule  37,  which  governed  intervention  prior  to  the  adoption  of
was not carried
Rule  24  of the Federal  Rules of Civil  Procedure,
over  into  the  present  Rule  24.  In  this  regard,  Moore's  Federal
Practice,  Second  Edition,  Volume  3-B,  Section  24.05,  notes  as
follows:

“Rule  24  has made one important  change  in substance.  It
did not incorporate  the provisions  of the Equity  Rule which
defined  the  status  of the  intervenor.  There  is  no requirement
that  the  intervention  be  in  subordination  to,  and  in
recognition  of,the  propriety  of the main proceeding. In brief,
the intervenor’s  claim  or defense  is to be heard  on the
merits.”

;

And,  in  In  Re  Oceana  International,  Inc.,  49  FRD  329,  333-334
(S. D. NY,  1969; rehearing  1970),  the Court  stated  that  —

“Once  intervention  as  of  right  has  been  granted,  an
intervenor  should  be  entitled  to  litigate  fully  on  the  merits,
see  Park  &  Tilford,  Inc.  ¥ Schulte,  160  F 2d  984  (2d  Cir.
1947),  and  to  be  considered  a  party  for  all  purposes,  /n  re
Raabe,  Glissman  &  Co.,  71  F.  Supp.  678,  680  (S. D.  N.  Y.
1947).”

The  Court

having  necessarily  found  that  Defendants-
Intervenors  interest  were  not  adequately  represented  by  the
existing  parties,  it  is  incongruous  and  unreasonable  to  then
subordinate  Defendants-Intervenors  participation,  in protection  of
their  own  interest,  to  that  of  the  original  parties  and  previous
intervenors.

;

Moreover,

Defendants-Intervenors

of  no
basis  upon  which  their  participation  should  be
reasonable
or otherwise
subordinated  to that  of the previous  intervenors
circumscribed  in  any  manner  different  from  the conditions, or
lack of conditions,  imposed  upon  the  previous  intervenors.

perceive

can

4.

“Thenew  intervenors

shall

not  initiate  discovery

proceedings  except  by permission  of the court upon application in
writing,  accompanied  by a  showing  that  no  present  party  plans to

1a212

"i

or  is willing  to  undertake  the  particular  discovery  sought  and  that
the  particvlar  matter  to  be  discovered  is  relevant  to  the  current
stage  of the proceedings.”

Defendants-Intervenors  are  “parties”  to the  instant  action  and
should  not  be  precluded  from  initiating  discovery  proceedings
pursuant  to  Rule  26  of  the  Federal  Rules  of Civil  Procedure  in
furtherance  of protecting  their  interest  in the litigation.

“So  once  intervention  has  been  granted  the  intervenor
becomes  a  ‘party’,  within  the  meaning  of the  Rules,  ‘entitled
_to  litigate  fully  on  the  merits.’  Park  &  Tilford  v.Schulte,  2
Cir.,  160  F2d  984,  note  1, certiorari  denied,  1947,  322  U.S.
761,  68  S. Ct.  64, 92  L.  Ed  347;  see:  French  v.Gapen,  1881,
105  U.  S.  509,  525,  26  L.  Ed  951;  Galbreath  v. Metropolitan
Trust  Co.,  10  Cir.,  1943,  134. F 2d  845,  859.”  Hartley  Pen
Co.  v. Lindy  Pen  Co., 16 FRD  141,  153,  20  FR  Serv  24c  31,
Case  2 (S. D, Cal  1954).

—

While  it is within  the  sound  discretion  of the  Court  to  place
bounds  on  the  extent  of discovery,  such  discretion  should  not  be
exercised  so  as  to  affect  a  party’s  substantial  rights.  Goldman  v
Checker  Taxi  Co.,  325  F2d  853, 856  (CA  7, 1963).

The  foregoing  condition  is  unduly  restrictive  and  deprives
Defendants-Intervenors  of  the  liberal  treatment  intended  to  be
accorded  discovery  rules  by. the  Federal  Rules  of Civil  Procedure.
It  is submitted  that  any  limitations  upon  the  right  of discovery
should  be  made“ at  the  time  discovery  is  sought,  taking  into
account  the  purpose  of the  requested  discovery  and  its  relevancy
to the proceedings.

5.  “No  new  intervenor  shall  be  permitted  to seek  a delay of
any  proceeding  in this cause;  and he shall be bound  by the  brief
and  hearing  schedule  established  by the court’s  Notice  to Counsel,
issued  March  6, 1972.”

While  Defendants-Intervenors  do  not  intend  to  seek  to  delay
the  proceedings  for  frivilous  reasons,  they’  object  to  being
summarily  foreclosed  from  making  any  application  to the  Court

Ia213

for  adjournment  or  continuance  of the  proceedings  for good cause
shown.

The  instant  case  was  commenced  on  August  18,  1970,  and
from  the  date  of  commencement  until  the  Court’s  request  for
submission  of a so-called  Metropolitan  Plan,  on  October  4, 1971,
the case  was concerned  solely  with  the City  of Detroit  school
the establishment  of
out as a case involving
district.  What  started
attendance  zones  in the  City  of Detroit  so  as  to perpetuate  racial
segregation  of students in  that  school  district  has now  tumed  into
a case  which  may  affect  numerous  independent  school  districts.  In
this regard  the  case  has  been  expanded  far  beyond  the
constitutional  mandate  requiring  that  the  Detroit  school  district
maintain  a non-segregated  school  system.

There  is no  established  judicial  precedent  for  the adoption  of
such  a  broad  and  sweeping  plan as the so-called  Metropolitan  Plan
submitted  to  this Court.  In this  regard  this case presents  novel
questions  which  bear  upon  the  structure  and  operation  of school
districts  independent  from  the  City  of Detroit  and  would  require
that  certain  laws  of  the  State  of  Michigan  dealing  with  the
organization  and  operation  of  school  districts  be  directly  or
indirectly  declared  unconstitiutional.

In  short,  the  posture  of  the  instant  action  has  changed
drastically  from  its inception  to the  present  stage  of proceedings,
The  desire  and  need  for  expeditious  resolution  of  the  matters
before  the  Court  is conceded.  However,  a rush  to judgment  at the
expense  of  depriving  interested  and  affected  parties  a  fair  and
ample  opportunity  to  prepare  a case  in protection  of their  interest
would  be improvident  and  unreasonable.

In  Goss  v  Board  of  Education  of  the  City  of Knoxville,
Tennessee,  444  F  2d  632  (CA  6,  1971),  the  Court  refused  to
impose  a  rigid  timetable  in a school  desegregation  case,  noting  as
follows:

ad

“Therefore,  it  has  taken  the  federal  judiciary  of  this
country  17  years  to  come  up  with  final  answers,  it  will  be

la214.

judicially  arrogant  for  us  to  tell  the  school  authorities  of
Knoxville  to  get  the job  done  in  the  few  months  that  will  be
available  before  the  opening  of the  1971  fall  session.

..

“The  hope  or dream,  that  one day we will have  become  a
people  without  any  motivations  born  of our  different  racial
beginnings  will  have  a  better  chance  of fulfillment  if patience
accompanies  our  endeavors.  Strident  and  truculent  judicial
commands  could  indeed  exacerbate  what  may  now  remain  of
racial  bias  and  prejudice.”  /bid,  at pp.  639-640.

Defendants-Intervenors  had  no  occasion  or  standing  to
intervene  in  this  case  until  the Court  undertook  consideration  of a
‘remedy  directly  affecting  them.  Defendants-Intervenors  were
granted  leave  to  intervene  only  five  (5) days  ago  and  counsel  did
not  even  have  copies  of  the  so-called  Metropolitan  plans  until
three  days  ago.  At  this  time  counsel  for  Defendants-Intervenors
cannot  foretell  all  future  requirements  in  connection  with  the
protection  of Defendants-Intervenors  interest.

Defendants-Intervenors  agree  that  delay  merely  for  the  sake
of  delay  should
not  be  countenanced.  It  is  resepectfully
the  Court  should  reconsider  its
submitted,  however,  that
declaration  absolutely  foreclosing  any  request  for  continuance  or
adjournment  and  permit  the  filing  of such  request,  the  same  to
be  considered  by  the  Court  and  determined  on  the  basis  of
inherent  fairness  to  all  concerned  as  well  as  the  desire  for
expeditious  conclusion  of the  proceedings.

6.  “New  intervenors

or
cross-complaints;  nor  will  they  be permitted  to seek  the joinder of
additional  parites  or  the  dismissal  of present  parties,  except  upon  a
showing  that  such  action  will  not  result  in delay.”

file  counterclaims

will  not

Defendants-Intervenors  are  “parties”  to  the  instant  litigation
within  the meaning  of the  Federal  Rules  of Civil  Procedure  and  are
entitled  to  litigate  fully  on  the  merits.  Hartley  Pen  Co.  v  Lindy
Pen  Co.,  16  FRD  141,  153,  20  FR  Serv  24c31,  Case  2 (SD  Cal.
1954).  Fundamental  due  process  requires  that  a  party  sought  to
be affected  by a proceeding  shall  have  the right to raise  such issues

Ta  215

or  set  up  any  defense  which  he may  have  in the cause.  As noted  in
Holt  v  Virginia,  381  US  131,  136(1963),14  LEd  2d 290, 85 S Ct
1376  -

“The  right  to  be  heard  must  necessarily  embody a right
to  file  motions  and  pleadings  essential  to  present  claims  and
raise relevant  issues.”

While  the  disposition  of any  counterclaims,  cross-complaints
or other  pleadings  rests  with  the  sound  discretion  of the Court, it
is submitted  that  a  denial  of the  right  to  file  any  pleadings,  and  a
prospective  rejection  of same  without  consideration,  is unreason-
able.

7.  “New  intervenors  are  granted  intervention  for  two
principal  purposes:  (a) To  advise  the  court,  by brief,  of the  legal
propriety  or  impropriety  of  considering  a  metropolitan  plan;  (b)
To  review  any  plan  or  plans  for  the  desegregation  of the  so-called
larger  Detroit  Metropolitan  area,  and  submitting  objections,
modifications  or  alternatives  to it or  them,  and in accordance  with
the  requirements  of the  United  States  Constitution  and  the  prior
orders  of this court.”

Defendants-Intervenors  have  intervened  in  this  action  for  the
purpose  of  protecting  their  interest  in  the  subject  matter  and
submit  that  their  participation  in  these  proceedings  may  not
properly  be  circumscribed  so  as  to  preclude  them  from  fully  and
adequately  protecting  such  interest.

in

that

note

larger

Defendants-Intervenors

the  above-quoted
condition  the  Court  refers  to  “plans  for  the  desegregation  of the
Defendants-

or

determination  as  to whether  the larger Detroit  Metropolitan  area  is
unlawfully  segregated  and  that  consideration  of any  plan  on  the

submit

_

8.  ‘New  intervenors  shall  present  evidence,  if any  they  have,
through  witnesses  to  a  number  to be set, and limited,  if necessary,
by the court,  following  conference.”

Ia 216

Defendants-Intervenors  have  no  objection  to  the  above
condition  as stated  but  reserve  the  right  to present  objections  to
the  extent  any  ruling precludes  Defendants-Intervenors  from a full
and  fair  presentation  of their  case  in  protection  of their  interest.

9.  “With  regard’  to  the  examination  of witnesses,  all  new
intervenors  shall  among themselves  select  one  attorney  per witness
to  act  for  them,  unless  one  or  more  of the  new  intervenors  show
cause  otherwise.”

Defendants-Intervenors  submit  that  the above-quoted  con-
dition  is unreasonable,  improper  and  will  serve  to  hinder,  rather
than  assist,  the expeditious  trial  of this  proceeding.

In granting  the  motions  to intervene,  the Court  acknowledged
the  independent  interest  of  the  various  intervenors.  To  require
then  that  parties  with  varied  and  independent  interests  must
relinquish  their  right to  have  their  cause  presented by the counsel.
of their  choice is unreasonable.

In Chandler  v Fretag,  348  US  3, 10 (1954),  99  L Ed 4, 75S
Ct  1, the  U.  S. Supreme  Court,  with  emphasis,  stated  as  follows:

_..  If  in  any  case,  civil  or  criminal,  a  state  or federal
court  were  arbitrarily  to  refuse  to  hear  a  party  by counsel,
employed  by and appearing for him,  it reasonably  may  not  be
doubted  that  such  a  refusal  would  be  a  denial  of a  hearing,
and,  therefore,  of due process  in the constitutional  sense.”

The  problems  involved in  all of the  new  intervenors  selecting  a.
single  attorney  to  examine  or  cross-examine  a  witness  not  only
poses  practical  problems  for  the  intervenors  and  their  counsel  but
will,  in all likelihood,  serve  to  unnecessarily  burden  the  hearing.
For  example,  if  a  witness  called  by  Plaintiffs  is  tendered  for
cross-examination,  counsel  would  need  time  to  confer  among
themselves  in  order  to  determine  who  was  to  conduct  the
cross-examination  and  what  the  extent  and  nature  of  the  cross-
examination  would  encompass.  It is submitted  that  the Court  can
adequately  control  the  examination  by individual  counsel  through

1a217

‘its  power  to  regulate  the  extent  of  cumulative  and  improper
examination.

Defendant-Intervenors

are  informed  that  prior-admitted
intervenors  were  not  subjected  to the condition  above-quoted  and
were  permitted  to  protect  their  interests  through  the  counsel
chosen  and  employed  by  them.  As  noted  hereinbefore,  we  do not
perceive  any  reasonable  basis  for  proscribing  the  participation  of
Defendants-  Intervenors  having  permitted  previous  intervenors  to
participate  in  a  manner  adequate  to  protect  their  interests  and
of  their  own  choosing.  The  only  reason
through  counsel
Defendants-Intervenors  did  not  request intervention  at an  earlier
date  is  because  there  was  no  indication  that  these  proceedings
would  affect  their  interest.  The  proceedings,  however,  now  affect
their  interests  and  they  should  be  accorded  the  right  to  be  fully
represented  by their  own  counsel.

The  management  problem  attendant  to  the  presence  of  a
number  of  attorneys  representing  different  parties  does  not
provide  a reasonable  basis  for the emasculation  of the fundamental
right  of  a  party  to  have his case  presented  by counsel  employed
for such  purpose.

Defendants-Intervenors  respectfully  request  that  this  Court
reconsider  the  conditions  imposed  in  connection  with  their
intervention  and  permit  Defendants-Intervenors  to  protect  their
interest  in  these  proceedings  consonant  with  established  rules  of
fairness  and  due  process  customarily  accorded  to  parties  litigant.

Respectfully  submitted,

BUTZEL,  LONG,  GUST,  KLEIN  & VAN  ZILE

OBJECTIONS  OF GROSSE  POINTE  SCHOOLS
TO CONDITIONS  ON INTERVENTION  BY
RULING  AND  ORDER  DATED  MARCH  15,  1972

GROSSE  POINTE  SCHOOLS,  Defendant  Intervenor,  by  its
attorneys,  Hill,  Lewis,  Adams,  Goodrich  & Tait,  objects  to  the
conditions  placed  on  its participation in  this cause  as  contained in
paragraphs  | through  9 of, the  Ruling  and  Order  on  Petitions  for
Intervention  entered  by this Honorable  Court  on  March  15,  1972,
as  follows:

1)  Grosse  Pointe  Schools  objects  to  conditions  1 and  2, to
the extent  the same  are more  restrictive  than those  proposed  in
Grosse  Pointe  School’s  letter  of suggested  conditions  addressed  to
this  Court,  dated  March  13,  1972,  a  copy  of  which  is  hereto
attached.

2)  Grosse  Pointe  Schools  objects  to  conditions  3 through  9,
for  the  reason  that  Grosse  Pointe  Schools  has  been  granted  inter-
vention  by  this  Court,  of  right,  under  Rule  24(a)  (2)  of  the
Federal  Rules  of  Civil  Procedure,  and  it should  accordingly  be

1a  219

granted  the  full unsubordinated  status  of a party  defendant  to this
cause  so  that  it may  adequately  protect  its interests.

3)  In  support  of  the  objections  herein  contained,  Grosse
Pointe  Schools  adopts,  by  reference,  the  reasons  therefor
contained  in  the  Objections  to  Conditions,  filed  by  Defendants-
Intervenors  Allen  Park  Public  Schools,  et al.

PLAINTIFFS’  PROPOSED  FINDINGS,  CONCLUSIONS  AND
ORDER  ON  DETROIT  —  ONLY  DESEGREGATION  PLANS

On  the  basis  of the entire  record  in this action,  including
particularly  the  evidence  heard  by the  Court  from  March  14,:
1972,  through  March  21,  1972,  the Court  now  makes  the fol-
lowing  Supplementary  Findings  of  Fact  and  Conclusions  of
Law.

Supplementary  Findings  of Fact

1.  Defendant  Detroit  Board’s  desegregation  Plan  A  (D.X.
C-10)  would  not  accomplish  substantial  desegregation,  and  it is
educationally  unsound  and  of  doubtful  feasibility.  (Foster,  Tr.
293-297,  March  16,  1972)

1.1  Detroit’s  experience,  like  that  of  school  systems  else-
where,  has  been  that  freedom  of choice,  student  option  plans, and
controlled  transfer  have  been  ineffective  as  desegregation  devices.

Ta  225

(Foster,  Tr.  295-296,  March  16,  1972; Progress  Report,  November
1, 1971,  p.16; McDonald,  Tr.  70-72, March  14,  1972).

1.2  At  the  high  school  level,  Plan  A  proposes  an  enhanced
version  of the  specialized  curriculum  model  which  was  an  almost
total  failure,  as  a  desegregation  plan,  during  1971-72.  (McDonald,
Tr.  57,  March  14,  1972;  Progress  Report,  p.  13-16  Foster,  Tr.
295,  March  16,  1972)  No  witness  for  the  Detroit  Board  was  able
to assure  the Court  that  desegregation
at the high  school  level
would  be significantly  improved  under  Plan  A.  (Rankin,  Tr.  598,
613,  650-651;  McDonald,  Tr.  61,  83,  85,  90-91,  March  14,  1972;
D.X. C-11,  pp.  1-2)

1.3  At  the  magnet  middle  school  level,  grades  3 to 8, Plan  A
could  affirmatively  affect  only  20,000  of  the  approximately
140,000  students  in  those  grades,  and  one  side-effect  has  been  in
1971-72,  and  would  be,  to  intensify  segregation  at  other  schools.
(Rankin,  Tr.  608,  612,  615,  March  21,  1971;  D.X.  C-11,  p.5;  Pro-
gress  Report,  [Appendix, Individual  Magnet  Middle  Schools  Trans-
fer Reports]  )

1.4  The  magnet  middle  schools  component  of Plan  A would
set  up  a  limited  number  of greater  resource  schools,  which  would
be attended  by a  higher  proportion  of the  white  students  in those
grades,  and  would,  by virtue  of the  50/50  or  60/40  (black/white),
racial  quota  system,  assign  a higher proportion  of black  students  at
those  grade  levels  to  lesser  resource  schools.  (McDonald,  Tr.98,
March  14,  1971;  Rankin,  Tr.  607,  March  21,  1971)
Such arrange-
ments  were  characterized  by an  expert  witness  for the plaintiffs  as
educationally  unsound  and  by  an  expert  witness  for  the  Detroit
defendants  as  unconstitutional.  (Foster,  Tr.  294,  296,  March  16,
1972;  Guthrie,  Tr.  491,  March  17,  1972)

1.5  The  cost  factors  that  would  be  associated  with  the
implementation  of  Plan  A,  even  as  proposed  and  certainly  if it
were  to  be  expanded  in  the  hope  of  increasing  its effectiveness,
compel  the  finding  that  it would  be economically  less  feasible  and
practicable  than  plaintiffs’  plan of desegregation  (PC-2),  as  amend-
ed  (McDonald,  Tr.  101,  March  14,  1972;  Foster,  Tr.  296,  March
16, 1972)

La  226

1.6  The  practically  unanimous  expert  testimony  in  this
record  is  to  the effect  that,  educationally,  desegregation  is
especially  important in the early  grades,  and the Court so finds.
The  Court  also  finds,  as  was  testified  by an  expert  witness  for the
defendants,  that  the  omission  of  grades  |  and  2 from  Detroit
defendants’  Plan  A  is  educationally  unsound.  So,  too,  if
practicable  arrangements  can  be  made,  is the  omission  of kinder-
garden  children.  (Rankin,  Tr.  648-649,  March  21,  1972;  Guthrie,
Tr.  495, March  17,  1972; Foster,  Tr.  333-334,  March  16,  1972)

1.7  In several  of their submissions  to the Court,  usually  in the
context  of  espousing  or  commenting  upon  metropolitan  ap-
proaches,  the  defendants  have  indicated  that  pupil  assignment
plans  are  more  effective  in  accomplishing  desegregation  than
magnet  plans.  See,  for  example,  defendant  Detroit  Board’s  Basic
Guidelines  for  a  Metropolitan  Detroit  Area  Integration  Plan,
February  29,  1972, p. 33; and see  State  Defendants’  submission  of
February  1,  1972,  Metropolitan  Magnet  Plan.  The  Court  agrees
and so  finds.

1.8  The  Detroit  defendants  have  criticised,  upon  educational
grounds,  the  omission  of  kindergarden  to  grade  3 students  from
other  plans  in  this  record.  See  Objections  of  Detroit  Board  of
Education,  March  4,  1972,  pp.  12-13.  This  Court  finds  that
criticism  also applicable  to Detroit’s  Plan  A.

2.  The  Detroit  defendants  have  also  submitted  as  a plan  of
desegregation  for  the  Court’s  consideration  a Special  Humanities
Schools  plan  (D.X.  C-12)  referred  to  in  testimony  as  Plan  C.
(McDonald,  Tr.  49,  March  14,  1972) One  form  of this plan,  which
would  operate  only  at  grades  3 to 6,  would  affect  not  more  than
40,000  children  on  a part-time  basis  (approximately  40 percent,  at
most,  of their  school  time).  Its additional  cost  would  approximate
the additional  transportation  costs  roughly  projected  for plaintiffs’
12-grade  desegregation  plan.  (Compare  D.X.  C-12,  p.  3  with
Foster,  Tr.  346,  March  16,  1972)  A  variation  of  this  Plan,  also
limited  to grades  3  to 6, would  involve  partial  pairing  of  ele-
mentary  schools  for humanities  programs.

1a227

Such  programs  may  be  educationally  innovative  or  otherwise
attractive,  but  none  of  the  testimony  on  behalf  of  any  party
supported  Plan  C  as  effective  to  accomplish  desegregation.  The
Defendant  Detroit  Board  preferred  Plan  A, and, in addition  to the
part-time  desegregation  feature,  it is undisputed  that  Plan  C would
leave  the  “base”  schools  no  less  racially  identifiable  (80 percent  or
more  black  or  white,  according  to D.X.  C12;  and  see  Rankin,  Tr.
616-618,  March  21,  1972)  than  they  are  today.  In addition,  the
school  pairing  variation,  by  its  50/50  racial  quota,  would  appa-
rently  as in the case  of  magnet  middle  schools  in Plan  A, assign  a
disproportionate  number  of black  pupils  to  nonspecial  schools  at
grades 3 to 6.

The  Court  agrees  on  this question  with  Detroit  Board  member
McDonald  who  testified  (McDonald,  Tr.  50, March  14,  1972):  “TI
don’t  think  that  that  would  accomplish  the  goal  of either  inte-
gration  or  desegregation.”  That  was  also  the  testimony  of plain-
tiffs’  expert,  Dr.  Gordon  Foster,  and  the  Court  so  finds.  (Foster,
Tr. 297-298,  March  16,  1972)

3.  The  Court  finds  that  neither  of  the  defendant  Detroit
Board’s  plans,  A or  C, would  accomplish  a  significant  amount  of
school  desegregation  in  the  Detroit  district.  The  Court  further
finds  that  both  plans  are  educationally  less  sound  than  available
alternatives,  including  metropolitan  desegregation,  and  that  Plan
A, which  is more  specifically  developed  than  Plan  C, is econo-
mically  less  feasible  than  available  alternatives,  including  plaintiffs’
Plan.  (Compare  McDonald,  Tr.  100,  March  14,  1972;  with Foster,
Tr. 346,  March  16,  1972.)

4.  Plaintiffs  have  also  submitted  to the Court  a desegregation
plan (PC-2,  PC-3),  which  has been  the subject  of considerable  testi-
mony.  (Foster,  Tr.  302-348,  March  16,  1972)

4.1  Measured  by  the  numbers  of  black  and  white  Detroit
pupils  who  would  attend  schools  together,  it  is undisputed  that
plaintiffs’  plan  would  accomplish  more  desegregation  of  the
Detroit  school  system  than  now  obtains  or  than  would  be achieved
by any  other  plan  proposed.  (Rankin,  Tr.  594,  596-7,  March  21,
1972; McDonald,  Tr. 49, March  14,  1972).

la  228

4.2  Although  the advisability  of implementing  plaintiffs’  plan
for  September  of  1972,  if a  substantially  different  plan  were  to
follow  shortly  thereafter,  was  challenged,  the  preponderance  of
the  evidence  is clearly  to  the  effect  that,  assuming  the availability
of  the  necessary  pupil  transportation  equipment,  it  would  be
administratively  feasible  to  implement  plaintiffs’  plans  for
September  of  1972.  (Foster,  Tr.  347-348,  March  16,  1972;
Guthrie,  Tr. 498, March  17,  1972)

4.3  Plaintiffs’  expert  testified  that  if.a  plan  must  be  limited
to  Detroit,  his  plan  is educationally  sound  and  should  be  imple-
mented  in  preference  to  continuing  the  present  segregation  and
the  Court  so  finds.  (Foster,  Tr.  299-301,  312,  348,  350-351,
364-365,  376,  390,  401, March  16,  1972;  Cf.  Guthrie,  Tr. 498-9,
March  17,  1972)  The  Detroit  defendants  have strongly  challenged
plaintiffs’  plan,  but neither  they nor  any  other  party  has presented
to  the  court  assertedly  preferable  plans  that  could  accomplish

-  equivalent  desegregation.

4.4  The  defendants’  criticism  of  plaintiffs’  plan  may  be
summarized  as  follows:  (a)  it fails  to  upgrade  the  socioeconomic
status (SES)  of Detroit  schools,  which  is an  important  determinant
of educational  quality;  (b) virtually  all schools  would  be majority
black,  which  would  accelerate  white  flight  to  the  suburbs;  (c)
suburban  children,  mostly  white,  will  continue  to  be  denied  the
benefits  of school  desegregation;  (d) racial  tensions  are  most  acute
among  lower  SES children,  so a desegregation
plan limited  to such
children  would  result  in greater  social  friction  than  would  a plan
involving  a  higher  percentage  of  higher  SES  children;  and  (e)  it
would  be administratively  imprudent  to implement  plaintiffs’  plan
if a  metropolitan  desegregation  plan, which  would  involve  another
round  of  administrative  changes,  is  to  be  adopted  thereafter.
(Rankin,  Tr.  572-589,  620-626,  March  21,  1972;  Guthrie,  Tr.
461-474,  March  17,  1972)

One  of the  defendants’  experts,  Dr.  Rankin,  agreed  that, given
the  present  degree  of segregation  in  Detroit  and  the  correlation
between  black  and.  lower  SES,
an  intra-Detroit  plan  would
increase  somewhat  the  SES  status  of  schools  attended  by  black

Ia  229

children.  That  is, there  would  be a leveling  effect,  but he also  testi-
fied  that  in  his  opinion  the  increase  would  be  educationally
insignificant.  (Rankin,  Tr.  620-623,  March  21,  1972)  Dr.  Rankin
also  testified  that  SES  considerations  were  not  a  factor  in  the
preparation  of Detroit’s  own  plans,  and  that  the loss  of benefits  to
suburban  children  would  be  true  of any  plan  limited  to  Detroit.
(Rankin,  Tr.  633-4,  March  21,  1972)

The  defendants’  criticisms  of  plaintiffs’  plan  were  almost
entirely  in  comparison  to  a  metropolitan  plan.  Dr.  Guthrie  did
testify  that  he  would  hesitate  to  desegregate  a  majority  black,
lower  SES  district  because  the  educational  benefits  at  least  in the
achievement  sense,  would  be  dubious;  but he also  testified  that  he
would  move  to  remedy  segregation  resulting  from  illegal  acts  and
practices  wherever  it  exists.  (Guthrie,  Tr.  487,  443-4,  499-500,
March  17,  1972)

Dr.  Foster  testified  that  the  concept  used  in  plaintiffs’  plan
could  well be  compatible  with  a  metropolitan  plan.  (Foster,  Tr.
348-350,  March  16,  1972)  While  there  would  be  substantial
administrative  changes  and  pupil  reassignment  should  a  metropo-
litan  plan  of  desegregation  subsequently  be  entered,  Dr.  Foster
testified  and  the  Court  so  finds,  that  implementation  of plaintiffs’
plan  as  an  interim  plan  would  provide  educational  benefits  and
would  be  preferable  to  continuing  the  present  segregation  for
another  year.  (Foster,  Tr.  348-350,  March  16,  1972)

4.5  The  defendants’  transportation  expert  testified  that  new
transportation  equipment  would  be  available  for  the  fall,  1972
term  if ordered  in  March  or  April.  Plaintiffs’  experts  testified  that
such  an  order  should  be  placed  immediately.  (Kuthy,  Tr.  134,
144,  155,  March  15,  1972;  Smith,  Tr.  420,  439,  442,  March  17,
1972;  see  also  Foster,  Tr.  383-384,  March  16,  1972) On the  basis
of the  present  record,  the  Court  finds  that  the  new  transportation
equipment  needed  to  implement  plaintiffs’  plan  in  September  of
1972  can  be secured.

4.6  Plaintiffs’  witness  Foster  estimated  that  the  annual
additional  cost  to  the  system  for  new  transportation  would  be
somewhat  over  4  million  dollars  (including  amortization  for
capital  expenditures)  2  percent  or  less  of  the  District’s  annual

1a  230

budget,  and  that  the  amount  would  be  lower  if buses  averaged
more  than  twotrips  (which  would  be feasible  and  not  uncommon)
or  if kindergarden  children  were  omitted  from  his  plan.  (Foster,
Tr.  346,  March  16,  1972)  Plaintiffs’  witness  Smith’s  testimony
indicated  a  lower  cost  figure  based  upon  his  computation  which
involved  more  children  per  bus  and  fewer  new  buses.  (Smith,  Tr.
417,  March  17,  1972)  The  testimony  also  indicated  that,  to some
extent,  the  existing  facilities  of the  local  public  transit  company
(DSR)  could  be  used  and  meshed  with  new  school  busing,  thus
reducing  at  least  new  capital  costs  still  further.  (Smith,  Tr.
420-421,  March  17, 1972; Foster,  Tr. 383, March  16,  1972)

The  foregoing  testimony  was  largely  uncontroverted,  and  on

the present  record,  the Court  finds it to be factual.

4.7  Taken  as  a  whole,  the  testimony  concerning  pupil  trans-
portation  established  that  it  is  a  long  standing,  educationally
sound  practice  and  safer  than  walking  to  school.  (Smith,  Tr.
408-409,  March  17,  1972;  Kuthy,Tr.  140,  156-157,  March  15,
1972)  There  are  limits  on  the  times  and  distance  that  children  at
various  ages  should  be  transported,  but  nothing  in the record  sug-
gests  that  the  additional  transportation  involved  in plaintiffs’  plan
(or those  of the  defendants)  would  approach  such  limitations,  and
the  Court  so  finds.  (Foster,  Tr.  333-334,  March  16,  1972)  The
Court  also  finds,  as  testified  by defendants’  expert  Kuthy,  that  a
metropolitan  pupil  transportation  system,  including  Detroit,
would  be  more  economical  and administratively  more  feasible  than
parallel  systems  in  Detroit  and  its  suburbs.  (Kuthy,  Tr.  134-136,
181,  184-187,  195-196,  March  16,  1972)

4.8  There  is  a  dispute  among the parties  about the  amount  of
new  transportation  equipment  required  to accomplish  the  desegre-
gation  called  for  in plaintiffs’  plan.  Basically,  the disagreement  is a
function  of whether  or  not  an  average  of 2 or  3 trips per bus may
,be  routed  and  accomplished  under  such  plan.  (Kuthy,  Tr.
“122-124,  March  16,  1972;  Smith,  Tr.  417,  430-431,  March  17,
1972)  The  need  for  new  transportation  equipment  might  further
vary  depending  on  the  degree  to  which  DSR  faculties  and  present
routes  could  be  utilized,  the  staggering  of  school  openings,  the
acquisition  of a  yellow  bus  fleet  for  the  city  of Detroit,  the joint
utilization  by Detroit  and suburban  districts  of existing  yellow  bus
equipment,  and/or  a combination  of any  of the  above.

— eeaeeeanaanan

Ia  231

4.9  Defendants’  expert  Kuthy  testified  that  it would  take  five
months  to  design  for  Detroit  a  75,000  pupil  transportation  plan,
and  several  months  thereafter  to  make  it  smoothly  operational.
(Kuthy,  Tr.  188-189,  March  16,  1972)  Plaintiffs’  expert  Smith
estimated  six  to  ten  weeks  for  designing  such  a plan, with  a short
period  in  September  to  iron  out  operational  details.  (Smith,  Tr.
418-419,  439-441,  March  17,  1972)  Based  upon  the experience  of
newly  desegregating  systems  elsewhere,  Dr.  Foster  also  thought
Mr.  Kuthy’s  estimate  too  long.  (Foster,  Tr.  347-348,  383-  384,
March  16,  1972)

The  Court  finds  that,  even  by the most  pessimistic  estimate,  it
would  be  practicable  to  prepare  and  implement  for  September  a
design  for  the  transportation  that  would  be  involved  in plaintiffs’
plan.  The Court also finds that,  while five  months  of  design  time
might  be  desirable,  the  task  could  be  done  effectively  in a shorter
period.

5.  Defendants’  expert  Dr.  Guthrie  testified  that  there  is  a
fairly high correlation  between  race  and  SES,  i.e.  black  families  are
proportionately  somewhat  more  likely  to  be  lower  SES,  because
of wide  ranging  prior  racial  discrimination  in our  society.  He testi-
fied  that  school  desegregation  plans  that give appropriate  consider-
ation  to  SES  factors  are  educationally  beneficial,  in  the  achieve-
ment  and  associational  senses,  for  all  children.  He  also  testified
that  programs  which  would  infuse  large  amounts  of  money  and
educational  resources  into  racially  isolated,  lower  SES  schools
would  not  be  educationally  productive,  in  the  achievement  or
associational  sense,  compared  to  a  sound  plan  of  desegregation.
Neither  Dr.  Foster  nor  any  other  witness  expressed  substantial
disagreement  with  these  views.  (Guthrie,  Tr.
,  March
17, 1972)

The  Court  finds  that  compensatory  education  alone  is inade-
quate  to  correct  the  educational  disadvantages  of  segregated
education.  The  Court  further  finds  that,  although  it may  not  be
constitutionally  required,  school  authorities  may  give  due  regard
in formulating  a plan of desegregation  to factors  such  as SES, how-
ever,  consideration  of  such  factors  may  not  be  used  to  delay,
impede,  limit  or  frustrate  the  timing  or  the  degree  of actual  dese-
gregation.

Ia  232

6.  In  any  pairing,  clustering  or  grouping  of  schools,  grade
structures  are  altered;  appropriate,  and  substantial  reassignment  of
faculty  is therefore  necessitated.  (Rankin,  Tr.  586-587,  March  21,
1972)  Moreover,  all  experts  agreed  that  simultaneous  desegre-
gation  of faculty  is educationally  sound  and  an  important  part  of
any  plan  of  school  desegregation.  (Foster,  Tr.  312,  353,  March
1972;  Guthrie,  Tr.  495-6,  March  17,  1972;  Rankin,  Tr.
,  March  21,  1972.  See  also  Detroit  Board  “Basic  Guide-
lines  for  a  Metropolitan  Detroit  Area  Plan,  p.  33;  Metropolitan
School  District  Reorganization  Plan,  p. 20; Metropolitan  One-way
Student  Movement  and  Reassignment  Plan,  p.  22)

7,  The  Defendant  Detroit  Board  selected,  and  submitted  to
the  Court,  Plans  A  and  C while  rejecting  alternative  plans  which
would  have  achieved  a greater  degree  of actual  desegregation.  See,
e.g.,  Modified  Feeder  Plan  C, which  subsequently  was  developed  in
plaintiffs’  plan.  (PC-2,  3). The  default  of the  Board  in submitting
plans  which  previously  had  failed  or  were  not  reasonable  calcu-
lated  to  produce  meaningful  actual  desegregation  in  light  of the
available  alternatives  constitutes  legal bad  faith.  (See Green  v. New
Kent  County,  391  U.S.  430(1968)).  The  preparation  of a plan by
plaintiffs  was  required  by  the  default  of  the  Detroit  Board  of
Education.  Expenses  incurred  by plaintiffs  in preparation  of such
plan were  necessitated  by that  default.

Supplementary  Conclusions  of Law

1.  This  court  has  continuing  jurisdiction  of this  action  for all
purposes,  including  the granting  of effective  relief.  Ruling  on  Issue
of  Segregation,  September  17,  1971;  Raney  v.  Board  of Educa-
tion,  391  U.S.  443(1968).

2.  On  the  basis  of the  Court’s  prior  finding  of illegal  school
segregation,  the  obligation  of  the  defendants  is  to  adopt  and
implement  an  educationally  sound,  practicable  plan  of desegre-  |
gation  that  promises  realistically  to  achieve  now  and  hereafter  the
greatest  possible  degree  of actual  school  desegregation.  Green  v.
County  School  Board,  391  U.S.  430(1968);  Alexander  v.  Holmes
County  Board  of Education,  396  U.S.  19(1969);  Carter  v.  West
Feliciana  Parish  School  Board,  396  U.S.  290(1970);  Swann  v..

Soc.

eo.

I a 233

Charlotte-Mecklenberg  Board  of Education,  402  U.S.  1(1971);
Davis  v. Board of School  Commissioner,  402  U.S.  33 (1971).

3.  Defendant  Detroit  Board’s  Plans  A and C are  legally  insuf-
ficient  because,  compared  to  existing  alternatives,  they  do  not
promise  to  effect  significant  desegregation.  Green  v.  County
School  Board,  supra,  at  439-440;  U.S.  v.  Jefferson  County  Board
of Education,  372  F.2d  836,  895-96  (Sth  Cir.  1966);  U.S.  v.
School  District  151,  301  F.Supp.  201,  232-33(N.D.I11.,  1969);
Bivins  v.  Bibb  County,  424  F.2d  97(1970).  In  Addition,  to  the
extent  that  plans  A and  C would  maintain  extra-resource  schools
which  would  be  attended,  on  the  basis  of race,  by a  higher  pro-
portion  of  the  total  white  children,  while  maintaining  lesser
resource  schools  which  would  be  attended  on  the  basis  of race  by
a high  proportion  of the  total  black  children,  they are  prima  facie
unconstitutional.  Brown  v.  Bd.  of Educ.,  347  U.S.  483(1954);
Hobson  vy.  Hansen,  269  F.Supp.  401(D.D.C.  1967); Brunson  v.  Bd.
of Trustees,  429  F.2d  820 (4th Cir.  1970).

4.  The omission  from  the  Detroit  defendants’  plans of kinder-
garden  and  grades  1 and  2 is  another  factor  in their  legal  insuf-
ficiency.  And  as  indicated  in  the  Findings  of Fact,  above,  the  cost
estimates  associated  with  plan  A  render  it  less  practicable  than
existing  alternatives.  U.S.  v.  School  District  151,301  FSU 20%\
233(N.D.111.,  1969);  Price  v.  Denison  School  District,  348  F.2d
1010,  1012(Sth  Cir.  1965).

5.  The  adoption  and  implementation  of  an  existing
educationally  sound  practicable  plan  may  not  be postponed  upon
the  contingency  that  something  better  may  turn  up.  U.S.  »v.
Baldwin  County  Board  of Education,  423  F.2d  1013  (Sth  Cir.
1970); Bradley  v.  Richmond  School Board,  325  F.  Supp.  828
(E.D.  VA.  1970);  U.S.  v.  Bd.  of Trustees,  424 F. 2d 625, 627  (Sth
Cir.  1970).
_
6.  Community  opposition  to  school  ibeildeinedaticin:  mani-
fested  by  white  flight  or  otherwise,  is an  impermissible  basis  for
limiting  the  requirement  of  desegregation  or  the  effectiveness  of
plans.  Monroe  v.  Bd.  of Commissioners,  391  U.S.  450,  459(1968);
Cooper v.  Aaron,  358  U.S.  1, 13 (1958).

la  234

7.  Factors  such  as  racial  tensions  and  socioeconomic  (SES)
composition  of schools  may  be  relevant  to  evaluating  the  educa-
tional  soundness  and  practicability  of alternative,  equally  effective
desegregation  plans.  But,  the  constitutionally  required  remedy  for
and particularly  as such
racial  segregation  is racial  desegregation;
factors  are  the  legacy  of prior segregation  and discrimination,  they
may not be  the basis for  limiting  the actual effectiveness  of  dese-
gregation  plans.  Swann,  supra,  91  S.Ct.  1267,  1280  Note  8;
Brunson  v.  Bd.  of Trustees,  supra;  U.S.  v.  School  District  151,
supra,  301  F.Supp.  at  231; Brewer  v.  Norfolk  School  Board,  434
F.2d 408,  411  (4th Cir.  1970).

8. Measured  by the legally permissible considerations, plaintiffs’
plan  is  the  only  effective,  educationally  sound,  practicable  plan
before  the  Court,  and  the  defendants  should,  pending  further
litigation,  undertake  all  necessary  steps  preparatory  to  imple-
menting  it  for  September  1972.  Carter  v.  West  Feliciana  Parish
School  Bd., supra.

9.  This  Court  reaffirms  and  readopts  its  prior  Conclusions
(See  Ruling  On  Issue  of  Segregation,  Sept.  27,  1971))  that  the
state  of Michigan  is constitutionally  responsible  for securing  to all
its  children,  including  the  children  of  Detroit,  equality  of
educational  opportunity  free  from  racial  segregation  and
discrimination.  The  Court  also  reaffirms  that  certain  of the state's
acts  and  practices  were  causal  factors  in  the  racial  segregation  in
and  of  Detroit,  inviolation  of  the  Constitution.  Bradley  ».
Milliken,  438  F.2d  945(6th  Cir.  1971);  Ruling  on  Issue  of Segre-
gation.

10.  As  indicated  in the  Findings

of Fact,  above,  no  Detroit-
only  plan of desegregation  will provide  complete  and adequate
relief  for  the  constitutional  violations  found  in  this  case.  This
Court  and  school  authorities  including  state  educatidfial  author-
ities,  are  obliged  to  seek and adopt the best available  plan.  Swann,
supra;  Green,  supra;  Carter,  supra,  Baldwin  County,  supra.  Con-
sidering  the  nature  of the  violation  and  the  powers  of this Court,
sense,  plans of metropo-
in  both  the jurisdictional and equitable
litan  relief  must  be  considered.  See Bradley  v.  Richmond  School
Board_____F.  Supp.___(C.A.  3353,  decided  January  5, 1972,

eee

1a  235

E.D.V.A.)  and  cases  stated  therein.  Moreover,  the  burden  of
coming  forward  with  a  sound,  constitutionally  adequate  plan
remains  with  the defendants.

11.  As concluded  above  (No.  5), timely  relief is imperative  in
this  action,  and  the  asserted  preferability  of  metropolitanism  is
not  a basis  for continuing  school  segregation  in Detroit.  Therefore,
unless  the  metropolitan  concept  becomes  a metropolitan  plan for
1972-73,  the  Constitution  requires  that  plaintiffs’  plan,  with
appropriate  final  embellishments,  be  implemented.  Carter,  supra;
Baldwin  County,  supra.

12.  In  making reassignment  of faculty  nessitated  by a system-
wide  alteration  of  pupil  assignments  and  grade  structures,  the
defendant  Detroit  Board  and  the  State  have  the  power  and  the
continuing,  present  duty  to  insure  that  such  faculty  reassignment
affirmatively  avoids  maintaining  or  creating  the  racial  identi-
fication  of schools  “simply  by reference  to the racial  composition
of teachers  and staff.”  Swdnn,  402  U.S.  at  18.  Moreover,  the scope
is broad  and  not  limited  to  the precise
of the Court’s  equity power
contours  of  the  violation.  Swann,  402  U.S.  at  15.  Therefore,  to
the extent  to  which  faculty  desegregation  is essential  to the effec-
tive  implementation  of a  plan  of pupil desegregation,  it may  be so
ordered.  United  States  v.  Montgomery  County  Board  of Educ.,
395  U.S.  225(1969).  In either  case  no  contract,  union  agreement
_  or  otherwise,  or  Board  policy  or  practice  may  impede  Fourteenth
Amendment  obligations.  United  States  v.  Greenwood  Municipal
Separate  School  District,  406  F.2d  1086,  1094  (Sth  Cir.),  cert.
denied  395  U.S.  907  (1969);  Berry  v.  Benton  Harbor,
F.
Supp.__.(W.D.  Mich.  1971).

13.  The  choice  by  school  authorities  of  less  effective  alter-
natives  puts  into  question  the  “good  faith”  of school authorities.
Green  v.  New  Kent  County  391  U.S.  430(1968)  Where  school
authorities  choose,  among  alternatives,  that  alternative  whose
natural,  probable,  forseeable,  and  reasonable  effect  is to minimize
desegregation,  such  action  is intentional  within  the  meaning  of the
Fourteenth  Amendment.  Ruling  on  Issue  of Segregation;  Bradley

la  236

v.  Richmond,  slip opinion.

Respectfully  submitted,

/s/

UNITED  STATES  DISTRICT  COURT
EASTERN  DISTRICT  OF MICHIGAN
SOUTHERN  DIVISION

RONALD  BRADLEY,  et al,

Plain tiffs,

vs.

WILLIAM  G. MILLIKEN,  et al,

Defendants,
DETROIT  FEDERATION  OF  TEACH-
ERS,  LOCAL  NO.  231,  AMERICAN  FED-
ERATION  OF TEACHERS,  AFL-CIO,

Defendant-Intervenor,

DENISE  MAGDOWSKIL,  et al,

Defendants-Intervenor,

ee”
ee ee ee ee eee

OFFER  OF PROOF

Civil  Action
No.  35257

Now  comes  SOUTHFIELD  PUBLIC  SCHOOLS,  defendant-
intervenor  herein,  by its  counsel,  CONDIT  and  McGARRY,  P.C.,
and  pursuant  to  the  court’s  ruling  and  order  on  petitions  for inter-
vention  (the  conditions  of  which  have  previously  been  objected
to)  and  submits  the  following  offer  of proof  for  the  court’s  con-
sideration:

1.  It  is  the  basic  position  of  SOUTHFIELD  PUBLIC
SCHOOLS  that  the  implementation  of a metropolitan  school  dese-
gregation  plan  is without  legal  propriety.  It is our  further  position
that  the  implementation  of  such  a  plan,  no  matter  tiow  well  in-
tentioned,  will  fail  to achieve  the  educational  objectives  advocated
by its proponents  and  only result  in the devastation  of local  school
districts  which  are  presently  imparting  quality  education  to  stu-
dents  regardless  of race,  color  or  creed.

2.  The  basic  educational  premise  of those  submitting  metro-
politan  desegregation  plans to the  court is that  induced  integration
of minority  students  with  white  sutdents  will  produce  significant
academic  achievement  on  the  part  of  the  minority  students.  This
position  is  allegedly  founded  upon  the  Coleman  and  the  Racial

la  238

Isolation  reports.  The  proponents  of the  plans have  further  expres-
sed  their  opinions  relative  to other  improvements  in the  quality  of
education  which  may  be  anticipated  upon  adoption  of their  pro-
posals.

3.  Defendant-intervenor  proposes  to  demonstrate  to  the
court  through  the  testimony  of  Dr.  David  J. Armor,  Professor  of
Sociology,  Harvard  University,  that  current  educational  research
does  not  support  the  claims  heretofore  made  by the  proponents  of
the  desegregation  plans.  Attached  hereto is a  brief outline  of Dr.
Armor’s  proposed  testimony.  The  court  will  note  that  Dr.  Armor
not  only  analyzes  the  Coleman  and  Racial  Isolation  reports  with
‘reference  to  their  proper  application  but  also  reports  relative  to
new  studies  concerning  induced  integration.

4.  The  proposed  testimony  of Dr.  Armor  not  only  addresses
itself to  the question  of quality  education  but also  to  the  ramifica-
tions  of  induced  integration  as  contemplated  by  the  proposed
metropolitan  plans  of desegregation.

It  is  respectfully  submitted  that  the  proposed  testimony  is
highly  significant  to  the  subject  matter  of this litigation  and  most
relevant  and  material  to proper  adjudication  of the same.

SOUTHFIELD  PUBLIC  SCHOOLS

CONDIT  and  McGARRY,  P.C.

By /s/

RICHARD  P. CONDIT
Attorneys  for Southfield  Public
Schools,  defendant-intervenor
860  West  Long  Lake  Road
Bloomfield  Hills,  Michigan  48013
645-5205

Dated:  April

10,  1972.

Ia 239

April  6, 1972

Richard  P. Condit
860 West  Long  Lake  Road
Bloomfield  Hills,  Michigan  48013

Dear  Mr. Condit:

I have  reviewed  the  materials  you  sent  describing  two  plans of de-
segregation  for  Detroit;  | have  paid  primary  attention  to  evalua-
tions or statements  based  upon  educational  resarch.  My own  evalu-
ation  of  the  educational  components  of these  two  proposals  fol-

lows:

A.  Existing  Studies:

1.  The  Coleman  report  and  the  Racial  Isolation  report
the only  authority
of  the U.S.  Commission  on Civil  Rights,
cited,  are  based  on  cross-sectional  studies  (done  at  one  point
in  time)  and  compare  the  experience  of naturally  integrated
black  and  white  students  to  naturally  segregated  black  and
white  students.  As  such  it is not  scientifically  valid  to  make
inferences  about  the  effects  of induced  school  integregation.

2.  With  cross-sectional  designs  it is impossible  to  make
cause  and  effect  inferences.  That  is,  if naturally  integrated
black  students  achieve  higher  than  naturally  segregated  black
students,  the  reason  may  be that  the  families  of brighter  black
students  moved  to  integrated  environments  in  the  first  place,
so  that  the  achievement  differences  are  not  a result  of integra-
tion  per se.

3.  Even  if the  achievement  differences  in  the  Coleman
study  were  entirely  due  to  the  effects  of integration,  the dif-
ferences  are  small.  Integrated  sixth  grade  black  students  are
still  1-1/2  standard  deviations  behind  white  students  in verbal
achievement  (as reported  by Coleman),  rather  than  2 standard
deviations  for  segregated  students  as  a  whole.  This  represents
at  best  a  gain  for  the  average  black  student  from  the  2nd  per-
centile  to  the  7th  percentile,  where  the  average  white  student

Ia  240

is  at  the  50th  percentile.  It  is  likely  that  most  of this  small
difference  is due  to  socio-economic  differences  between  in-
tegrated  black  students  and  segregated  black  students.

B.  New  Studies.

1.  New  studies  (Riverside,  Ann  Arbor,  White  Plains,
Rochester,  Project  Concern  in  Hartford,  and  my  own  soon  to
be  published)  are  Jongitudinal  in  nature  and  compare
induced-integrated  blacks  with  non-integrated  blacks  or
whites.  They  have  found  no  conclusive  evidence  for  achieve-
ment  gains as a result  of induced  integration.

2.  The  new  studies  also  show  that  racial  relations  may
worsen  (or  at  least  not  improve)  as  a result  of inter-racial  contact.

While  I am  willing  to  testify  about  these  research  findings  and
interpretations,  professional  commitments  will  prevent  me  from
providing  any  direct  testimony  until  at  least  May  15th.  With
regard  to  material  under  Section  B  even  this  date  is  contingent
upon  my  publisher’s  permission  to release  my  report.

Sincerely  yours,

David  J. Armor
Professor  of Sociology
Harvard  University

A SURVEY  AND  EVALUATION  OF EXISTING
SCHOOL  TRANSPORTATION  FACILITIES

Upon  receipt  of the order of the Federal  District  Court  of the
above  cause,  a  survey  and  evaluation  of  existing  transportation
facilities  was  conducted.  On  April  28,  1972  the  responses  of 61
school  districts  regarding  their  transportation  facilities  and  our
evaluations  of them  were  filed  with  the Federal  District  Court.

The  remaining  25  responses  have  been  received  and are

attached  as exhibits  per identifying  number.

Thirty  buses  in  the 86 school  districts  are available  to  trans-
port  pupils  on  routes  extending  into  other  districts.  Of the  thirty
buses  fifteen  are  located  in  the  northern-most  school  districts  of
Macomb  and  Oakland  counties.  The  fifteen  buses  may  have  no
utility  since  a school  bus  in  suburban  locations  does  not  average  4
speed  of more  than  three  minutes  per mile  throughout  the  months

Ia 243

of the school  year.  The  time  it would  take  to  transport  pupils  to
Detroit  schools  may be too great  for  it  to be used in trans-
portation  of children  to  or  from  Detroit  or  to and  from  any  point
en route  to Detroit.

Although  these  buses are available  to transport  a  passenger
load  outside  the  districts  which  own  them,  they  perform  a  func-
tion  which  must  be accomplished  each day before  or  after  the  trip
outside  the district.  The time  it  takes each  of  these buses to
accomplish  its  one  route  is somewhat  less  than  an  hour  and  the
bus  is limited  in  its  utility  for  extended  travel.  To  illustrate  the
effect  of  distance  and  the  time  distance  consumes  in a schedule,
we  have  made a calculation  of a  situation  in which  suburban  chil-
dren  are  transported  five  miles  inside  the  city  of  Detroit  and
Detroit  children  are  transported  on  the  same  bus  to the suburban
school  district:

Ia  244

In  this  illustration,  Royal  Oak  pupils  are  in  Detroit  48
minutes  before  their  classes  start.  They  are  in  Detroit  48 minutes
after  classes  and  before  their  bus  starts  its  return  to  Royal  Oak.
They  will  be  away  from  their  homes  as  much  as 9% hours.  Detroit
pupils  are  in Royal  Oak  for the  time  of their  classes  only.  They are
away  from  their  homes  as  much  as  8 hours.

In  the  event  it  is  determined  that  the  buses  would  be  used
only  for  one  way  bussing  into  Detroit  the  starting  time  would  be
48  minutes  later  and  the  returning  time  would  be  48  minutes
earlier  and there  would  be no  idle time  in Detroit.

The  final  determination  on  whether  or  not  the  30  available
buses  have  any  utility  for  use  on  a route  extending  into  Detroit
depends  on  a  decision  as  to  the  length  of  time  a  pupil  may  be
expected  to  be  away  from  his  home  or  community  school,  how
early  he  may  be  required  to  board  a  bus,  how  late  he  may  be
required  to  be absent  from  home  and  community  and  whether  or
not  cross  bussing  will  be accomplished  with a single  vehicle.

UNITED  STATES  DISTRICT  COURT
EASTERN  DISTRICT  OF MICHIGAN
SOUTHERN  DIVISION

RONALD  BRADLEY,  et al,

v.

WILLIAM  G. MILLIKEN,  et al,

Plaintiffs

Defendants,

LOCAL

AFFIDAVIT  OF DEFENDANT—INTERVENOR  GROSSE
POINTE  SCHOOLS  RE:  SCHOOL  DISTRICT
BOUNDARY  LINES  AND  TRANSPORTATION  FACILITIES

STATE  OF MICHIGAN  )

COUNTY  OF WAYNE  )

) SS

The  undersigned,  THEOS  I.  ANDERSON,  PH.D.,  being  first

duly  sworn,  deposes  and  says  as  follows:

1.  That  he is currently  the  Superintendent  of Schools of The
Grosse  Pointe  Public  School  System,  Wayne  County,  Michigan,
having been  appointed  to such  capacity  on  July  1, 1967.

2.  That  upon  his  information,  knowledge  and  belief,  based
partly  upon  a  review  of  the  historical  records  of  the  School
System,  the  following  constitutes  a summary  of the  establishment
of The Grosse  Pointe  Public  School  System.

Ia  255

The  boundaries  of  The  Grosse  Pointe  Public  School  System
were  established  on  December  23,  1921  as  the  “Rural  Agricultural
School  District  No.  1, Grosse  Pointe Township,”  Wayne  County,
Michigan,  by  consolidation  of  five  previously  existing  smaller
school  districts.  The  consolidation  was  ordered  and  approved  by
the  affirmative  vote  of the  electorate  of all of the five  constituent
school  districts  in existence  as of that  date.

The

Pointe

Grosse

School

presently
Public
encompasses  the  entire  geographical  area  of  the  City  of  Grosse
Pointe  Park,  the  City  of Grosse  Pointe,  the  City  of Grosse  Pointe
Farms,  the Village  of Grosse  Pointe  Shores  and  the City of Grosse
Pointe  Woods.  The  School  District  also  encompasses  a  portion  of
the City  of Harper  Woods.

System

The

which

school

smaller

the
districts
newly-formed  Rural  Agricultural  School  District  No.  1, Grosse
Pointe  Township,  were  originally  drawn  along  township  lines
and/or naturally  existing  boundaries,  and  were  not  drawn,  nor  did
consolidation  of same  occur  in  any  respect,  with  a view  to  or  for
the  purpose  of causing,  fostering  or  maintaining  racial  segregation.

composed

By vote  of the  electorate  of the  newly-formed  School  District,
subsequent  to  the  establishment  of the  School  District  boundaries
in  1921,  the  name  of  the  School  District  was  changed  to  The
Grosse  Pointe  Public  School  System,  Wayne  County,  Michigan,
and  in pursuance  of the  statutes  of the  State  of Michigan,  has been
designated  as  a  third  class  school  district.  The  boundary  lines  of
the  School  District  have  not  changed,  however,  since  their
establishment  in  1921,  with  the  exception  of a  small  portion  of
the  School  District  known  as  the  Hanstein  portion  which  became  a
portion  of the  School  District  of the  City  of Detroit  by reason  of
the  annexation  of  that  geographical  portion  by  the  City  of
Detroit,  in March,  1926.

At  the  time  of the  establishment  of the Grosse  Pointe  School
District  in  1921,  the  total  children  attending  the  School  District
was  947.  The  total  children  attending  the  School  District  as of the
fourth  Friday  following  the  commencement  of  school  in  1971,
was  13,529.

MN

~

la  256

There  are  presently  ten  elementary  schools,  three  middle
schools  and  two  high  schools  being  occupied  by students  of the
School  District,  all  of  which  school  buildings  were  constructed
subsequent  to  the  formation  of  the  School  District  in  1929  and
none  of said school  buildings,  or  additions  thereto,  were  located  or
constructed  in  any  respect,  with  a view  to  or  for  the  purpose  of
causing,  fostering  or maintaining  racial  segregation.

3.  That  upon  the  information,  knowledge  and  belief  of the
undersigned,  the  following  represents  an  accurate  statement  of the
transportation  facilities  owned,  chartered  or  being  used  by  The
Grosse  Pointe  Public  School  System  during  the  1971-72  school
year.

:

Because  of  population  density  within  The  Grosse  Pointe
Public  School  System  and  the  policy  of assignment  of students  to
neighborhood  schools
their  home,  the  degree  of
transportation  of students  within  The Grosse  Pointe  Public  School
System  is very  limited.

nearest

There  are  no  student  transportation  facilities  whatsoever
owned  by The Grosse  Pointe  Public  School  System.  Because  of the
necessity  of  transporting  certain  special  education  students  to
their
schools,  however,  the  school  system  has
contracted  through  outside  private  agencies  to provide  transporta-
tion  for such  special  education  students,  including  specifically:

respective

Trainable  mentally  retarded

Developmental  kindergarten

Adjusted  study  program

Developmental  primary

Adjusted  learning

Physically  handicapped

Such  students  are  picked  up at their  home,  transported  to the
educational  center  where  they  receive  their  instruction  and
returned  to  their  home  at  the  close  of the  day.  As  of the  date  of
this  Affidavit,  The  Grosse  Pointe  Public  School  System  is

Sa

Ia  257

transporting  115  such  special  education  students.

The  transportation  costs  for  these  students  for  the  1971-72

school  year,  on  an  estimated  basis,  are  as  follows:

East  Side  Cab:

400  miles  per  day
8 “‘bus  vans”  —
(capacity  15 to  18
students)  8 drivers

Checker  Cab  to Leland  School  —
two  ways  (for one  child)

Cost  per student

Total

Cost  per school  year  (180  days  x  $327.90)

..
estimate

Estimated  State  Reimbursement
(115  students  x  $200.00)

$324.50  per day

3.40  per  day

$327.90  per  day
$  2.85  per day

$59,202.00

$23,000.00
PM me aa SE

Net  cost  of  School  District  =

$36,202 .00

students,

education

approximately

In  addition  to  the  transportation  facilities  provided  for  the
special
1,200  students
attending  The  Grosse  Pointe  Public  School  System  utilize  the
public  ‘transportation  facilities  provided  by  the  Southeastern
Michigan  Transportation  Association  (SEMTA).  These  students
voluntarily  utilize  these  facilities  and  pay  a  fare  of 30 cents  each
way  during  the  hours  between  7  A.M.  and  5 P.M.  Regular  fare
during  this  period  for  other  members  of the  public  is 40  cents  to
70  cents  each  way,  depending  on  the  particular  bus zone  involved.

SEMTA  has  provided  special  bus routings  during  school  hours
to  provide  convenient  service  for  the  students  and  as
of  the
date  of  this  Affidavit,  there  are  28  special  bus  routings,  using  16
coaches  that  carry  45  to 51  passengers.

la  258

Further  Deponent  sayeth  not.

of

eee
