eee

RECOMMENDATIONS  OF DESEGREGATION  PANEL
REGARDING  ADDITIONAL  TRANSPORTATION  EQUIPMENT
NEEDED  FOR THE  IMPLEMENTATION  OF AN INTERIM
DESEGREGATION  PLAN

Pursuant  to  the  rulings  of the  court,  the  desegregation  panel
has  met  for  the  purpose,  inter alia,  of developing  a plan  for  the
transportation  of  pupils  in  the  desegregation  area.  It  is the  con-
clusion  of  the  panel  that  an  interim  plan,  to  be  effective  in
September,  1972,  will  require  transportation  equipment  in
addition  to  that  equipment  which  is  currently  available  in  the
desegregation  area.  Accordingly,  the  panel  does  hereby  recom-
mend  the  use  of state  funds  for the immediate  purchase  of, at least
295  additional  vehicles  to  be  used  in  implementation  of  the
interim  plan.*  The  panel  further  recommends  that  all  newly  pur-
chased  vehicles  meet  minimum  Michigan  standards  as  required  by-
the State  Board  of Education.

la  262

In  addition,  it  is  the  recommendation  of  the  panel  that  the
,  court  authorize  rental,  leasing,  or  other  contractual  arrangements
for  whatever  additional  transportation  equipment  may  be  needed
to implement  an  interim  plan more  expansive  than  that  plan  which
is now being developed  by the panel on the basis of the use of  the
newly  purchased  equipment  recommended  above.

It is the  further  recommendations  of the panel  that  the  court
authorize  the  panel
to  include  in  its  final  desegregation  plan
recommendations  as  to  any  other  solutions  to  the  issue  of trans-
portation,  including,  but  not  limited  to,  recommendations
regarding  the  purchase,  rental,  lease,  or other usage  of additional
transportation  equipment,  housing  of  transportation  equipment,
maintenance  of  transportation  equipment,  etc.,  as  may  be
necessary  to  further  implement  plans  to  be  effective  in February,
1973,  September,  1973,  and  thereafter.

*It  is the opinion  of the panel  after  extensive  investigation
that,
as of June  27,  1972,  not more  than  295  new  vehicles are
available  for  purchase  and  September,  1972  delivery.  The  panel,
therefore,  is developing  an interim  plan  consistent  with  such
constraint.

Respectfully  submitted  by the  panel,  as  listed  below  in alpha-

betical  order,  this  fifth  day of July,  1972.

/s/  William  Emerson

ORDER
ADDING  DEFENDANT  ALLISON  GREEN

At  a  session  of the  United  States  District  Court,  Federal
day
Building,  Detroit,  Michigan,  on  the
of

July , 1972.

[ith

The  Court  having  determined  that  transpoPtation  capacity
must  be  orderéd  by  the  Defendant  Detroit  Board  of  Education
and  paid  for  by the  State  Defendants,  and  it being brought  to the
Court's  attention  that  the  State  Treasurer,  upon  warrants  of the
State  Superintendent  of  Education,  is  responsible  for  issuing  his
warrant  for  payment  of  obligations  of  Defendant,  the  adding  of
Allison  Green,  Treasurer  of the  State  of Michigan,  is necessary  in
order  to grant  full  relief  to the parties.

WHEREFORE,  the  Court,  on  its  own  motion,  pursuant  to
Rules  19  and  21  of  the Federal  Rules of Civil  Procedure,  hereby
ORDERS  that  Allison  Green,  Treasurer  of  the State of Michigan,
be  made a party  Defendant in this action.  Copies of this Order will

la  264  —

be  served  forthwith  on  the Defendant  Green  by  the  United  States
Marshall.

This  court  having  heard  oral  motions  on  July  19,  1972,  for
entry  of judgments  in  accordance  with  the  provisions  of Fed.  R.
Civ.  P.  54(b)  and  for  certification  under  the  provisions  of  28
U.S.C.  1292(b)  in  connection  with  certain  orders  and  rulings  of
the  court  heretofore  entered;  the  court  determines  for the  purpose
of appeal,  and  subject  to  this  court’s  statements  at the  hearing  on
July  19,  1972,  that  there  is no just reason  for delay  and  that  each
of the  following  orders:

1.

Ruling  on  Issue  of Segregation,  September  27,  1971;

la  266

me.

2.  Ruling  on  Propriety  of  Considering  a  Metropolitan
Remedy  to Accomplish  Desegregation  of the  Public  Schools  of the
City of Detroit,  March  24,  1972;

3.  Findings  of  Fact  and  Conclusions  of  Law  on Detroit—

only  Plans  of Desegregation,  March  28,  1972;

4.

Ruling  on  Desegregation  Area  and  Development  of Plan,
and  Findings  of Fact  and.Conclusions  of Law  in Support  thereof,
June  14,  1972;  and

5.  Order  for  Acquisition  of Transportation,  July  11,  1972
shall  be deemed  final  orders under  Rule  54(b)  of the  Federal  Rules
of  Civil  Procedure  and  the  court  certifies  the  issues  presented
therein  under  the provisions  of 28 U.S.C.  1292(b).

PART  V

RECOMMENDATIONS

The  June  14,  1972  order  of  the  federal  court  states  “the  State

Superintendent  of  Public  Instruction,  with  the  assistance  of  the  other

state  defendants,  shall  examine,  and  make  recommendations,  consistent  with

the  principles  established

ve fi.e.,  in  Part  II  of  the  order/,  rues

ey

for  the  (1)  financial,  (2)  adminis

ve  and  school  governance,  and  (3)

contractual  arrangements  ..  .  inc luding  personnel  policies  fana7

procedures  .  ..  ."

1.

In  accordance  with  the  above  order  and  the  cxamination  of  the  issues

contained  in  this  document, it  is  recommended  that  no  substantial  —

alteration  in  the  financial,  administration  and  governance,  and

contractual  arrangements  of  the  existing  53  local  districts  that

presently  describe  the  school  districts  in  the  desegregation  area

take  place  during  the  interim  period.

2.

In  accordance  with  the  above  order  and  the  examination  of  the  issues

contained  in  this  document,  it  is  recommended  that  on  or  before  a

date  15  days  prior  to  the  implementation  of  interim  desegregation

the  Court  order  those  districts  to  be  desegregated  during  the  interim

period  to  effectuate  memoranda  of  agreement,  similar  to  the  draft

memorandum  of  agreement  contained  in  Appendix  B,  including  consideration

of  the  provisions  contained  in  Appendix  C,  in  order  to  effect  those

alterations  necessary  in  existing  financial,  administration  and  gover-

nance,  and  contractual  agrecmerts.

The  Court  should  specify  that  the

districts  may  modify  the  memorandum  of  avreement  at  such  time  and  in

such  manner  during  che  interim  period  as  miy  be  necessary  to  cffectuate

intevim  desegresation  as  adopted  by  the  Court.

I a 269

24

In  accordance  with  the  above  order  and  the  examination  of  the  issues
contained  in  this  document,  it  is  recommended  that,  for  those  districts
chosen  by  the  Court  to  be  desegregated  during  the  interim  period,  and

which  require  additional  monies  to  maintain  the  1971-72  level_of  opera-

tion,  a  system  be  established  whereby  their  financial  necds

reviewed  by  the  Municipal  Finance  Commission,  Michigan  Department  of

Treasury.

To  effectuate  this  recoumendation  would  require  the  Court

to  consider  ordering  the  levy  of  additional  millage  in  the  instance

that  a  lecislative  grant,  lean,  or  fund  transfer  does  not  prove  viable,

or  local  voter  support  is  not  obtained.

In  accordance  with  the  above  order  and  the  examination  of  the  issues.

contained  in  this  document,  it  is  recommended  that  there  be  no  interim

attempt  to  fully  equalize  school  facilities  or  finances  during  the

interim  desecregation  period  in  order  to  achicve  schools  of  substantially

like  quality,  but  that  other  services  be  equalized  to  the  extent  possible

through  cooperative  arrangements  and  memoranda  of  agreement.

In  accordance  with  the  above  order  and  the  examination  of  the  issues

contained  in  this  document,  it  is  recommended  that  those  schoo!  systems

involved  in  interim  desegregation  adopt  a  cormon  school  calendar  to

ensure  compatible  vacations,  in-service  training,  prof«  ssional  development

days  and  180  days  and  900  hours  of  student  instruction  for  the  interim

desegregated  pupils.

In  accordance  with  the  above  order  and  the  cxamination  of  the  issuygs

contairne!  in  this  document,  it  is  recommended  that  there  be  established

advisory  cluster  councils  of  education  censisting  of  four  representatives

appointed  by  the  outtyine  local  beards  of  cdscation  of  districts

within  the  interim  dc.eprepated  clusters  and  threes  r presoptatives

PART  VI

RECOMMENDATIONS  FOR  FINANCE,  GOVERWANCE,  AND  PLRSOWNEL  ARRANGEMENTS

Consistent  with  the  background  discussion  in  Part  II  of  this  report,
it  is  recommended  that  the  Court  consider  the  outcome  of  interim  desegrega-
tion  arrangements  before  effectuating  any  of  the  following  recommendations
except  as  the  recommendations  are  judged  appropriate  and  essential,  as
written,  regardless  of  the  outcom:  of  interim  desegregation  arrangements.
The  June  14,  1972  order  of  the  federal  court  states  “the  State  Super-

intendent  of  Public  Instruction,  with  the  assistance  of  the  other  state
defendants,  shall  examine,  and  make  final  recommendations,  consistent
with  the  principles  established  (i.e.,  in  Part  II  of  the  order),  for  (1)
financial,  (2)  administration  and  school  governance,  and  (3)  contractual
arrangements ‘incl uding  personne]  policies  and  practices.”

Instruction

SUBJECT:  Progress  in  Preparing  Recommendations  for  Appro-
priate  Interim  and  Final  Arrangements  for  the (1)
Financial,  (2)  Administrative  and  School  Gover-
nance,  and  (3)  Contractual  Arrangements  for  the
Operation  of the  Schools Within  the Desegregation
Area

Implications  for the Interim  Period

Effective  implementation  of  the  desegregation  plan  during

the interim  period  may  require the following  actions:

1.  Amendment  of,  or  a  moratorium  on,  the  provisions  of
Article  1X,  Section  6,  of  the  Michigan  Constitution,
which  sets a 15 mill  limit on  the property  tax.

2.  Amendment  of Act 62, Public  Acts of 1933, the Property

Tax Limitation  Act.

»  1a286

Amendment  of Act  31,  Public  Acts of 1966,  which  deals
of taxes within  a city.
with the assessing and collecting

Amendment  of Act  190, Public Acts of 1962,  which  deals
with  adoption  of  special  education  millage  on  an  inter-
mediate district  basis.

Amendment  of, or a moratorium on, certain  provisions  of
the School Code of 1955  which deal with the powers  and
duties  of local  school  districts.

Amendment  of, or a moratorium  on,  certain  provisions  of
Act  336,  Public  Acts  of  1947,  the  Public  Employees  Re-
lations  Act.

Amendment  of, or  a moratorium  on,  certain provisions  of
Act 4, Public  Acts  of 1937,  the Teacher Tenure  Act.

Amendment  of  Act  36,  Public  Acts  of  1945,  the  Public
School  Employees  Retirement  Ac*.

Amendment  of the  Rules  and  Regulations  Governing  the
Certification  of Teachers.

10.

Amendment  of  Federal  Statutes  and  Guidelines  as  em-
bodied  in Title  | of  the  Elementary  and Secondary  Ed-
ucation  Act of 1965,  which  provides  approximately  $25
million annually to districts in the desegregation  area,  and
is based  on  concentrations  of children  from  low  income
families.

menting  an  effective  plan  for  desegregation  of the  Detroit  Public

2.  The  jurisdiction  of this  Court  is invoked  under  28  U.S.C.
§1331(a),  1343(3)  and (4), this being a suit  to redress  the depriva-
tion  under  color  of Michigan  law,  statute,  regulation,  custom  and/
or usage of rights,  privileges  and immunities  secured  by the  Thir
teenth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments  to  the  Constitution  of  the
United  States,  and/or  by  42  U.S.C.  § $1981,  1983,  1988  and
2000d  providing  for the  equal rights of all citizens  and  persons  and
for  the  same  rights  to  the  full  and  equal  benefits  of all  laws  and
proceedings  for  the  security  of  persons  and property  as is enjcyed
by  white  citizens  and  persons.  This  is  an  action  for  injunctive
relief,  and  for declaratory  and  other  relief  pursuant  to  28  U.S.C.
$ $2201,  2202.  The  matter  in  controversy  exceeds,  exclusive  of
interest  and  costs,  the  sum  or  value  of  ten-thousand  dollars
($10,000.00).

in this cause

Il.  Defendants

4.  The  original  Detroit  School  District  Defendants  are  the
Board  of  Education  of  the  City  of  Detroit,  its  Superintendent
(now  Charles  Wolfe),  and  the members  of the Board  of Education.
5.  The  original  State  Defendants  are  Governor  William  J.
Milliken,  Attorney  General  Frank  J.  Kelley,  the  Michigan  State
Board  of Education  and  its Superintendent,  John  W. Porter.

6.  The  intervening  Detroit  defendants  are  (a) the Detroit  Fed-
eration  of Teachers  Local  231,  American  Federation  of Teachers,
AFL-CIO  and (b) Denise  Magdowski,  et al.

7..  The  intervening  suburban  defendants  are  Allen  Park  Public
Schools,  School  District of the City of Berkeley,  Brandon  Schools,

Intermediate  School  District,  the  Oakland  County  Intermediate

Ill.  Additional  Allegations  To Conform  To The  Evidence
10.  Various  actions  by the  original  Detroit  and  State  defen-
dents  and  their  agents,  in some  instances  supported  by or  in con-
junction  with  State  laws  have,  wholly  apart  from  any  actions  of
the  suburban  defendants  or any  of them,  significantly  contributed
to  the  containment  of  black  children  in racially  identifiable,  vir-
tually  all-black  schools  operated  in  the  City  of Detroit.  The  pat-
tern  of official  segregation  and  containment  has  been  an  expand-
ing ae as the black  community  within  Detroit  has expanded,  but

 agerenee

1a295

with  the  consistent  result  of establishing  and  maintaining  racially
identifiable  black  Detroit  schools  surrounded  by racially  identifi-
able,  virtually  all-white  schools  throughout  the  tri-county  area.
These  actions  have  included,  but  are  not  limited  to, new  school
construction;  discriminatory  reimbursement  of  transportation
funds;  the  validation  and  reimposition of the  boundaries  of  the
Detroit  School  District,  imposition  of a segregatory  pupil  assign-
ment  policy  for  the  Detroit  Public  Schools,  creation  of segregated
school  regional  boundaries,  recision  of steps  toward  desegregation,
and  the  particular  carve-out  of regional  school  governance  units
wholly  within  the  Detroit  School  District  by Act 48 of the Public
Acts  of 1970; the transfer  and/or transportation  of students  across
school districts  and within  school  districts with  the purpose and/or
effect  of official  school  segregation;  limitations  on  operation  of
bonding  authority;  and  the  operation  of state  aid  and  financial
limitations,  in  conjunction  with  other  official  actions  of segrega-
tion  which  left  the  Detroit  School  District  virtually  bankrupt and
further  identified  it as  a  black  school  district.  This  consistent  and
repeated  discrimination  by State  officials  and  agencies,  manifested
by, among  others,  the  acts  listed  herein,  is causally  related  in a
significant  manner  to the present,  nearly  total  segregation  of black
children  within  the  tri-county  area  in  virtually  all-black  public
schools  within  the  jurisdiction  of the  original  Detroit  defendants,
their  agents  and  successors.  In  carrying  out  this  pattern  and prac-
tice  of official  segregation,  the  State  and  its agencies  have  advan-
taged  themselves  of existing  school  district  lines  and jurisdictional
boundaries  with  the effect  of further  entrenching  the containment
of black  students  in black  Detroit  schools;  without  any  reference
to any  actions  of  suburban  defendants,  the  prevailing  pattern  of
racially  identifiable,  virtually  all-white  schools  in  the  suburbs  of
Detroit  is a  result,  in part,  of the  official  policies  of containment
and  segregation  of  black  children  in  racially  identifiable  and  vir-
tually  all-black  schools  within  the  City  of  Detroit,  as  described
above.

11.  State  agencies  control  the  instrumentalities  whose  pre-
sence  or  cooperation  is  necessary  to  remedy  the  current  but  ex-
panding  pattern  of official,  substantial  racial public  school  segrega-
tion  described  above.  Suburban  school  districts  —  like the Detroit
School  District,  and  like  intermediate,  regional  and indeed alt local
school  districts  —  are  subordinate  governmental  entities  created

1 a 296

and  fashioned  by the  State.  Each  is given  varying  powers  to assist
in  carrying  out  the  State  responsibility  for  education;  the  size  of
each  varies  and  the boundaries  of each  are  irregular,  little  related
to  the  boundaries  of  other  governmental  units,  and  are  often
crossed  by school  children  or  school  personnel  in order  to further
various  school  programs.  The  present  school  district  boundaries
serve  no.  compelling  state  interest.  The  State  Board  of Education.
has  considerable  authority  and  power  —  including,  but  not  limited
to, distribution  of funds, accreditation  or  other  approval,  and  gen-
eral supervision  and responsibility  for public  education  throughout
Michigan  —  at its disposal  to  assist  in planning  and  implementing
any  desegregation  plan  for  the  Detroit  public  schools  which  may
extend  beyond,  or involve  districts  or  schools  located  without,  the
present  geographic  boundaries  of  the  City  of  Detroit.  The  State
Board  and  Superintendent  have  the authority  to require  the  elimi-
nation  of  segregation  among  and  within  school  districts  in

12.  The  Detroit  metropolitan  area  has  grown  as a series  of
interrelated  and  overlapping  economic,  recreation,  service,  and
governmental  units  with  many  persons  locating  in the suburbs  but
working  and  enjoying  services  in  Detroit,  and  others  living  in  De-
troit  but  working  and  enjoying  services  in the  suburbs.  The  school
and  housing  opportunities  for  black  citizens  in the  Detroit  Metro-
politan  Area,  however,  have  been  and  remain  restricted  by discrim-
inatory  governmental  and  private  action,  to  separate  and  distinct
areas  within  the  City  and  a  few  other  areas  of historic  racial  con-
tainment  in the metropolitan  area.

13.  Plans  of  desegregation  limited  to  the  present  geographic
limits  of the  City  of Detroit  will  accomplish  more  actual  desegre-
gation  than  now  exists  within  that  school  system,  but  in  the  con-
text  of  available  alternatives  not  limited  to  those  geographic
boundaries,  such  plans:  (a) will  not  achieve  the effective  desegrega-
tion  of the  public  schools  in  Detroit,  (b) will  not  alter  the racially
identifiably  black, identity  now  shared  by  most  Detroit  schools,
but  will  rather  extend  that  identity  to  all Detroit  schools.  In light  —
of realistic  and  feasible  desegregation  measures  not  limited  to De-
troit  which  are  capable  of being taken  now,  plans of desegregation
limited  to  Detroit  do  not  hold  promise  of  establishing  “just

1a297

~  schools”  in  place  of the  present  “white”  and  “black”  schools  in
that  school  system,  and  such  plans  consequently  will  not  remedy
the constitutional  violation  which  is the subject  of this lawsuit  nor
prevent its recurrence.

14.  A  far  more  effective  plan  of  desegregation  is constitu-
tionally  required;  considering  the  practicalities  of  the  situation,
the only  feasible  and  effective  plan  must  involve  crossing  the
boundary  lines  between  the  Detroit  school  district  and  other
school  districts  in  the  tri-county  area  for  the  limited  purpose  of
effectuating  meaningful  desegregation.
-  15.  The  pupils,  teachers,  resources  and  facilities  of  the  fol
lowing  defendant  local  and intermediate  school  districts  are  neces-
sary  in  this  cause  for  the  limited  purpose  of accomplishing  the
effective  desegregation  of the  Detroit  public  schools  now  and  here-
after,  including;  but  not  limited  to,  inclusion  in the desegregation
area  for pupil and  staff assignment:

Wayne  County  Intermediate;  Oakland  County  Intermediate;
Macomb  County  Intermediate;  Lake  Shore;  Roseville  City,
East  Detroit  City;  South  Lake;  Grosse  Pointe;  Lakeview;  Cen-
terline;  Fitzgerald;  Warren  Woods;  Fraser;  Harper  Woods;  Van
Dyke;  Warren  Consolidated;  Hazel  Park  City;  Hamtramck;
Lamphere;  Madison  Heights;  City  of  Troy;  Ferndale  City;
Berkeley  City;  Highland  Park;  Royal  Oak  City; Clawson  City;
Birmingham;  Oak  Park  City;  Southfield;  Bloomfield  Hills;
Farmington;  South  Redford;  North  Dearborn  Heights;  Crest-
wood;  Cherry  Hill;  Redford  Union;  Taylor;  Dearborn  City;
Dearborn  Heights;  Fairlane;  Romulus  Township;  Inkster  City;
Wayne-Westland  Community;  Westwood  Community;  Ecorse;
Allen  Park;  Southgate  Community;  River  Rouge  City;  River-
view  Community;  Wyandotte  City;  Lincoln  Park City; Melvin-
dale-North  Allen.

16.  The  pupils,  teachers,  resources  and  facilities  of  the  fol-
lowing  defendant  school  districts  are  necessary  for  the  limited
purpose  of accomplishing  the  effective  desegregation  of the  De-
troit  public  schools  now  and  hereafter,  including,  but  not  limited
to, review  of any  additions  to  school  building  capacity  and  staff
hiring  to  determine  their  present  or  potential  effect  on  the  school
desegregation  plan:

Ia  298

Novi Community;  Holly  Area; Huron  Valley;  Lake  Orion  Com-
munity;  Oxford  Area  Community;  Rochester  Community;
Walled  Lake  Consolidated;  Avondale;  Brandon  Township;
Clarkston  Community;  South  Lyon  Community;  Waterford;
Mt.  Clemens  Community;  Anchor  Bay;  Richmond  Com-
munity;  Romeo  Community;  Chippewa  Valley;  Clintondale;
L’Anse  Creuse;  South  Lake;  Utica  Community;  Armada  Area;
New  Haven  Community;  Plymouth  Community;  Huron;
Northville;  Van  Buren;  Flat  Rock  Community;  Gibraltar;
Grosse  Ile Township;  Trenton  Public  Schools;  Woodhaven.

17.  Plaintiffs  hereby  incorporate  by reference,  as  additional
allegations  to  conform  to  the  evidence,  the  Findings  of  Fact  in
Support  of Desegregation  Area  and  Development  of Plans  issued
by  the  District  Court  June  14,  1972,  paragraphs  1 to  91.  (These
Findings  of Fact  are  attached  as  Exhibit  B.)

WHEREFORE,  plaintiffs  respectfully  pray  that  the  Court
order  a  speedy  hearing  of  this  action  according  to  law  and  the
guidelines  established  by  the  Sixth  Circuit  in  its  Opinion  and
Order  of June  12,  1973,  and  upon  such  hearing:

a.  Enter  a  decree  to  remedy  the  segregation  found  in-

cluding  an  interim  remedy;

b.  Enter  a  decree  approving  an  effective  metropolitan
plan  of  desegregation  for  the  Detroit  Public  Schools  which
promises  realistically  to  work  now  and  hereafter  to maintain  a
racially  unified,  non-discriminatory  system  of  public  school-
ing.  Such  plan  should  include  the  utilization  of all  reasonable
and  feasible  methods  and  tools  of desegregation  as set  forth  in
the  guidelines  established  by  the  Sixth  Circuit  in  its decision
en  banc  of June  12,  1973,  and  the  controlling  decisions  of the
Supreme  Court;  provided,  however,  pursuant  to  the  decision
of the  Sixth  Circuit,  that  existing  governmental  and  organiza-
tional  structures  shall,  in any  case,  be maintained  to the extent
possible,  pending  a  reasonable  opportunity  for  the  Michigan
Legislature  to act  to alter same;

c.  Enter  a  decree  enjoining  defendants  to  implement  no
later  than  the  1974-75  school  year  and  maintain  thereafter
such  an  effective  metropolitan  plan  for  the  desegregation  of
the Detroit  Public  Schools;

ee

Ia  299

d.  Award  to  plaintiffs’  attorneys  reasonable  counsel  fees
for  services  rendered  and  to  be  rendered  in,  and  reimburse-
ment  for all out-of-pocket  expenses  of, this action;

e.  Retain  continuing  jurisdiction  of  this  cause  and  grant
such  other  and  additional  relief  as  may  appear  to the Court  to
be equitable  and just.

Respectfully  submitted,

TRANSCRIPT  OF PROCEEDINGS  AND  TESTIMONY

WITNESS  —  ROY  L. STEPHENS,  JR.

(1)

KK

Proceedings  had  in  the  above-entitled  cause  before  Honorable
Stephen  J.  Roth,  United  States  District  Judge,  at  Detroit,  Mich-
igan, commencing  Tuesday,  April  6, 1971.

eK

{13]  ROY  L.  STEPHENS,  JR.,
was  called  as  a  witness  by  Plaintiffs  and  being  first  duly  sworn
testified  as  follows:

THE  COURT:  Your  full  name,  sir.

THE  WITNESS:  Roy  L. Stephens,  Jr., S-t-e-p-h-e-n-s.

DIRECT  EXAMINATION  BY  MR.  LUCAS:

Q.  Mr.  Stephens,  will  you  tell us  where  you  live?

A. 5340  Kellen  Lane,  Bloomfield  Hills,  Michigan.

Q. What  is your  occupation?

A.  Iam  Chairman  of the  Board  of Jensen-Miller  Company.

THE  COURT:  Just  a  point  of physical  facility  here.  Wouldn’t

it be better  if you  put your  lectern  over  to that  side?

[14]  MR.  LUCAS:  Yes,  I think  it would  be.

THE  COURT:  Then  counsel  could  see  the  witness.

Q. Mr.  Stephens,  have  you  held  a  position  with  the  Detroit

Board  of Education?

A. Yes,  I was  a  member  from  July,  1959  through  June  30,

1966, a six-year  term.

Q. Did  you  have  any  connection  with  the  Board.  or  any  part-
icularized  interest  with  the  Board  of  Education  prior  to  your

Ila2

elections?

A.  Yes.  For  two  years  prior  to  my  election  I was  the  repres-
entative  of the  Detroit  Council  of Parent-Teacher  Associations  to
the  Board  of Education.  In that  capacity  it was  my  duty to attend
the  School  Board  meetings,  to  transmit  thoughts  or  statements
that  the  Detroit  Council  of  Parent-Teacher  Associations  might
want  to  make  and  also  to listen  to  the  proceedings  and  to  report
back  to  the  Detroit  Council  of Parent-Teacher  Associations  on  the
proceedings  of the Board.

Prior  to  that  I was  for  two  years  legislative  chairman  of that
same  body  and  in  that  capacity  I was  interested  in legislation  as it
pertained  to  schools  and  in particular  as  it pertained  to  the  school
district  of the City of Detroit.

[15]  Q.  Did  you  hold  any  position  on  committees  appoint-
ed  by  the  Detroit  Board  of  Education  for  studies  of the  school

system? y
member  of the Citizens  Advisory  Committee  on  School  Needs.

I served  as  Chairman  of the  Finance  Commitee  and

Q.  When  was  the report  of that  committee  rendered?

A.  That  was  completed  in  1959.

Q.  Do you  know  the  inception  of that  study?

A.  1957.

Q.  As Chairman  of the  Committee  on  Finance  was  this  with
regard  to the  finance  of the Committee  itself or as  to financing  the
public  schools  of the City of Detroit?

A.  It had  nothing  to  do  with  the  financing  of the Commit-
tee  itself.  It  was  known  as  the  Romney  Committee  and  had
contributions  from  the  Ford  Foundation  and  other  organizations.
It had  to do with  the  financing  of Detroit  schools.

Q.  In  1957  did  you  also  hold  another  position  with  a citizens

group  in connection  with  public  schools  in  Detroit?  Particularly |
refer with  regard  to  Proposition  D?

A.  Yes.  I had  forgotten  that  for  a moment.  There  was a cit-
izens  committee  organized  to  imform  the  citizens  of Detroit  about

4

eeeraae

Ila3

the  needs of the schools  and  it was  known  as  the Citizens  Commit-
tee  for  Proposition  D.  It was  a  two  and  one-half  mill  increase  in
millage  which  was  being asked  for and  it failed.

[16]  Q.  Do  you  have  children  in  the  public  schools  of the

City of Detroit  at the  present  time?

A.  No, not  at the present  time.

Q.  Did  you  have  children  in the  public  schools?

A.  Yes,  I had  three.

Q.  When  were  your  children  in school,  sir?

A.  My children  were  in  school  in  the  City  of Detroit  all dur-
ing  the  time  I was  a  member  of  the  Board  and  my  oldest  son
attended  Cass  High  School.  My  daughter  attended  Ford  High
School  and  elementary  schools  prior  to  that.  My youngest  son  at-
tended  Dow  Junior  High  and  all  my  children  attended  McKenny
School  for their  elementary  school  education.

Q.  Did  you  live  anywhere  near  the Cass  School?

A.  No.  I lived  in  the  McKenny  School  area.  I lived  on  Braile,
which  was  in  the  far  northwest  about  halfway  between  Seven  and
Eight  Mile  Road  and  fairly  close  to  Lahser.

Q.  Did  your  son  get  to  Cass  by means  of bus  transportation?

A.  Yes.  My  son  attended  Cass  on  the  public  transportation

system.

Q.  What  subcommittees  or  full  committees  did  you  work

with  prior  to your  election  as an  officer  of the  Board?

A.  I was  Finance  Committee  Chairman.

Q.  Of the  Board  itself?

A.  Of the  Board  itself.

Q.  All right.

[17]  A.  I served  as  vice-president  for one  year  and  president

for one  year.

Q.  What  year  were  you  president  of the  Board?

lla4

A.  1964.

Q.  You  then  were  a  member  of the  Board  during  the  period
of  time  in  which  the  Equal  Education  Opportunities  Study  was
conducted?

A.  Yes, I was.

Q.  Mr.  Stephens,  were  you  also  on  the  Board  at the  time  the
findings  and  recommendations  of the  Citizens  Advisory  Commit-
tee  were  approved  or  adopted  by the  Board?

A.  Yes,  I was.

Q.  Was  the  Citizens  Advisory  Committee  appointed  by  the

Board or at the instigation  of the Board?

A.  Yes, it was,

Q.  Was  this  a  recommendation  of the  original  Romney  Com-
mittee  that  a  separate  study  be done  with  this  facet  of the  school
system?

A.  I’m  not  certain  but  I believe  that  it was  recommended.

Q.  Who  provided  the  funds  for the  study?
A.  The  major  portion  of the  funds  for  this  study  were  prov-

ided  by the Board  of Education.

Q.  What  was  the  source  of information  which  the  Equal  Edu-

cational  Opportunities  Committee  used?

[18]  A.  Primarily  they  had  their  own  staff people.

°Q.  Assigned  to them  by whom?

A.  Assigned  to them  by the  Board  of Education.

Q.  These  would  be Board  employees,  is that  correct?

A.  Yes.

Q.  All right.

A.  They  went  into  the  schools  and  looked  at  the  buildings,
looked  at  the  physical  plant,  looked  at the  programs,  talked  to the
people  in  the  schools,  teachers,  citizens,  students,  parents,  formed
their  own  opinion  from  their  own  research.

Q.  Did  they  conduct  hearings  where  parents  in  community

groups  would  appear?

A.  Yes,  they did.

Q.  Were  they divided  into  various  subcommittees?

-

Ia

A.  Yes,  they were.

Q.  Were  any  studies  conducted  at their  request  of the  Detroit
School  system?  For  example,  were  any  studies  made  of the  racial
composition  of various  schools?

A.  Yes.  They  requested  information  regarding  the  race  of
students  and  teachers  and  we  do not  have  any  notation  of race  on
any  records  and  so,  therefore,  it  was  necessary  to make a racial
count  and  I believe  we  did  this  probably  two  or three  times  from
perhaps  1961  to  1963.

[19]  Racial  counts  were  taken  simply  by  having  the  admi-
nistrator  in the  school  count  the  number  of whites  and  Negroes  on
his staff  and  the  individual  teachers  made  the  same  kind  of count
in the classroom  of the  students.

Q. This  was  a matter  that  came  before  the  Board  of Educa-

tion  for its approval  or  disapproval?

A.  Yes,  it was.

Q,  And  the  Board,  I take  it, voted  to conduct  this study?

A,  Yes,  we  felt  that  they  needed  the  data  or  they  wouldn’t

have  asked  for it so  we  approved  it.

Q.  I direct  your  attention  to  Plaintiff's  Exhibit  3 which  is al-
ready  in  evidence,  page  61.  This  is  the  report,  findings  and
recommendations  of the  Citizens  Advisory  Committee  on  Equal
Educational  Opportunities.

(22P  27.

Q,  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  Directing  your  attention  to  page  61  with

[23]  your  experience  both  as  a  person  interested  through  var-
ious  organizations  and  commissions  in the public  education  of the
City  of Detroit  and  familiarity  with  its data  and  as  a Board  mem-
ber, whether  or  not  you  agree  with  that  conclusion  and  would  you
state  that  conclusion  if you  do, sir.

{24]...

A.  Yes,  at that  time  I agreed  with  it.

eK

Ila6é

Q. ( By Mr.  Lucas)  What  does  the  statement  refer  to, sir?

A.  Essentially  it recommends  that  the pupil  transfer  policy of
the  Detroit  Public  Schools  should  be  liberalized  to  permit  any
child  at  any  grade  level  regardless  of race,  religion,  nationality  or
social level  to  transfer  to  any school  in  the system  under  the fol-
lowing  conditions:  Then  it lists two  major  conditions.

[25]  Q.  What  is the  predicate  initial  statement  in  paragraph

C, that  is the particular  basis  for the recommendation?

MR.  BUSHNELL:  I ask that  it all be read into the record  if

Mr. Stephens  is going to testify  from  it.

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  Please,  if you  will  read  from  the  beginning

of that  paragraph.

A.  This  is a paragraph  C, “Boundaries  and  Bussing:  Numerous
public  schools  in Detroit  are presently  segregated  by race.  The alle-
gation  that  purposeful  administrative  devices  have  at  time  been
used  to  perpetuate  segregation  in  some  schools  is clearly  substan-
tiated.  It is necessary  that  the  Board  and  its administration  inten-
sify  their  recent  efforts to desegregate  the  public  schools.  This  re-
port  will  now turn  to  the side of  the segregation  problem  that
seems  unquestionably  within  the  province  of the  Board  of Educa-
tion.  It is possible  for the Board  by various  arrangements  within  its
power  to  modify  segregated  conditions  within  the  school  system.
Already  the Board is on record  with certain  policies  which constitute
moves  in  this  direction,  for  example  the  policy  of  1959  on  trans-
porting  pupils.  ‘That  the  school  be  the  nearest  one  having  enough
space  provided  it  is not  or probably  will  not be needed  to serve
pupils  of its attendance  [26]  area  or  of an  attendance  area  as close
or closer  than  the  proposed  sending  school.’

“Administration  of  this  policy  can bring about desegregation
and has  done  so  in recent  instances.  Below  the subject  committee
submits  recommendations  intended  to help the  Board  to move  far-
ther  in  the  direction  of taking  all reasonable  steps  toward  desegre-

'

lla7

gation  within  the framework  of its  major  responsibility,  which  is
to  provide  the  best  possible  education  for  all  children.  All  such
proposals  should  be  evaluated  in  the  light  of their  general  educa-
tional soundness.”

ledge,  whether  or  not  schools  were  located.  It is a policy  question

{30}...

wee

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  Were  there  optional  attendance  zones  for

every  set  of schools?

A.  No.

Q.  Were  they  only  in certain  areas?

A.  Certain  areas.

**s

[31]...

-

A.  Well,  I will  say  that  central  administration  determined

which  areas  were  optional  areas.

ee

A.  Well,  I'd  say  this:  that  my  observations  would  lead  me  to
believe  that  in many  instances,  I’m  not  saying  this was universal,  in
many instances  students  who were  white  would select  the predom-

inantly  school  and  students  who  were  black  would  probably  go to
a school  that  was  predominantly  black.

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  Were  the  zones,  the  optional  attendance

zones  between  black  schools  and  white  schools?

A.  Frequently.  And  I would  say  that  was  probably  our  major

reason  [32]  for wanting  to eliminate  that  practice.

Q.  Was _ it then  adopted  another  policy  called  open  schools?
A.  Yes.

Q.  Would  you  tell  us  what  the open  schools  policy  was?
A.  In  the  open  schools  policy  we  estimated  whether  the  total
number  of the  schools  we  had  in Detroit  were,  if taken  asa  total,
overpopulated  to  the  extent  of about  115  percent  of the ‘capacity.
If a  student  was  in a school  that  was  over  115  percent  of capacity,
it was  considered  to  be  more  crowded  than  the average.  He could
then  transfer  to a school  that  had  seats  available,  and  these  schools
were  designated  by  administration  and  by  the  Board  as  open
schools.

In  most  instances,  he  was required  to provide  his own trans-
portation,  but  if he  was  in  a  school  with  more  than  15  percent
overcrowding,  in  other  words  more  than  115  percent  of capacity,
he  could  ask  for  a  transfer,  and  if he  could  find  a  school,  one  of
the  open  schools  that  had  vacancies,  he  could  ask  for  transfer  to
that  school  and  be transferred.

Many  students  were,  I would  say  probably  more  than  2,000.

Q.  Were  the  open  schools  during  the  time  you  were  on  the

Board  mainly  white  schools  or  black  schools?

[35]...

eee

A.  Well,  my  belief  is that  most  of the  schools  that  had  been
declared  open  schools  were  schools  that  were  more  than  90  per-
cent  white  in their  student  population.

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  What  was  the  result  of  the  open  school

policy  in terms  of integration  or  desegregation  of the  system?

A.  Initially  it  resulted  in  integration  to  the  extent  that
schools  such  as  Osborn  or  Henry  Ford  High  School,  which  were

lla9

[36]  more  than  90 percent  white,  would  receive  as many  as  200,
maybe  200  black  students.  And  in  that  respect  they  were  integ-
rated to the extent  of some  5 to 7 percent  of their  population.

Q.  You  said  that  was  the  initial  results?

A.  Yes.

Q,  It was  the  subsequent  result  of the open  schools  policy?
A.  There  probably  would  have  been  subsequent  results,  but  I
can’t  testify  to  those  because  I haven’t  had  up-to-date  ratio  count
information.  I don’t  know.

Q.  Do  you  know  whether  or  not  white  students  in  schools
which  were  heavily  or  predominantly  black  would  be  eligible  for
transfers  to  these  schools,  such  as  Ford,  Osborn,  which  you've
listed?

A.  They  were  at  that  time.

Q.  Do  you  know  whether  or  not  they  utilitized  that  option?

A.  Yes.  Some  did  to the extent  that  I've  indicated.

[145]  Q.  (By  Mr.  Flannery)  Mr.  Marks,  could  you  put  into
words  for  us,  if you  will,  or  describe  for  us  the  evolution  of the
racial  residency  patterns  in the  City  of Detroit  as  they  are  reflec
ted  in  the  exhibit  and  if you  would  care  to  do  so  from  closer  to
the  exhibit  that  would  be  fine  with  me,  whichever  is more  con-
venient  for you.

(The witness  approached  the exhibits.)
A.  The  form  of presentation  of these  individual  maps  is that
of percentage  of non-white.  In  1940,  the first count  that  there  was
ever,  whenever  there  was  an  official  census  related  to specific  areas
of residency,  it clearly  showed  major  areas  of concentration  as be-
ing black,  non-white  population  as a central  city population  group
but  with  some  major  areas  of dispersion  such  as  West  Warren-
McGraw;  Warren  City  Limits,  Eight  Mile-  Wyoming,  areas  on  the
east  and  on  the west  of Highland  Park  and  some  population  on  the
east  side.

There  was  some  scattering,  if this was a  map showing  indiv-
iduals  and  not  percentages  related  to  totals,  we  would  see  some
scattering  or  some  scatteration,  blacks  would  be  represented  in
some  of  the  individual  census  tracts,  not  presently  appearing  in
color.  That  was  the classic  Detroit  pattern.  Historically  blacks  have
lived  within  this what we call the older city bounded  by Livernois,
bounded  on  the  west,  [146]  bounded  by Conner  on the east and
bounded  in general  by Six  Mile  on  the  north.

iiaiity 1950,  the  ‘SOs  was ‘the  census  period  that  followed:Workd
War  11.  During World  War  II, Detroit,  of course,  was,  the issue  in
Detroit,  of course,  was where  would  housing’ be place? ‘In the war
years  and  in  the  post-war  years’  there  was  a-desperate  need  for
housing  to  be  built  in the city, primarily  for war workers  and men
after the  war for  the ‘returnees  and/or Veterans.  It was of great con-
cern  about  where  this housing  was  to  be placed:  because.  vacant
land, had. been held. I, mean there was  extensive,  yacant land, so
much,  so, that in  the, period  following. as in. the period 1945. to
1955,  approximately  200,000  homes  were, built i in Detroit, west of
Livernois,  north  of Six  Mile  and  east  of Conner.

Ila  12

There  was  almost  200,000  homes  in a  ten-year  periodbuilt  in

what  we  call  middle  city section.

Only  in  three  areas  could  blacks  buy new  houses.  Those  are
areas  of established  occupancy,  Eight  Mile-Wyoming,  and  the  So-
journer  Truth  area,  or  what  is called
the  Dog’s  leg sec-
tion  of  Detroit,  far  southwest.  So  that  out  of  the  200,000  new
homes  built  in  that  ten-year  period,  blacks  occupied  perhaps  one
per  cent  of those  or  were  able  to  purchase  one  per  cent  of them.
And  the  restrictions  of the  market  and/or  the  economy  were  ev-
ident  in  that  these  lines  could  be  seen  as  screens  or  barriers  to the
movement  of black  people  into  the  housing  market  generally.

[147]

By  1970,  however,  beginning  in ‘60, some  beginnings  in  1960,
evidenced  that  a  breakthrough  past  the  Livernois  line  was  begin-
ning,  and  that  the  movement  north  of Six  Mile  was  showing  fur-
ther.  It was  showing  further  opening  of areas  that  previously  had
been  prescribed.  The  far east  side,  on  the  other  hand,  was  just  as
solid  as ever.

By  1970,  the  breakthroughs  really  were  occurring,  so  much  so
that  it gave  people  in  our  city  great  hope  that  we  were  ending re-
strictions  in  the  housing  market,  that  people  were  being  viewed  as
people,  able  to  compete  and  buy  housing.  But  the  theory  on
which  our  office  was  operated  was  that  rather  than  restrictions  be-
ing ended,  the market,  as  the real  estate  market,  people  who  mark-
eted  housing  had  simply  acknowledged  that  there  was  great  expan-
sion  of  black  population.going  on.  It  was  a  great  movement  of

whites  to  the  suburbs,  a great  loss  of white  population  by the  cen-
tral  city,  and  that  blacks  had  to  acquire  properties.  These  addi-
tional  [148]  blacks  in  our  city,  both  from  natural  growth  and
from  population movement,  that  is movement  to  the  city,  had  to
be  housed  somewhere  .  So  that  all  we  saw  happening  was  simply
the  relaxation  of a previous  line-restricting  barrier  and  at least  the
beginnings  of a  new  line  forming  roughly  at Greenfield-Southfield
movements.  There  had  been  some  movements  beyond  this that do
not  show,  some  individual  families  in  percentages  too  small  to be
represented  on  this map,  but  there  is some  dispersion  there.

There  is almost  no  dispersion  in  the  areas  east  of Conner,  ex-
cept  that  which  is represented  in the  two  tracts,  census  tracts  788
and  781  on  the east  side.

Ila  13

Q, Thank  you,  Mr.  Marks.
(There  witness  returned  to the  stand)
Q,  Did  I understand  you  to  say,  sir,  that  as  early  as  1940
there  was  some  black  occupancy  in the Sojourner  Truth  and  Eight
Mile-Wyoming  area?

A.  Yes.  The  depression  years,  like  any  population  group,
some  people  hated  the central  city, some  people  hated  the  conges-
tion  or  disliked  it,  wanted  to  escape  it and  they  sought  housing
elsewhere.  While  you  and  I might  question  if we  had  pictures  of
some  of that  housing  we  might  question  whether  they  were  mov-
ing  to  freedom  or  whether  they  had  just  moved  shacks  and
[149]  tarpaper  shacks  and  that  to a new  area,  because  there  was
some  very  depressing  housing  there.  But  it still represented  people
searching  for  something  better  and  away  from  the congestion  even
at that  time  of the central  city.

Q.  Were  there  in those  two  areas  that  I mentioned,  were  there

public housing  projects?

A.  During  the  war  years  the  City  of  Detroit  placed  tem-
porary  public  housing  facilities  and  permanent  facilities  in  the
Sojourner  Truth  area.  As  I am  sure  most  everyone  in this  room
knows,  there  was  a  1941  riot  in the  Sojourner  Truth  area.  It was  a
period  of extensive  community  controversy  over  the placement  of
that  public  housing.  Then  the  question  of who  would  occupy  the
public  housing  was  a  matter  of extended  public  debate  and  at the
point  at  which  the  debate  was  resolved  and  the  first  occupants
moved  to  take  possession,  first  black  families  were  to  move  in,
there  was  a  riot  on  that  day  that  delayed  it to  block  their  taking
possession  of  the  property  and  delayed  their  ultimate  occupancy
of the project.

Q.  Mr.  Marks,  in reviewing  Exhibit  16, you  have  described  for
us  in  words  the  concentration  or  the  evolution  of  the  concen-
tration  as  it has  occurred.  Do  you  have  an  opinion,  sir,  as  to  the
factors  underlying  the  pattern  as  it has evolved,  and,  if so,  what  is
that opinion?

[150]  A.  There  are  many  factors  that  determine,  that  oper-
ate  in the  housing  market.  There  are  many  factors  that  determine
what  a person  views  as  suitable  housing  for himself  and  his family,
what  he might  view  as an  appropriate  location  for himself.

Ila  14

Housing  is very much tied in with ones sense of status,  ones
sense  of his  own  being.  People  consume  housing  for  more  reans
that just  shelter.  But  if we  were  to try to explain  that  pattern,  the
pattern  that  has  emerged  on  that  map  strictly  in the  terms  of the
combination  of the obvious  racial  patterning  there,  we  would  have
to explain  how  there  could  be, as  we  knew  there  was  in the black
community,  people  who  were  employed  year-round  at  fulltime
jobs as  fulltime  teachers  as  doctors,  some  laywers,  some  business
men.  There was plenty  of  range of  economic  level in the  black
community,  a  range  that  made  these  individuals  eligible  to  pur-
chase  housing  anywhere  in the City of Detroit.

Obviously  they  were  not  anywhere,  were  not  allowed  to pur-

chase  anywhere.

And,  similarly  if we  ask  the  question  the  other  way:  What  is
the  nature  of the  housing  outside  of where  Negores  live?  Was that
suburban  housing  too  expensive  a  character  that  Negroes  could
not  possibly  compete  for  it?  We  have  to  answer  that  in  the  nega-
tive  also,  that  the housing  that  was  represented  outside  of those
broad  limits  of  Livernois  [151]  and  Six  Mile  and  Conner  that |
have  given  contained  properties  that  might  range  anywhere  from
what  might  be  called  rural  slum—as  a  matter  of  fact,  a  stone’s
throw  from  one  of our  most  important  high schools  in the  far west
side has housing  that  can only be viewed  as rural slum and ranges
all  the  way  up  to some  of the most  expensive  and  valued  property
in our  city.

So  the  explanation  of why  Negroes  live  where  they  live  fails

us if we  apply strictly  an  economic  test.

There  are  many  screens  which  sift  out  people  which  have
historically  prevented  blacks  from  living where  their  choice  would
suggest.  But  the  history  of the  Commission  on  Community  Rela-
tions  is the history  of an  agency  that  year  in the year out  from  our
creation,  engaged  in what  we  call  firefighting.

[152]  So  that  as  black  folks  sought  housing  in  that  market
and  as  the  numbers  of  black  people  in  our  city  increased,  each
year  the  number  of housing  cases  that  we  would  have  to deal  with
where  there  was  evidence  of  rock-throwing  or  mob  action  or  ef-
forts  to enforce  covenants  or  conspiracies  to  prevent  people  from
moving  in,  these  kinds  of  things  literally  fill  file  cabinets  in our
office  and  at  some  points  we  have  charted  the  nature  of housing

-

Ila  15

incidents  in the  private  market  that  evidenced  clearly  the  opposi-
tion  of  some  white  people  to  the  movement  of  black  families.
That is just one  factor.

It doesn’t  deal  at  all  with  the  questions  of who  markets  hous-
ing, how  does  housing  get marketed  and  how  do people get valued.

We  might  note,  as  infamous  as  the  system  was,  and  it had  a
name,  called  the  Grosse  Pointe  System,  where  people  were  valued
by their,  given  ratings  so  that  a  black  man  had  no  rating at all, an
Italian  might  have,  I can’t  recall  the  specific  rating  system  but  I
remember  a  friend  of mine wondering  where a butcher  might  fit or
where  a Pole  who  was  a butcher  might  fit in that  rating  system.

I think,  as  a  matter  of fact,  one  of my  commissioners  when
this  issue  was  at  its  peak  was  quite  convinced  that  he  could  not
have  moved  into  the  Grosse  Pointe  area.  As much  as  a community
was  operative  in the Grosse
was  labeled  here  and  the  plan  [153]
Pointe  area,  clearly  status  was  also  a  factor  in  the  movement  of

able”  that  was  clearly  the  rating  given  to  black  people  whether

In  other  sections  of  our  city  that  same  rating  of people  by

wake  of all of the  restrictive  convenants,  the  restrictive  convenants
if we  had  a  map  that  showed  or  described  the pattern  of areas  ex-
cluded  from  Negro  occupancy  by, at one  point,  an  instrument  that
had  legal  standing,  we  would  be  covering  literally  three-quarters
of that  map.

These  public  barriers  have  been  eroded  over  the  years  and  so
they  no longer  have  standing  in  the  eyes  of the  courts,  no  longer
have  standings,  at  least  public  standing  in  private  business  prac-
tices.  But  the  reality  of these  traditions  still  continues  to this day
even  though  this doesn’t  exist.

Now,  in  our  agency  in our  city  and  state  and  in our  nation,
statutes  which  prohibit  discrimination  or  at  least  permit  a  dis-
crimated  —  an  individual  who  might  feel  he  [154]  has  been  dis-
criminated  against,  permit  him  to  appeal  to  a  court  of law  or  an

Il a  16

a

administrative  tribunal  of  some  sort  for  relief.
pattern  does  persist.

Nevertheless  the

Q.  Mr.  Marks,  what  governmental  policies,  if  any,  played

what  role  in the process  you  have  described?

A,  Well, government  was-

MR.  BUSHNELL:  Excuse  me,  Mr.  Marks.  Might  I ask  that  the
level  of government  be  established  either  in the  question  or  in the
answer,  local,  state,  federal.

Q,  At all levels,  Mr.  Marks,  but  if it  is not  clear  from  your
context  I will  ask  you  to  identify  which  level  you  are  talking
about  at a particular  moment.

A.  The  building  of these  cities  that  really  do occur  following
the  war  was  with  the  help of the  Federal  Government,  the  depres-
sion  years  first  had  produced  government  aid  to cities  in the  form
of subsidies  for  urban  renewal,  for  slum  clearance  in  the  building
of housing  projects  but  the  major  expansion  of  cities  was  done
with  the  help  of the  government,  FHA  and  other  kind  of guaran-
tees.

As  part  of  our  unhappy  governmental  history  there  is clear
evidence  that  the  Federal  Government  at  some  points  proscribed
and  described  ways  in which  undesirables  were  to  be  kept  out  of
neighborhoods,  ways  in  which  any  influence  that  might  lower
property  values  might  be  [155]  excluded  from  a  neighborhood,
advisories  that  later  became  parts  of the  code  of ethics  of the real
estate  profession.  Real  estate  —  as  part  of their  profession,  one  of
the  items  of their  code  was  to help keep out  of any  neighborhood
any  undesirable  influences.  They  ranged  everywhere  from  I sup-
pose  packing  houses  right  on  through  to  the  reality  of  helping
keep  black  folks  out  of neighborhoods.

That  is perhaps  a  negative  influence  of government.  There  are
other  things  which  were  part of our  Detroit  history,  also  an  unfor-
tunate  history,  but  a reality  nevertheless.

Public  housing  in  Detroit  was  segregated  from  its very  begin-
ning.  It was  built,  beginning  in  1934,  as  public  housing  for whites
or  for  blacks,  so  that  the  agency  I worked  for  when I joined  the
agency  in  1947,  the  agency  already  had  policy  that  said  that  the
problems  we  were  experiencing  in  our  community  grew  out  of

Ila  17

conditions  which  were  themselves  inherently  unequal.  And  that
tion  as  such  was  one  of those  patterns  and  practices  of our
city that  had  to  change  so  that  any  man,  white  or  black,  could  be
acknowledged  before  the  law as an  equal.

Public  housing  stayed  segregated  until  a  Federal  Court,  this
court  as  a matter  of fact,  that  is this district,  Judge  Lederle  ruled,
first  he spoke  out  clearly  in  [156]  the  community  in  1950,  I be-
lieve  in 1952;  then  he ruled  shortly  after  the  Supreme  Court  ruling
on  the  basic  question  of segregation  in schools  but  Detroit  main-
tained  public  housing  segregation  officially  to the point  of his rul-
ing.

It maintained  token  integration  some  three  or  four  years  be-
yond  that  and at which  time  it was  officially  ended  and  out  tenant
selection  practices  developed  to  conform  completely  with  the
right  of any  person  to  seek  public  housing  anywhere  in  the  city
that it was  available.

Q.  Do  you  know,  sir,  whether  there  are  still  projects  that  are

all black  or virtually  all black?

A,  There  are  projects  which  are  virtually  all black,  yes.

ee

[157]  CROSS  EXAMINATION  BY MR.  RITCHIE:

Q.  Mr.  Marks,  throughout  your  answers  to  the  direct  exami-
nation  you  used  the  word  “containment.”  Now,  in  your  own
words,  exactly  what  do you  mean  by the  policy  of containment  of
blacks within  the residential  areas  of the City of Detroit?

A.  Well,  when  a  piece  of  property  is not  freely  available  to
any  person  who  has  the  desire  to  purchase,  the  money  to  pur-
chase,  the  desire  to purchase  as  I say  and a willing  seller,  we  would
view  that  as  any  example  of individual  restriction,  that’s  a refusal
to  consummate  a  deal  on  a  piece  of property  that  is available  on
the market  with  a person  who  is desirous  of purchasing.

When  that  pattern,  when  evidence  of  a  pattern,  not  just  of
individual  refusals,  but  a  pattern  that  exists  throughout  a  total
area,  we  call that  containment.  It is just as  effective  a barrier  as  if a
wall were  built  in the community.

[184]  ALLEN  E. PRIESTLEY

was thereupon called  to the  stand  for and  on  behalf of the  plaintiffs
and,  having  been  first  duly  sworn,  was  examined  and  testified  as
follows:

[185]  DIRECT  EXAMINATION  BY MR.  LUCAS:

Q,  Mr.  Priestley,  what  is your  occupation?

A.  I am  a  vice  president  and  chief  title  officer  of  Burton

Abstract  & Title  Company.

Ila  20

Q,  How  long  have  you  been  a  title attorney  with  the Burton

Abstract  & Title Company?

A.  26 years.

Q.  Mr.  Siistches habe ioaibicient  ia aii  a

the records  of  the ineston ‘Atannast &  Tile Cobibatay  I  mae
to racial  covenants  in housing  and the practices  of  that  company
with respect  to  said covenants?

A,  IT have.

Q,  I show  you  what is an original  of  an affidavit  and ask you

to examine  it and  identify  it and  your  signature  thereof.

A,  Yes.  This is the affidavit.

Q.  Mr.  Priestley,  in order  to  expedite  the matter,  would  you
simply  read your  affidavit  into the record,  and after that  | want
you  to answer  these  gentlemen’s  questions,  if they  have  any.

A,  All right. This is entitled  Affidavit.

“I,  Allen  E.  Priestley,  am  presently  the  vice  president  and
chief  title  officer  of  Burton  Abstract  &  Title  Company.  Having
been  a title  attorney  with  said  [186]  company  in  Detroit  for  26
years,  I am  familiar  with  the  records  of the  company.  From  1910
through  February  15,  1950,  almost  all  new  subdivisions  through-
cut  Metropolitan  Detroit  contained  restrictions  and  covenants
against  ownership  and  occupancy  by  Negroes,  except  in  some
cases  where  Negro  residence  was restricted  to  domestic  servants
and  the  like.  Subdivisions  were platted  and recorded  in large  num-
bers  from  1920  to  1930  and  1945  to  1950.  In  the  intervening
years,  due  to  war  and  depression,  relatively  fewer  subdivisions
were  platted  and  reported.  The  attached  map,  Exhibit  A, accurate-
ly reflects  any  understanding,of  the  location  and  extent  of race-
restrictive  covenants  within  the  city  limits  of  Detroit.  A  similar
pattern  existed  in the  suburbs  to the  considerable  extent  to which
they  have  been  subdivided  by  1950.  On  February  15,  1950,  FHA
reversed  the  previous  policy  requiring  homogeneous  areas  and  re-
fused  to  insure  or  finance  properties  restricted  by explicit  racial
covenants  entered  into  after  that  date.  Thereafter,  most  new  sub-
divisions  did  not  contain  explicit  race  restrictions.  Restrictions on
lot  size and inspection  and acceptance  of purchase  restrictions  by
builder  and  subdivision  economies  persist.  To  my  knowledge,  the
FHA  prohibition  on  racial  covenants  in  no  way  was  directed  at

Ila  21

other  practices  of  owners,  builders,  brokers,  bankers  and  buyers.
In practice,  as well, the FHA policy  was not in  [187]  force against
jots  previously  subdivided  and  racially  restricted  by covenant  but
contained  in deeds
later developed  and sold. The racial  restrictions
continued  to  be reported  by Burton  Abstract  & Title  Company  in
its abstracts  and  title  insurance  policies  until  December  16,  1969.
On that  date,  as a result  of a request  of the  Department  of Justice,
dated  November  26,  1969,  to  the effect  that  the  continued  listing
of racial  restrictions  would  be  a  violation  of Title  8 of the  Civil
Rights Act of 1968,  a memorandum  was sent  to all agent and officers
of Burton  Abstract  & Title Company  agreeing to delete  or  omit  such
racial restrictions. The  letter  of  the Department
of Justice  and  the
response  of Burton  Abstract  & Title  Company  thereto  are  attached
herewith  as Exhibit  B to this  Affidavit.  Approximately  9 percent  of
transactions  handled  by Burton  Abstract  & Title  Company  are  han-
died  on  abstracts  only,  without  title  insurance  or  commitment.  In
those  cases  the  racial  restrictive  covenants  shown  on  the  chain  of
title  are  either  struck  out  or otherwise  erased.”

Signed:  “‘Allen  E, Priestley.”

And  it’s notarized  or  sworn  to,
Q.  Mr.  Priestley,  just  for  clarification,  that  erasure  in  the

asterisk  also  dates  from  the  1969  date,  is that  correct?

A.  Yes,  it does.

CROSS-EXAMINATION  (BY MR.  BUSHNELL)
[196]  Q. As  a  title  person  and  as  an  attorney  did you  or  did
Burton  believe  that  racial  restrictions  were  of any  force  and  effect
subsequent  to  the  Supreme  Court  decision  in approximately  1946,
as a matter  of law?

A.  We  were  not  completely  convinced  in  this matter  because
Sites  versus  Magee  and  the  related  cases  at that  time,  I believe  this
was  in  ‘46,  merely  established  that  the  restrictions  could  not  be
enforced  as  between  parties.  We  were  still  not  convinced  that  a
civil  damage  action  might  be  maintained  between  the  parties  and
because  of this  we  were  not  convinced  that  we  should  omit  such
restrictions  from  our  title  work  until  a  later  decision  and  this  is
referred  to  in  our  memorandum  that  is one  of the  exhibits  here,
this later  decision.  I don’t  recall  the citation  myself offhand.

{(322]  KARL  TAEUBER

was  thereupon  called  as  a  witness  on  behalf  of the  plaintiffs  and,

[349]...

A.  Segregation  indexes  of the  same  type  that  I’ve  been  discus-
sing have been calculated  for a number  of cities  for a number  of the
ethnic  groups  of European  origin.  The  census  does  identify  indivi-
duals,  whether  they  were  born  in another  country  or  whether  their
parents  were  born  in  another  country  and  then  identifies  in  the
census  fifteen  or  twenty  separate  nationality  groups.  And  we  can
compare  the  residential  patterns  of  one  nationality  group  with
that of another  group  or with  all native  whites  generally.

As  I say,  this  has  been  done  for  a  large  number  of such  com-
parisons,  and  the  segregation  indexes,  depending  on  the  particular
groups  involved,  tend  to  be  ten,  twenty  or  very  rarely  about
thirty,  compared  to  these  indexes  of 70,  80,  90 for the  black  ver-
sus  white  comparison.  This  means  that  the  specific  answer  is that
the  black  segregation  [350]  is much  greater  than  the  segregation
of nationality  groups  from  the rest  of the  population.

If  I may,  I'd  comment  that  this  represents  some  degree  of
change  over  the  last  half  century,  so  that  at an  earlier  point  in our
history,  say  1910;  there  was  not  that  great  difference  between  the
segregation  of  black  groups  and  of  nationality  groups,  but  the
nationality  segregation  measures  diminished  very  rapidly.  And
since  that  time  the  measures  of  segregation  of black  from  white
have gone  up very  dramatically.

Il a 24

Q,  Doctor,  with  respect  to  the degree  of segregation  between
blacks  and  whites,  how  does  Detroit  compare  to  the  other  cities
that  you  have  studied.?

A.  With  respect  to  the  specific  index  value,  Detroit  is not  at
all  unusual.  The  specific  value  cited  in  the  book  on  page  39 was
84.5  for  1960.  This  puts  it in the middle  range  of cities.  There are
some  cities  that  are  more  segregated  and  some  that  are  less.

ee

(S372...

A.  Yes.  My  approach  to  this  is  to  consider  that  there  are
several  types  of  causes,  possible  causes  of residential  segregation
by race.  One  obvious  one  may  be  referred  to  as  Negro poverty  or
economic  status.  We know  that blacks  in American  cities  are  poorer
that  whites.  We  know  that  poor  housing  tends  to be located  some-
what  differently  from  more  expensive  housing  so  that  this  might
be one  contributory  factor  to segregating  the races.

Another  factor  might  be that  one  group  prefers  to live with its
own  members.  This  is  depending  on  which  group  you  look  at.
Let’s  say  the  Negro  preference  or  choice  [358]  interpretation,
Negroes  live  in a  segregated  fashion  because  they  prefer  to. There
has  also  been  considerable  literature  and  discussion  of  the
possibility  that  there  are  mechanisms  of discrimination  by which
whites  or  white  institutions  make  it difficult  for  Negroes  to exer-
cise  a choice  and  to  obtain  housing  and  live  wherever  they would
like.

My own  feeling  is that  all  of these  contribute  to the  patterns
of  segregation  in  United  States  cities  but  that  the  economic  or
poverty  interpretation  is usually  much  exaggerated  in part  because
we  have  sterotypes  of how  our  cities  are  organized  and  of what the
black  populations  are  like.

I believe  the  choice  argument  is easily  exaggerated  in part be-
cause  of too  easy  an  analogy  of what  is believed  to have  happened
to  some  of our  nationality  groups  in  the  past  and I believe  the
major  factor  that  persists  is discrimination  of one  sort  or  another
whereby  whites  deny  access  of blacks  to housing  in many  parts of
the  city.  If  I may  I could  comment  pretty—briefly  on  the basis for
’  these  conclusions  about  the  minimal  role  of economic  status  and

the choice.

Il a 25

The  economic  status,  first  I might  note  that  let’s  take  an  ec-
onomic  measure  that  is directly  relevant  to the  housing  people  get
and  this  is how  much  rent  do  they  [359]  actually  pay  now.  A
black  household  that  is paying  a rent  of say  $76.00  —

derpinnings  for  my  argument,  saying  that  a non-  white  family  that

economic  status  for  a  $76.00  monthly  rent  housing  unit  anyplace
in the city.

In  1960,  the  census  shows  us  that  within  the  City  of Detroit
the  median  rent  paid  by non-white  families  living  in rented  houses
was  $76.00  a  month.  The  median  is that  figure  which  cuts  the  to-
tal distribution  in half;  half paid  more  and  half paid  less.

For  whites  in  1960  the  median  rent  paid  in the  City  of De-
troit  for rental  housing  was  $64.00  a month,  so  that  the  non-white
population  on  the  average  was  paying a higher  rent  within  the  city
than  was  the  white  population  and  yet  the  non-white  population
had  a  radically  different  distribution  [360]  and,  indeed,  a concen-
trated  residential  pattern  and  we  do  not  find  that  rich  blacks  live
in the  same  areas  as  white  blacks,  that  the  only difference  —  I got
that wrong  —

THE  COURT:  A contradiction  in terms.
A,  These  days  that  term  may  be  in use.  The  rich  whites  and
rich  blacks  do  not  live  intermixed  throughout  the  city.  The  poor
whites  do  not  live  in the same  areas  that  the poor  blacks  do. There
is something  more  than  simply  economic  status  that  accounts  for
this.

I have  looked  at  this  in  much  more  complex  statistical  pat-
terns.  For  instance,  I  considered  simply  the  value  of  owner-
“occupied  housing  and  rental  level  of renter-housing  in each  tract.
One can check  each  tract  as to  the value of  housing,
whether
$10,000,  $10,000  to  $15,000  and  so  forth,  how  many  housing

units  rent  for  more  than  $150  a  month  and  how  many  for less
than  $20.00.  One  can  say  this  is the  basic  economic  structure of

Now,  we  know  for  the  city  what  share  of all  of those people

don’t  have  the figures  at hand  but  in any  case  one  would  then  find

can they say, thereisa  hypothetical  distribution  of blacks  throughout

find  that  it’s  about—I  did  it  for  1940,  1950  and  1960,  and got
values of 20, 17 and  17.

So  that  I say  in simply  looking  at  the economic  status in this
relatively  simply  way,  we  find  that  the  economic  factors  would
produce  about  one-fourth  or  one-fifth  of the  segregation  that  we
actually  find  when  we  go  out  and  measure  where  blacks  and
whites  live.

Now,  using  more  complicated  models  which  I won’t  go  into
unless  necessary,  I find  that  the  role  of economic  factors  is some-
what  less than  this would  say.  [362]  This said  it accounts  for may-
be  one-fourth  of  the  segregation  and  I think  that  is an  exaggera-
tion  because  the model  is a very  simple  one.

A  second  explanation  that  I said  in addition  to  the  poverty
one  is the  choice  one  and  I think  the  kind  of testimony  you  may
get  from  blacks  living in Detroit  may  be more  relevant  but  I would

Ila  27

note  two  facts,  first,  on  a  national  basis  polls  by the  Louis  Harris
organization  in  1963  and  in  1966,  after  some  of the  Black  Power
rhetoric  had  become  quite  common,  it showed  64 pércent  and  68
percent  respective  of the  black  population  sample  saying  that  they
prefer  integrated  neighborhoods  and  only  one-fifth  or  less  saying
they preferred  to live  in black  neighborhoods.

I also  find  in  my  examination  in  Detroit  and  in  these  nine
other  cities  that  I studied  more  intensively,  that  the  blacks  who
lead  the  invasion  from  predominantly  black  areas  to  white
areas  on  the  periphery  as  the  ghetto  expands,  that  these
blacks  tend  to  be  the  wealthier  and  more  educated  blacks  and  I
interpret  this  as  indicating  that  those  who  can  afford  to  do  so
among  the  black  population  try and  escape  the  worse  housing  and
seek  better  housing.  One  of  the  things  they  seek  is housing  that  is
less  segregated  so  that  this  is  an  expression  in  their  behavior  of
their  choice,  [363]  without  just  asking  them  what  they  believe,  it
is a matter  of seeing  how  they  actually  behave.

I don’t  think  the  choice  factor  is very  relevant.

I think  there  is considerable  evidence  in our  society  for  the
past  as  well  as  for the  current  situation  of various  types  of discrim-
ination  in  the  housing  market  where  people  in all levels  of the  real
estate  industry,  whether  it is  a person  showing  the  home  to a black
who  is looking,  whether  it is the  person  managing  the  financial  ar-
rangements  for  this  or  financing  the  developer,  there  is consider-
able  discrimination  against  blacks  trying  to  keep  black  residential
areas,  keep  blacks  in  defined  residential  black  areas.  There  is con-
siderable  evidence  of this  that  I have  examined  and I believe  that
this  is the  major  factor  accounting  for  the  kind  of segregation  that
occurs,  not  just  in  the  present  but  also  in  the  past,  because  the
structure  of the  city  is well  established  now.

Any  individual  person  coming  to  it sees  the  way  it is structur-
ed,  sees  the  social  patterns  that  have  been  developed,  and  hence,
tends  to  accommodate  to  that,  so  that  we  have  not  only  current
patterns of discrimination  but  also  past  patterns  producing  the situ-
ation  that  has persisted  for at  least  the  last  30 years  and  developed
over  the  [364]  period  prior  to  that.

I think  it is this  that  has produced  and  sustained  the high de-

gree  of segregation  that  I find  in the  United  States  cities.
