tiffs and  being first duly sworn,  testified  as  follows:

eK

[445]  Q. (By Mr.  Flannery)  Mr.  Sloane,  do you  have  an  Opin-
ion as to whether  the policies of the FHA and its predecessor  agencies
and  the  Veterans  Administration  have  contributed  to  racial  res.
idential  separation  of people  in our  urban  areas?

A,  Yes, I do.
MR.  BUSHNELL:  I would  repeat  or  reinstitute  my  continv-

ing objection.

THE  COURT:  Yes, it will be so  understood.
MR.  FLANNERY:  Thank  you.
Q,  (By  Mr.  Flannery)  Mr.  Sloane,  would  you  tell us  what  that
opinion  is, and  would  you  also  tell  us  the  basis  for  that  opinion?
And  so  that  I may  avoid  interrupting  you,  would  you  describe  the
policies  to which  you  refer  and  the manner  in which  they  operate?
A,  In my  opinion,  the  Federal  Housing  Administration  and  to
a  lesser  extent  the  Veterans  Administration  have  been  formidable
factors  in  developing  the  patterns  of racial  residential  separation
that  exists  in metropolitan  areas  today.  The  reason I say  that  toa
lesser  extent  the  Veterans  Administration,  is that  the  Veterans  Ad-
ministration  is a newer  agency,  and  it merely  followed  policies  and
practices  which  the FHA  was  responsible  for initiating.

[446]  The  FHA  was  created  as  part  of the National  Housing
Act  of  1934.  It  was  the  first  major  direct  involvement  with  the
Federal  Government  in  what  previously  had  been  largely a private
enterprise  operation  —  housing.  This  presented  the  Federal
Government  with  an  opportunity  to intercede  and  impose  its own
policies  subject  to  constitutional  safeguards  on  what  had  been  al-
ready  clear  evidence  of discriminatory  practices  by various  repre-
sentatives  of  the  private  housing  and  home  finance  industry,  real
estate  brokers,  builders,  mortage  lenders.  Instead,  FHA in its early
days was  perhaps  the most  active  exponent  of racial  discrimination
and  racial  segregation  in  housing.  Its early  underwriting  manuals,
as  early  as  1935  to  1938, gave  instructions  to their  underwriters  as
to  the  various  factors  that  bear  on  how  they  would  appraise
houses.

?

eK

Il  a  29

1448]...

Q. (By  Mr.  Flannery)  Mr.  Sloane,  what  is your  opinion  with
respect  to  the  effects  of the  policies  of the  FHA  and  the  VA  on
residential  segregation  in urban  areas?

[449]  A. The  policies  of FHA,  in my opinion,  and  to a lesser
extent  those  of VA  were  formidable  factors  in the development  of
the racial  residential  patterns  that  we  have  today  in metropolitan
areas.

FHA,  as  I said  earlier,  was  created  in  1934  and  its policies  in
its early  years  were  to actively  promote  racial  segregation,  in fact,
racial  discrimination.

In  the  early  underwriting  manuals  which  were  entered  as  ex-
hibits,  FHA  gave  advice  to  its  underwriters  as  to  the  factors  on
their  appraisals  of particular  houses  for purposes  of FHA mortgage
insurance.

In  Plaintiff's  Exhibit  56  of  the “FHA eadieuiitan manual
with  revisions  to  June  1,  1935,  FHA  in a section  entitled  “Pro-
tection  from  Adverse  Influences”  stated  as  follows  in Section  310:

“‘Important  among  adverse  influences  besides  those  men-
tioned  above  are  the  following:  Infiltration  of inharmonious  racial
or nationality  groups.”

In  its  1938  underwriting  tes  FHA  had this to  say  to  its

underwriters  concerning  quality  and  accessability:  —

Q. Excuse  me.  Would  you  identify  the’ exhibit number  and

the page?

A.  Exhibit  56-A,  Section.95!.  This  is the  underwriting  man-
ual  with  revisions  to  February,  1938.  They  said  in  Section
951:[450]  “If the children  of people  living  —”

Just  a moment.

“If the  children  of people  living in  such  an  area  are  compelled
to attend  schools  with a  majority  or a considerable  number  of the
pupils  represents  a  far  lower  level  of  society  or an  incompatible
tacial  element,  the  neighborhood  under  consideration  will  prove
far less stable  and  desirable  than  if this condition  did  not  exist.”

II  a 30

FHA  went  beyond  its advice  to  underwriters  by recommend-
ed  restrictive  covenants  be  filed  to  assure  against  undesirable  con-
.  ditions  being  in existence.

In  Plaintiff's  Exhibit  56-A,  the  underwriting  manual  with  re-

visions  up  to February,  1968  —

:

Q,  What  date?

A,  February,  1968.
Q.  1968?

A.  I’m  sorry.  ‘38,  1938.  In  Section  980,  which  is entitled
“Protections  from  Adverse  Influences,”  in  one  of  these  subsec-
tions  they  recommend  recorded  restrictive  covenants,  which
would  prevent  this  adverse  influences.  And  one  of the  adverse  in-
fluences  that  they recommend  the covenant  prevent,  the  provision
would  be  prohibition  of  the  occupancy  of  properties  except  by
the  race  for which  they  intended.

[452]  .A.  FHA’s  policy  of promoting  housing  segregation  and
housing  discrimination  lasted  until  1947  quite  clearly.  Until  that
time  they  were  still  advising  their  underwriters  against  inharmoni-
ous  racial  groups.

In  1947,  there  was a slight  change  of form  in FHA  policy,  was
not  one  of  substantive.  They  stopped  referring  to  inharmonious
racial  groups  and  began  referring  to  inharmonious  user  groups.
This  is  well  after  World  War  Two.  This  did  not  actually  reflect  a
change  in FHA  policy.

The  first  really  substantive  change  in  FHA  policy  came  as  a
direct  result  of the  United  States  Supreme  Court  decision  in Shelly
versus  Kramer,  which  ruled  that  racially  restrictive  covenants  were
judicially  unenforcible,  and  it was  nearly  two  years  after  that  deci-
sion  that  FHA  changed  its policy  from  one  of encouraging  racially
restrictive  covenants  to  one  of  refusing  to  insure  mortgagors  on
properties  which  carried  racially  restrictive  covenants  filed  of
record  after  February  15,  1950.  The  Supreme  Court  decision  was
rendered  in  May  of  1948.  Thus,  it was  nearly  two  years  after  that
decision  that  FHA  policy  changed.

[453]  In  the  meantime,  the  Veterans  Administration  loan
guarantee  program  had  been  initiated  shortly  after  the  end  of

_— |.

Il a31

World  War  Two,  and  Veterans  Administration  tended  to  follow
FHA policies  and  practices,  although  the Veterans  Administration
must  be  said  had  never  did  actively  encourage  racial  discrimina-
tion.  Its  policy  from  the  beginning  was  one  of neutrality.  It fol-
lows  FHA’s  lead in  1950  by also  declining  to  guarantee  loans  on
property  carrying  racially  restrictive  covenants  filed  after February
15, 1950.

FHA  and  VA  policy  for roughly  the  next  decade  continued  to
be one  of neutrality,  that  is  they  would  willingly  insure  lending
institutions,  finance  builders,  regardless  of whether  these  lending
institutions  or  builders  followed  racial  homogeneous  policies  or
practiced  housing  dsicrimination.

In  1959,  the  Commission  found  that  after  some  twenty-five
years  of  FHA  activity,  less  than  2  per  cent  houses  provided
through  the  FHA  programs  had  been  occupied  by black  families.
The  problem  was  more  than  one  of whether  FHA  policy  had  from
1950  to  ‘60  been  one  of neutrality  or  one  of actively  promoting
discrimination.

FHA  had  done  so  much  to  sow  the  seeds  of housing  discrimi
nation  and  to  lay  the  foundation  for racial  separation  in metropo-
litan  areas  to  harden  the  patterns  of  [454]  residential  segregation
that  exist,  to  harden  the  practices  of the real estate  industry,  that
even  today,  some  thirty-five  years  after  FHA  begun,  we  are  still
reaping  the  results  of  what  FHA  did  in  this  first  decade  of
existence,  and  that  is  a  real  problem.  So  that  the  posture  of
neutrality  is  certainly  no  longer  capable  of reversing  the  patterns
of residential  segregation  which  this  federal  agency  was  so  largely
responsible  for creating  in the  first  instance.

It was  not  until  November,  1962  that  FHA  and  VA  became  at
least  legally  positive  forces  for  residential  desegregation.  That  was
as a result  of the  issuance  of the  executive  order  on  equal opportu-
nity  in  housing  issued  by  President  Kennedy  on  November  20,
1962,  which  directed  FHA  and  VA  and  other  departments  and
agencies  having  functions  relating  to  housing  to  take  all  actions
necessary  and  appropriate  to  prevent  discrimination  in  housing
‘
provided  in their  programs.

The  Commission  has done  a good  deal  of work  in determining
how ‘effective  FHA  and  VA  and  other  federal  agencies  have  gone

Il a 32

about  their  assignment  of preventing  discrimination,  and unfortup.
ately,  their  performance  has  been  characterized  by a  passive  and
unaggressive  attitude.  This is in sharp  contrast  to their  rather  force.
ful  policies  and  practices  with  respect  to  promoting  housing  dis.
crimination  and  housing  segregation.  When  it came  time  for  their
policies  to  [455]  change  180  degrees,  they  were  not  nearly  as
days in pro-
forceful  or  aggressive  as  they  had  been in  their earlier
moting  housing  segregation.

In  1968,  the  Federal  Fair  Housing  Law  was  enacted.  It was
Title  Eight  of the Civil  Rights Act  of 1968.  And  FHA  as well as all
other  agencies  having  housing  responsibilities  were  then  under not
only  a President  directive  but  a  Congressional  directive  to prevent
discrimination  in  nearly  all  housing  in  the  country.  And  two
months  after  the  Federal  Fair  Housing  Law  was  enacted,  the
United  States  Supreme  Court  in  Jones  versus  Mayer  & Company,
relying  on  a  1966  Civil  Rights  Law,  enacted  pursuant  to the Thir-
teenth  Amendment,  ruled  that  discrimination  on  the basis  of race
is prohibited  with  respect  to  all housing.  private  as  well  as  public.
So  that  as  of  today  FHA  is under a Presidential  directive  to end
discrimination  under  a  Congressional  directive  to  end  discrimina-
tion  and  under  a congressional  mandate  to end  discrimination.

And  on  the  basis  of  our  recent  analysis  of  their  activities,
which  is reported  in our  report  of last  October,  the  Federal  Civil
Rights  Enforcement  Effort,  their  activities  still  are  characterized
by  posture  of  timidity,  passivity,  lack  of  aggressiveness.  This,
again,  in  sharp-contrast  to  their  zeal,  to  the  zeal  with  which  they
promoted  housing  discrimination  back  in the  first  decade  of their
existence.

[456]  Q.  (By  Mr.  Flannery)  Mr.  Sloane,  can  you  tell  us
whether  back  in  1950  FHA  continued  to  insure  property  with
restrictive  covenants  if the  restriction  had  been  filed  before  1950?

A.  Yes,  they  did.  Their  refusal  to insure  mortgages  on  proper-

ty  only  applied  with  respect  to  covenants  filed  of  record  after
February  15, 1950.

Q.  Can  you tell  us,  sir,  whether  FHA  furnished  to developers

a proposed  or  model  restrictive  covenant?

A.  They  set  forth  provisions  which  contained  what  a  model
covenant  should  contain.  I  have  no  actually  seen  the  model

-_—

Il a 33

covenant,  which  a  number  of  people  say  that  FHA  did  provide.
But  they  did  set  forth  the  provisions  of  what  such  a  covenant
should contain.

Q.  Can  you  state,  sir,  whether  on  the  basis  of information
known to your  commission,  findings  made  by  your  commission,
and investigations  made  by the  Commission,  disclose  whether  the
proposed model  covenant  included  racial  restrictions?

A.  Yes, it did.
Q. Mr.  Sloane,  do  you  have  an  opinion  with  respect  to the
idential  racial  segregation  and  the policies
relationship  be
and activities  of publi housing, Public  Housing  Authority  and  its
predecessor  agencies?
A.  Yes, I do.
Q. Could  you  in the  manner  that  you  have  described  for  us,
the  [457]  evaluation  of FHA  policies,  describe  the policies  of the
public housing  sector?

A.  Yes.  In my  opinion,  the  public  housing  program,  which  is
~  combined  federal-state  agency  program,  has  been  a very  strong
factor  in  the  development  of  the  racial  residential  patterns  that
have  developed  today.  The  policy  of the federal  public  housing  of-
ficials  has  been  different  in its  history  from  that  of FHA.  Public
housing  program  which  began,  was  created  in  }937  as  part of the
#hicourage  racial  dis-
United  States  Housing  Act,  never  did  actively
crimination  or  racial  segregation.  In  fact,  it had  perhaps  the most
progressive attitude  of any  federal  agencies  concerned  with  housing
at that  time.  It insisted  on  a  racial  equity  formula  to  insure  that
minority  families  got  their  fair  share  of public  housing.  This  is in
contrast  to  FHA,  which  as  I noted  earlier,  as  of  1959  less  than  2
per cent  of its FHA  housing  had  been  occupied  by black  families.
The  United  States  Housing  Authority  insisted  on  the  racial
equity  formula.  However,  it left  to  the discretion  of local  housing
authorities  the  decision  as  to  whether  public  housing  would  be
provided  on  a  racially  integrated  or  racially  segregated  basis.  Al-
most  invariably  it  is provided  on a racially  segregated  basis.  This
was in the North  as  well  as  in the South.

[458]  It was  not  until  the  issuance  of the executive  order  on
equal  opportunity  in  housing  in  November,  1962,  twenty-five
years  after  the  public  housing  program  began  that  the  official
policy  of the federal  agency  charged  with  the  responsibility  for ad-

Il a 34

ministering  this  program  changed  to  one  of prohibiting  racial seg.
regation  in public  housing.

As early as 1954  after  the  decision  in  Brown  versus  Board of
Education  on  legally  compelled  school  segregation,  it  was  quite
clear  to public  housing  officials  that for local housing  authorities
to segregate  families  by race  was  a  violation  of the Fourteenth
Amendment.  In  fact,  the  Commission  in  1961  wrote  to  the  then
Public  Housing  Administration  which  was  the  successor  to  the
United  States  Housing  Authority  and  asked  how  they  could  justify
a  policy  of  permitting  local  housing  authorities  to  segregate
families  by  race  in  view  of  not  only  the  Brown  versus  Board  of
Education  decision  but  subsequent  decisions  by  United  States
Court  of Appeals  dealing  specifically  with  legally  comparable  seg-
regation  of public  housing  and  they conceded  that  the decisions  all
seemed  to suggest  that  such  segregation  was a violation  of the Con-
stitution,  but  they were  not  prepared  to do anything  about  it until
they were  commanded  to by the  President.

[462]  Q. (By  Mr.  Flannery)  Mr.  Sloane,  would  you  describe
for  the  Court  [463]  in summary  fashion,  if you  will, precisely  tlie  ”
policy  techniques,  and  by that  I refer  to tenant  assignment  policies
and  site  selection  policies  with  respect  to  public  housing  that  has
produced  the  results  which  you  have  already  stated  in conclusion-
ary  form.

A.  Until  the  issuance  of the  executive  order  on  equal  oppor-
tunity  in housing,  the  typical  tenant  assignment  policy  which  was
permitted  by the  Federal  Public  Housing  Administration  was  one
of assigning  tenants  on  the  basis  of  their  race.  This  was  supple-
mented  by a site  selection  policy  permitted  by the  Federal  Hous-  _
ing Administration  whereby  local  housing  authorities  would  select
sites designated  for Negro  occupancy  or  white  occupancy  and  then
would  assign  tenants  to  those  projects  on  the  basis  of their  race
and  this  was  changed  following  the  issuance  of the executive  order
on  equal  opportunity  in  housing,  a  policy  which  has  evolved  toa
point  where  now  the  Public  Housing  Administration,  its successor,
the  Housing  Systems  Administration,  insists  on  a balanced  site se
lection  policy  of local  housing  authorities  to  assure  there  are  sites
outside  areas  of racial  concentration  as  well  as  within  and  a tenant

Il a 35

on the
assignment  policy  which  no  longer  permits  assignments
basis of race  but is a  form of first come,  first served tenant assign-
ment policy.

[464]  Q.  Can  you  state,  sir,  whether  these  amendments  to
policies  in  your  opinion  have  dispelled  the prior  effects  of the
prior policies?

“A.  No,  they  have  not.  For  one  thing

the program  has been  in
existence  for  well  over  thirty  years.  It  has  produced  something
over  800,000  units,  low-income  housing.  The  patterns  of  such
cities in which they are located  are so clear
housing in the various
that  it would  take  a monumental  effort  to  reverse  these  patterns,
black  families  know  the  housing  projects  that  they  are  supposed  to
be living  in; white  families  know  the housing  project  they are  sup-
posed to be living  in because  the  patterns  of rigid  racial segregation
were  established  over  a  thirty-year  period  and  very  difficult  to re-
verse

Some  of te  more  recent  policies  of the  Federal  Public  Hous-
ing officials  have  been  aimed  at  eliminating  some  of the effects  of
the past but  these  policies  in my  opinion  still are  too  tentative,  not
nearly  forceful  enough  to  make  much  headway  in terms  of revers-
ing the rigid  patterns  that  have  developed.

Q.  Mr.  Sloane,  could  you  describe  for  us  again  in  summary
fashion  the effects,  if any,  on  residental  racial  segregation  resulting  —
from  the policies  of regulatory  agencies?

A.  There  are  four  agencies,  if I understand  your  question  cor-
rectly,  that  are  involved.  These  are  agencies  which  regulate,  [465]
supervise  and  benefit  most  of the  nation’s  mortgage  lending  insti-
tutions.  There  is one,  Federal  Home  Loan  Bank  Board  which  regu-
lates  and  benefits  savings  and  loans  associations  responsible  for
something  of over  40  per  cent  of the  nation’s  home  financing  and
agencies  which  regulate  and  benefit  the  nation’s  commercial  and
mutual  savings  banks.  These  would  be the office  of the Controller
of Currency  with  the  responsibility  of national  banks,  the  Board
of Governors  of  the  Federal  Reserve  System  with  responsibility
for  state  bank  members  of  the  Federal  Reserve  System  and  the
Federal  Deposit  Insurance  Corporation  which  insures  deposits  of
all  national  banks,  all  Federal  Reserve  member  banks  and a large
number  of state  banks  which  are  not  members  of the  Federal  Re-

Il a 36

serve  System.

Their policies  over  the years has been  one of  complete  neut-
rality.  Their  concern  has  been  limited  to  the  safety  and  soundness
of investments  and as far as these  agencies  are concerned  the
lending  institutions  who  may  have  responsibility  have  been  free to
practice  racial  discrimination,  if they  so  chose.

This  has  been  changed  as a matter  of law  by the enactment  of
Title  8  of  the  Civil  Rights  Act  of  1968,  which  prohibits  dis.
crimination  in mortgage  lending.  These  agencies  have been slow to
adopt  procedures  aimed  at  assuring  lending  institutions  that  they
regulate  are,  in fact,  not  practicing  [466]  racial  discrimination.

One  of the  problems  has been  that  the  policies  of the agencies
has  tended  to  reflect  the  policies  of the  financial  institutions  that
they regulate.

%

For  example,  in  1961,  we  asked  a  question  of each  of  these
four  agencies  whether  they  thought  it would  be  appropriate  for a
lending  institution,  mortgage  lending  institution  to  consider  the
race  of  the  would-be  borrower  or  the  racial  composition  of the
neighborhood  into  which  he  wanted  to  move  as  an,  a  factor  in
determining  whether  to make  a loan  and  each  of them  said  yes.

‘One  answer  was  suggested  that  not  only  was  it permissible  but

it probably  was  the  obligation  of the  lending  institution  —

.

Q.  Earlier  in  your  testimony,  Mr.  Sloane,  you  referred  to a
study  that  has  been  completed  but  as  I understand  has  not  yet
been  disseminated  concerning  the  effects  of  programs,  fairly
recent  programs,  as  I understood  your  testimony  with  respect  to
home  ownership  for  lower  income  families.  Can  you  state  what
what are the
that  program  is and  with respect  to  racial segregation
investigative  findings  of the  investigators  of your  commission?

A.  This  program  was  adopted  as part  of  the  1968  Housing
Urban  Development  Act.  It’s a  program  of federal  subsidies  to en-
able  lower  income  families  to become  home  owners.

The  commission  recently  concluded  an  investigation  of how
this  program  is operated  in  four  metropolitan  areas  and  what  we

Ila37
found  was  that  the  very  same  patterns  that  [468]  exist  in  the
housing  market  in general, that  is, white  families  by  and  large liv-
ing in suburban  areas,  central  cities  —  housing in central cities oc-
cupied by black  families  has been  repeated  almost  precisely  in this
The  usual justification  for  the  pattern  in the  general housing
market  which  is.  that  economics  is  a  key  factor  and  the  black
family  can’t  afford  housing  in  suburban  areas  and  that  is  no
application  with  respect  to  the program  which,  by statute  is offer-
ed to  families  who  are  within  the  same  income  limits  blacks  as
well as  whites.  Housing  is subject  to cost  limitations  which  are  the
same  for all  families,  black  as  well  as  white,  yet  we  found  new
housing  in  metropolitan  areas  is being  built  in the  suburbs  being
occupied  almost  entirely  by white  families  and  in a few  instances
black  families  are  occupying  new  subdivisions  housing  in all black
subdivisions,  whereas  existing  housing  under  the program  is by and
large  in the  central  city occupied  almost  entirely  by black  families.
In other  words,  the  very  same  pattern  in  the  general  housing

market  is being repeated  in the  program  with  no economic
justification  at all.

Q. Can  you  tell  us  what  percentage  of the  market  of home  is

financed  through  FHA?

A. Currently  my  rough  estimate  would  be  20  to  25  per  cent

of the new  housing  market  annually.

A.  Yes.
Q. Do  you  have  a  figure  as  to its highest  amount  in the past?
A. In  the  early  1950’s  the  combination  of FHA  and  VA  was

responsible  for in excess  of 40 per cent  of the market.

Q. Would  that  be the highest  amount  of the market  financed?  _
A. I'm  not  entirely  sure,  it may  have  been higher.

per cent  of the market?

A.  Yes.  I did  some  checking  to find  out  specifically.

when  FHA  and  VA  were  the  dominant  forces  in  the  housing in-
dustry.  They  were  either  actively  promoting  housing  segregation

ing market.

of 80 per cent  of the single  family  sales  market.

which  emanated  from  one  of  those  agencies  you  have

[496]  ...
Q. If the  Federal  Government  was  involved  in  80  per  cent  of
the  housing  and  if the  policies  of the  Federal  Government  as  re-
flected  in the  agencies  you  described,  active  discrimination  or  pas-
sive  and  neutral  behavior,  what  is your  opinion  as  to  what  the
responsibility  of  the  Federal  Government  was  toward  the  dis-
crimination  we  have  in housing?

A.I  think  the  Federal  Government  bears  a  large  respons-
ibility.  First,  certainly,  for being an  active  prompter  of discrimina-
tion  practices  on  the  part  of  the  industry  with  which  it  had  to
deal,  that  there  is a great  burden  which  it had  to  sustain,  a great
responsibility  to  reverse  the  patterns  of racial  residents  which  in a
large part it was  responsible  for creating.

To  go  from  a  posture  of actively  encouraging  discrimination
to one  of neutrality  hardly  sustains  that  burden.  It can  be sustain-
ed  only  by at  least  as  monumental  an  effort  and  as  aggressive  an
effort  to  reverse  the  pattern  it helped  to  create  which  created  the
problems  in the  first  instance.

[497]  Q. Has such  effort  been  made?

A.No,  it has not.
Q. In your  opinion,  are  the  FHA  and  VA  and  the  other  four
fiscal  agencies  which  you  have  identified,  in your  opinion,  are  they
today  complying  with  the President’s  order  of  1962?

A.In  a  literal  sense  I suppose  you  could  say  they  are  com-
plying  but  there  are  a  variety  of activities  which  are  within  their
discretion  to  undertake  to  comply  much  more  vigorously  than
the  manner  in  which  they  are  complying  right  now  and  in  my
opinion  they  have  not  undertaken  the  full  range  or  adopted  the
full  range  of  practices  and  policies  which  would  be  sufficient  to

I1a40  ©

amount  to  the  kind  of aggressive  posture  necessary  to  reverse  the
pattern  which  they  are  responsible  for  creating  in  the  first
instance.

In a literal  sense  I suppose  the  agencies  could  say,  “We  are
complying  with  not  only  the  Presidential  Executive  Order  but also
the  Congressional  mandate,  Title  8, of the  Civil  Rights  Act”  but
they  have  not  undertaken  the  full  activity  or  undertaken  the
posture  of  fair  housing  which  would  make a difference  between
the  terms  of what  is happening  in  the  metropolitan  area.

THE  COURT:  Well,  taking  that  in  that  light  and  applying
what  you  know  about a city,  any  city  in  the  United  States  with
the same  problem  and  with  the same  color  makeup,  do you  have an
opinion  as  to  what  would  happen  to  what  are  depicted  there  on
the  map,  Exhibit  2,  as  now  white  areas?  Would  they,  as  Mr.
Ritchie  suggests,  lead  really  to  an  abandonment  in  large  numbers
by  white  folks  so  that  when  you  ended  up  with  the  city  you'd
have  a  city  that  was  no  more  integrated  than  it  was  when  you
started.  Do you  have  an  opinion  on  that?

A. Perhaps  I have  something  resembling  an  opinion  or  at least
something  I  can  relate  in  my  own  experience  which  may  be
relevant  here.  There  is one  school  of  thought  that  talks  about  a
tipping  point  theory,  both  in  terms  of  housing  and  in  terms  of
education,  as  some  magical  percentage,  say  35,  40  or  50  per cent
blacks,  suddenly  the  entire  school  system  or  the  entire--school  or
the  entire  area  will  turn  completely  black.  This  is  a matter  of con-
jecture  and  it’s very  difficult  to  say  yes  or  no  to  it. And I believe
you  said,  your  Honor,  that  Detroit  school  system  is 46  per  cent
black.

MR.  RITCHIE:  No,  that’s  the  city  population.  [511]  Mr.

Sloane.

A. The  same  principle  has  been  discussed  in a conjecture with
respect  to  residential  population.  Experience  in  Washington  is
perhaps instructive.  Washington  metropolitan  area  has not  changed
by more  than  one  percentage  point  in terms  of the  per  cent  white

SS...

Ila  41

and  per  cent  black  in  the  last  seventy  years.  It’s  always  been
roughly  75  per  cent  white,  25  per  cent  black.  The distribution  of
the  population  by  race  in  the  Washington  metropolitan  area  has
changed  very  dramatically  to  the  point  where  the  Northern
Virginia  suburbs  has  something  like  6 per  cent  black  and  94 per
cent  white.  The  Maryland  suburbs  and  suburb  of  Montgomery
County  has  something  like  4 per  cent  black  and  96 per cent  white,
whereas  60, 70 years  ago  there  was  an  even  distribution.

But  talking  about  integrating  the  schools  of  Washington,  of
course  Washington  Public  Schools  are  now  in excess  of 90 per cent
black,  and  it’s very  difficult  to talk  about  acheiving  school  integra-
tion  with  that  kind  of percentage.  But  if you  think  in terms  of the
metropolitan  area  as  a  whole,  the  problem  becomes  a  good deal
less difficult  because  then  we’re  dealing  with  a  school  population
as  well  as  a  residential  population  which  is  roughly  75  per  cent
white  and  25 per cent  black.

I think  in  a  situation  like  that  you  have  to  talk  in  terms  of
metropolitan  wide  desegregation,  residentially  as  well  as  in educa-
tion.  [512]  And  it  is,  in  fact,  I think  an  exercise  in  futility  to

predominantly  black  as  to make  racial  integration  an  impossibility.
It  is, however,  not  an  impossibility  if you  talk  in  terms  of  the
metropolitan  area  as  a whole.

THE COURT:  I don’t  want  to keep  pressuring,  but  let me  ask
you  this  question:  Suppose  that  you  and  I were  to  meet  ten  years
from  now,  could  you  give  me  an  opinion  now  that  you  think
would  be  valid  as  to  an  effect  in  history  ten  years  from  now?
A. Depends  on  what  was  done  rather  than  just  the  act  of

physical  desegregation.

In  our  experience  with  the  Commission  it’is  —  one  of  the
reasons  why  in  some  cities  desegregation  has  not  worked  is that
people  involved  have  felt  that  the  only  obligation  is to  bring  the
children  together  and  that  is it, whereas  there’s  a good  deal  of pre-
paration  that  has  to  be  done,  good  deal  of work  after  the  physical
desegregation  has  been  accomplished  to  make  sure  it works. So it
would  depend  -  I would  hope  that  the City of Detroit  and  people
involved  would  be sufficiently  enlightened  so  that  they  would  take
the  steps  [514]  necessary  to  make  sure  that  desegregation  works
and  that  it is quality  eduaction  for  children  of all  races.  But  that
would  depend  on  what  was  done  following  the  act  of simple  de-
segregation.

{515}  THE  COURT:  Well,  it’s  a  phrase  I’ve  used  before,
maybe  I can  use  it again  and  maybe  it expresses  your  view. I take
it if  I can  paraphrase  your  answer  that  you  can  achieve  integration
of the  schools  in this  city or  any  other  simply  “‘by  the  number’?

A. You  can  accomplish  desegregation  simply  by the  numbers.
But  just  ticking  off  the  numbers  doesn’t  necessarily  mean  that it’s
going  to  work  and  that  education  is  going  to  be  as  good  as  it
should  be  for all children  or  that  you  can  avoid  problems.  You got
to  take  the  steps  necessary  to  avoid  the  problems  and  to,  if any-
thing,  improve  the  quality  of education  that  the  children  are  get-
ting. That  is the  act  of physical  desegregation  is the  first  thing.  It is

|

Ila  43

an  essential  pre-condition  to  successful  desegregation  because
without  it you  don’t  have  desegregation  at all.  But  it’s only a first
step.

Q.  In  the  public  housing  projects,  let’s  take,  for  example,
Brewster  Douglas.  What  is  the  racial  composition  of  Brewster
Douglas  today?

A.  There  are  two  white  persons  residing  in  Brewster  Douglas

today.  The  rest  of them  are  black.

Q.  Is the  same  pattern  true  for  Sojourner  Truth?

A.  I don’t  know  of any  whites  residing  in Sojourner  Truth.
Q.  Based  on  your  knowledge  of  the  statistics  of  the  public
housing  project  has  the  racial  identity  of  the  project  —  do  you
[589]  have  an  opinion  whether  that  racial  identity  has  persisting
effects?

A.  Most  definitely  it  does.  The  project  originally  designated
for  black  occupancy  has  persisted  to  this  day  and  has  totally  black
occupancy.  The  two  white  residents  of  Brewster  Douglas  are
Catholic  nuns  who  are  doing  a  kind  of  live-in  social  work,  is what
they  have  told  me.

Q.  In  the  private  housing  market  you  mentioned  that  6,500
homes  changes  hands  from  white  families  to  black  families
approximately  every  year  in recent  years?

A.  Since  1950,  yes.

{590}  Q.  Where  are  those  6500  homes  located?  Do  you  have

an  opinion  on  where  they  are  located?

A.  Yes,  in  the  sections  of  the  city  on  the  map  there  that

range  in color  from  green  to blue  to  orange  to  red.

Q.  Of  these  6500  homes  how  many  of  them  each  year,  ap-

préximately,  were  in the  previously  all-white  neighborhood?

.  A.  Well,  the  areas  west  of Livernois  with  the  exception  of the
area  between  the  railroad  tracks  and  Livernois,  going  down  to the
Dearborn  border,  contain  few,  very  few, just  a  handful  of black
residents  except  for  the  Eight  Mile,  Wyoming  area  in  1960.  But,

Ila5l

since  1960  thousands  of  homes  have  changed  from  white  oc-
cupancy  to  black  occupancy  moving  progressively  westward  from
Livernois  to Wyoming  to  Schaefer.  There  are  some  black  families
living  as  far  west  as  Southfield  at  the  present  time  with  an
occasional  black  family  that  lives  west  of  Southfield.  Some  of
them  bought  repossessed  houses  but  there  have  been  some  private
sales  to  black  families  west  of  Southfield.  Outside  of  the  two

200  persons  west  of  the  Southfield  Freeway  but  there  is con-
siderable  real  estate  change  visible  on  the streets  east  of the South-
fied  Freeway  and  there  is  a  neighborhood  succession  in  those

.  During  the  ‘S0’s?

A.  During  the  ‘50’s  and  —  yes,  during  the  *50’s  primarily.
Q.  Could  you  describe  any  of these  other  incidents?

Ila  54

A.  About  1955,  I  believe,  we  sold  a  house  on  Riverview.
Seven  Mile  Road.  This  was  a  repossessed  house  owned  by the
Veterans  Administration.  And  when  the  black  family  went  out to _
clean  the place  up  to move  in after  closing  the sale,  they  left in the
evening,  and  house  caught  fire.  And  the  VA restored  it, and  it Was
considerably  damaged,  three  or  $4,000.00  worth  of damage.

They  fixed  this,  and  they  were  cleaning  the  next  day  to move
in and  then  seven  fires —  it was  obvious  that  it was  arson  —  were  set
in this house.

Pe
At  this  time  I had  written  a  letter  to  the  Veterans  Admin-
” “tyation in  Washington,  locally  and  everyone  for  some  kind  of
protection  and  the  destruction  of government  [596]  property.  At
this time  I was  getting  calls  during  this  period  from  people  in the
neighborhood.  And  one  man  did  identify  himself.  And  after  the
second  fire  he  called  me  and  said  that  the  FBI  knocked  on  his
front  door,  and  when  he  answered  the  front  door,  they  kicked  in
the  side  door  and  the  back  door  and  searched  his  house  from  the
basement  through,  looking  for  evidence  of this  fire.  And  he said
two  other  neighbors  —  and  he  didn’t  identify  them  —  the  same
thing happened  to them  that  night.

Q.  Did  you  ever  have  occasion  to  have  listing  cards  with  the

notations  X and  XX on  them?

A.  Yes,  I have.  I have  seen  those  of other  brokers.
Q.  And  what  are  those  cards?
A.  The  X and  XX  would  denote  the  race.  The X is white; the

XX  is black.

Q.  Where  do  you  have  most  of your  listings  over  the entire

period  of your  operation?

A.  My  office  is on  the  east  side,  buy  many  of our  sales  have

been  northwest,  kept up  with  the  movement  of the  turnover.

Q.  Are  most  of your  listings  in eithét  black  areas  or  changing

areas?

All  in black  or  changing.
Have  you  had  difficulty  getting  listings  in white  areas?

.  Yes.[597]}
.  Could  you  describe  some  of those  difficulties?
-  There is a  little  more  sophistocation  used  today  if we  |
attempt  to  get a listing.  But  primarily,  I think,  at  this  point  we aré =
handicapped  because  in  the  periphery  of every  area  that  there  is

Ila  55

integration  Movement  on  the  outside  of  this,  we  are  sent  the
results of the  Brickley  Amendment,  I think  we  call  it that,  the  Fair
Housing  Act,  that  says  you  can’t  solicit,  and  that  is  used  on
persons  that  they  don’t  want  bothering  them.  There’s  always
someone  in there.

Q.  Do  you  ever  co-broke  with  white  brokers  in  the  changing

and black  areas?

A.  I found  this to be something  that  -  if it’s not  in a changing
area,  in an  all black  area,  you  don’t  get much  cooperation.  And  it’s
easy  to  circumvent  simply  by saying  that  “We  got something  tied
up on  it; we  have  somebody  interested.”  And  if we  find  that  the
house  is there  much  longer,  we  can  go around  behind  them  and  get
somebody  to  call  on  them  and  they’ll  show  it to  them.  We've  had
many  instances  like  this  until  it’s  a waste  of time.
Q.  Could  you  explain  what  co-broking  is?
A.  Co-broking  is to  cooperate  with  another  broker  who  has a
listing,  a broker  that  has a listing.  And  if I want  to  cooperate  with
him, to show  my  customer,  we  split  the  commission.

Q.  Now,  you  said  that  you  would  have  somebody  go around
and  check.[598]  Would  that  somebody  be white  who  would  call?

A.  Yes.
Q.  And  then  the  house  would  be  shown?
A.  It  would  be  shown  and  they  wouldn’t  know  anything
about  the  sale  on  it,  and  this  is  quite  typical  of  the  cases  that
we've  tested.  Does  it cost  you  money  to  try  and  get  housing  for
black  clients  in white  areas?

A.  Absolutely.  There  are,  I  found,  people  who  honestly
would  not  care  who  they  sold  to if they  thought  they  wouldn’t get
in trouble.  And  this  is the  way  they  express  it,““Would  I get  any
trouble  if I listed  my  house  with  you,  and  if you  knew  who  you
was  going  to  sell  it to,  if you  sold  it to  a  black,  would  that  mean
trouble  for us?”

We’ve  had  a  number  of  cases  where  the  people  —  all  of  this
when  somebody  wanted  to  sell  and  you’re  trying  to give service  —
well  then,  if  you  don’t  get  it  you  lose  a  lot  of time,  and  that’s
money  in  this  business.  In  fact,  I’d  given  up  on  it out  of disgust
and  knowing  that  I can’t  keep  a  business  if I continue  to  try  to
work  in  the  predominantly  white  neighborhoods,  to  try  to  find
that one  that’s  brave  enough  to let  me  have  his property  and  try  to
sell it for him.

Il  a 56

Q.  Now,  over  the  history  of  these  years,  have  you  come  to

conclusions  about  this  process  of giving  up?  [599]

A.  I —  I have.  I at  fifty-four  —  I started  out  thinking  that |

could  help  to  correct  the  situation  by putting  all of the  effort  that
I could  and  giving  people  an  equal  opportunity  in  the  housing
market,  and  there  are  so  many  evasive  ways  that  people  can  use to
avoid,  even  though  the  law  says  that  you  can’t  do  it, until  it’s q
loss  of time  and  money.  And  you just can’t  make a living  doing it.
And everyone  that  I’ve ever  had been involved  in cost me alot of time
and  money,  cost  me  money.  For  everyone  of those  incidents,  and
I’ve got lots of them,  they cost me  a wad of money  because  of them.
Police  harassment  was  the  biggest  one,  taking  my  license  and
trapping  me.  And  every  time I get one  of these and my name  goeson
the  paper,  I start  getting  tickets  for doing  the  same  thing  I’m doing
right now, and it’s no  coincidence.

Q.  Does  it have  an  effect  on  your  clientele?

A.  There  are  people  that  would  not  list  with  me  if I’m  the
first  one  to  sell.  The  realtors  recognize  that,  because  they used to
not  sell  the  first  house  in  the  block.  In  fact,  some  of  them
wouldn’t  sell  until  the  third  house  is sold  to  black  before  they
would  make a sale.

Q.  Now,  when  you  use  the  term  “realtor”  —

A.  That’s  the  white  organization.

Q.  Okay.  Fine.  Have  you  yourself  had  an  experience  recently

in trying  to purchase  a home?  [600]

A.  Yes,  I did.

Q.  Would  you  describe  for us  that  experience?

A.  We  wanted  a  large  lot,  and  my  wife  didn’t  want  stairs
anymore.  And  so  we  decided  that  we  wanted  a  ranch  home  with
some  land  around  it, and  we  just  wasn’t  able  to  find  one  available
in Detroit  that  we  wanted.  And  so  we  went  to Southfield,  and we
looked  Southfield,  Bloomfield,  and  various  places.  It  was  rather
comical  some  of the  experiences  that  we  had.

I'd  like  to  point  out  that  I would  not  take  my  children  with
me.  My wife  and  I were  conditioned  on  what  we  were  going  to run
into  and  the  treatment  that  we  were  going  to get,  and  we  got just
about  what  we  knew  we  were  going  to get.

We  went  out  in Bloomfield  Hills.  They  advertised  in the paper
a  piece  of property  that  was  for sale.  We  had  no  idea  that  we  were

.

Il  a 57

going to buy  it but just  look  at  it.  My wife just said,  “See  what  it
is.” $85,000.00.

We  go  there  and  the  salesman  on  the  scene  comes  in and  he
speaks to us,  and  he  said,  “Well,  I don’t  know  why you’d  want  to
buy this  house  because  it floods  every  time  it rains,  a good heavy
dew  and  things  flooded  all over.”  And  he  said,  “It’s  in bad  shape,
bad location,  the  sewers,  the  water  —”  and  he gave  us any  number.
And  all he did was  follow  us around  and  we  were  laughing  because
we knew.  [601]

Now,  this  should  have  been  embarrassing  for  me  because  he
didn’t  know  if I could  afford  to  buy  it, but  the  idea  was  to try to
prevent  me  from  wanting  to buy it.

Q.  Is  this  typical  of  some  of  the  things  that  you’ve  run  up

against  throughout  your  business?

:

A.  Another  case  I’ve run  into  and  looked  at a house  in South-
field,  and  I asked  the  gentleman  who  had  an  open  house  there  if
we  could  come  in  and  look  at  it.  He  said,  “Help  yourself.  Go
ahead.”

We  looked  around,  and  I asked  him,  I said,  “What’s  the price

on  this house?”

He said,  “65,000.00.”
Well,  I knew  that  was  high,  knowing a little  about  housing.  So

I said, “Well,  we  think  we  like  it and  might  be interested  in it.”

He  said,  “Well,  people  in  Florida  now  and  I’ve  had  two  other
people  who  might  already  decide  to  take  it and  then  they  might
take it off the  market.”

Two  blocks  away  I  went,  another  company.  The  same
company  had  another  salesman,  a  young  fellow.  I went  in  and
looked  at  his.  And  I thought  he  was  the  most  honest  of all that  I
had  gone  around.  He  showed  me  a  house,  and  he  also  had  his
listing  book  there  laying  on  the  table,  and  I know  what  they  are.  I
know  what  to  look  for.  [602]

Q.  Would  you  describe  what a listing  book  is?
A.  Listing  book  is  where  they  have  the  listing  of  these
~
properties  with  the  photo  and  with  the  information  and  the price
on  them.  And  he showed  me  through  the  house,  gave  me  the price
on  it. It wasn’t  what  we  wanted,  and  so  I asked  him  about  the  one
that we  had just looked  at.

He  turned  the  page  over  there  and  it  was  $45,000.00  not

$65,000.00.

Il a 58

And  these  are  the  kind  of things that  is a  waste  of time and
effort  for  me  to try to go in a white  neighborhood  because  it’s too
costly.

Q.  What  year  was  this?

A.  1968.
Q.  Were  you  able  eventually  to get a house  in Southfield?
A.  Yes.  I went  in to look  at a house.  A company  showed  it to
me.  We  met  by  appointment  and  showed  it  to  me.  And  I made
them  an  offer  on  the  house.  Everybody  thinks  he’s  paying  too
much,  so  I made  an  offer.  Being in the  business,  I thought  I could
see  if I could  get  it cheaper.  We  were  going  to  buy  it even  at the
price,  but  we  made  an  offer.  We submitted  an  offer.

Three  days  later  I get  a  telephone  call  from  the  owner  who
.recognized  me,  I had  sold  some  fifteen  years  ago  some  commercial
property  for  hifn  and  didn’t  remember  him,  [603]  didn’t
remember  the  name.  And  he asked  me  if I had  shown any  interest,
submitted  an  offer.

I said,  “I  got  an  offer  on  your  place.  I given  it to him.  I knew

it was  an  offer.”  .

And  so  he said,  “Well,  they  haven’t  submitted  the  offer  to me.

The  company  didn’t  submit  the  offer.”

I said,  “Well,  they got an  offer  and  $1,000.00  deposit.”

And  he  said,  “Well,  if I don’t  get the  offer,”  he said,  “you can

deal  directly  with  me.”

Well,  this  would  have  been  the  most  unethical  thing  that one
could  do,  even  though  I asked  them  to  show  the  listing  with -
share  the  commission  with  me,  which  they  refused  to do.  But even
at that rate  I wouldn’t  go directly  with  the man.

I called  them  and  told  them  the  conversation,  and | advised
them,  I said,  “Now,  this  man  has  called  me  and  asked  me  if the
offer  had  been  submitted  and  said  that  he could  take  my offer and
told  me  if you  didn’t  present  it  that  I could  bring  him  an  offer
direct.  Naturally,  I’m  not  going  to  do  anything  like  this.”  And so
my offer  was  accepted.

Q.  Had  that  company  previously  made  any  representations  to

you  about  whether  the  offer  had  been  made?

A.  They  told  me  the  offer  had  been  turned  down.  [604]  ~

types that  you’ve  run  up  against  when  you  try  to  get  houses  for

A.  This  is the  case.  It is not  unusual  that  when  in an  all white  ~

area,  when  white  person  would  seek  me  out  —  my  picture  is in the

worth,  so  they  call  me.  And  now I can  get  that  kind  of a listing

waste  of time  and effort.  —

Q.  At  the  present  time,  given  the  history  of your  dealings  in

the market,  are  you  still  having  these  types  of difficulties?

A.  I stay  away  from  them.  I’ve been  —  I’ve  been  licked,  and I
just  don’t  like  wasting  my  time  and  my  effort.  And  I don’t  like
taking  people  like  a  doctor  I took  out  in Livonia,  I believe  it was,
Livonia  or  Taylor,  I think  it was  Livonia,  I don’t  remember  now
when  one  town  stops,  able  to  buy  and  pay  cash  for  a  piece  of
property  and  walk  to  the  door  and  the  man  is there  and  when  you
start  to  go  in  he  comes  out,  closes  the  door  and  said, “‘We’re
closed.”

And  I said,  “Well,  I’ve  got some  customers  here  and  I’m  a real
estate  broker  and  they  want  to  see  the  [605]  model  because
they’re  interested  in buying:”

He  said,  “I  told  you  we’re  closed,”  and  he  walks  on  around.
And  this  kind  of  thing  was  not  bad  for  me  because  I’m
immuned  to  it,  but  it  was  so  embarrassing  to  him,  until  I asked
him  to  let  me  initiate  a  case  for  him,  and  he  says,  “I  wouldn’t
want  to be connected  with  it, you  know,  I’ll find  some  place  else.”
He  ultimately,  subsequently  purchased  in  Palmer  Woods.  But

he was  —  he was  embarrassed.

organization.  They  interchange  these  properties,  give this informa
tion  on  property  that  we  list,  we  turn  over  to our  fellow  brokers.

do, you get appointments  by phone,  that  is  people who express  an

[695]  Q.  What  was  the  recommendation  of  the  Urban

location?

A.  It  was the recommendation  of  the Urban  League to the

them to find housing  that was outside  of the ghetto areas and to
generally  seek a  wide or spacial distribution  of the families in the
affected area throughout  the geographic  area of the City of De
troit. [696]
'

lowed by the city?

A.  May I continue?

|

Q. Certainly.

families were placed for the most part by the City of  Detroit,  | am
now of families  that  were  seeking  housing  in the private
speaking
in pub-
as distinguished  from  families  that  were interested
market
lic housing  accommodations.  Those  families  were  placed  for the

site.

A.  At  that  time  we  had  not  achieved  an  open  occupancy
policy which event was expressed by the Detroit Housing  Commis-
sion.

Q.  Can you explain that? [697]
A. Well, very simply,  we SOught and worked  for many years
back in the early ‘50’s  to  encourage  the city administration,  the
mayor’s  office,  the  common  council  and  the  Detroit  Housing
Commission,  to  adopt  a policy  which  without  qualification  would
give expression  to  an open occupancy  policy  in both public  and
in the City of Detroit,  but  at  that time  we were
private housing
unsuccessful
in our effort.

Q.  What was the policy  of the city, specifically?
A.  The policy  was and I don’t  remember  the policy  verbatim
now, I don’t have it before  me  and I don’t  remember  it verbatim,
but the policy was not clear as to what the city’s position  would
be. I can tell you what the practice  is which might give you some,
and give the Court some indication  as to what the policy was.

Q.  When  you  say the City  you are talking  about  the

responsible  agency  of the city,  the Housing  Commission?
A.  Yes, I am speaking of the Housing Commission.

Il a 66

Q.  All right,  sir.
A.  Because  the  Housing  Commission  is the  one  that adopted
the policy.  The practice  was  to place in the public  housing  projects
black  families  in projects  that  were  passively  designated  for black
occupancy.  The  practice  in  the  private  area  was  of a hands off
policy  and do not disturb  the racial  characteristics  of the neighbor.
hood  outside  of  those  that  were  [698]  composed  of high black
intensification.

The  executive  director  of  the  Housing  Commission  at that
time  was  a  prominent  member  of the  Detroit  Real  Estate  Board.
Q.  What  eventually  happened  in  terms  of  relocation  of the

people  from  the Gratiot  redevelopment  area?

A.  Well,  the  most  —
Q.  (Continuing)  I take  it federal  funding  was  restored  at some

later time?

:

A.  Federal  funding  was  restored  and  our  findings  were  that
the  families  that  were  seeking  housing  in  the  private  market  with
marginal  incomes  sometimes  pooled  their  resources  and  purchased
housing  in areas  that  were  adjacent  to the Gratiot  redevelopment.
There  were  some  families  that  were  able  to  move  out  into
other  areas  that  had  been  occupied  fairly heavily  by black people.
But  for  the  most  part  those  families  who  were  renting  sought
housing  accommodations  in areas  that  were  contiguous  to  the af-
“fected  development  area.

Q.  What  was  the racial  characteristic  of the  contiguous  affect-

ed area?

_  A.  Predominantly  black.

eee

yestor  did not care  whether  the money  was  black  or  white,  but the

Q.  Did  you  as  part  of this  study do any  investigation  of Fed-

eral Housing  Administration  practices  at this time?

A.  In the various  discussions  that  we  had  in our  seminars,  and
I think  that  it has been  documented,  the  history  tells  us  that  the
Federal  Housing  Administration  worked  more  closely  with.  the
pretigious  and  highly-acceptable  Detroit  Real  Estate  Board,  and
the documentation  that  I alluded  to was  the fact  that  the  practices
of FHA and the policies of FHA  closely  followed  the practices  and
the policies expressed  by the  Real  Estate  Board.

Q.  Directing  your  attention  to  another  area,  subsequent  to
the Gratiot  Project,  has  there  been  any  substantial  public housing
[706]  or  urban  renewal  development  with  public  housing  in the
Detroit  area?

:

A.  In the area  of public  housing,  the answer  would  be no.
Q.  Are  you  aware  of any  attempts  or  any  trend,  from  your
experience  on  the  Housing  Commission,  away  from  large projects
to other  types of projects?

A.

Yes,  I am aware  of  that fact,  because  we discussed it
numerous  times  during  my  tenure  on  the  Commission.  And  the
philosophy  is,  and  we  have  learned  from  the  past,  that  it is not
best  for  the  people  or  the  city  to  have  these  large  high-rise  con-
structions  in  public  housing.  Too  many  instances  they’re  not  the
best  thing  for  the  children  who  reside  there.  And  then,  again,  we-
have seen,  and the records  of the Housing Commission will attest to
the  fact  that  in too  many  instances  they become crime traps with
the  high-rise  construction  in the  elevator  — elevators  that  have to
go up  these  large  heights..  And  we  found that it  was sociologically
speaking  unsound.  So  that  what  we  sought  to  do  was  develop  a
program  of public  housing  which  would  cause  the  placement  of
families  who  qualified  for  public  housing  into  single-family  units
dispersed  or  scattered  throughout  the  neighborhood.  When I say
neighborhood,  I am speaking  now in the larger sense of the Detroit
Community  or  the Detroit  neighborhood.

Q.  Do  you  know  whether  or  not  the  Housing  Commission
recommended  some  scattered  site  housing  in  areas,  City  of De-
troit, that  were  not  primarily  occupied  by black people?

Il a 68

A.  A recommendation  was  made  to  the  Common  Council to
the  effect  that  we  put  into  operation  a program  of scattered  sites
When  I say  “we”  I mean  the  Public  Housing  Commission.  The
Housing  Commission  in the  area  of public  housing  develops  scat.
tered  site  placement  for  families  who  are  qualified  for  public
housing.

Q. Were  these  sites in primarily  white  areas  approved?

A.  Some  of the  sites  were  in white  areas  and  some  of the sites
selected  were  in areas  that  were  occupied  by black  families.  To the
best  of my  knowledge,  the  areas that were  recommended for pub-
lic  housing  sites  in  areas  that  were  occupied  by  white  families
principally  were  not approved.

tionable.

ROBERT L. TUCKER
was thereupon called  to the stand  for and on behalf of the
Plaintiffs,  having been  first duly sworn,  was  examined  and este
as follows:

eee

Ila  73

[766]  ...
A. I also  feel  a bit  relieved.  The  causes  of racial  segregation,
in my  judgment  based  on  my  experience  and  my  studies,  are
multi-faceted  or  numerous,  but  I would  respectfully  suggest  that
the  essential  cause  is  the  only  life  that  we  as  black  and  white
ple  in  the  country  have  known.  We  have  never  known  any-

thing, at least  to that extent  of the problem,  but  segregation.

The  cause  then,  I think,  is related  to the  origin  of the  problem
and the  origin,  I think,  is in the  original  system  of chattel  slavery
from which  we  have  never  overcome.

The  matter  of  the  state  of black  people  in  the  entire  city
derived  principally  from  that  experience  and  has  been  different.
The  development  economically,  [767]  educationally,  socially  and
culturally  has  been  different and I would  have  to  add  the  fact  of
his blackness  itself,  rendering  black  people  highly visible,  is  a very
crucial cause.  It makes  it possible.

Given  that  as  a basis  then, we  must  consider  other  forces  that
contribute  to  both  the  existence  and  continuing  of the  problem
and  I think  the  first  force  that  has to be recognized  is the  entry  of  .
the  United  States  Government  itself  upon  at  least  the  housing
scene  in  the  1930’s  when  it spoke  to  the  problem  which  already
existed  at the. time.

And  the efforts  of the  government  to meet  housing conditions
upon  observation  have  contributed  to  both  the  perpetuation  and
extension  of  segregation  and  these  are  reflected  in  policies  that
were  formulated  as  a  part  of the  government’s  effort  to  meet  the
housing and to provide  housing  both  in the private  or conventional
sector  as  well  as  what  has  come  to  be  regarded  as  the  public
housing  sector,  more  accurately  described,  in  my  judgment,  as
efforts to provide housing  for poor  people.  -

I think  that  these  policies  combined  with  the  nature  of the
circumstances  itself,  I think, converage  to be  the  major  cause  of
segregation  in housing today.

Tucker,  what  communities in the metropolitan  area  was the sub
ject of your inquiries?

that ar
A.  The  subject  of the  inquiries  were all communities
a part of the standard  metropolitan  statistical  area.  The communi
ties that received  specific  and particular  attention,  given judgments
as  to  the  best  deployment  of resources  and  manpower  were  thor
communities  where  there  was  a  high  degree  of employment and
high  degree  of minority  employment  and  an  observable  degree of
low minority  residents.

A.  Among

them  would  have  been  the cities  of Warren, Mich-
igan,  Dearborn,  Michigan,  Grosse  Pointe,  Highland  Park, Ferndale,

troit  area  that  had some  degree  of industrialization
involved.  |
should  have stricken,  | suppose,  Grosse  Pointe.  I’m  sorry.

Q.  What  were  you attempting to find  out?

A.  Whether  there  was a relationship  between  the incidence of
high  minority  employment  and  the  low  incidence  of minority  res
idency  and  whether or not that was based upon  to  any degree
discrimination  on  the basis of either  race,  color, religion  or  nation-
al origin.

[777]  Q.  How  did  you  go  about  trying  to  find  it out,  Mr.

Tucker?

A.  The technique  was to utilize  the —  more accurately  was to
employ  the approach  established  by Section  809 of the Title Eight
of  the  Civil  Rights  Act  of  1968.  The  vehicle  estabished  by that
provision  is one that requires  the secretary  to  move  to  the com
munities,  to  engage
in on going dis
the state  and  local  officials
cussions  about the conditions  that are observed  for the purpose  of
determining in the  language  of the statute  the extent  to which, if
any, discrimination  exists  and the procedures  and methods  —  part
phrasing  now —  by  which  the problem  can be eliminated.
‘In discharging  that responsibility,

in the fall of 1969, we selec
ted  from  each  program  element  of the  Department  of Housing and

REY
Pi

Ila  77

Urban  Development,  meaning  from  the  Urban Renewal  Depart-
ment, from the Model Cities Department, from FHA, from Metro
Planning  and Development,  all technical people who have
anything to do with providing goods and services to those com-
munities. And on the team was an Equal Opportunity person who
went into the field, surveyed the conditions, talked to the offic:
ial talked to the residents and returned to the regional office
with their findings  and reports.
On the basis of this field examination,  to used an overworked
government  expression,  “task  force  [778]  undertakings,”  we  de-
cided to then concentrate our efforts on those communities  that
had in fact applied for federal funds.

Now,  I think to make  a distinction,  under Titlé  Eight, Section
309, the obligation of  the secretary is to do this irrespective  of
funds  or not.
whether  the communities  have asked  for  federal
That must be ongoing.

!

It  was  determined  then  to  combine  this  responsibility  with
the program responsibilities  in the hope of having hopefully great-
er  appeal,  if  not  greater  effect,  but  certainly  being  able  to
command  the  attention  of decision-makers  in  the  communities.
And we arrived at  our —  our approach  and our findings in this
manner.

see is accountable  —  strike that —  can be attributed  to a desire by
minority persons to “live together” only to  a  minimum degree and
certainly  to  no greater  degree than  that quality  or characteristic
would determine  the housing  choice of  other groups.

partment  of Housing  and  Urban  Development.  We examined  most

expert testimony and adducing that testimony  in open court from

divorce subjective responses  to that question.

concentration  despite  efforts  [788]  to do  something  about  that,
we have an experience
on which to draw.

row

how would  you  define  a segregated  school?

Il a 83

A.  A segregated  school  might  be defined  as  one  in which  an

characteristics  of that  school  as  a  function  of color,  as a function
of race.
“Q.  In  terms  of  the  educational  employment  opportunities
available  what  effect  is  racially  isolated,  or  racially  separate
schools, what effect  does this have on the black child?
~  4.  In terms of employment?
_Q..  In terms of his perception  of himself vis-a-vis whites.
--  A.  Kenneth  Clark  pointed  out  in a  publication  in his book
Dark  Ghetto  that  black  youngsters  readily  begin  to perceive  very
early,  as a  matter of fact as early  as  their  elementary  school  days
that when a  school  itself  is racially  isolated  this is reinforced  by
the neighborhood  which  is racially  isolated.  The  youngster  begins
to perceive  very  early  in life that  opportunities  in the greater  soci-
ety are very  restricted  and  will also reflect  the restricted  opportun-
ities that are  available  to him,  not  only  in that  neighborhood  but
in the school as well.

Unfortunately  in our  society  which  yet  tends  to  operate  dual
school  systems  both  in the  North  and  South,  we  find at the same
time separate neighborhoods  which  [866]  are  also  related  to  a
great extent  to dual  school  systems  in America  that youngsters  can
find available  models  of black  youth  who  have  gone  through  essen-
tially segregated  systems.and  have  had  employment  opportunities
after  completing  the  segregated  school  system.  There  is a relation-
ship though between  the  child  who  grows  up in a system,
a segre-
gated  school  system,  who  perceives  that  opportunities  are  re-
stricted  in that  particular  system.

There  are  implications  for  that  and  how  he perceives  his later

role in the greater society.

'

Q.  Does  this affect  his educational  motivation?

A.  Very much.

.  Q.  Would  it affect, from  your  experience  and studies,  Doctor,

does it affect his choices,  educational  choices?

A.  Very  much.  If  I might  speak  from  personal  experience

¥ery much  a part  of the conversation of the youth that  I attended
the  public  schools  with  and  I attended  an  all  black  elementary
school  and  J attended  an  all  black junior high school  and at the
time  I attended  Northern  High  School  from  ‘49  to  ‘52,  in ‘49,
Northern  was, I would guess about 90 to  93 per cent  black. By the
time  I graduated  it was  98 per  cent  black,  The  general  converss-
tion  centered  around  the point  of [867]  view  that,  “Why should]
struggle  to graduate  from  this  high  school?  Why should  I struggle
to complete  my education  when  in fact there might  be and probe
bly will be little  relationship  between  the education  that  is ve
afforded me here and opportunities  after graduation?”

So there isa very  direct  relationship  in terms  of the verse
of youngsters  who are involved  in  a segregated
school  system.  It
was  also my  experience  as  an  undergraduate  student  at  San Fran-
cisco  State  College  where  I was  very active in student government
as  an  undergraduate,  it was  my  experience  that  black  youths who
attended  mixed  schools,  multi-racial  schools,  schools  in  which
there  was  a population  mix  between  black  and  whites,  these parti-
cular youngsters  tended  to  have  a greater  range  of career oppor
tunities  in  mind  than  black  youngsters  who  attended ses.
segregated  high schools.

Black  youngsters  who  attended,  in terms  of my  own  personal
experiences,  who  attended  multi-racial  elementary  and  high
schools  seemed  to  have  had at that time higher  levels  of motive
tion  as  it relates  to' academic  achievement.  They  were  also willing
to  take  much ‘greater  risks  in terms
which  blacks  had historically  been  excluded  from.

of seeking  out those careers in

Q.  What  do you  understand  by the  term  “community  percep

tion of a school”’ in  this particular  instance?

dents  hold  toward  that  school  in terms  of whether  the  school it ei

saf, what it can  do  for  their  youngsters,  what  kind  of opportun-

"When  parents perceive  that  a particular  school  in their district
or community  is not  necessarily  a very  sound  one,  is not  one  that
facilitates  academic  achievement,  then  that  particular  perception
or attitude  towards  the school  on  the part of the  general  commun-
ity can  readily  relate  to  the  amount  of support  that  the  parents
will make available  to that  school.

I should  add to that  when  parents  are  of the opinion  that  their
youngsters  are  in a  sound  supportive  educational  system  they  are
more  likely  to  reinforce  the  attitude  at  home  that  education,  in
this instance,  is particularly  good for the child.

Q.  (By Mr.  Lucas)  Dr. Green,  in terms  of the community  per-
ception which  you  discussed,  is the community  perception  related
in any way  to the racial  proportion  in schools?

II a 86

A.  Yes.  In  general,  North  and  South,  and I say  North ang
South  because  I have  worked  both  in  southern  communities x
well  as  in northern  urban  communities,  there is a very  strong fee.
ing  on  the  part  of  black  parents  that  whenever  a  school  is per.
ceived  as  a  black  school  that  the resources  that  are  made  available
to. that  school,  that  teaching  personnel  that are  often  placed  [870]
in  that  school  are  substantially  different  and  inferior  to what is
found  in essentially  all  white  schools.  This is a general  perception
on  the part of the  black  community.

The  black  community  even  expects  that  the  treatment  that
will  be afforded  youngsters  in a school  that  is identified  as a black
school  will  be  substantially different  from  the  treatment  that is
afforded to whites  in white  schools.

Q. The  white  parents, is their  perception  a factor  there?

A.  Very  much  so.

Q.  Would  you  explain  that,  please?

A.  I think  the  perception  of the  white  community  is a func
tion  of  how  practices  have  been  structured  in the  past,  ed-
ucationally.  [871]  You  can  readily  perceive,  and  perhaps  justifi-
ably  so,  that  schools  that  are  essentially  white  will  have  available
to  it greater  resources,  more  adequate,  more  abundant  resources
than  schools  that  are  identifiable  as  black  schools.

[877]  Q.  What  do achievement  tests  measure?
A.  Achievement  tests  in general  tend  to  measure  educational

inputs.

Q.  What  do you  mean  by educational  inputs?
A.  We  assume  that  a  youngster  has  had  the  opportunity  to
have  been  exposed  to a  body  of data  over  a  given  period  of time.
Then  a certain  test  is administered  to  a child  to assess  whether  or
not  the  youngster  has  in  fact  successfully  been  able  to  cope  with
or comprehend  or understand  the data  made  available  to him.

Educational  achievement  tests  tend  to  be  a  much  more  direct

measure  of educational  input  than aptitude  tests.

THE COURT:  May I backtrack  here a minute,  with your  leave,

Mr. Lucas?

MR.  LUCAS:  Certainly,  sir.

THE  COURT:  Am  I to  understand  that  you  are  satisfied,
based  on  your  own: experience  and  studies  and  that  of other  ex-
perts  in the  field, that, generally  speaking,  there  is no achievement
difference;  scholastically.  speaking,  between  white  and  black  chil-
dren at the entry level?

A:  That  in general  there  is  a body  of data  that  supports  the
point  of view  that  the  discrepancy  between  black  and white  stu-
dents at the  entry level tends  to  be  minimal,  very  small.  [878]
are  some  differences  as  a  function  of geographic  region.  I

can very  safely  predict  that the entry level differences  between
black and white youngsters  in the City of Detroit are much small
than  the entry  levels between  the black and white youngsters  ip
Wilcox  County,  Alabama.

I could also safely predict  that the entry level between black
and  white  youngsters  in the Flint,  Michigan  and Pontiac,  Michigan
and  Benton  Harbor,  Michigan  areas are probably  minimal at the
point  of entry  into  the public school  system.

This  is not  to say  that  they are exactly  similar.  This is not the
case  at all, but in general,  generally  speaking,  the differences at the
point  of entry  into  the  public  school  system  tend  to  be  minimal
and  in many  instances  they  tend  to not  be statistically  significant,
if I might  be that  precise.

THE  COURT:  Is this  supported  by  studies  made  of pre-but-

almost-school-age  children?

A.  Again —

THE  COURT:  I am  thinking  in terms of the four or four anda
half  or  the  five-year-old  who  has  not  yet  entered  the  schod
system.

A.  In general,  the  differences  are  much,  much  smaller  and in

some  cases  minimal  than  one  would  find at year  second,  the [879]
second  grade  level.  Again  there  are  other  complicating  factors. If
we  take  black  youngsters  three and a half years of age who have
been  afforded  a  very  impoverished  upbringing  for  the  first  three
and  a half years of life and compare  their educational  achievement
with  white  affluent  three  and  a  half  year  olds,  the  differences, of
course,  would  be much larger.  But if  we take comparable
groups in

Q.  Asa  psychologist,  Doctor,  is there a difference  in the per-

Q.  What  is that perception?
A.  Very  early,  as  I indicated  in the earlier  testimony,  a black
youngster perceives that his stutus in  an all-black school is an in-
ferior  one,  and  this  is systematically  reinforced  by the  greater  so-
ciety,  not  only  by the  greater  society,  but  it is reinforced  by his
experiences  with  youngsters  who  attend  schools  that  are  predom-
inantly  white  and  they  come  in  contact  with  each  other  during
extracurricular  activities  such  as  football  games,  basketball  games,
after-school  activities,  but  the perception  is not  only reinforced  by
other youngsters  but  also  reinforced  by teachers  as well.

{921}...

One  need  only  experience  numerous  visits  within  predomin-
antly black  schools  in urban  communities  and you will find that

their  status  in  a predominantly  black  school  as  much  lower  than
that of the status of a teacher
in a mixed school.

other  in a public  educational  setting  cannot  help leave  that  public
school  setting not knowing each  other.

maybe  one,  two  or three.  Therefore,  when  I became  an  undergrad-
uate  student  for a brief period  of time  at Wayne  State  and a longer

iences  and exchanges  and  associations  between  groups  of  white

Q.  Is it correct  to say  then, sir, that  in your  professional Opin-
ion  the  ultimate  end  is to  achieve  a  thoroughly  integrated  and

A.  I would  say  my  goal  is to  maximize  equal  opportunity  in
American  life, and  a very  significant  part of that  thrust  is the elim-
ination  of dual  educational  learning  experiences  for children  with-
in our  public  educational  system  here in the United  States.  So, my
overall  goal is to maximize  equal  opportunity  which, at the same
time,  will  reduce  discrimination,  reduce  poverty  and  integral con-
flict.
I'see  the  elimination  of those  school  systems  as being  a very

it.

school forces, and  I see  them  as being interrelated.  I can  never get

tended,  most  likely,  a uniracial  school,  including  yourself,  sir. I’m

periences  with  blacks  on  an  ongoing  basis through  their school
-  There  may  be  some  exceptions  here.  However,  this  can’t

treat blacks —

Q.  You  say  that,  sir,  and  you  say  that  asa  black  man.  This  is
your conception  of the prevailing  white  attitude,  whether  it is con-
sciously  recognized  in the  white  individual  or  not.  You see this as
the prevailing white  attitude.

.

A.  I would  like  to  finish  my  statement  by saying  that  this  is
not  also  acted  out  in  terms  of unfair  behavior.  There  are  whites
who  have  this  attitude  maybe  even  unconsciously  felt  who  strive
to overcome  this  by being  very  fair  in their relationships
with the
blacks.  But,  I strongly  feel  —  and  there  is data  to  support  this.
Nevitt  and  Sanford  have  done  research  in which  they were  able  to
systematically  document  in large  numbers  of undergraduate  white
students  this  kind  of tacit  attitude  about  people  of color and spe-
cifically  black  Americans;  that  somewhere  along  the  line  they are
inferior.  This  is true  at  the  university  level, at the PhD.  level,  even
regarding  blacks  who have  attained  good  training  at schools.  This
is true  even  at  schools  such  as  Harvard,  Yale,  Fordham  and so
forth.  I would  like to add  that early  experiences  in the school  set-
ting to a  great  extent  could  offset  this  prevailing  American  atti-
de.  I see  it as  a white  American  attitude.  I think,  though,  that

the  school  itself  could  play  a very  meaningful  and  very  [960-4]

We  did not  educate  the  white  adults  who  went  to the Detroit

ber when Central was  predominantly  white.  I can remember when

lation.  When  my  older  brother  attended  Northern  High School,  he

cent white.  All the whites have fled. Northwest  Detroit  has fled to

cantly  related  to  what  we  have  not  done  in our  public  schools.  If

community  was  built  because  the  Detroit  Public  School  System
did not  perform  its  functions,  It  was  built  because  we  had  all
white  elementary  schools,  all  white  junior  high  schools  and  all
white  high schools.

hood  they  moved  in when  they moved  into  the neighborhood  was

School.”

up  in the northern  area. The remainder  of the feeder pattern
indicates  that  the children  attending  those  schools  in the periphery
of  the  Jefferson  attendance  area,  that  being  the  green  and  blue
area,  attend  the Jefferson  Junior  High School.
