[1339]  GORDON  FOSTER  was  thereupon  called  as  a
witness  on  behalf  of the Plaintiffs  and, being first duly sworn,  was
examined  and testified  as follows:

THE COURT:  And  your full name,  sir?

THE WITNESS:  Gordon  Foster.
THE COURT:  You may  be seated.
THE WITNESS:  Thank  you.
[1340]  DIRECT  EXAMINATION  BY MR.  LUCAS:
Q.  Dr. Foster,  where  do you live?
A.  430 Miller  Road, Coral Gables,  Florida.
Q.  Where  are  you employed, sir?
A.  The University  of Miami,  which  is also in Coral Gables.
Q.  What  position  do you  hold  with  the University?
A.  [hold  the position of associate  professor  in education.
Q.  Do you  hold  any  other positions  in connection  with the,

University  or by contract?

A.  Technically  no,  except  that  my  time  is assigned  to other

than teaching  matters, if that is what you mean.

Q  Allright,  sir. Could  you give us that assignment?

is. Se
ae

A.  Currently,  I'm director  of  the  Florida  School  Desegre-
gation  Consulting  Center,  which  is a federally-funded  center  under
Title 4 of the Civil  Rights  Act.

Q.  Is that  the Civil  Rights  Act  of  1964?

A.  That’s  correct.
Q. What  are  the  responsibilities  assigned  to the Center  in that

|
Act?

A.  The  Center  essentially  is involved  in  two  major  activities,
according  to  the  1964  Act.  One  comes  under  Section  403, which
has to do  with  providing  technical  assistance  to school districts in
the State of Florida  who  so  request.

[1341]  Q.  What  do you  mean  by technical  assistance?

A.  Technical  assistance  on  all  matters  relating  to  problems
generated  by school  desegregation.  We  prefer  to  call  them  oppor-
tunities,  rather  than  problems,  but  the  law  reads  problems.  The
second  part  of the  program  under  which  we  are  funded  in Section
404,  which  has  to  do  with  establishing  institutes,  workshops,
things of this  nature,  in service  activities  largely  with  professional
school  personnel,  again  upon  request,  and  concerning  problems
related  to the school  desegregation  process.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  do you  have  afty other  responsibilities,  particu-
larly with  respect  to  graduate  education  of school  administrators?
A-Y’m  chairman  of what  amounts  to our  Department  of Edu-
cational  Administration,  which  has  to  do  with  preparing  school
administrators  at  the  Masters  and  six-year  specialist  degree  and
doctorate  levels.  Also  for  the  past  two  years  —  I’m  not  currently,
but  I have  been  Director  of  the  federally-funded  program  under
the Higher  Education  Act  to  prepare  administrators  for  leadership
positions  in desegregated  schools,  primarily  the  South,  Southeast,
but also to some  extent  clear  around  the country.

Q. What  type of preparation  does  this involve,  Dr. Foster?
A.  It  involves  a  rather  intensive  experience-oriented
preparation  of  people  that  are  selected  from  the  entire  country,
[1342]  biracially  obviously,  with  a heavy  emphasis  on  preparation
of black administrators.

Qa Dr. Foster,  would  you  give  your  education  background?

Ila  162

A.  I have  a  Bachelor  of Arts  degree  from  Oberlin  College  in
Oberlin,  Ohio,  a  Master  of  Education  degree  from  Miami  Uni-
versity  in  Oxford,  Ohio,  with  emphasis  on  administration,  a
Doctor  of  Philosophy  degree  from  Ohio  State  University  in
Columbus,  again with  emphasis  on  educational  administration.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  referring  to  your  previous  professional
experience,  can  you  tell  us  what  other  positions  you  have  held
aside  from  the ones  you  have  already  mentioned?

A.  I have  been  employed  as  an  elementary  and  secondary
teacher,  as  elementary  and  secondary  administrator.  I have  been
employed  for a brief period  on  assignment  to the Cincinnati  Public
Schools  as  a  research  —  I don’t  remember  the  exact  title,  but  |
guess  research  assistant  or  something  in the  central  office.  I have
been  director  of four programs  under  the Elementary  and  Second-
ary  Educational  Act  in Southwestern  Ohio  having  to  do  with  co-
operative  research  between  and  among  school  districts  and  uni-
versities  in  Southwestern  Ohio.  I have  been  employed  by Miami
University  in Oxford,  Ohio,  by the  Ohio  State  University,  by the
University  of  Miami  in  Florida  to  teach  primarily  in  the  area  of
school  administration.

Q.  Would  you  give  us  your  graduate  level  teaching  experi-
ence,  [1343]  Dr.  Foster,  with  particular  emphasis  on  the  types
of courses?

A.  I  have  taught  in  the  three  schools  I  mentioned  such
courses  as  the  general  principles  or  basic  principles  of educational
administration,  courses  on  how  to  perform  successfully  as  an
elementary  principal  or  secondary  principal,  school  buildings,
school  maintenance,  this sort  of area.

[1344]  Q.  You  say  school  buildings,  are  you  referring  to

construction,  Doctor?

A.  This  would  come  under a course  in school  buildings,  yes,

general  planning,  site selection,  this sort  of thing.

I  have  taught  courses  in  school  community  relations  and
another  course we call a  task area course.  I taught courses in
school  building  surveys  which is more of a practical type approach
to problems  of school  construction  in which  students  traditionally
of site selection,
go out and work  with the schools  and problems
district  reorganization,  this sort  of thing.

Il a 163

I have  also  taught  some  courses  at  Miami  in  Flordia  in  the

field of junior  college  education.

Q.  Doctor  Foster,  could  you  give  us  some  of your  publica-

tions and  research  papers,  please?

A.  Well,  when  I  was  a  doctoral  student  at  Ohio  State
University  I worked  as  a junior  author  under  John  Ramseyer  on
several  publications  for  the  American  Association  of  School
Administrators.  I can’t  remember  the  exact  title  of them.  One was
a  brochure  for  the  Committee  on  Advancement _of School
Administration  call,  “The  Case  for  the  Residency”: at ‘and  another
one  was  a  survey  which  the  AASA  did  on  preparation  programs
about  1965  of superintendents  in all universities  in the  country.

[1345]  I  had  a  short  article  in  the  Saturday  Review  of
Literature  around  1962  or  ‘3,  I believe.  I published  chapters  in
school  law  books,  particularly  in  a  book  called  The  Ohio  School
Law  edited  by  Robert  Simpson,  and  published  by W.H.  Anderson
Company  in Cincinnati.

Q.  When  was  that,  sir?

A.  That  was,  I  believe,  in  1967  and  it  has  been  reprinted
within  the  last  year  with  a  supplement.  I published  actually  the
results  of  speeches  at  the  National  Organization  for  Legal
Problems  in  Education  in  conventions  in  their  two  yearbooks,
speeches  I made  at  the  conventions,  and  a whole  series  of what  we
call  pot-boiler  materials,  basically  school  surveys,  desegregation
studies,  this  sort  of thing  which  really  aren’t  counted  in academic
circles as hard  publications.

Q.  You  mentioned  your  position  as  a  consultant  with  regard
to the  ten  Ohio  school  district  studies.  Have  you  held  or  had  any
other consultant  activity?

A.  I have  worked  individually  in  various  consulting  jobs  in
several  states  to  do  with  different  aspects  of  education,  I also
spent  two  or  three  years  working  with  an  organization  in
Pennsylvania  known  as  Educational  —  I can’t  remember  the exact
name  -  Associated  Educational  Consultants  —  out  of Pittsburgh,
Pennsylvania and while  I  was working  with them  I think I worked
on  something  like  twenty  or  twenty-five  what  [1346]  is called
master plans in the State  of Pennsylvania  for different  districts.

Ila  164

Q.  What  occasioned  that  particular  series  of studies,  Doctor

Foster?

A.  A  school  district  reorganization  statute  in  the  State  of
Pennsylvania  which  required  all school  districts  to prepare  ten  year
master  plans  and  present  them  to  the  State  Department  for
approval.

:

Q.  This  included  construction  plans,  expansion,  closing  of

schools,  this sort  of thing?

A.  Yes,  but  the  particular  thing  I was  generally  associated

say in Jacksonville  a  segregated  school  or,  in  turn,  a  segregated

{1392}  Q.  Dr.  Foster,  in  your  experience  can  you  give  us
some  of the basic techniques  utilized  by school  systems  in order to
establish  and  maintain  or  perpetuate  segregation?

A.  Well,  there  are  several  ways  in  which  school  authorities
manage  to maintain  purposeful  segregation,  and  I use the word
and I think it has an
it has been used before,
purposeful  because
important  adjectival  meaning.  It was  used,  for  [1393]  example, in
the  Citizens  Committee  in  °62  in its report  to the  Detroit  Board  —

[1396]  ...

You  may  proceed.  The  objection  is overruled.
A.  (Continuing)  In  addition  to  the  Detroit  Committee
using  this  term  it  was  also  used  by  the  U.S.  Civil  Rights  Com-
mission  in its racial  isolation  study  in  the public  schools  in  1967,

ett
sia

bess sei

lia  171

As  I said,  I think  it  has special  connotation  because  it
indicates  that  actions  of school  authorities  have  resulted  and  that
if they don’t act in  a  neutral way the decisions  they make and the
actions  they take tend  to  further  racial isolation  or to lessen  it  and
they  tend  to  increase  containment  of  minority  groups,  or  to
decrease  it, and,  generally,  to increase  integration  or to  decrease
integration.
I think generally  what we are interested  in studying
this sort of thing  is what the results  are of actions.

The first  method  or technique  I might cite that is used  to

maintain  segregation  would be the use of optional  zones.

Would  it be  possible  for  me  to step  to the  board  to illustrate?

[1397]  Q.  Please  do.

(The witness  proceeded  to  the  blackboard.)
A.  Optional  zones  are sometimes  also referred  to  as dual
zones.  I think it will be easier  for me  to

zones  or dual overlapping
this briefly.
illustrate

(The  witness  drew  a  sketch  on the board.)

A.  If you  have, let’s  say,  two high school  districts,
District  X
zone  you
Y, frequently  when  you set up an optional
and District
carve
the zone  out of one district,  occasionally  two,  but assume
we carve  it olit of District  Y and the children  in this optional  zone
are then permitted  to  go to  either  high school  X or high school  Y,
this becomes  in a  sense  an overlapping  zone  because  if we refer to
the boundaries  of  school  District  X  at  this  point  it  not only
includes  the previous  boundary  but also  takes  in the optional
zone.

District  Y in turn  would  include  its previous  boundaries,  also
including  the optional  zone.  I think this may explain  the origin  of
the connotation of the word “overlapping”.

Essentially  optional  zones are set up  for two or three reasons,
one  is  to  allow  white  students  or  black  students  the  option  of
attending  one  of  the  two  attendance  areas  which  make  up  the
boundaries  of  the  zone  and  another  is  for,  occasionally  for
religious  purposes  to  provide  alternatives  [1398]  for  persons  of
different  religions.  Sometimes  these  are  set  up  for socio-economic
reasons  and  I have  on occasion seen  them  set  up  by  boards  or
superintendents  as  political  gimicks  in order  to  help  pass  a  bond
issue or one thing or another  or a  school board or superintendent

Ila  172

will  set  up  temporary  optional  zones  as  @  favor  to  certain
constituents  in return for assistance  in helping  the school board
with  one thing or another.

I think in the frame work in which we operate  they are used

primarily  for maintaining  segregated  patterns.

A second  method  or way in which segregation  is maintained
would be through  the use of changing  zone lines or manipulating
zone lines. In its grossest form this would be called gerrymander-
ing but many times school authorities will move small areas of one
attendance  zone into another. | think as school attendance officers
in the spring, set out to establish attendance areas for the next fall
they have to have this sort of maneuverability in order to  allow for
proper  use  of  utilization  of  buildings  in  order  to  allow  for
changing  student  populations,  and so it is almost  essential  they
have some freedom within which to  change zones according  to  the
needs of the district.

A third —  well, as far as the zone changing  [1399]  operation
I  might cite another  technique  which  I think falls under this
roughly  and that is the feeder pattern business because essentially
when  you  change  feeder  patterns  you  are  changing  zone
boundaries,  so that if you have a feeder pattern set up in which
certain high schools or junior high schools are fed into a  particular
senior  high  school  and  that  pattern  is  changed  then  the

class or groups  of  classes was or were kept intact when they went
to the receiving  school. This is referred to,  I believe as “intact
bussing.”
.
Another  example —

to black students  of an option  to  transfer  out  of an all black or
predominantly  all  black  school  or  to  a  previously  all  white
school?

A.  Yes,  it can  but  experience  proves  largely  it doesn’t  operate

that way.

Q. You  mean  doesn’t  operate  in  the  sense  no  black  chiki

exercises the option?

A+1  think  many  black  children  would  exercise  the  option,

but not in any  large numbers.

spect to. white  children  under  an  open  enrollment  policy?  Do

Ila  174

A. Not that I am aware  of.
Q. What  happens  to white children  who are in minority  ina
particular  school,  that is to say,  maybe  20 per cent of the
population  of the school is  white and the rest being black?

A. In most instances  it has been my  experience  that the white
use this as an escape  valve to  go to  the school  in which they are
the predominant race.

Q.  You spoke of this in terms of open enrollment,  would this
also be the same thing as transfers  as sometimes  used instead of
the term  “‘open enroliment”  —  free transfer?

A.  Yes.  It  seems  to me it  is very important  that  a school
system  [1402] set up a  rather rigid policing system  for its attend-
ance area operation and it.is very important  how children  and the
public at  large perceive the school’s stance in this because. in some
districts  the public perceives  it as being a  rather loose situation in
can transfer  rather  freely  by  falsifying  addresses
which  children
and moving  in with relatives  and this sort of thing. So it is very
important,  I think, once you set  up  an attendance  area assignment
or arrangement  that the schools  exercise  every caution  to see that
it is followed  and that it  works.

Q.  Doctor  Foster,  are  there  administrative  devices  under the
concept  of  an open enrollment  or transfer  which can provide that
desegregating  effect that is the opportunity  for black children to
get out of a racially  separate  situation  into an integrated  school?
A.  Yes.  If  you put in an operational  clause in your policy
which  in effect  would  be what  we call a  majority-to-minority
transfer arrangement,  this would safeguard  this device being used
only  for desegregation  purposes.
Q.  Does  that —  go ahead.
A.  1 was going on with another  thing.

segregation  plans, Doctor Foster,  provide for a  substantial  [ 1403]
disestablishment
of racial separation  in schools?

the results.
A.  I think

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  Let  me  rephrase  that,  Doctor,  just to be
technical  about  it.  In your experience,  Doctor,  what has been the
effect of the use of even this minority  transfer  provision?

A.  In my  experience  the effect  has been  zero  as far as white
children  transferring  to predominantly  black  schools.  On the other
end there have  been  for periods  of time  school  systems  in which
the blacks operated
to transfer  to majority  white  schools  and in
many instances,  never  in large  numbers,  but where  they  did in
many instances  after a brief experience  they  would  return  to the
school  in which  they  were  the majority  for one reason  or another.

Q.  Are  there  other  administrative  techniques  you  have

identified,  Doctor?

A.  Yes.  I think  a  fifth one would be the use of grade patterns
or grade organization  patterns.  For example,
a typical  school
[1404]  system  would  have  an organizational  structure  of K
thorugh  6, 7 through  9, and  10  through  12.  Frequently  in a large
system  —  what would I call it  —  the buffer  grades  between  these
three levels,  in this case I just  mentioned  grades  6 and 7 or to some
extent grade  9 would be used as what we call swing grades.  That is
to say in any given  school  K  to grade  6 might  be transferred  to
junior  high  for certain  reasons  or grade  7  might  be transferred
back  to the elementary  school  for certain  reasons  and very
typically  a  system  that has a certain  organizational  structure  but
was  then  violated  in almost  half  or more  of the schools  I
examined.  I think  the use of the so-called  swing  grades  is certainly
a  legitimate  exercise  on  the  part  of  administrative  authorities
because  they have to, again,  have to have some  maneuverability  in
terms of  pupil assignment  to  take care  of capacity  problems  and
shift  population  problems.  It  simply  becomes  a  question  of
whether  this device  is used to  promote  further  segregation  and  I
think in many cases it is.

[1405]

Q.  What  about  school  construction,

A.  Well, a final general  category  of  methods  in which segrega-
tion and isolation are maintained  would be the total area of school

Ila  176

construction,  and  this  includes  quite  a  few  corollary  operations,
such  as  I think  one  of the  most  important  ones  at  the moment  is
the size of site. At this day. and age  in selecting a site
site  selection,
it is obviously  very  important  to  consider  what  effect  it will  have
on  segregation  now  and  in the future.  The  closing  down  of schools
would  be a  part of the general  operation.  The size of  school that
you  build  would  also  be  involved.  Another  operation  as  part  of
this used by some systems  is the use of  portables  in one  way or the
other.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  how  does  the  size  of the  school  which  is built

affect  the containment  or segregation  of students?

A:  Many  times  you  find  that  minority  groups  in  order  to
further  contain  or  isolate  are  housed  in  what  we  might  call
out-sized  —  that  is  to  say  typically  in  a  particular  system  you
would  have  elementary  schools  which  would  average  600  to  800
pupils,  and  then  you  will  come  along  and  find  an  all-black  school
which has 1800 to 2,000 pupils.  This I think is an example.

Q. What  about  the  converse,  Doctor,  where  a  school  is
deliberately  kept  to  a  smaller  than  average  size?  How  does  that
affect  segregation?  .

:

‘[1406]  A.  I think  most  frequently  the  small  sized  school
affects  segregation  unfavorably  by the  use  of small  primary  units
or  elementary  units  to  maintain  a  close,  walk-in  type  neighbor-
hood  school pattern.  I didn’t  mention  in the use  of grade structure
or  grade organization  the fact  that  in many  systems  you  will  find a
differential  between  minority  group  schools  and  majority  group
schools  in the  way  they are  organized.  For example,  in some  cities
the  all-black  or  predominantly  black  schools  will  be  classified  or
grouped  as  K through  12 schools,  or  in Jacksonville,  for example,
the  secondary  schools,  the  black  schools  are  all  7  through  12,  -
where  the  white  schools  are  10  through  12 or  9 through  12.  So,
quite  often  you  will  find  differential  organizational  structures
according  to race.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  have  you  made  a  study  and  analysis  of.

optional  zones  in the  Detroit  school  system?

A.  Yes,  I have.

‘A.  The  Northwestern-Northern  optional  zones  —
Northwestern  is  to  the  southeast,  Northern  is to  the  southwest.
Northern  is  to  the  northeast  and  the  optional  area  in between  is
made  up  [1428-A]  of the  Thirkell  and  Goldberg  attendance  areas.
[1429]  Q.  (by  Mr.  Lucas,  continuing)  Directing  your  atten-
tion  to  136-A,  can  you  describe  the  non-white  population  patterns
in Northwestern  and  Northern?

A.  According  to  the  1950  census  map  Northwestern  was
about  40  percent  colored  white,  which  would  indicate  0 to  5
percent  non-white.  The  southern  and  southeastern  portion  of the
Northwestern  attendance  area  included  blue,  green,  orange  and  red
areas  in about  equal  proportions,  indicating  generally  a population
of 25  to  100  percent  non-white  in  the southern-southeastern  part
of Northwestern.  In Northern,  the entire  eastern  two-thirds,  north-
easterfi  two-thirds  of the  Northern  sector  were  colored  orange  or
red, indicating  a  population  of 50  to  100  percent  non-white.  The
western  portion  was  colored  about  half white  and  half green,  with
some  orange,  indicating  generally  a  non-white  population  of 0 to
25 percent.

Q.  And  the  optional  area  of Thirkell  and  Goldberg  Schools,

Goldberg
and Thirkell  between  the  two high  schools,

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  examining  those  two  high  school  attendance

integration?
In  my  opinion  Newberry  could  have  been  transferred,  the
Newberry  elementary  zone  to  Chadsey,  which  would  have  not
only  held  the  integration  status  of  the  two  high  schools,  but
relieved  some  of the overcrowding  in Northwestern.

Q.  Dr. Foster,  will you  step to the map,  please.
Directing  your  attention  to the optional zone  between North
western  and Western, would you describe the  zones  in question and

A.  The  Northwestern  High  School  attendance  zone  is to the
north,  with  the optional  area  in cross-  hatch  in the middle.  To the
south and further  south  from  the optional  area is the Western High
School  attendance  area  which  borders  the  south  part of the
7
district  and the River.

orange  or  red,  indicating  a  non-white  population  of 50 to 100

[1435]  Q.  And,  Western?

representing  0 to  5 percent  non-white  and  the  green  area  which

does it contain?

optional  zone  was  revised  in  the  southern  and  southeastem
portion  to  drop  off  the  area  south  of Myrtle  ——  I believe  it is
Myrtle  Avenue. It is depicted on the map by the red boundary line
here.

zone?

A.  Yes,  it does.

western.  I  believe  this  comprises  the  Kennedy  Elementary

[1445]  Q.  Dr.  Foster,  do  you  have  an  opinion  as  to  the
affect  of the  operation  of the  optional  attendance  zone  for the
period of time in which  it was used?

;

A.  My  opinion  is  that  the  optional  zone  between  North
western  High  School  and  Western  High  School  allowed  for a
two-way  traffic,  as  in all the  others,  of whites  to  go  into Westem
from  the  optional  zone  for as far back as the zone  existed  prior to
1959  and  until  such  time  as  the  different  portions  of the  zone
were  stopped.  It also  allowed  blacks  in the optional zone  to move
into  Northwestern,  thereby  increasing  segregation  in  both
instances.

'

slipping  in any  way,  would  you  examine  the Mackenzie  zone  and
tell  me whether  the non-white  percentage  is reflected  there?

A.  In  the  Mackenzie  zone  I  believe  it  is  entirely  white,

indicating  a 0 to 5 percent  non-white  population  in  1950.

Q.  And  the  optional  attendance  area?

[1499]  A.  The  optional  attendance  area  is entirely  white,

representing a percentage  of 0 to  5 percent  non-white.

Q.  Is that the Sherrill  area you referred  to,  Doctor?

A.  That is  part of the Sherrill area, yes.

Q.  Directing  your  attention  to  the  Chadsey  zone  and
immediately  below  the  Sherrill  area  that  is  included  in  the
optional  zone,  what is the demographic  information  there?

A.  The southern  part of the Sherrill  attendance  area, which is
in the Chadsey  High School  zone, is all blue,  representing  25 to 50
percent  non-white.

Q.  Would  you describe  the rest of the Chadsey  zone?
A.  The major  portion  of the balance  of  Chadsey  zone is
white,  representing  a  0 to  5 percent  non-white  population.  To the
northeast  we have a  red area representing 85 to  100 percent nom
white.  Immediately  below  this we have  a  green area, and to the
southwest  we have a  portion  colored  green,  representing  in both

Ila  199

cases a 5 to  25  percent  non-white.
On the extreme  eastern,  north-
eastern  pocket  there is a  blue area,  representing  25  to 50 percent
non-white.

Q.  In  1960,  Dr.  Foster,  what  was  the  enrollment  at Chadsey

High School?

A.  In  1960 Chadsey S¢nior High School had 2,692  students.
Q. What was its percentage  black?

A.  The  percentage  black  for  Chadsey  Senior  High School  was

41.3  [1450]  percent.  And  the  Mackenzie  School,  Doctor?

A. In 1960  the Mackenzie  School  had  a  population and

student enrollment  of 2,799.

Q.  What  was its percentage  black,  please?

A.  Its percentage  was  12.2  percent  black.

Q. What  was  the  capacity  of  Chadsey  High  School  at  that

time?

A.  The  1960  capacity  figure  for  Chadsey  was  3,328,
indicating  that  they  had  available  space  of  approximately  636
students.

.

Q.  And Mackenzie?
A.  The  capacity  for  Mackenzie  Senior  High  in 1960  was

2,832, indicating  they had available  space  of 33 students.

Q.  Neither  school  then  was  overcrowded,  is that  correct?

A.  That is correct, sir.

A.  A total  of  341  blacks  were  in  Mackenzie  Senior  High
School.  This  reflects  a  percentage  of 5.2  as  opposed  to  a system

A.  In the Mackenzie  —
Q.  Let’s  talk  about  the  constellation  first  of all.
A.  The  number  of whites  in ‘60-61  was  9,640.

1960,  representing  a  percentage  of 25.3  as  compared  to  a  system
wide percentage  for whites  of 19.6  in that  year.

Q.  Doctor  Foster,  do  you  have  an  opinion  as  to  the  effect  of :
the optional  attendance  area  in  the  portion  of the  Sherrill  zone  as  _
shown  between  Mackenzie  and  Chadsey?

A.  In  my  opinion  the  effect  of  this  optional  zone  was  to
allow  whites  in  the  optional  area  to  attend  Mackenzie  from  1958
when  it was  established  until  1963  when  the  zone  was  dissolved
and  at  the  same  time  to  allow  blacks  to  go  to  Chadsey  to  the
south.

Q.  What  effect,  if  any,  did  this  have  on  the  pattern  of

assignment  of pupils  to schools  in those  areas?

A.  In my opinion  this  furthered  the  segregated  status  —

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  Dr.  Foster,  from  your  examination  of the

carve-out  of a portion  of the  Southeastern  zone  in an  area  which  is
0 to 4.9  percent  non-white,  according  to  the  1960  census  between
that Southwestern  and  Denby,  as  to the  effect  of such  a carve-out?

A.  The  data  which  I went  over  indicated  that  there  were  no

effect  was  that  the  creation  of  the  optional  zone  out  of  the

the optional  zone  to  attend  the  then  all-white  Denby  High  School
rather than Southeastern,  which  was  30.9  percent  black.

A.  Yes,  I have  it right  here.

A.  In  1960  in  the  Southeastern  zone  there  were  7,962  black

School,  reflecting  a  percentage  of  9.3,  compared  to  a  system
average  of 12.4.

Q.  Directing  your  attention  to  the  Denby  High  School  area,

A.  The  number  of  white  students  in  Denby,  in  the  Denby

constellation  in 1960-61  was  10,007.

Q.  And  what  was  the number  in high school?
A.  Of that  number  there  were  2,529  in high school.
Q.  And  what  percent  was  that?
A.  That  reflected  a  percentage  of  25.3,  as  compared  toa

system  percentage  of 19.6.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  do  you  have  an  opinion  as to the effect of the
utilization  of an  optional  area  taken  from  the  Southeastern  zone
between  Southeastern  and  Denby  on  the  racial  characteristics  of
those  schools?

A.  In  my  opinion  the  creation  of  such  an  optional  zone
allowing  the  whites  to  go into Denby rather  than be assigned to
Southeastern  would  lead  the  general  population  of Southeastem
[1463]  to  perceive  their  school  as  an  isolated  school,  as  a com
tained  school,  and  therefore  of less worth  as a high school.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  directing  your  attention  to  the  Southwestem
and  Western  High  School  zones,  would  you  describe  both  of those

A.  On  the  1959  overlay,  the Western  zone  is to the northeast,
and  the  Southwestern  attendance  area  is  to  the  southwest.  In
between  you  have  almost  two  optional  zones,  but  we  are  dealing

is part  of  the  optional  zone.  The  northern  section  includes

elementary  schools.  I believe  there  is a  portion  south  of the

goes Longworth,  Chatfield  and  Regular.  I would  have  to  see  the
book  to  make  certain.  But,  that  appears  from  the  map  to  be
accurate.

Q.  Directing  your  attention  to  Plaintiffs’  Exhibit  136-A,  the

In the  1960 census,  the northern  portion,  please.
A.  The northern  portion  of the Gillies—
Q. No, the northern  section  of the Southwestern  option.
A.  The  total  option?
Q.  Yes, please.
-  A.  It is all white,  therefore  representing  a  non-white  popula-
tion of  0 to  5 percent  and, I believe,  this was the same as was
found in 1960.

Q.  Is there  a change  in the  Southwestern  zone  in terms  of the
particular  portion  of  it and  its  non-white  population?  You  may
refer to Exhibit  136-A  and  136-B.

A.  In  the  period  from  1950  to  1960  the  southwestern
ion  of the  Southwestern  attendance  area  went  from  blue  and
white  to  completely  red  indicating  a  considerable  transition
approaching  [1467]  an  all-black  area.

A.  On  the  current  underlay,  Mark  Twain,  Boynton,  Fisher

and Jeffries.

map dated  when?

A.  Dated  in  1969.
Q.  Referring  to  the  second  portion  of  the  Southwestern
option  which  has  been  described  as  the  Gillies  area,  will  you
describe  the change,  if any,  in that  area?

A.  The  Gillies  area  in  1950  on  the  northern  edge  and  on  the
western  edge  was  white,  representing  a  0 to  5 percent  non-white
population.  The center  and southern  portion  were  green represent-
ing a 5 to 25 percent  non-white  population.

In  1960,  on  the  census  map  of  the  northern  portion  stays
about  the same  and the southern  portion  moves  from  green  to
blue representing  an  increase  in black  population.

Ila  210

Q.  How  long  did  the  Southwestern  option  continue  beyond

1959, Dr. Foster?

A.  The Gillies  area which is the southern  part of the option
was  eliminated  in  1962-63.  The  optional  zone  at  the  northern
portion  of  Southwestern,  as  I understand  it, was  eliminated  in
1970-71  for  the  tenth  grade.  It  was  still  in  existence  for  the
eleventh  and  twelfth  grades  which  are  going  largely  [1468]  to
Western.  The  tenth  grade  is assigned  to  Southwestern  this current
school  year.

a  line  south  of  Longworth  had  an  option  of  attending  Western

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  do  you  have  an  opinion  as  to the  effect  of the
utilization  of  these  optional  areas  between  Southwestern  and
Western  High School?

A.  From  the  data  which  I have  gone  over,  my  opinion  is that
’  the optional  zone  allowed  the  whites  in the optional  area  to attend
Western,  not  only  allowed  them  to,  but  in fact,  they did, and  the
blacks  in  the  area  attended  Southwestern.  This,  in  turn,  would
have  the  effect  of heightening  the  segregated  situation  in  the  two
high school  areas  and  in the system  as  a whole.

Q.  But,  with  respect  to  the  Gillies  portion  of  the
Southwestern  option,  did  this  provide,  is your  opinion  the  same  or
does it provide  anything  else  at  the Gillies  area?  There  is non-white
population,  a  larger  percentage  of non-white  shown  in  the  Gillies
area,  is that correct?

A.  That  is correct.  Well,  I think  the  two-way  release  situation
would  hold  for  Gillies  because  it allowed  the  blacks  in that  area  to
move  into  Southwestern  and  stay  there  and  the  whites  to go  to
Western.

High School  in  1969  before  the  change  in  the option  at  the  tenth
grade in 1970-71?

A.  In -1969,  Southwestern  had  an  enrollment  of  1483  and

this enrollment  was  86.7  percent  black.

Q.  In  1970-71  what  is the enrollment  of Southwestern  with

the elimination  of the option  of the tenth grade?

A.  In  1970-71  the  enrollment  in  Southwestern  is  1,767,

1969, and  a  percentage  black  of 74.3,  representing  a decrease  of
1969 of 12.4 percent.

Ila  214

Q.  Doctor,  from  the  period  1960  to  1971,  do  you  have  an
opinion  as  to  any  techniques  which  would  have  been  available
which  would  have  desegregated  Southwestern  and  Western  other
than  elimination  of  the  optional  zone?  Are  there  any  other
techniques  available?

¥

A.  Well,  again  I think  if we  would  have  a  drawn  boundary
line  east  and  west  instead  of north  and  south  it is quite  likely that
a  much  better  racial  mix  could  have  been  obtained  between  the
two  high  schools.  Other  possibilities,  of course,  would  have  been,
which  would  have  probably  had  less effect  but  [1474]  still  tended
to  desegregate  the  situation,  would  have  been  moving  Gillies
optional  zone  into  Western,  but  I think  you  asked  things  that did
not  affect  the optional  zone.

addition  was  made  to  the  Southwestern  Building  Complex  which
included,  I believe,  some  shops,  I believe  a dining room  area  and, if
I  remember  correctly,  a  physical  education  facility  was  either
improved  or  added  and  I believe  that  some  additional  student
capacity  was  involved,  but  not  very  much.  A  minimal  additional
student  capacity.

My understanding,  if I recollect  the  figure  correctly,  the addi

tion was  priced at around  $4 million.

-
-
Q.  Doctor  Foster,  do  you  know  of  any  administrative
- problems,  such  as  capacity,  which  may  have  occasioned  the  use  of
_  an optional  zone  in this particular  area?

Ila  216

A.  No, sir.  In  1960  the  Burroughs  capacity  was  1,472.
Q.  The  Burroughs  is a predominantly  white  school?
A.  Yes,  it is, representing  an  enrollment  of 98 over  capacity.
In  1960  Greusel,  which  is a  predominantly  black  school,  had a
capacity  of 928,  and  the  enrollment  figures  represent  an  enrol}:
ment  of 71  over  capacity.

Q.  In  your  opinion,  was  there  any  administrative  reason  in

terms  of capacity  —

A.  As  a matter  of fact,  there  was  no  less capacity  available in
either  junior  high  school  from  the  way  I read  the  figures,  and it
would  appear  from  the  map  that  Greusel  in distance  is a good deal
closer  to the optional  area  than  the  Burroughs  Junior  High School,
about  half the distance  or  a third,  I would  estimate.

Q.  Doctor  Foster,  is  there  a  technique  of  desegregation,  an
[1478]  administrative  technique  which  could  have  been  utilized in
1960  to desegregate  both  Burroughs  and  Greusel?

A.  Well,  for  one  thing  the Thomas  optional  zone,  elementary
attendance  area  could  have  been  assigned  to  Burroughs,  which
would  further  integrate  or  desegregate  the  Burroughs  population
and  would  bring  the  per  cent  black  population  in both  Burroughs
and Greusel  to a much  more  similar  figure.

Q.  Do  you  have  an  opinion,  Doctor  Foster,  as  to the effect of

the utilization  of the optional  zone  in this particular  instance?

A.  In  my  opinion  the  effect  of using  this  optional  zone  wa
to  further  increase  segregation  in  the  two  junior  high  schools and
to  indicate  to  the  resident  population  that  whites  could  option
out,  and blacks  could  go to their  predominantly  racial school.

[1489]  thereupon  resumed  the stand  as  a witness  herein  and,
having been previously  duly sworn,  was  examined  and testified  fur—
ther as follows:

DIRECT  EXAMINATION  (Continued)  By Mr.  LUCAS:

Il a  220

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  did you  make  a  study  of  zone  changes  and  -
feeder  pattern  changes  in  the  Detroit  School  System  from  data
available  to you?

A.  Yes,  I did.
Q.  Do  you  have  some  examples  of  such  changes  in  feeder

patterns  and zone  lines  that  you  have  particularly  examined?

m
A.  Yes,  I have.
Q.  Directing  your  attention,  Dr.  Foster,  to Plaintiffs’  Exhibit
109-M  —  could  you  step  down  here  —  you  may  refer  to  your
notes.

The exhibit  has a label Jefferson  [1490]  Junior  High  by one  of
the  schools  located  thereon.  Would  you  examine  the  exhibit  and
explain  it, Doctor,  in  terms  of your  analysis  of the  fact  situation.
A.  This  particular  exhibit  is  centered  in  the  Northwestern
constellation.  It involves  the  Jeffries  Housing  Project  which  is in
the center  of our black  marked  exhibit.

In  terms  of elementary  attendance  area  it involves  primarily
the Couzens,  Edmonson  and Poe  Elementary  areas  which  surround
the  Jeffries  Housing  Project  and  our  feeder  elementary  schools
basically  go to Jefferson  Junior  High.

In.  Exhibit  78-A,  which  is  our  boundary  book,  in  ‘59-60  it
states,  “Children  living in the  Jeffries  Homes,  in the  buildings west
of the  John  Lodge  Expressway  —  ”  and  the  John  Lodge  Express
way  would  be roughly  the  black  line  just  where  Jefferson  Junior
High  borders  on  and  it is easy  to  determine  because  it is racially
separated  in terms  of the census  map,  the  blue  on  one  side and the
orange  on  the other  for the Jeffries  Homes.

Q.  Green  representing  what, just for the record?
A.  5  to  25  percent  and  the  orange  representing  50 to 85
percent.  “The  children  west  of  the  Expressway  in  the  Jeffries
homes  attend  Hutchins  Junior  High  School  for grades  7-B  to 9-A.”  -
The  black  line  with  the  arrow  points  [1491]  to  the  Hutchins
_  Junior  High  School  which  is in  the  Northern  constellation.  Then
they go to Northwestern  at the senior  high level.

The  children  living  east  of the  John  Lodge  Expressway  attend
Jefferson  for  grades  7-B  and  9-A  and  then  in  turn  go  to North
western.  I believe  in  terms  of numbers  we  have  no  transpo

records  for  1959-60,  but  in Plaintiffs’  Exhibit  63, which  has to do

in 1961-62  we  have  no  transportation  or  bussing  but  in  1962-63,
$2 children  were  still bussed on  this run, and then in 1963-64  it
had diminished  to  17 children.

1,973 pupils, 69.9 percent  of whom  were  black.

A.  The  capacity  of  Hutchins  in  1960  was  1757  stations,

indicating  an  overpopulation  of 216  students.

Q. (By Mr.  Lucas,  continuing)  Dr.  Foster,  what  was  the effect
in terms  of pupil enrollment  of the  assignment  of the students  to
the Hutchins  Junior  High School?

A.  The  changing  of  the  feeder  pattern  from  Jefferson  to
Hutchins  of the  group  of 219 students  who were  bussed  increased
segregation  in  both  Jefferson  and  Hutchins  in  terms  of sending
more blacks  to  Hutchins  which was already  nearly all black and in
terms  of sending  less  blacks  to  Jefferson  which  was  46.3  percent
black.

record for such a transfer?

the  change  was  216  over  capacity,  Jefferson  was  129  over
capacity,  and  it would  appear  that  it wouldn’t  have  affected  either
junior high to that extent.

Q. The  effect,  Doctor,  then,  of  the  removal  of Vandenberg
and Vernor  from  the Ford feeder  pattern  into  the Mumford  feeder
[1518]  pattern,  what was  the effect  in terms  of race?

A.  The  effect  of this move  in  1967-68  of the  transfer  back  of
the  two  elementary  schools  was  to  increase  the  segregation  at
Mumford,  to  take  blacks  from  the  Ford  High  School  and,  there-
fore, increase  the  segregated  pattern  there,  and,  in my  opinion,  it

Il a  236

reinforced  inevitably  the  perception  that  Ford  would  be  kept
white  as  a  matter  of  basic  policy  and  that  Mumford  would be a
racially  contained  isolated  high school  attendance  area.

Q.  Doctor,  1967,  what  could  have  been  done  to  relieve  the
racial  separation  or  to integrate  the  schools  in that  particular  area?
A.  Well,  in  my  opinion,  instead  of  moving  Vandenberg and
Vernor  back,  they  could  have  remained  at  Ford  because  I do not
think  capacity  was  that  big  a  consideration  especially  since  the
Redford  High  School  at  that  time  I think  was  something  like 900
or  1,000  over  capacity.  But  essentially  it  seems  to  me  that  an
excellent  alternative  at that juncture  would  have  been  to  consider
the  rezoning  of the  four  high  school  areas  to the  northwest  of the
city, Ford,  Redford,  Mumford  and Cooley.

Q.  How  would  that  have  been  rezoned?
A.  If you  look  at  the  total  population  and  racial  makeup of
these  four  high  school  areas  at  that  time  the  figures  I get  are  tor
the  four  schools  total,  a  white  population  of  8,847  students, a
black  population  of 3,096  students  which  would  have  [1519]  left,
if the  schools  were  completely  balanced  racially,  and  I doubt  if
they  could  have  been  that,  but  they  could  have  approximated  that
1 assume  by zoning  changes.  The  average  black  population,  if they
would  have  been  balanced,  would  have  been  about  26 percent
black.

As  a  matter  of fact,  from  reading  the  data  I believe  that is in
his  report  to  the  Superintendent  or  the  Board  in  1967,  Mr. Her-
rickson  suggested  part  of this  possibility,  which  was,  I believe, to
involve  only  Ford  and  Mumford.  It is my  opinion  that  if all four
schools  would  have  been  involved  in such  a redistribution  it would
have  worked  out  even  better  than  Mr.  Henrickson’s  very  able, |
think,  and  excellent  sugestion.

botham  the  MacDowell  Elementary  School  in  1960  had  an  enroll-

in thinking of these  three  schools at that  time,  and  that  is that  118
pupils  were  being  bussed  to  Higginbotham  from  the  Angell
a area, which  is  down  in  Center  City,  a  considerable

,

tern  was  to  provide  segregated  student  ratios  at  all  three  of th
elementary  schools,  and  in  terms  of things  that  could  be done or
could  have  been  done  at  that  particular  time  to  correct  the segre-
gated  situation,  it  is  my  opinion  that,  first  of  all,  the  students
being  bussed  from  Angell  could  have  been  dropped  off at any

put.  This  having  been  done,  zone  lines  could  have  been  redrawn at

uation  which  ,  in  my  opinion,  would  have  helped  to  stabilize  th

Ila  241

Q.  Do  you  have  an  opinion,  Doctor,  as  to  the  perception
created  by  the  maintenance  of  the  Higginbotham  School  under
those  circumstances,  including  the  transportation  of  black
students  from  Angell  into  it?

A.  Well,  it is obvious  that  if you  transport  black  children  past
white  schools  to  an  all  black  school  that  the  community  is going
to perceive  this  as  a  segregated  intent,  a  segregated  action.  If you
have  a  boundary  situation  which  isolates  and  enforces  black
students  to  a  particular  area  when  the boundary  lines  could  be
changed  to effectuate  a better  pattern  racially,  then  it seems  to me
that  community  perceptiori  would  also  be  that  the  school  is not
doing  what  it could  in terms  of integration  and  equal  opportunity.
Q.  Doctor,  from  your  examination  of  the  data  in  1960  are
there  any  administrative  reasons,  any  administrative  problems
which  would  indicate  to  you  a  reason  why  this  boundary  ¥ was
maintained  rather  than  drawn in  some  other  fashion?

A.  In terms  of school  capacity  there  are  none,  no.

Q.  Are  there  any  administrative  reasons  that  you  know  of,

Doctor,  which  would  mitigate  against  changing  these  lines?

[1527]  A.  No, sir.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  | would  ask  you  about  the  Hally  example.  |
think  I  misspoke  myself  and  said  “Holly”  It’s  actually
Hally.

A.

The  Hally  Elementary  School  is  in  the  Mumford
constellation.  The  situation  here  is  very  similar  to  the
Higginbotham  just  described  in  testimony.  In  1960  Hally  Elemen-
tary  School  had  an  enrollment  of about  180  students,  was  94 per-
cent  black,  it had  a  capacity  of 1088,  indicating  92  percent  more
students  were  enrolled—

Q.  92 percent,  Doctor?

Ila  242

A.  I beg  your  pardon,  92  more  students  were  enrolled  than
capacity.  Immediately  to the  north  of Hally  we  find  the  Hampton
Elementary  School  which  in 1960  had  an  enrollment  of 908; per-
centage  black  population  of  .1  percent;  the  capacity  of  992
students,  indicating  room  at  Hampton  was  available  for  84 add-
itional  students.

Immediately  to  the  west  of Hally  we  had  Fitzgerald  which in
1960  had  an  enrollment  of  1322  students,  percentage  black  19.6,
capacity  of 1472, indicating  150 pupil stations  unused.

To  the  northwest  of  Hally  we  had  the  Bagley  Elementary
School  with  871  students  enrolled  in  1960,  completely  white,  a
capacity  of 1,056,  indicating  unused  pupil stations  of 185.

[1529] So we  find  a predominately  black  school  with  94 per-
cent  black  surrounded  by  either  all-white  schools  or  nearly  all-
white  schools.  The  effect  of this, in my  opinion,  was  that  you had
segregated  ratios  at  all  of  these  schools  in  the  cluster  I just dis
cussed,  Hally,  Hampton,  Fitzgerald  and  Bagley  and  in  addition
there  was  overcrowding  at  Hally  of  92  students  and  there  was
under-utilization  of  student  stations  at  Hampton,  Fitzgerald  and
Bagley.

:

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  from  your  examination  of  this  area  at  that
time  were  there  administrative  techniques  available  which  would
have  relieved  the  overcrowding  and  also  reduced  the  segregation
in those  schools?

A.  In my  opinion  zone  lines,  zone  boundary  lines  could  have
been  redrawn  to  take  care  of the  overpopulation  at  Hally  and to
effectuate  a  better  racial  ratio  or  balance  in all  four  of these ele-
mentary  schools.

Q.  Using,  for  example,  two  of the schools,  Doctor,  could the

technique  of pairing  have  been  used?

A.  Yes, but-
MR.  BUSHNELL:  Objection  as  leading,  if the Court  please.
THE.COURT:  He may  answer.
A.  Quite  easily,  but  I think  just  changing  zone  lines would
have  sefved  to  do the same thing.  The more  recent  technique  of
[1530]  pairing could  have  been  used  or  clustering.

is based  on  what we call the Princeton  plan, gen-
erally,  where  you  take  two  schools  of  similar  grade  structure  or
6 schools.  If
to it, and an example  would  be  two  K  through

with  a desegregated  balanced  student  enrollment.

were  in  the  Mumford  constellation  something  like  eight  schools
which  were  structured  organizationally  on  a  K through 8 pattern.
These  included  Vandenberg,  Vernor,  MacDowell,  Higginbotham,
Pasteur,  Schulze  Elementary,  Hampton,  and  Hally.

[1536] A. Prior to the change?

Q.  What  was  the  effect  of the  change  in  the  Stellwagen  and

Clark  Schools  from  the  feeder  pattern,  Doctor?

A.  The  effect  was  to  remove  the  almost  completely  white
attendance,  elementary  attendance  areas  of  these  two  schools
from  a school  which  was  22  percent  black  at that  time,  and move
them  into a situation  which  was  entirely  white.

Q.  What  happened  to the junior  high students?
A.  The  junior  high  students  remained  at  the  seventh  and
eighth  grade  in these  two  elementary  schools  and  from  the ninth
grade  moved  on  to Finney.

have been  a senior  high.

A.  Further,  the  effect  of this  was  to isolate  the  blacks  and  to

A.  At this time  186  blacks  were  coming  from  Angell,  which  is

Junior  High  zone.

Q. What  was  the enrollment  and  percent  black  at Greenfield?

A.  In  1960  Greenfield  Park  had  an  enroilment  of  1605

students,  11.9  percent  of whom  were  black.

-Q.  What  was  its capacity?  _  ¥
™
A.  1592 capacity.

Q. Doctor,  did  you  study  this  feeder  pattern  in  the  schools,
the other  elementary  schools  in this  area,  and,  if so,  do  you  have
an opinion  as  to  an  organization  pattern  in that  area  which  could
have been used to desegregate  those schools?
MR. BUSHNELL:  Well, that  is leading.
_  THE COURT:  No, he may  answer.
A.  Yes.  I think  there  is some.  I could  give  some examples of
some  alternatives  which  might  have  been  effectuated  to  ease
115S4}  the Segregated  status  of Nolan  and  Cleveland.  First of all—

II a 258

Q.  Let  me  interrupt  you  and  ask,  you  have  not  made  any
study  and  you  are  not  recommending  a  specific  plan,  as  I under.
stand  it?

A.  No,  not  at  all.  This  is, as  it were,  not  a  present  situation
but  in  the  past  and  I am  merely  giving  examples  of things that
might have  been  done  to promote  desegregation.

Q.  All right, sir.

A.  Generally  speaking,  if you work  on any kind of a desegre.
gation  plan or study  you have  to  consider
the system  as a whole.
You  can’t  isolate  a particular  school  and  its neighbor  and  come up
with  a  complete  answer  unless  you have  all of  the  data at any
given  time.  On  the  other  hand,  it is quite  obvious,  I think,  with
some  experience  that
you can see things in looking  at the data and
maps  that can  be rather  readily  accomplished.

First  of all, if the  students  from  Angell  had  not  been  coming
to  Greenfield  Park  and  we  ascertained  they  could  have  gone
elsewhere  with  improvement  in  segregation,  to  several  white
schools.

Q.  You  mean  desegregation?

A.  Yes.
Q.  All right, sir.
A.  Sorry.  This would  have  given  more  space in the area to
which  [1555]  we  are  now  paying  attention.  Greenfield  Park,
without  these  children,  could  have  been  zoned  into the Cleveland
area,  giving  it  a considerable  number  more  of the  white  students.
All  of Courville  which  is  the  bifurcated  zone  that  we  have been
discussing  could  have  then  been  zoned  to  Nolan,  giving  it a cor
_  siderably  greater  number  of black  students.

Another  possibility  would  have  been  to zone  Atkinson,  which

is immediately  east  of Courville,  into  the  Nolan  constellation o
Nolan  attendance  area.

Q.  What  was  the  enrollment  at  Atkinson  and  racial  percent

age?

A.  In  1960  Atkinson  had  an  enrollment  of 827  with  a black

percentage  of 98.4.

Q.  Do you know  which  junior  high school  it fed into?

A.  At that time it fed into Cleveland.

II a 259

Q.  Is it about  equidistant  between  Cleveland  and  Nolan?

A.  I would  say  so,  yes,  sir.

Q. All right.  Go ahead.

A.  Other  possibilities  to  effectuate  desegregation  in  this  area
at the  time  -  Greenfield  Park,  which  is in  this  section,  and  Cour-
ville  immediately  to  the  east  are  what  I would  constitute  or con-
sider a fine  example  of two  schools  which  could  be paired  or zone
lines could be  redrawn  by what  we  call  reverse  gerrymandering  in
such a way as to  racially  balance  these  two schools
which have or
which  had  at that  time  practically  [1556]  identical  enrollment  and
quite opposite  racial  patterns.

Another  possibility,  I believe,  would  have  been  to re-draw  the
Davison  and White  zone  lines.  Davison  is an  elementary  attendance
area in the  Cleveland  district which  based  on  the  1960  census  data
is entirely  orange,  representing  a population,  general  population  of
50 to 85 percent.  The  Davison  student  population  at that  time  was
89.6 percent  black.

Sitting  immediately  east  of  Davison  is the  elementary  zone
known  as  White.  In  the  1960  enrollment  White  had  1,096
students,  40.2  percent  of  whom  were  black.  Again  it would  be
possible  to  redraw  zone  lines  or  if preferred,  to  pair  these  two
schools  in such  a  way  as  to affect  a much  better  segregation  status
approaching  racial  balance.

Q. Would  this  have  any  effect  on  the  racial  identifiability  of

those schools  in this area,  Doctor?

A.  In  my  opinion  the  population,  racially,  is identified  in
such  a way  that  racial  identifiability  could  have  been  largely  erad-
icated at that  time.

Q. Doctor,  your  examples  have  been  given in reference  to the
existing feeder patterns  between  Cleveland  and Nolan in that area.

described  as  98.4  percent  black,  are  there  areas  [1SS7]  from  the
1960 census to the north of that particular school which have a
different  designation  in terms  of non-white  population?

A.  There  are such schools  in the Osborn  constellation. I be-
lieve the one immediately  north is VanZile.  To  the east of that is
the Law Elementary  School,  both  of which  from  the census data,

—o

“

population  appears  to  be between  0 and  5S  percent  in  the  1969

the  Cleveland  attendance  areas  in  such  a  way  as  to  effectuate

In 1967-68,  Davison  and  White  Elementaries  were  transferred

from the Pershing  High School  attendance  area to  the Osborn.  As

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  What  was  the  racial  composition  of

Davison  at that  time,  Doctor?

A.  ‘67-68,  Davison  was  95.5  per  cent  black  and White  to the
immediate  east  was  38.3  per  cent  black.  Pershing  Senior  High at
the  same  time was 51.2  per cent  black  and Osborn  Senior  High was
12.5  per cent  black.

Q.  What was the effect of that transfer,  Doctor?
A.  In  my  opinion  from  the  standpoint  of  integration  the
effect  was good  and also  from  the  standpoint, the administrative
[1592]  standpoint  of capacity,  it seemed  to have  no  problems, but
essentially  in terms  of integration  it increased  the blacks  in Osbom
which was predominantly  white  and  decreased  the black  population
‘in Pershing  which  was  approaching  50 per cent  black.

Q.  You  mentioned  capacity,  Doctor,  how  did  that  affect or

not affect  the capacity  problems?

A.  The  capacity  for  Pershing  at the  time  was  2,570  with a
over population  of 484.  For Osborn  the  capacity  was  2,630 with
an  over  population  of 429.°So  this  move  tended
to affect a closer
agreement  between  the two  over  capacities.

A.  Yes.  In  my  opinion  it increased  the amount  of segregation
in  Pershing  by adding  more  blacks.  It made  Osborn  a more  [1595]
predominantly  white school  by taking  the  blacks  from  Davison  into
Pershing.

_

Q.  Doctor,  were  there  other  alternatives  available  administe

tively for the purpose of desegregation?

MR.  BUSHNELL:  Could  I have  that question  again?

Q.  (By.  Mr.  Lucas)  Were  there  other  alternatives  available

from  the  point  of view  of administration  and desegregation?

A.  Well,  one  possibility  would  have  been instead  of moving
Davison  back  into  Pershing  to  allow  these  two  schools,  Davison
and  White,  to remain  in Osborn and transfer  some  attendance area
zones  in  the  western  part of Osborn to Pershing.  For example, the
Law  Elementary  School,  which  is  in  the  northwest section of —
Osborn  in  1970  had  397  students,  14.9  percent  of  whom wer
black.  That  could  have  been  transferred  into Pershing.

;

Il a  265

-Q.  Now,  that’s  immediately  on  the  eastern  boundary  of

Pershing?

‘

A.  On the eastern  boundary,  yes.

’-Q.  That  would  be a contiguous  zone?

A.  It  would.  Another  possibility  would  have  been  to  add  a
portion  of Pierce  Elementary,  which  is immediately  south  of that,
to Pershing,  depending  on  the  need  for capacity  balance.  This  sort
of moving  would  have  favorably  affected  integration  and  also
could  have  operated a little  more  efficiently  in  [1596]  regard  to
overcapacity.

THE  COURT:  Your  objections  are  overruled.  “The  over-
whelming  and  incontrovertible  inference  to  be drawn  from  these
data is that  placement  of teachers  by Detroit  Board  of Education
follows  in general  with  some  departures  a definite  racial  pattern
which  is illustrated  in the  graphs showing  [1612]  percentages of
Negro  pupils  and  Negro  teachers  by districts.  See  appendix,  II-X.
Where  the  schools  are  mixed,  Negro  teachers  are  sent  to  these
areas.  Where  the  student  membership  is all  white,  Negro  teachers
are rarely  sent.  A second  fact  which  is very  clearly  established  is
that Negro  teachers,  while  on a stated  basis of assignment  close  to
home,  are  actually  assigned  instead  on  the basis  of the racial  com-
position  of the  school.  For  example,  in  the  Jackson  Junior  High
School,  which  is  in  a  predominantly  white  neighborhood,  the
student  membership  is  mixed  by  bussing  from  an  overcrowded
area,  and  five  Negroes  have  been  placed  on  the  staff.  Data  also
shows  that  Negro  administrators  are  placed  only  where  Negro
children  and  Negro  teachers  are  in the  majority.  There  is but one
exception  to  this,  the  placement  of  a special  education  assistant
pancipal in a  school where there are some Negro children in
attendance.”

portation  to  Relieve  Overcrowding.”  It  previously  has  been

Q. Doctor,  what  is  your  understanding  of  the  term  “intact

bussing””?

A,  My understanding  of the  term  “intact  bussing,”’  is that  in
the late  50's  and  early  °60’s  children  in  the  Detroit  system  were
bussed —

MR.  BUSHNELL:  If  the  Court  please,  that  answer  is  not

responsive.  The question  was  the definition  of intact  bussing.

MR.  LUCAS: I asked  him first of all if he made an  analysis  of

MR.  BUSHNELL:  If it is responsive,  then  I move  to strike  the

MR.  LUCAS:  Strike  the question.
Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas,  continuing)  Will  you  go  into  the  Detroit

system, Doctor,  on  transportation.

A.  Answering  it generally,  counsellor,  my  answer  would  be
that the  intact  bussing  is  the  practice  of transporting  classrooms
[1623]  of children  intact  from  one  school  to another  and leaving
them intact when  they are  educated  at the receiving  school.

Q. Doctor,  when  such  transportation  occurs  from  a  school
which is 90 percent  or  more black to a  school  which is
tly a white  school,  what  effect,  if any,  does  this  have
in terms of tacial segregation  on  those children?

A.  This would  lead  to  what  we  call  classroom  segregation  or
segregation  within a particular  school.  It  could be sometimes  re-
“gregation,  but  essentially  it  is  a  segregated  situation  within  a
school which could  be segregated  or not segregated  generally.

Il a 280

=

Q.  Doctor,  in  your  experience  with  school  segregation  ang

school  desegregation  plans,  is this  a technique  which  you  have had
to deal  with in  the past?

A.  On occasion,  yes,  sir.

Q.  Doctor,  did  you  examine  data  or  relevant  information
with  respect  to  the  transportation  practices  in  the  Detroit  school
system  in connection  with  this  type  of bussing,  intact  bussing?

A.  Yes, sir.

Q.  What  did your  examination  reveal,  Doctor?

A.  It is my  understanding  from  the  data  that  there  was  intact

bussing generally  in the  late  *50’s,  as  I said,  and  early  °60’s.

Q.  How did  that  intact  transportation  operate,  Doctor?

[1624]  A.  It involved  transporting  classrooms  in whole from
one  school  to  another  receiving  school  and  at the receiving  school
the classrooms  were  kept intact  for instructional  purposes.

Q.  Was  this  policy  changed  at any  time,  Doctor,  as far as you

know?

A.  It is my  understanding  it was  changed  in  the  middle  ‘60's

but  I don’t  remember  the exact  date.

Q.  What  would  the  change  be,  Doctor?  What  type of bussing

would  result  in terms  of relieving  overcrowding?

A.  A  change  from  intact  bussing  would  imply  that  children

were  collected—

Q.  Excuse  me.  Let  me  make  myself  a  little  clearer.  What

happens  when  you  change  from  intact  bussing?

A.  I think  I understand  the question.

Q.  All right.

A.  You  simply  gather  children  up on  a geographical  basis and
transport  them  and  assign  them  at random  to whatever  grade they
are  in  the receiving  school  rather  than  keeping  them  in an  infact

Q.  Doctor,  what  do  you  understand  by  the  term  “one-way

bussing”  in general?

oh

ek
as

lla  281

A. One-way  bussing  is  to  be  defined  as the bussing  of
students of one  race  only;  for  example,  if you  bus  black  children
out of an inner-city  situation  to  white  suburban  schools,  but  you
[1625]  don’t  bus  white  children  the other direction  back  into  the
inner city, for vice-versa,  this  would  be called  one-way  bussing.

Q.  Doctor,  in  your  examination  of  the  transportation  to
lieve  overcrowding  of the  Detroit  School  System,  were  you  able
to form  an  opinion  as  to  the  transportation  practice  with  respect
to the so-called  one-way  bussing?

A.  Yes. I went through  these pages which run from  1960  to
1970 and I think  it includes  all  of those  years  with  the exception
pethaps  of  “62  or  ‘61-62  for  which  there  were  no  records,  and
during this  period  | could  find  only  one  instance  of students  from
a majority  white  school  being  bussed  to  a  majority  black  school
and this  instance  was  the  transfer  that  we  have  spoken  of earlier  in
our  testimony  from  Jefferson  Junior  High  School  which  is a  pre-
dominantly  white  school  to Hutchins  Junior  High School.

Q.  Hutchins  was  white?
A.  Predominantly  black.  In  this  case  the  children  who  were
transported  were  from  the  Jeffries  Housing  Project  and  were,  if
not all black,  very  close  to it.

Q. Would  that  be  an  example  of  transportation  of  black

children  to a white  school,  Doctor?

A.  This  would  be  an  example  of  transportation  of  black

children

to a black  school.

{1626}  Q.  Even  though  the  zone  was  a predominantly  white

zone?

A.  That's  correct.
Q  Doctor,  what,  in your  opinion,  is the effect  of a policy  of
one-way  transportation,  that  is  to  say,  blacks  to  white  schools
instead of both  directions?

A.  In my opinion  the effect  of this is deleterious  to the  whole
process  of desegregation  and  in  all  districts  in which  | worked  I
think  I could  say  unequivocally  it has reached  the  point  where  it is
to longer  followed  if for no other  reason  than  the  blacks  ref, use  to
80 along  with  it.  They  simply  won't  go  along  with  it any  longer
and they insist  on  two-way  bussing.

percent  black.

-have  been  in  the  western  portion  of the  Mackenzie  district  her

Q.:  Doctor  Foster,  what  do you  understand  by an  open x

policy?

A.  An  open  school  policy  is synonymous  with  a eiealagl
choice  policy  or  an  open  transfer  policy.  It  is  usually  “—

II a  289

ding  capacity,  so  that  a  school  system  which  has  available
spaces in a given  number  of schools  will  transfer  children  usually
on a first-come,  first-service  basis  to  those  schools  if  they  so
request, and  usually  it  is done  at  the  student’s  [1645]  expense,
transportation  wise.
_-Q.  Doctor,  you  have  examined  the  pupil  transfer  forms  con-
tained  in  Plaintiffs  Exhibit  82,  and  have  you  examined  the

eats

~  A.  I think SO,  yes.

[1647]...

A.  My  understanding  of  the  data  was  that  from  approx-
imately  1961  to  1966  the  Detroit  system  had  an  open  school
policy  in which  I believe  in  the  spring  of  the  year  schools  were
listed and  published  in  which  space  would  be available  for the  fall
and that  this  policy  was  discontinued  in  1966—I  beg your  pardon.
It was  not discontinued,  but  it was  altered  to include  the  corollary
that the transfer  of such  children  would  have  to improve  the inte-
grated status  of the school.

Q. Would  that  be the sending  or receiving  school?
A.  The receiving  school  is my  understanding.
Q.  Doctor,  from  your  examination  of the  transfer  form  appli-
cations  contained  in Plaintiffs’  Exhibit  82,  can  you  describe  what
change,  if any,  took  place  from  ’61  up  until  the  present  time  with
respect to those  transfer  forms?
MR.  BUSHNELL:  The exhibit  speaks  for  [1648]  itself.  There
has been prior testimony  as  to this.  This  is cumulative.

THE COURT:  Yes, but  I just as soon  he summarizes  it for me.

A.  I will  need  to  refer  to  my  notes,  your  Honor,  because

there have been  a few changes  and  it is a little  complicated.

In going  through  Plaintiffs’  Exhibit  82,  to  the  best  of  my
ability,  the  first  arrangement  was  a Form  32 in  1962  to ’66 which

Il a 290

had  no  criterion  for  integration  and the  form  did  not  ask for race.
Then  in  the  change  I referred  to just  earlier  starting  in  1966, the
Form  32  was  changed  to  ask  for  the  race  of the  student  and the
form  states  that  transfer  will  contribute  to  the  integration  of the
receiving  school.

This  was,  I think,  one  of several  criteria  that  was  listed  —  that
were  listed.  Then  there  was  a switch  in  1970  in transfer  policy and
a new  form  known  as  Form  36 in which  there  was  no  blank space
for  school  preference  and  there  was  no  mention  of race.  The fom
was  not  titled  “Transfer  Request,”  but  “Pupil  Adjustment  Appli-
cation,”and  I believe  that  in  January  of  1971  there  was  a new
form,  Form  1061,  which  is in another  exhibit.

Q.  I will  show  you  Plaintiffs’  Exhibit  83, Doctor,  and ask you

if that  is the  new  form  to which  you  refer?  |

[1649]  A.  Yes.  Form  1061]  dated  January  °71.  Again it is

called  a  “High  School  Pupil  Transfer  Request.”  It lists  the school
to  which  the  pupil desires  transfer,  the  receiving  school,  it calls for
the  race  of the  student  and  the  fact  that  the  transfer  will  contr
bute  to  integration,  which  obviously  is a  return  to  the  old Form
32 style.

A.  “District  boundaries  prior  to  1956  were  established  by the
staff officer in charge of the level  involved  in consultation  with  the
district  principal  of the  elementary  level  and  with  the principal  of
the  school  in  cases  involving  secondary  areas.  In  determining
school boundaries  the principal  factor  used  was  the capacity  of the
school  and the area  surrounding  the school  that  the capacity  could
serve.  Pointe  C. School  boundaries  for the Winterhalter,  Hutchins,
Durfee  and Mackenzie  Schools  were  determined  strictly  within  the
lines  of the  policies  and  philosophy  of  the  Board  of  Education
mentioned  previously.  The  changes  were  recommended  by  the
School  Housing  Commission  subject  to  the  Superintendent’s
approval.  Boundaries  for  the  Higginbotham  and  MacDowell
Schools  were  established  at  the  time  the schools  were  constructed

Il a 292

by  selecting  an  area  which  the  capacity  of  the  school  cou
[1667]  house.  Historically  speaking,  when  the  Higginbotham
School  was  opened  it was  located  in an  area  which  had  much  room
with  a  temporary  war  housing  project  being recently  constructed,
With  the  removal  of  the  temporary  war  housing  project  and the
resulting  decline  in  the enrollment,  the boundaries  were  still maip-
tained.  It is understood  that  the  boundaries  of the  Higginbotham
School  have  never  been  changed.  and  that  resistance  to an adjust.
ment  in  the  MacDowell-Higginbothem  boundaries  results  from
social  barrier  established  within  an  ethnic  group.  Point  D. At the
present  time  there  are  no  optional  areas  at  the  elementary  level,
There  are  two  optional  areas  in  the  west  district,  five  optional
areas  in  the  southwest  district,  one  each  in  the east  and  southeast
districts,  four  optional  areas  in  the  northwest  district,  eight
optional  areas  in  the  center  district.  All  these  areas  mentioned are
at  the  secondary  level.  The  optional  areas  are  determined  by the
School  Housing  Commission  subject  to  the  superintendent’s
approval  and  according  to  the  philosophy  and  policies  established
by the Board  of Education.”

Q. Thank  you,  Doctor.
Doctor  Foster,  from  your  examination  of the pattern  of con-
struction  in  this  school  system,  1960  to  1970,  do  you  have  an
opinion  as  to the effect  of that  pattern  of construction  on segrega-
tion in the Detroit  School  System?

A.  My opinion  is that construction  practices  were  followed  in
such  a way  as  to  increase  Segregation.  I say  this  because  of the

Il a 306

large  number  of schools  that  were  opened  that  were  [1697]  either
all  black  or  all  white  or  with  a a  number  of one
race  or the other  upon  opening.

Q.  Doctor,  what  is  the  average  life  expectancy  for  a school

building,  if I may  use  that  term?

A.  If  I could  state  a  range  I would  say  forty  to  fifty years,
generally.  At  least  in  the  past  it hasn’t  been  so  much a problem of
the  building  as  the  utilization  of the  building  and  the  space  in it
which  deteriorates  sometimes  faster  than  the building.

Q.  Are  there  any  nationally  recognized  standards  of school
construction  in  terms  of age  expectancy,  utilization  expectancy  of
buildings?

A.  I believe  as  a  rule  of thumb  that  is the  national  standard.

Q.  Doctor,  I take  it  many  school  districts  are  forced touse

school  buildings  longer  than  that,  is that  correct?

A.  Yes,  sir.

Q.  Doctor,  in  your  opinion  does  the  placement  of a school
have  a  long  term  effect  on  the  attendance  patterns  in a school
district?

MR.  BUSHNELL:  The  replacement?

MR.  LUCAS:  The  placement.

MR.  BUSHNELL:  I don’t  understand  you.

MR.  LUCAS:  Location.

A.  Would  you  repeat  the question?

[1698]  Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  Does  the  location  of a school ina

particular  place have  a long term  effect  on  a school  system?

A.  In  terms  of the  nature  of the  pupils  assigned  to the school,

do you  mean?

Q.  Yes, sir.

A.  Yes,  it does.
Q.  Are  there  alternatives  in schoolhouse  construction  whic
can  or  should  be considered  by a school  district in  terms  of aca
ing the racial  composition  of student  bodies?

II a  307

A.  In terms  of site selection  there  are,  yes.
Q.  What  are  some  of the  alternatives  which  can  or should  be

utilized,  in your opinion,  Doctor?

A.  It is customary  in  this  day  and  age  to  consider  the  prob-
lem of integration  or  segregation  very  carefully  in selecting  sites
for school  buildings  and,  well,  this  was  pointed  out,  I believe,  in
the bit I read  from  the Michigan  State  Department.

Q.  What  effect  in terms  of perception  of the community  does
it have  when  a school  is opened  with  an  overwhelming  enrollment
of one race  or the other?

A.  Generally  the  community  perceives,  in  my  opinion,  that
the school  has  been  thought  of as  being,  going  to  be  an  all  white
school  or  all  black  school  and  in  either  case  generally  that  it is
racially  isolated.

[1699]  Q.

Doctor  Foster,  did  you  examine  the  current
attendance  patterns  and  boundary  lines  in  the  City  of Detroit  to
determine  what  some  of the  techniques  could  be  used  to desegre-
gate the existing  pattern  of school  assignment?

A.  Yes, I did.
MR.  BUSHNELL:  I would  object.  That  assumes a fact  not  in

evidence.

THE COURT:  You  may  answer.
Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  Would  you  like  to  step  to  the  map,  Doc-

tor, and give us some  of those  examples?

A.  I considered  rather  briefly  the possibility  of some  desegre-

gation techniques  and —

MR.  YOUNG:  I would  object  at  this  time.  This  testimony
clearly goes  to remedies  as  opposed  to  the  right to relief.  It is not
relevant at this time.

;
THE COURT:  No, I think  not.  You  may  answer.
A.  In looking  at any  school  system  the sort  of what  we might
call soft spots  in terms  of desegregation  would  be  in border  areas
where  you  have  white  and  black  population  bridging  up  against
each  other.  So,  merely  as  an  attempt  to  be  illustrative  of where
there  are  segregated  practices  now  because  of boundary  lines,  and
Where  these  could  be  changed  a  bit  to  improve  the  situation,  I

Il  a 308

simply  made  a quick  swing  around  the  [1700]  Detroit  system, ang
I have  seven  or  eight  examples  I think  I can  recite  rather Quickly.

First  of  all,  in  the  northwest  section  the  elementary  zone of

Bow  and  Vandenberg  abut  each  other.  They  are  both  K-6 schook,
They  are  both  in  the  Ford  High  School  attendance  zone.  Bows
806  white,  177  black.  Vandenberg  is 27  white,  391  black.  Bows
17.8  per  cent  black,  and  Vandenberg  is 93.5  per cent  black.  Inmy
opinion  these  schools  could either  be paired  or the zone lings
could  be  redrawn  in such  a way as  to  effect  a racial  balance  which
would  come  out  at  about  40  per  cent,  or  approach  40  per cent,
however  you  did  it,  black  for  the  two  schools,  instead  of th
disparity  which  now  exists  from  17.8  per  cent  to  95.3 per cent.
Going  south,  the  Crary  Elementary  School,  a  K  through 8A, isin
the  Cooley  zone,  and  the  J.  R.  King  School,  also  in  the Cooley
zone,  is a  K through  8B.  These  two schools are both  in the Cooley
zone.  Crary  is  20.6  per  cent  black,  and  King is 91.8  per cent  black.
These  two  schools,  in  conjunction  with  the  Cerveny-Stratfon
area,  could  be redrawn  in  their  zone  lines,  or  some  grouping  com
bination  or  non-contiguous  zone  pairing  between  Crary  and King-

Q.  Doctor,  just  very  briefly  what  do  you  mean  by nor

contiguous  zone  pairing?

A.  Affecting  desegregation  between  two  schools  which  ar
not  [1701]  contiguous  in  such  a  way  as  the  students  would cros
over  another  area  to get to  their  destination.

Q.  Is this  a  technique  used  frequently  in desegregation  plans?

A.  Very  frequently  these  days,  yes.  Going  further  south, the
Burns  Elementary  School,  which  is 59.8  per  cent  black,  the Dossin
Elementary  School,  which  is  5  per  cent  black,  and  the  Edison
School,  which  is 2.7 per cent  black could  be grouped  or  zone lines
redrawn.  Burns  is a  K-6  school  as  it now  stands.  Dossin  is a K-7A.
Edison  is  a  K-8B.  The  grades  past the  6th  grade  could  be ince
orporated  with  Cadillac,  which  is  in  with  Burns  area.  Cadillac
would  be  made  into  a  junior  high  situation.  These  three  schoo
could  be  grouped  in  such  a  way  as  to  effect  an  approximationol
racial  balance.

Q.  Doctor,  with  respect  to that  example  and  perhaps  some  of

of the school  year. I have two other
examples.  As  we  swing  around  into  Finney  we  have  two  K-7-A
schools,  the  Hamilton  School,  which  is a  K  -  7-A,  70.8  percent
black,  and  the  Stellwagen  School,  which is also a K through 7-A
school  and  was  originally  in  the  Jackson  attendance  area, isi
with  no blacks.  It  would  be possible  to  pair these tw
school
schools,  or  perhaps to rezone  them  in such  a  way as  to effects
racial  balance  approaching
40 percent  black.  I beg your pardot,
sir.  I misread  my  notes,  I believe.  Hamilton  at Stellwagen are now
both K-8 schools, I believe,  and my suggestion in making these K7
a’ a

Ila  311

was  to  move  the  eighth  grade  to  Jackson,  which  is just  below
them.  The  final  example  would  involve  two  K-6 schools,  Macomb
Elementary  School,  which  is  in  the  southern  end  of Denby,  and
the Hutchinson  Elementary  School,  which  is in the western  end  of
Finney,  and  a third  school,  the  Goodale  School,  which is not a K
through  9 school.  If the junior high portion  of the Goodale  School
were  transferred  to  Arthur  and  Marquette,  which  are  junior  high
schools  in the  general  vicinity,  Arthur  being here,  and  Marguette  —
I can’t find it.

MR.  BUSHNELL: I think  it is to the  right down  there.
[1706]  A.  Here  is Marquette.  I’m  sorry.  This  would  relieve
three  K-6  schools  which  could  be  grouped  at  the  present  time.
Hutchinson  is  now  97.3  percent  black.  Macomb  is  2.8  percent
black.  Immediately  adjacent  to it and  north,  Goodale  is .7 percent
black.  If these  three  schools  were  grouped,  a  balance  of approx-
imately  45  percent  black  would  be  effectuated.  Again  I think I
should  point  out,  counsellor,  that  these  are  merely  illustrative
situations  which  exist  now  which  could  be  acted  upon  in  the
future or could  have  been  acted  upon  this past year.
