[1885]  W. DEAN  EDMUNDSON

was  called  as  a  witness  by  Plaintiffs,  and  being  first  duly  swom
testified  as  follows:

KE

[1856]  DIRECT  EXAMINATION  BY  MR.  LUCAS:

[1863]  Q.  Directing  your  attention  on  the  map  to  Jackson

Junior  High,  what  grades  does  it serve  at the  present  time?

eex

A.  Seven  through  9.

Q.  Has  there  been  any  impaction  in  that  area  in  terms  of

growth of student  population?~.

A.  Yes,  there  has.

t

Q.  What  does  that  result  from?

A.  There  has  been  an  increase,  particularly  in this  area  and  in
this area—this  is the  Carstens  area—which  has  resulted  primarily  or
which  was  caused  primarily  by the  increase  at Jackson.

Q.  In  your  deposition  last  November  you  indicated  the  pri-

mary  increase  came  from  the  Parkside  Homes  housing  project.

MR.  BUSHNELL:  The  page?

MR.  LUCAS:  Page  8.

MR.  BUSHNELL:  All right.

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  What  was  the  reason  for  this  iffux, and
you  said  primarily  the  Parkside  housing  project.  Can  you  locate  it
on  the map?

A.  Yes,  it is right  in here.

[1864]  Q.  Is  that  a  federal  housing  project  as  far  as  you

know?

A.  It is  a housing  project.

Q.  Operated  by  the  Housing  Authority  of  the  City  of  De

troit?

A.  Yes.

Q.  How  long  has  that  project  been  there?

A.  I don’t  know.  It was  there  when  I came.

lila 2

Q.  Has  there  been  a  change  in  its  characteristics  since  you

have  been  there?

A.  I couldn’t  tell you  that.

Q.  Has  it changed  from  primarily  families  without  children

and  the elderly  to families  with  children?

A.  I have  heard  so, but this is hearsay.  I don’t  know  of  my

own  personal  experience.

Q.  Have  you  had  an  influx  of children  from that project?

A.  We  have  had  some.  It  has  fluctuated,  depending  on  the
placements  made  by the  Housing  Commission,  the families  in that
area.

Q.  Is there  a  proposal  to  build  a  new school  on the  grounds

of that  housing  project?

A.  Not  on  the grounds of the housing  project.  There is no

room  in the housing  project  grounds  to build  a school.

Q.  Is there  room  on  the grounds  immediately  adjacent  to the

area?

A.  It depends  on  who  you  talk  to.

-

to this
Q.  Has the School  Board  asked  for some  land  adjacent

{1865]  housing  project?

A.  We have  done  so.

Q.  When  did  that  take  place?

A.  Well,  we  asked  for it first,  I think,  two  years  ago.

Q.  When  was  the  most  recent  request?

A.  Probably  this morning.

before  the Park  Commission
Q.  And  was there  an appearance

or similar  agency,  a hearing  with  respect  to that  property?

A.  There  has  been  at  least  two  appearances  before  the  Park

Commission.

Q.  When  was  the  most  recent  one?

A.  Do you  want  the exact  date?

Q. Well,  was  it within  the  last  couple  of weeks?

A.  That’s  correct.

Ill a3

Q.  And  what  kind  of  school  was  proposed  to  be  built  adja-

cent to that  project?

A.  Initially  we  asked  for a primary  school,  K-3.
Q.  K-3?
A.  That’s  right,  and  the  one  reason  for  that  was  that  it is a
mile and  six-tenths  from  the  furthest  point  in the  Parkside  project
for kindergarten  youngsters  and  primary  youngsters  to attend  the
Hamilton  School  which  serves  their  area.

Q.  Are there  any  children  in the area  you just pointed  to?
A.  Yes.  The  project  goes  up  —  well,  this  map  doesn’t  show  it

[1866]  exactly.  It goes  up to about  here.

Q.  They are on up  around  the interchange  where you are

pointing?

A.  No,  I didn’t  point  there.  I pointed  right  here,  I believe.
Q.  So,  in  this  general  area are you  presently  bussing  children

within  that  school  zone?

A. We  are bussing every child from the Parkside  project  to

the Hamilton  School.

a

Q.  What is the reason for their  transportation?
A.  The  reason  for  that  transportation  is that  coming  out  of.
that project  and getting  to  and from school  there is one route
available  to  the  kids,  and  we  have  700  youngsters  attending  the
Hamilton  School,  and  when I first  acquired  this  headaches—that
was, I guess, ‘68, September  of  ‘68~—I was assigned  to the area
which  contains  this school,  and we  were having nothing  but  troub-
le getting the kids back and forth to school.  We  have had 700 kids
channeled down one street,  and I don’t care what color the kids
are or what color the property  owners are, you aren't going  to
make this without trouble,  and we have had it. Going back to  1966
we have had incidents,  and if you will check the police  records:
you  will  find  that  guns  and  dogs  were  brought  into  play  against
the  children  and  property  owners.  We  have  had  scooter  patrols,
and after I got charged  with this area at  least one patrol  car on
duty morning  and night. Our administrators  would escort the kids
halfway
home,  and we [1867]  still  had problems. We asked  for the
Primary  unit  to  reduce  the  number  of youngsters  that  would  have

:

aS

Illa4

to  run  the  gauntlet.  The  Parks  and  Recreation  Commission  denied
the  request  on  the  grounds  that  they  were  short  of park  area.  We
have  attempted  to  find  some  other  solution  to  this  problem.

«
One  of  the  proposals  was  bussing  a  portion  of  these  young
sters  to one  of the schools  I mentioned,  and  that  was  flatly  refused
by the  residents  of Parkside.  Well,  this  is rough  history.

Finally,  to  reduce  the  number  of youngsters  that  would  have
to  run  the  gauntlet,  we  have  agreed  to  bus  the  primary  grades
while  we  were  trying  to  find  some  permanent  solution  to  this
problem.  The  Parkside  people  themselves  negotiated  with  the DSR
to  double  that  run,  because  it only  took  five  minutes,  and  at no
additional  cost  the  DSR  then  transported  the  remainder  of the
youngsters.

Q.  You  say  cost?

A.  To the  Board  of Education  or  anybody  else.
Q.  The  Board  is paying  for that  transportation?

A.  The  Board  is  paying  for  the  transportation,  but  on  the

basis  of one  run.

eee

eastern  School,  in that  area?

A.  I did,  but  that  was  about  ‘64,  my  last  association  with

Northeastern.

testified  as  follows:

’  “The  reason  we  discontinued  that  was  because  under  that
policy  practically  every  white  kid  in Northeastern  got out.”

“Where  did  they  go to?
“Answer:  Well,  at  that  time  our  high  schools  weren't  full,

“Question:  Was  there  any  school  that  most  of  them  ended

up in?

Illa 5

Q.  I thought  we  were  misunderstanding  each  other.

A.  That’s  right.

at all possible.

A.  Refuses  a  Region  8 junior  high  school  placement.  Feels
[1888]  harassment  was  racial  in  nature.  Butzel  States  that  his

A.  Right.  I would  like  to,  if I may,  proceed.  The  procedure
that  was  adopted  included  a  procedure  that  in  a  case  such  as  this
where  a youngster  from  another  region  was  requesting  a  transfer.
that  the  home  region  examine  the  case  and  make  certain  that
every  possible  effort  had  been  made  to achieve  an  adjustment  and
make  a  recommendation  before  we  could  act  on  it.  The  earlier
ones  were  not  processed  in this  manner.

Q.  By earlier  ones,  you  mean  the  previous  year?

‘

A.  No,  the  ones  that  we  already  referred  to in this testimony,

[2042]  JOHN  W.  PORTER
was  called  as  a  witness  on  behalf  of Plaintiffs  and, being first

duly  sworn,  testified  as  follows:  :

Q.  What  is your  occupation,  Dr.  Porter?

A.  1 am  State  Superintendent  of Public  Instructions  in Mich-

igan.

Q.  How  long have  you  held  that  position,  sir?

A.  Since  October,  1969.

Q.  What  position  did  you  hold  prior  to  that  time?

A.  Associate  Superintendent  in the  Department  of Education

for Higher  Education.

Q.  Is your  position  elective  or appointed?
A.  Appointed  by  the  State  Board  of  Education,  which  is

elected.

Q.  What  are  your  duties?

A.  They  are  twofold:  I am  chief  executive  officer  of the De

partment  of Education,  which  is one  of the  19  state  departments
of government  responsible  for supervision  of elementary  and secor-
dary  education,  adult  education  and  planning  coordination  of
higher  education  and,  secondly,  I am  Chairman  of the  State  Board
of Education  for policy  in education.

[2043]  Q.  What  is your  personal  educational  background?

A.  My  public  education  was  received  in Fort  Wayne,  Indiana
I had a  Bachelor  of  Arts  Degree  from  Albion  College,  a private
college  in Michigan.  I have  a Political  Science  Master’s  Degree from
Michigan  State  University  and  a  PhD.  in  administration  in higher
education’#1  Michigan  State  University.

Illa  19

Q.  What  is  the  budget  in  terms  of  responsibility  for  local
school  districts  of  the  State  Department  of  Education  for  this
year?

A.  Are  you  referring  to  the  budget  —
MR.  BUSHNELL:  If the  Court  please,  I don’t  understand  the
question  and  I sense  Doctor  Porter  doesn’t,  either.  This  is not  how
education  is financed  in Michigan.
:
A.  Could  you  rephrase  it?
Q  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  What  was  the  amount  of  state  aid  for

public education?

A.  The  amount  of aid  for  1970-71  was  approximately  three-

quarters  of a billion  dollars.

MR.  BUSHNELL:  Billion?

A.  Billion.  That  is state  aid.
Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  This  is  money  given  directly  to  local

school  districts  and  intermediate  school  districts?

A.  That  is money  given  directly  to  the  local  school  districts,  a
majority  of it.  There  is  a lump  sum  grant  for  intermediate  school
districts  separate  from  that  amount.  The  money  to  [2044]  local
school  districts  is  based  upon a state  aid  formula,  the  money  to
intermediate  school  districts  is  based  upon  a different  disburse -
ment  factor.

Q.  Would  you  very  briefly  describe  the  intermediate  school
district  and  its  relationship  first  with  the  State  Board  and  then  in
tum  with  the local  school  districts?

A.  The  intermediate  school  district,  the  intermediary  level  of
education  in  the  state,  basically  it  performs  regional  services  for
the  State  Department  of Education  in  terms  of collecting  enroll-
ment  information,  providing  special  education  services,  collecting
certain  forms  that  are  required  by the  state.  Intermediate  school
boards  are  created  through  local  initiative  based  upon  local  school
district  board  members.

There  is no  direct  responsibility  and  supervision  between  the
state  education  office,  the  intermediate  school  district  and  the
local  school  boards.  In  other  words,  the  State  Department  of
Education  supervises  to  the  extent  that  the  Constitution  calls  for

Ill a 20

that  in  the  local  school  district  operations  and  to  date  has  not
done  this  through  intermediate  school  districts.

Q.  Do  the  intermediate  school  districts  coincide  with  the
county  boundaries  or  do  they  sometimes  extend  across  county
lines?

A.  Intermediate  school  districts  do  not  in  most  instances co
incide  with  county  boundaries.  Several  years  ago,  in  fact  1955,
this  [2045]  was  changed.  Intermediate  school  district  boundaries
now  coincide  with  the  school  district  boundaries  which  in most
instances,  except  for  a  few  cases  they  do  not  follow  county  lines.
We  now  have  59  intermediate  school  districts  and  83  counties,
That  would  give you  some  idea  of the  discrepancy.

Q.  Is  the  Wayne  County  intermediate  district  coterminus
with  Wayne  County  or  does  it extend  across  into  other  counties?

A.  The  boundaries  are  basically  coterminus,  a  couple  of
isolated  instances  whereby  one  area,  one  school  district  which  is
part  of a  community  college  that  is located  in Wayne  County, the
school  district  buttresses  into  Oakland  County.  We  have a couple
of isolated  cases  like  that  but  basically  it follows-the County line
Q. Does  the  State  Department  of Education  have a division
funded  in  part  by  federal  funds  dealing  with  equal  educational
opportunity,  I think  it is sometimes  called  Title  4?

A.  We  do  have  an  Office  of Equal  Educational  Opportunity.

Q.  Is  it  funded  in  part  by Title  4 of the  Civil  Rights  Act of

,

1964?

A.  Seventy-five  per  cent  of the  office  is funded  by Title 4 of

the Civil  Rights  Act  of  1964.

Q.  What  is its function  in the  State  Department?

A.  That  office  is responsible  for identifying,  first  of all, those
school  districts  that  have  imbalance  as  far as  racial  enrollments ar
concerned.  To  date  we  have  identified,  this  [2046]  is not partof
the  public  policy  of  the  State  Board,  but  we  have  identified 38
school  districts  that  are  administratively  imbalanced  in the schools
based  upon  this  factor  that  if  more  than  50  per  cent  of the
students  are  of  a  minority  extraction  that  is  considered  to be
imbalance  and  there  are  38 school  districts  out  of some  617.

Ill a 21

Q.  I think  I know  what  you  mean,  Doctor,  but  for  clarifi-
cation  you  mean  more  than  SO  per  cent  of  the  students  in  a
particular school  or in a district?

re In a school.

Q.  Or entire  district?

A.  In a particular  school.
Q.  What  is  the  responsibility  of  this  division  of  the  State

Department  with  respect  to working  with  local  school  districts?

A.  The  responsibility  of the  office  is to  work  cooperatively.
There  are  no  supervisory  mandates,  to  work  cooperatively  with
these  38 school  districts  to develop  desegregation  plans  so  that  the
racial imbalance  might be improved.

Q.  Are  there  some  levels  of funding  from  the  Federal  Govern-
ment  to  local  school  districts  with  respect  to Title  4 or  is this  level
funding directed  toward  the  intermediate  district?

A.  Any  funding  to  date  from  the  Federal  Government  for
desegregation  purposes  has  been  directly  to  local  school  districts
when  such  school  districts  were  under  court  order  or such  districts
{2047}  were  under  some  mandate  legislatively.

We  have  not  had  any  cases  like  that  in  Michigan.  There  have
been  a number  in  the  southern  states.  We  do, however,  anticipate
if the current  legislation  from  the  Nixon  administration  is passed
that  1.5  billion  dollars  for  desegregation  is passed,  that  Michigan
would  be entitled  to about  40 million  dollars.
Q.  That  is a special  assistance  fund?
_A.  Yes,  and  we  are  in  the  process  now  of developing  some
tentative  guidelines  that  could  be approved  by the  Federal  Govern-
ment  to implement  that  new  statute  if it is enacted.

MR.  BUSHNELL:  Might  I ask  for  the  record  that  Doctor

Porter  identify  what  this  is that  he is reading.

MR.  KRASICKY:  It’s four  paragraphs  long.
A.  The  statement  that  I have  been  requested  to read  by coun-
sel, the  statement  that  the  State  Board  of Education  adopted  May
13,  1970,  following  several  meetings  with  the  Detroit  Board  of
Education,  their  counsel,  regarding  the  plan  of the  Detroit  Public
Schools  to provide  for regional  boards  of education.  The  statement
is based  upon  a  previous  statement  by  the  State  Board  of Educa-
tion  and  the  Michigan  Civil  Rights  Commission  which  was  adopted
in 1966.  The  statement  reads  as  follows:

“The  State  Board  of Education  on  April  23,  1966,  adopted  a
joint  policy  statement  with  the  Michigan  Civil  Rights Commission
which  directed  local  school  boards  to  consider  the  factor  of the
racial  balance  and  along  with  other  educational  considerations  in
making  decisions  about  selection  of new  school  sites, expansion  of
present  facility,  reorganization  of school  attendance  districts  and
the transfer  of pupils  from  overcrowded  facilities.

“Each  of  these  situations  according  to  the  policy  statement
presents  an  opportunity  for  integration.  The  [2050]  recent
decisions  of the  school  districts  to  address  themselves  to  the  issue
of racial  isolation  in schools  is encouraging.

“An  effort  to  provide  equality  of  educational  opportunity,
the  Detroit  Board  of Education  has  taken  an  initial  step  to address
itself  to  the  many-faceted  problems  of racial  segregation  and  the
barriers  which  it creates.

“The  Detroit  plan  with  its  strength  and  weaknesses  is  an
effort  to  solve  the  problem.  The  path  to  quality  educational
opportunity  is  neither  smooth  nor  precisely  marked  and  merely
assigning  students  to  different  schools  in  an  effort  to  bring about
racial  balance  in  those  schools  will  be a meaningless  gesture  unless
immediate  steps  are  taken  to  provide  additional  services  in
improved  programs  to. insure  a  balance  quality  educational  attain-
ment  as well  as a balanced  racial  composition.

“Unless  this  factor  is  given  top  priority  school  districts
addressing  themselves  to  racial  integration  simply  by  providing
racial  balance  can  probably  expect  increased  rather  than decreased
-  tensions.

——

Ill a  24

“Consequently  the  Detroit  Board  of  Education  will  not  auto
matically  make  the  Detroit  School  System  better;  however,  with
the  support  of students,  teachers  and  parents  and  with  the recog.
nition  that  the  students,  teachers  and  parents,  and  with  the  recog.
nition  that  the  decision  hopefully  [2051]  represents  a step  toward
quality  education  for  all  students,  such  decisions  can  be consider-
ed  fundamental.  Actions  taken  by  Michigan  school  districts  to
reduce  the  harmful  educational  effects  of racial  isolation  are  cop-
sistent  with  the  equal  educational  opportunity  position  of the
State  Board  of Education.

“Once  such  actions  are  taken  by duly  constituted  authorities
public  support  is vital  to  their  success.  The  State  Board  of Educa
tion  urges  public  support  of conscious  and  constructive  efforts  to
achieve  racial  integration  in the  schools.”

Q.  Is the  date  on  there,  Doctor?

A.  May  13,  1970.

Q.  And  that  was  directed  to  an  Arpil  7th  plan  and  it is now

adopted  by the  Board?

A.  That  was  directed  to  the  principal  features  of  the  April

7th  plan as  adopted  by the  Detroit  Board.

Q.  Doctor,  the  statement  makes  reference  to  a  1966  policy
statement  directing  local  Boards  of Education  to consider  the fact
of  racial  balance  along  with  other  educational  considerations  in
making  decisions  about  selections  of  new  school  sites,  is  that
correct?

A.  That’s  correct.

Q.  And  expansion  of the  present  existing  facilities?

A.  I’m  not  certain.

Q.  Well,  let  me  just  give  this  to  you  and  let  you  make  refer

ence  to it.  I think  it is the  first  paragraph.

A.  Yes,  not  the  expansion.  That  term  refers  to when a school
district  intends  to  provide  for  additional  students,  whether  or not
they  should  add  to  the  existing  building  which  might  cause  isola
tion  and/or  whether  they  ought  to  consider  constructing  another
building.

Q.  In another  location?

Ill a  25

A.  In another  location,  right.

Q.  Now,  it  also  says  reorganization  of  school  attendance
[2053]  district.  Are  you  talking  within  a particular  school  district,
Doctor,  or  are  you  talking  about  between  two  separate  school
districts?

;

attendance  districts,  and  this  refers  to attendance  within a district.

A.  That’s  right.

Q.  It also  deals  with  the  transfer  of pupils  from  overcrowded

facilities?

A.  Yes.

are  familiar  with  Act  244  and  Act  48 of the  State  Legislature.

A.  Yes.

Q.  What  is the  responsibility  of the  State  Board  of Education
witi  respect  to  drawing  attendance  or  regional  boundaries  for the
Detroit  Board  of Education  under  Act  48?

A.  The  State  Board  of Education  had  no  direct  involvement

in the drawing  of boundaries.

Q.  Does  it  have  responsibility  in  the  future  with  respect  to

Aci  48?

A.  It  does  have,  since  the  Act  indicates  that  after  each
decennial  census  boundary  lines  for  any  school  district  that  has
100,000  population  must  be redrawn,  and  if the  constituent  repre-
sentatives  of the  regional  boards  are  not  able  to  agree  upon  some
boundaries  that  are  consistent  with  proportionate  populations,  the
Stae  Board  of  Education  may  draw  such  boundaries.  | assume
therefore  that  the  State  Board  would  have  no  direct  involvement
in  ‘his  until  the  1980  decennial  census,  odd  year,  which  would be
1931.

Ill a  27

Q.  Do  you  know  whether  the Commission  that  drew  the  pre-
sent  [2056]  boundaries  had  the  1970  census  figures  in  Detroit
available  to  them  at  that  time,  Doctor?

A. I  can’t  say,  because  there  are  two  different  Acts,  Mr.
Lucas.  The  first  Act  required  the  Detroit  Board  of Education  to
undertake  an  exhaustive  plan  of regionalization,  and  then,  as  the
Court  knows,  the  law  was  amended  in  1970  and a  three-member
commission  was  to  be  appointed  if the  legislature  could  not  draw
boundaries  that  were  approved  by  the  Superintendent  of Public
Instruction.  The  legislature  did  not  act.  I was  not  able  to  act.  A
three-member  commission  was  appointed  by  the  Governor  and  it
did proceed  to carry  out  this  mission.

population  which  was  sometime  in  April  1971,  and  this  body
acted in  1970.

but not  all epople.

fact.

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  The  quote  -from  James  B.  Conant,  “I
believe  it accurate  to state  that  a high  school  accommodating  all of

tunity  is  an  accurate  reflection  of the  role  of the  comprehensive
high school.

“While  this  study  was  not  designed  to  examine  individual

interesting  to  look  at  some  selected  background  characteris.
tics in achievement.  As Table  2-14  shows,  there  exists  in Mich-
igan  a strong  relationship  between  race  and  achievement.  The
latter  measured  in  this  table  is in  terms  of non-promotion  or
retention  at  a given grade  level.  The  higher  their  percentage of
Negroes  in a school,  the  greater  is the  percentage  of students
either  failing  one  or  more  subjects  or  being  retained  at that
grade  level  for  another  year.  Tables  2-15  and  2-16  further
demonstrate  this  relationship.  Looking  at sixth  grade  achieve
ment  levels  in  Detroit,  Grand  Rapids  and  Flint,  the higher the
percentage  of Negroes  in schools,  the  greater  the  probability
that  the  school  will  be  below  the  city  achievement  average.”

[2065]  Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  Dr.  Porter,  does  the State  pay  the
ortation  in the  State  of Michigan?
pays  roughly  75  percent  of  the  cost.  Last  year

cost of pupil  t
A.  The  State

@  appropriation  was  $29  million.

Q.  Do  you  know  what  percent  of the  school  children  in  the
State  of Michigan  are  transported  to  the  school  at public  expense?

A.  Yes,  I do.  40  percent  of all students  in Michigan  are  trans-

ported. That  equates  out  to about  833,000  students  last  year.

Q.  Dr.  Porter,  is  there  some  formula  in  terms  of  distance
which  makes  a  child  eligible  for  transportation  that  would  be
aided by the state?

A.  Yes.  It  is  a  very  complicated  formula  that  197 computa-

tions,  and we  are  in the  process  right  now  of reducing  this—

Q.  (Interposing)  You  mean  the  financial  formula  is compli-

cated.  But,  in terms  of distance  from  his home  to  the  school—

A.  A  mile  and a half  outside  the  city  limits.  Until  this  year
the legislature  amended  the  Act  based  upon  the  recommendations
of the State  Board  of Education  to allow  for in-city  transportation
which  we  had  never  had  before.  The  legislature  did  not  approp-
tiate  funds  for  that.  So,  now  the  funds  that  are  now  used  are
basicallyfor  rural  areas  and  suburban  [2066]  areas  where  the
students  live  a mile  and a half from  the  school.

Q.  When  you  say  “city,”  is  there  some  limitation?  For
instance,  would  Grosse  Pointe,  Harper  Woods,  areas  like  that  that
surround  the  City  of Detroit,  are  they  eligible  for transportation?

A.  In  the  in-city.  But,  if  the  students  come  across  the  city
boundary  lines  they  live  more  than  a  mile  and  a  half,  which  is
quite  prevalent  throughout  the state,  then  they  are  eligible  for the
funds.

Q.  Well,I  think  my  question  may  have  been  confusing.  Is
there  some  type  of city—is  it just  anyplace  incorporated  as  a city
that  is differentiated  from  the  rural  areas,  or  certain  cities  elegible
for this state aid at the present  time and  receive  the  funds—

whether  under  the  old  practice  whether  any  city  has  ceased  state

,  aid for transportation  within  the city.

A.  Yes,  we  have  a  number  of instances  where  the  city would

have  a number  of instances  where  [2067]  that  exists.

his ride outside  the  city  limits  transportation  is assisted?

A.  That's  right,  or  where  the  student  lives  in the  city but the
bus  has  to  go  outside  of the  city and  come  back  he is also eligible.
This,  however,  does  not  negate  local  city  officials,  school  board
officials  from  providing  transportation.  There  is  no  prohibition
against  that.

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  You  said  the  legislature  changed  the law
but  didn’t  provide  the  money.  Now,  they  are  eligible  for state aid
but it is unfunded  now,  is that  what  you  are  saying?

A.  The  law  was  changed  last  year  to permit  in-city  bus trans
portation  but  in  changing  the  law  the  legislature  said  our depart-
ment  had  to  disburse  the  funds  to the eligible  existing  areas which
meant  that  since  they  did  not  increase  the  amount  of  funds
appropriated  we could  not  provide  for in-city  transportation.

Q.  If a  child  lives  in the city  and lives  more  than  a mile anda
half  from  the  school  to  which  he  is assigned  he  may  not  receive
the state  aid because  it is unfunded  at the  present  time?

A.  That  is correct.
Q.  But  if he  lives  the  same  distance
away and lived outside
the  City  of Detroit,  for example,  then  he could  receive state aide?

A.  That  is correct,  or any  other  area.

that would  be a total  of 617.

these  school  districts  in Michigan  during  the  current  school  year?

A.  Approximately  2.2 million.

[2081]  Q.  Now,  you  made  reference  to  a  1966  amendment
to  the  statute.  Prior  to  that  time  your  office  supervised  the

A.  °62?

Q.  Yes.

A.  Prior  to  the  amendment  of  1962  in  terms  of Act  307  of
the Public  Acts  of  1937  we  approved  the adequacy  of the site.  We
approved  whether  or  not  the  proposed  building  was  educationally
feasible,  and  approved  the safety  and  health  standards.  The  legisla-
ture  in  1966  struck  those  requirements  and  indicated  approval
would be contingent  upon  Health  and  fire  and  safety  approval  of
the Fire Marshal  of the  Department  of Public  Health.

adequacy  of a site, exactly  what  did  that  entail,  can  you  tell  us?

then.  But,  basically  we  would  take  a  look  at  whether  or  not  the
site  was  consistent  with  some  of  the  information  placed  into
evidence  earlier  in  terms  of  transportation,  in  terms  of balanced
enrollment,  in terms  of access  to the  facility,  and  in terms  of the
kind  of  educational  program  that  had  been  identified  in  the
specifications,

A.  1966.
Q.  1966.  Do you  know  the circumstances  that  --
A.  (Interposing)  1962.  I’m sorry,  1962.
Q.  "62.  Do  you  know  the  circumstances  that  brought  about

the change in the law?

A.  The  Department  prior  to  1962  was  in  the  process  of
fequiring  school  districts  to  consolidate  and  reorganize.  Michigan
had in 1945  approximately  6,000  school  districts,  and one  means

by  which  we  consolidated  was  to  require  that  new  facilities  be

Department  from  approving  such  plans.

Q. Doctor,  public  instruction

in the State  of  Michigan is
supported  by funds  from  two  tax sources,  the first  | would
characterize  as a  property  owner  assessment,  the millage  they levy
on property  owned  in a  school  district  and on top of that the
funds the school  district  receives  from the state.  Is that basically  a
correct  statement?

Ill a 44

A.  Basically;  however,  the school  districts  do receive,  de
pending  on certain  circumstances,
federal  funds and other sources
which  would  amount  to anywhere  from  3  to  7 or 8 per cent.
Taking  that  factor  into  consideration,  basically  property  tax and
state aid are the basic sources  of  revenue  for school  districts.

Q.  What  is  the source  of the funds that  the individual  school
district  would receive  from the state  itself?  Is it  not the sales tax
and also an assessment  against  cigarettes  and liquor?

A.  Well,  there  are certain

of the sales
restrictions,  3 cents
tax or 2 cents  of the sales  tax,  we have  income  tax, some  of the
[2093]  funds  come  from  that.  We have  other  taxes,  cigarette  taxes
other aid
and  the funds  come  from  that.  General  fund  resources,
from  the general  fund  would  make  up  the bulk  of the thre
quarters  of a billion  dollars  for schools.

Q.  Does  the  state  provide  funds  from  the  sources  just
described  to  all  the  school  districts  in  the  state?  Are  there  any
which  would  not  receive  it?

A.  Yes, there  are  one  or  two  districts  in the  state  that  would
not  receive  state  aid  because  there  is a formula  based  upon state
equalized  evaluation  and when a district  such  as  River  Rouge hasa
state equalized  of $40,000.00 it is conceivable  that  they  would not
get state  aid.  We have just a couple districts  that  would  fit into that
category.

Q.  Would  you  tell us  what  those  two  districts  are?

A.  I could  not.

Q.  With  regard  to  the  districts  that  do  receive  these  funds

from  the  state,  is  it  your  statement  your  office  has  virtually n
control  or  supervision  over  the  districts  themselves  with  regard to
attendance  areas,  school  siting and  so  forth?

A.  In terms  of school  attendacne  and  the  location  of building

sites,  that  is correct.

Q.  In other words,  the  funds  are given by the state to tk
school district  and the independent  school district allocates the
funds  as they see fit themselves?

ty

us

Illa  45

_ {2094} A. That  statement  is basically  correct,  but  according
to the information  in our  office  about  75 percent  of the operation
of any school district is taken up in the salaries  of the teachers and
another  5 per  cent  for  other  instructional  services,  so  to respond
to that  question,  once  we  know  how  many  teachers  are  in a dis-
trict and once we have in our disposal the salary  schedules,  we
pretty  much know  where  the money is going to  go becuase  80 per
cent of it goes  into  instructional  expenses.

Q.  | appreciated  the fact  your  office  knows  where  the money
goes,  but as  for exercising  a direct  control  over  the  disbursement
you do not exercise  such  control?

A.  That  is correct,  except  that  the  budget  must  be balanced
and submitted  to  our  office  and  they  must  submit  to our  office  at
the end of the year an  audited  financial  report.

Q.  There  is  however,  an  exception  to  that  and  that  is  re-
garding  the  additional  funds,  the compensatory  educational  funds,
would  you call  them  Title  3 funds?

A.  There  are  several  discretionary  fund  amounts,  the compen-
satory  education  fund  at  the  state  level  would  be one  fund  where-
by the money  must  go directly  to  particular  school  districts,  that
is correct.

-  Q  Those  funds  are  in  fact  under  your  supervision,  aren’t

they?

A.  That  is correct.
[2095]  Q.  Are  funds  of that  nature  given  to Detroit?
A.  Yes,  they are.

ing the City of Detorit?

A.  Many, yes.
Q  Would  you be in  a position  to describe  any of those parti-

cular communities  receiveing  these  funds?

A.  Not  with  any  degree  of  specificity.  There  are  approxi-
mately
38 school  districts in the  Wayne  intermediate
area and |
would  imagine  most  of  them in one way or  the  other receive  some
Section  3 funds as well  as some Title  1 funds.  The district  receives
the funds based  upon  our assessment  data.

Ill a 46

Q.  Does  your  department  have  any  supervisory  control  o
authority  over  the  creation  and  operation  of bussing  routes  within
the  State  of Michigan?

A.  Yes.

Q.  What  is the extent  of that  authority?

A.  It is based  upon  the  statute  in  terms  of the  disbursement

of  state  aid  to  the  tune  of  about  $29  million,  but  that  is, as |
indicated  before,  basically  for the outlying  areas  of the  state.

Q.  Does  your  office  on  occasion  define  the  route  the buses

-

will  take  between  the schools?

A.  No.  The  language  in  the  statute  is  pretty  specific.  The
school  [2096]  district  following  the  statute  and  th  regulations
pretty  much  comes  up  with  a  bus  route  that  is  consistent  with
approval.  We  do  not  specifically  tell  them  which  was  the  bus has
to travel.

Q.  Has  your  office  on  past  occasions  ever  created  a bus

route  between  different  districts?

A.  Not  to my  knowledge.  I have  no  information  on  that.
Q.  Would  it  be  your  statement  that  bus  routes  as  they pre
sently  exist,  exist  only  within  the  districts  themselves  and  not
between  districts?

A.  No,  that  wouldn’t  be  a  fair  statement  because  in terms of

non-K—12  districts  we  have  students  going  from  the  non-K-l2
district  into  the  K—12  districts  and  in terms  of our  area  center we
have  many  students  riding  buses  from  cities  into  area  centers.
Where  there  might  be  a  cooperative  program  such  as  in
Ida, Michigan and  Monroe,  where  all  of the  students  go  to the Ida
Public  Schools  that  have  special  education  needs,  such  as mentally
handicapped  or  physically  handicapped,  the  buses  then  to out to
all  of  the  school  districts  in  that  immediate  area  to  bring  thos
type  of students  in  to  one  school  district,  so  all  over  the state we
would  have  buses,  depending  on  the  circumstances,  going  from
one  school  district  to  another  school  district  to  provide  selected
and  specialized  educational  services  not  being  provided  in the
other  school  districts.

_  Q.  Would  it be your  opinion,  based  on  your  knowledge  of the
authority  of your  office,  and  the  statutes  of the  state,  that  you
would have  such  authority?

A.  Yes.  I think  we  must  take  into  consideration  that  in  1963
the  legislature  adopted  in  Michigan  a  new  constitution.  That
constititution  provides  certain  supervisory  powers  for  the  State
Board of Education  that  has  not  heretofore  been  tested.

I suspect  based  upon  the  new  constitution  and  some  decisions
rendered  recently  by  the  Supreme  Court  that  the  supervisory
powers  of the  State  Board  of Education  might  well  be far greater
than what  the statutes  now  provide.

gram?

dollars  in  education,  state  funds,  indicates  that  a  district  must

vious  year  for  that  billing.  In  other  words,  you  cannot  use  th
money  to supplant  the local  money.

Q. You stated  that the State  Board does approve  or dis-
approve  the  certification  of  teachers,  sets  the  standard  for
certification.  Are you familiar with the term ESRP,  Emergency
Substitutes and  Regular Positions?  Is this a statewide  term?

Ill a 52

A.  No,  that  is  a  Detroit  Federation  of  Teachers,  Detroit

Public  School  term.  It is not  a State  of Michigan  term.

Q.  You  understand  that  to  be  a  ‘teacher  who  is not  fully

certified?

A.  I know  what  it means,  yes.

Q.  Do  you  approve  a  temporary  placement  of these  teacher

or is that  subject  to review?

A.  No,  we  do  not  approve  the  temporary  placement of the
teachers.  What  we  do  is  usually  approve  a  laundry  list.  What
happens  is  the  Detroit  Board  of  Education  will submit  the names
of 200  or  300  people  who  meet  the  permit  standards,  either they
are  eligible  for  full  year  permits  or  90  day  substitute  permits
{2118]  which  is  the  term  you  are  referring  to.  What  we do is
within  30 days of the time  the  teacher  is hired  or  before  December
Ist,  certify  to  the  local  school  districts  that  those  people  ar
eligible  to  perform  in  the  classroom  on  a  substitute  basis  or ona
full—semester  basis  as  per  their  placement  by  the  Detroit  Public
Schools.

Q.  You  don’t  decide  on  the  place  but  decide  on  whether  they

meet  those  qualifications,  is that  correct?

A.  Right.  But  we  only  do  that  when  the  school  district  ha

certified  that  no  fully  certificated  teacher  is available.

hiring  additional  teachers,  regular  teachers,  substitutes  o
specialized  skill  teachers?

A.  Section  3 has  three  requirements  in it. One  of the require
ments  is  that  a  school  district,  a school  may  hire  teacher  aides,
these  are  not  certificated  people,  to  assist  the  teacher  but ther
must  be a certificated  teacher  in the classroom.

Q.  Some  of the funds  may  be used  to hire  these  people?

A.  That  is correct.

MR.  YOUNG:  If  it  please  the  Court,  I would  like  to  very
briefly  take  up  two  matters  in  connection  with  Dr.  Porter’s  testi-
mony of last  Friday.

First  of all,  Dr.  Porter  inadvertently  indicated  with  reference
to state  aid  reimbursement  for  transportation  that  for  1970-71
there  was  statutory  authority  for reimbursement  for in—city  trans-
portation,  but  because  of  a  lack  of funds  transportation  in  rural
areas  first  had  to  be  reimbursed  by state  aid  money.  However,  in
checking  the  statute  it is indicated  that  an  amendment  to the  state
School  Aid  Act,  Section  11-J,  1970,  the  most  recent  amendment
indicates,  and  I quote:

“It  is  the  intent  of  the  legislature  that  beginning  with  the
school  year  1970—‘72  transportation  cost  within  cities  and  villages
will be reimbursed.”

Thus,  for  the  current  year  there  is  not  authority,  let  alone
funds.

teacher.

A.  Yes.

_  High School?

A.  Yes,  it is.

MR.  LUCAS:  Excuse  me,  your  Honor.  Just  for  correction  of
the record,  counsel  stated  that  the  abridged  version  of the  Equal
Educational  Opportunity  report  is  the  item  in  evidence.  It  may
also  be.  But,  the  unabridged  version  is marked  Plaintiffs’  Exhibit
3. You might  want  to prepare  that,  counsel.

Q. (By Mr.  Lucas) Go ahead,  sir.
A.  “November  3, 1960.
“TO:  Honorable  Nathan  Kaufman,  Chairman
Committee  on  Equal  Educational  Opportunity.
“We  should  like  to  begin our  presentation  by reviewing  with
you  briefly  the  development  of our  organization.  We feel it is
significant  as  it  represents  an  attempt  on  the  part  of people
who  make  up  this  organization  to  effectively  deal  with  the
frustrations  historically  inherent  in  [2244]  attempting  to pro-
vide  for  minority  group  children  an  adequate  education

Ill a 64

' :

within  the  Detroit  Public  School  System.  A majority of the
people  of the  Negro  race  moved  into  the  now  Center  District
from  other  school  districts  within  the  limits  of  the  City  of
Detroit.  Although  better  housing  conditions  were  but  one  of
the  motives  for such  a move,  of equal  importance  was a desire
to  provide  their  children  with  a  more  equitable  and  enriched
educational  experience.

“They  were  aware  of the  increased  population  within  their
new  geographical  area,  and  accepted  the  counselling  ot  the
then  new  administration  of  the  Board  of  Education,  to the
effect  that  additional  tax  monies  would  have  to be made  avail-
able  if educational  standards  within  the  City  of Detroit  were
to  be  improved,  or  even  maintained.  Consequently,  each of
them  made  a  strong  personal  investment  in  the  millage  cam-
paign  of Spring  1959.  In  this  campaign,  initially,  their  efforts
did  not  meet  the  wholehearted  approval  of  the  Negro  com
munity,  since  from  past  experience,  particularly  involving
other  millage  campaigns,  members  of  the  Negro  community
had observed  that  the  results  of the  expenditures  [2245]  of
monies  obtained  from  additional  taxes,  had  little  effect  on the
facilities,  the  equipment,  or  the  curriculum  available  to their
children.

“Despite  this  resistance,  they  were  aware  that  there  would
be  less  justification  for  demanding  adequate  educational
opportunities  for  their  children  if they  did  not  accept  their
responsible  share  for  the successful  passing  of the  millage pro-
gram.  As  a  consequence  of  their  efforts,  their  respective
schools  voted  overwhelmingly  for  the  millage  program,  and
they  logically  expected  that  positive  results  would  follow
their  efforts.

“Their  first  disillusionment  occurred  only  a few  months,  but
yet  a few  weeks  after  the  passage  of the  millage  —  they wer
rewarded  with  the  creation  of the  present  Center  District.  In
effect  this  District,  with  a  few  minor  exceptions,  created  a
segregated  school  system.  It  accomplished  with  a  few  marks
of the  crayon  on  the  map,  the return  of the  Negro  child from
the  few  instances  of  an  integrated  school  exposure,  to the
traditional  predominantly  uniracial  school  system  to which he
had  formerly  been  accustomed  in the City  of Detroit.

Ill a 65

_
“Their  attempts  to  meet  this  threat  [2246]  to  their
children’s  educational  experience  through  existing  school
organizations  met  with  little  success.  Their  conferences  with
District  and  City-Wide  administrators  including  the  super-
intendent,  Dr.  Samuel  Brownell,  resulted  in only  rationaliza-
tions  concerning  segregated  housing  patterns,  and  denials  of
any  attempts  at  segregation.  When  it  was  pointed  out  that
regardless  of  motivation,  that  segregation  was  the  result  of
their  boundary  changes,  little  compromise  was  effected,
except  in  one  or  two  instances,  where  opposition  leadership
was  most  vocal  and  aggressive.
“Concurrent  with  boundary  changes,  it  was  alarmingly
noticeable  that  the  school  population  within  the  Center
District  was  rapidly  increasing,  and  that  the  priority  building
program  would  have  little  positive  effect  in dealing  with  the
problem.  Attempts  to  discuss  this  problem  with  school  and
district  administration  gave  promise  of  only  minimal  relief.
“Finally,  it had  been  earlier  noted  by new  residents  moving
into  what  is now  the  Center  District  that  prior  to and during
its change  from  a uniracial  (predominantly  white)  to a biracial
[2247]  ‘system  and  again  to  a  uniracial  (predominantly
Negro)  school  system  that  the  quality  of their  children’s  pre-
vious  educational  experiences  did  not  equip  them  to compete
on an  equal basis  with  resident  children  in the same  grade
classifications.
“These  experiences  made  them  aware  that  no  one
Organization  composed  of  one  or  several  schools,  could
effectively  coordinate  the  mutual  concern  of  the  many
parents  residing  within  the  Center  District.  Thus  out  of the
several  discussions  of groups  of people  whose  primary  concern
was  the  adequate  and  equitable  education  of their  children,
this  organization  was  born.  It is felt that  no  better  description
of its  purpose,  its  objective,  and  its  reason  for  being  can  be
found  than  in  the  preamble  to  its  Constitution,  which  is:

“PREAMBLE:  Our  interest

is  in  equal  educational

opportunities  for all persons  within  the City of Detroit.

Ill  a  66

es  |

“We  do  not  believe  that  such  opportunities  are possible

within  a segregated  school  system.

“We  oppose  a  policy  of  containment  of  minority  groups
within  specified  boundaries,  an  [2248]  example  of which is
the  Center  District.  While  the  above  is of utmost  concem  to
us  we  are  also  aware  that  there  is need  for  improvement  and
enrichment  of the  standards  within  this  district  in practice as
well  as  in  theory.

“We  believe  that  once  standards  have  become  reasonably
adequate,  that  such  standards  should  be maintained.  It should
be  further  recognized  that  future  population  shifts  brought
about  by urban  redevelopment  will  adversely  affect  the above
goals  in  the  Center  District,  unless  there  is anticipation  of the
impact  of this population  growth  upon  this district.

“Since  the  inception  of our  organization  we  have  noted  the

following:

“The  public  school  system  of the  City  of Detroit  is divided
into  nine  administrative  districts,  one  of which  is the  Center
District.

“Yet,  every  day,  when  the  children  in  this  city  leave  their
homes  to  go  forth  to  public.  schools, approximately  one  out
of  every  four  leaves  a  home  in  the  Center  District.  Of the
154,969  children  enrolled  in  public  elementary  schools as of
September  30,  1960,  36,264,  or  23.4  percent  of these  [2249]
children  leave  a home  in the Center  District.

“There  are  221  elementary  school  buildings  in  the  Detroit
Public  School  System.  Of these  28  are  in  the  Center District.
This  means,
then,  that  the  23.4  percent  of  the  total
elementary  school  population  is  assommodated  in  127
percent  of the  buildings.

III a 67

_- “Fifteen  percent  of these  children  sit  in classes  of 40 to 44
_~ students  per class.  This is in comparison  to:

_  “Sixty-two  and  one-half  percent  of  all  the  children  in  the
city’s  elementary  schools  who  sit  in  classes  of 45  to  49  are
children  in  the  Center  District.  These  schools  in  the  Center
District  find  their  capacities  short  by 6,352  pupil stations.  In
other  words,  their  capacities  are  overtaxes  to the  extent  of 16
percent;  and  the  future  building  [2250]  program,  as set  forth
by the  superintendent’s  report  of October  17, 1960,  will make
available  only  Il, 189  additional  pupil  stations  within  the next
ten-year  period.  However,  this  will  be insufficient  to meet  the
demands  of the  Center  District.  Therefore,  it is apparent  that
a bussing  program  will  have  to  become  a  permanent  part  of
the  school  housing  program.  Thus  the  manner  in  which  the
bussing  program  is  administered  becomes  a  matter  of  acute
concern.

“Presently,  children  are  being  bussed  by  grades.  Under  this
system a number  of problems  are  created:

1) It makes  necessary  a reorganization  of the  bussing school,
as well as the school  into  which  the children  are  bussed.
2) They  are  not  integrated  into  the  school  into  which  they
are bussed,  except  in minor  instances.
3) There  is a possibility  of the separation  of the  family  unit.
4) Parents  are  unable  to  establish  a good  rapport  with  the
teachers  and  administrators  [2251]  in  the  new  school  since
there  exists  a  time  limit  in  which  these  children  will  be
members  of that school.

“It  is recommended  that  a  policy  of bussing  by geographical
areas  instead  of  by  grades  be  instituted  so  as  to  eliminate  the
above problems.

Ill a 68

a

“The  emphasis  on  curricula  objectives  are  not  comparable in the
various  school  districts  of  the  Detroit  School  System.  There  is;
tendancy  in  the  Center  District  to  stereotype  the  education
capacity  of  the  children.  This  means  that  children  entering  the
schools  in  this  district  whose  background  enables  them  to
comprehend  an  enriched  educational  program,  are  not  challenged.

‘‘For  example,  one  student  in  the  Hutchins  Intermediate
School  who  desired  to prepare  for entrance  into  an  Eastern  college
found  that  Latin  was  not  offered,  and  only  after  considerable
effort  by members  of the  community,  along  with  his  family,  was
Latin  placed  back  in the  school  curriculum.  Many  other  instances
can  be cited  upon  request.

“Conversely,  children  whose  initial  capacity  is  retarded  by de
prived  socio-economic  [2252]  circumstances  also go unchallenged.
The  District  Administrator  has admitted  that  no  program  exists to
take  care  of these  children.

“The  curriculum  and  counselling  as  they  now  exist,  do not
encourage  students  to  achieve  their  maximum  capacities.  We feel
that  the  responsibility  for  any  inequities  in  the  education
experience  offered  to  any  group  of children  within a given school
system  must  be assumed  by those  persons  charged  with  the overall
responsibility  of administering  that  system.

“Therefore,  we  recommend  that  strong  policies  be adopted by
the  top  administration  to  erase  inequities  of  the  Detroit  Public
School  System,  and  a  policy  of  supervision  through  all levels of
administration  be instituted  at all levels  of administration  to insur
equal educational  opportunities  to all children.

“The  Citizens’  Association  for Better  Schools.”

{2350}  ...
Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  The  statement,  to  refresh  your  memory
again,  of July  Ist upon  your  inauguration  as  president  of the  Board
reads  as  follows:

et

“This  Board  in past years  held  to perpetuating  segregation  and
must  now  undo  the wrongs  of the  [2351]  past.  I recognize  as
I'm  sure  that  you  do  that  merely  assigning  students  to
different  schools  would  not  have  performed  the  task  that
we  wanted  unless  we  could  have  provided  improved  programs
as  well  as  additional  services.  But,  it was  at least  a start  in the
right  direction.  Neighborhood  schools  and  bussing  are
emotionally  charged  words,  and  generally  those  who  expose
them  completely  overlook  other  factors.  There  is, in  fact,  a
great  deal  of inconsistency  and  hipocracy  that  all  too  oftens
surrounds  the  lip  service  paid  to  these  two  principles.  Judge
Tuttle  several  years  ago  stated  that  neighborhood  concept
changes  as  factors  change  for  those  who  most  frequently  use
the term.  He  points  out that past practices  were  if  a  white
child  living in a  black  area  was allowed  to  attend  a  white
school  out  of  his  neighborhood,  then  the  concept  took  on
another  meaning,  and  when  a  black  child wasn’t  to  attend a
white  school,  the  argument  was  in  favor  of the advantages  of
the neighborhood  plan.  Cases  in point  can well be illustrated
by  what  occurred  years  back  in  Detroit.  A  pocket  of black
students  living  in the  Eight  Mile-Wyoming  area  were  assigned
to  Northern  High  when  they  should  have  been  attending
Redford  High.  Students  at Hally,  which  is south  of  Six  Mile

Ill a 73

Road,  fed  into  Mumford  when  the  student  population  was
predominantly  white.  The  Parkside  Homes  students  were
bussed  to  [2352]  the  A-L.  Holmes  School.  Boundaries  were
changed  frequently  as  the  racial  composition  of  a  school
changed,  and  in  those  cases  no  one  had  petitions  circulated  to
recall  Board  members.  Where  was  the  city’s  sense  of  value
then?  As  one  of  the  Board  members  affected  by  the  recall
movement,  I can  only  say  that  I personally  regret  it. It seems
to  me  that  those  signing  the  petitions  are  in defiance  of the
Supreme  Court  and  are,  in  effect,  blocking  the  doorways  of
our  schools  as  others  have  done  in  the  past.  I’m afraid  I can’t
agree  with  those  who  have  expressed  the  fears  that  our  plan
the  city.  In  my  opinion,  it  already  is.
would  polarize

toward  greater  integration.

“—no  definite  policy  as  I remember  the  conference.  That  was
the  only  time  it was  ever  really  brought  out  and  discussed  in my
presence  other  than  —  that  the  difficulty  was  not  any  policy  on
the  part  of the  Board.  In fact,  he  had  been  trying  to  place Negro
teachers  in areas  where  there  was  all-white  enrollment,  but  that  he
had  some  very  sad  experiences  in  certain  areas  where  he  would
send  colored  teachers  out  in  the  morning,  and  in  the  afternoon
none  of  the  children  would  report  to  school.  There  wouldn’t  be

lll a 76

any  class,  and  then  a  violent  protest  on  the  part  of the  parents,
and  when  that  happened  he  felt  it was  good,  for  the  good  of all
the  children  in  the school  and  the sake  of peace  in the community
why  the  thing  happened  that  the  teacher  was  withdrawn  and  sent
to  some  other  area.  He  mentioned  that  as  being  the  only  —  the
chief difficulty  in their  attempt  to place  Negro  teachers.  [2549]

**He  said  several  times  in  conferences,  and  I remember  he
would  like  to  place  more  —  that  he  would  like  to  place  more
generally  than  he  had  been  able  to  do  at  that  time,  but  that

[2550]

“| think  that the whole  climate  has changed  now.  I don’t  think
that  sort  of  thing  happens.  At  least,  I have  not  heard  it happen.

very  sympathetic  to  that  situation  and  he  was  doing  everything

ing colored  teachers  out  to  see  what  the community  reaction  was.

referred  to  a  few  minutes  ago,  that  there  weren’t  so  many
qualified  Negro  teachers  available.”

[2569]  MR.  CALDWELL:  The  Plaintiff’s  Exhibit  6-A,  the  purpose
for  introducing  Plaintiff's  Exhibit  6-A  is that  at this  session  of the
Board  the  motion  was  made  and  carried  that  the  Board  express  its
agreement  with  the  interim  findings  of  the  court  in  the  Sherrill
School  lawsuit,  and  for  that  purpose  I will  offer  the  exhibit.

MR.  VON  ENDE:  Mr.  Caldwell,  could  you  note  where  that

resolution  appears?

MR.  CALDWELL:  On  the  exhibit,  third  page.  I’m quoting:
“It  was  then  moved  that  this  body  express  itself  as  being in
complete  agreement  with  the interim  findings  and  suggestions
of  the  court,  two  of  which  have  been  proved  already,  the
third  being,  according  to  the  court,  that  the  Board  shall
commit  itself  to  propose  other  methods  of  speeding  racial
integration  of students,  and  that  the goal shall  be achievement
of  substantial  integration  in  high  schools  and  junior  high
schools.”

And  then  after  discussion  on  the  next  page;
[2570]  “It appears  that  the  motion  was  then  carried.”
MR.  VON  ENDE:  Your  Honor,  my  objection  is merely  to the
relevancy  and  probative  value.  I just  don’t  see  the  probative  value

—

Ill a 78

of  the  fact  of  the  Board’s  agreement  in  principle  with  the
recommendations  of the court.

MR.  YOUNG:  I would  merely  reiterate  the  statement  that  we

were  not  parties  to that  lawsuit.

MR.  CALDWELL:  Your  Honor,  the exhibit  is offered  for this
purpose:  In the  interim  findings  in  the  Sherrill  School  case  —  that
was  Civil  Action  No.  22092  —  the court  stated  its finding  as  to the
ineffectuality  of the open  school  program  in achieving  integration.
It  also  recommended  that  the  Board  should  discontinue
participation  in any  apprenticeship  program  which  is not  an  inte-
grated  program,  and  it also  stated,  and  I’m quoting  from  page  2 of
Exhibit  6 which  is the interim  findings:

“Substantial  integration  of  faculty  and  professional  per-
sonnel  should  be  achieved  in  all  schools  by  the  beginning  of
the  February,  1965  term.”

Now,  the  Board  minutes  which  I’m  offering  at  this  time,
Plaintiff's  Exhibit  6-A,  merely  express  the  Board’s  view  that  the
interim  findings  were  correct,  and  that  the  Board  concurred  in

ALVIN  SKELLY

KOK

was  called  as  a  witness  on  behalf  of the  Defendant  and  being  first
duly  sworn,  testified  as  follows:

eke

—

Ill a 82

Q.  Let  me  again  digress  slightly.  You  talked  about  bond

failures.  By that  you  mean  what,  sir?

A.  Twice  in  the  past  10  years  the  Board  of  Education  was
seeking  to  remedy  these  unmet  facility  needs  and  sought  the
approval  of citizens  under  the law  to obtain  additional  bond  funds
and  in  the  last  10  years  there  were  two  failures.  The  bonding
authority  was  voted  down.

Q.  By the citizens  of the district?

A.  By the  citizens.

Q.  Doctor,  why  did  the  Detroit  School  District  have  to  seek
voter  approval  for  bond  issues  when  under  the  law  of  this  state
other  districts  have  certain  bonding  authority  without  vote  of the
people?

A.  Well,  this  is  a  historic  anomaly.  Detroit  as  a  first  class
school  district  has  had  what  is the  equivalent  of 3 percent  of the
state  equalized  assessed  valuation,  whereas  other  school  district
have  had  five  percent.  Translated  into  arithmetic  here  the  state
equalized  assessed  valuation  is around  $5 billion.  At the  3 percent
level  we  would  have  a  continuing  authority  with a ceiling  of $150
million.  We were  always  at the  edge of that  $150  million.

Q.  Asa  matter  of fact  it was  2 percent  originally?

A.  At  one  time.  The  state  legislature  saw  fit  to  give  us  the

[2618]  additional  1 percent  a few years  ago.

Q.  Following  failure  of the  bond  issue,  was  it not?

A.  Correct.

Q.  As  these  bond  issues  failed,  did  the  Board  of  Education

seek  further  legislative  relief?

A.  We  sought  to  persuade  the  legislature  that  the  school  dis-
trict  here  had  needs  at least  equal  to  those  of other  school  districts
in  the  state  and  probably  far  in  excess.  Recently  the  state  legis-
lature  granted,  in  May  of  this  year,  the  additional  2  percent
authority  and  it  was  enacted  finally  by  the  Governor’s  signature
just a short  while  ago.

Q.  So  as  of less  than  six  weeks  ago  Detroit  for  the  first  time

III a 83

in history  is on  parity  with  every  other  school  district  in the state
with  respect  to bonding  authority,  is that  correct?

A.  Right.

Kelley  haven’t  done  anything.

Mr.  Lucas  has been  guilty  of it, also,  testifying  from  the  stand.  Our
motion  is based  on  the  proofs  submitted  by  the  plaintiff.  That  is
what  41-B  contemplates,  whether  or  not  they  have  established
their  case  by  the  [2709]  preponderance  of  the  evidence.  They
have  had  six  long  weeks  to  do  it in.  They  have  had  every  oppor-
tunity  to  put  in  proofs,  and  I recited,  I thought  fairly,  what  the
Governor  was  accused  of  doing  and  what  the  Attorney  General
was  accused  of  doing,  but  no  one  testified  to  the  fact  that  they
didn’t  do anything.

I think  we  should  stick  to  the  record  in the  case,  if the Court

please.

Finally,  when  Mr.  Ritchie  suggested  to  this  Court,  and  in  all
my  years  of  practice  I have  never  heard  it suggested  before,  that
this  Court  has  the  authority  to  define  prescribed  by law  and  give
the  superintendent  all  this  power  —  well,  I’m  not  going  to  labor
that.  But,  I submit  that  is not  the law.  This  Court  can  define  what
it means,  but  it  can’t  prescribe  the  powers  originally  under  our
separation  of powers,  if the Court  please.

Mr.  Lucas  never  addressed  himself  to  the  proofs  in  the  case.

cinnati  settled  the  issue.  The  Court  said  at  this  stage  of  the
proceeding  the  Governor  and  the  Attorney  General  belonged  in.
So,  they  stayed  in  and  sat  through  six  weeks  of testimony,  and

now  the question  is have  they established  the right  to relief against

[2712]  I represented  to  the  Court  that  the  state  did  not
exercise  any  supervision  over  the  allocation  of  funds  between
‘puildings  within  the  School  District.  That  has been  turned  around
in regard  to  Section  3, and  I think  that  has to be answered  if even
very  briefly.  About  three  years  ago  the  legislature  set  up  a program
for  disadvantaged  children,  special  funds,  and  it  tried  to  adopt
criteria  that  would  allow  some  kind  of a reasonable  system  for the
allocation  of these  funds.  Presently  the  program  works  as  follows:
the  legislature  has  appropriated  $17.5  million  for this  year,  and  in
adopting  certain  criteria  these  have  to  do  with  socio-economic
deprivation  and  a  high  percentage  of  students  with  low  achieve-
ment  skills,  and  schools  are  rated  on  a  scale,  and  grants  are  made
from  the  $17,500,000  for  those  particular  schools.  That  is how
the  program  works.  Of  course,  the  state  supervises  the  program,
and  the  legislature  has  spelled  out  very  carefully  how  it is to  be
conducted,  and  the  citation  for  the  section,  if the Court  please,  is
Section  3 of  Act  312  of  °57,  MSA  15.1919(53).  Sure  the  state
pays  out  millions  of dollars  in support  of the  schools.  And  there  is
another  section  of this  act  which  states  what  the  money  is to  be
spent  for,  and  the  legislature  has  exercised  control.  I cited  that
Lansing  case  that  Mr.  Lucas  suggested.

Perhaps  I  didn’t  fully  inform  the  Court  [2713]  of  the
proposition  that  that  case  definitively  held  that  the  legislature  had
plenary  power  over  education  for  the  Superintendent  of  Public
Instruction,  and  this  was  prior  to  the  Constitution  of 1963.  Since
then  the  argument  is  based  upon  the  non-legal  opinion  of  Dr.
Porter  which  Mr.  —  I guess  I’m  so upset  with  you  I forgot  your
name.  But,  it  is  counsel  for  intervenor,  Mr.  Ritchie.  Anyway,  he
made  reference  to  this  money  paid  out  under  Act  312  of °57, and
there  is  a  section  that  specifies  the  particular  purpose  for  which
the money  can  be used.

But,  we  can  sit  and  hope  about  what  Article  8,  Section  3
means  in the broad  powers  of the State  Board.  But,  one thing is

a —

III a 86

crystal  clear,  and  that  is that  in the area  of enforcing  the  civil  right
to  education,  the  power  is in  the  State  Civil  Rights  Commission,
and  we  know  the  plaintiffs  don’t  choose  to  join  them  as
defendants.

Finally,  in  regard  to  the  remedy,  last  Friday  the  Sixth  Circuit
Court  of Appeals  upheld  the  decision  of Judge  Keith  calling  for a
very  comprehensive  integration  of the  Pontiac  School  District.  The
State  Board  of Education  was  not  a defendant  in this  case,  and  Dr.
Porter  was  not  a  defendant  in  this  case.  The  Governor  was  not  a
defendant  in  that  case,  and  the  Attorney  General  was  not  a
defendant  in  that  case,  and  from  the  opinion  in  that  particular
case  the  Court  [2714]  ordered  a  plan  for  the  integration  of  the
school  system  at  all  levels,  student  body,  faculty  and  adminis-
tration.  It was  a very  broad  relief.  Now,  if there  should  be  need  for
the  State  Board  and  the  Superintendent  of  Public  Instruction,
should  there  be need  for relief or  a remedy,  not  relief,  under  Court
Rule  19  I'm  sure  we  will  be  back,  and  we  will  help  the  Court  in
any  way  we  can.  But,  we  are  entitled  to a ruling  on  our  motions  to
dismiss  under  41-B,  and  the  decided  cases  is  the  Sixth  Circuit
indicate  that  the  Court  has  to  look  at  the  record  and  determine
whether  or  not  by  a  preponderance  of  the  evidence  they  have
established  a  right  to  relief  against  us,  and  whether  or  not  under
the  facts  and  the  law  they  have a right  to relief  against  us,  and  we
submit  they  have  not.  Therefore,  we  respectfully  ask  this Court  to
grant  both  motions.

thereupon  resumed  the  stand  as  a  witness  on  behalf  of  the

[2962]  ...

Q.  (By  Mr.  Bushnell,  continuing)  You  mentioned  that  the
1960  part  of the  definition  of “equal  educational  opportunity”  at
that  point  in time  was  site  size  as  well  as  physical  plant?

A.  Yes.

Q.  Will  you  elaborate  a  bit  on  that.  Why  was  site  size  a con-

cern?

A.  Well,  historically  the  sites  that  had  been  acquired  for
Detroit  schools  and  the  standards  of outdoor  physical  education
had  grown  over  the  years,  but  the  schools  in  the  inner  city which
had  been  built  in  the  nineteenth  century  typically  had  [2963]
sites  of an  acre  or  small  fraction  one  way  or  the  other.  The  school
occupied  virtually  all  of  the  site  except  a  very  narrow  band  of
front  yard.  There  was  no  physical  education  area  and  certainly  no
parking  area.  So  this  standard  had  grown  over  the  years  so  that  in
the  schools  that  had  been  constructed  in  the  post-war  period  and
the  immediate  pre-war  period,  particularly  in northeast  and north-
west  Detroit,  had  become  a  characteristic  that  an  elementary
school  should  have  at its  site  a  full  city  block  which  consisted  of

Ill a 88

usually  four  to  five  acres.  From  the  site  standard  which  we
developed,  working  with  the  city  agencies,  we  adopted  site
standards  which  provided  or  called  for  an  objective  of at  least  an
elementary  school  site  of five and  a half acres,  a junior  high site  of
20,  and  a  senior  high  site  of 30  to  35  acres.  These  site  size  stan-
dards  became  the  basis  of  our  site  selection  and  acquisition,  not
that  we  always  acquired  all  of that  amount  of land  in  the  initial
stage,  but  we  selected  sites  which  were  capable  of expansion  to
those  sites.

Q.  You  say  “we”  established  site  standards  of five  and  a half

acres  for elementary,  20 acres  for junior  high,  and  so  on  -

A.  Growing  out  of  the  staff  conferences  with  the  city
agencies  these  site  standards  were  recommended  to  the  Common
Council  and  to  the  Board  of Education  and  adopted  dee them  in
both  instances.

[2964]  Q.  Were  those  site  standards  in any  way  directed  by

the  State  Department  of Education  at that  time?

A.  No, they  were  not.

Q.  Or by  the  then  site  superintendent  of public  instruction

under  the  constitution?

A.  No.  In  deriving  them  we  used  standards  of the  National
Recreation  Association  and  attempted  to  adapt  state  standards  at
that  time.  I wasn’t  familiar  with  them.

eK

{3011}...

Q.  Is  there  any  resistance  on  the  part  of  parents  or  of  the

community  to transport  primary  kids?

[3012]  A.  Yes.  There  is  a  considerable  reluctance  to  trans-
port  kindergarten  and  primary  children  to  the  point  that  we  very
seldom  do transport  kindergarten  children.

Q.  Or primary  unit  children,  Ist,  2nd  and  3rd grade  kids?

A.  When  we  go into  a geographic  transportation  plan we  start
at  least  with  the  2nd  grade  and  sometimes  with  the  Ist grade,  but
this is accepted  with  a great  deal  of reluctance.

Ill a 89

Q.  Why  is it the  policy  to  return  children  as  soon  as  there  is
space  available  in  their  neighborhood  at  this  elementary  school
level?

A.  Well,  both  because  of the  expectation  of the  parents  that
they  will  and  should  have  a school  which  their  children  can  attend
locally,  and  also  because  of the  cost  to  the  district  of continuing
transportation.

Q.  You  mean  it is cheaper  to build  than  it is to bus?
A.  We  made  a  comparison  a  few  years  ago,  and  I think  the
relationship  is  still  approximately  the  same;  that  transportation
over  a  seven  year  period  will  pay  the  cost  of elementary  school
construction.

* eK

A.  Well,  I can’t  think  of any  others  at the  moment.  I’m afraid

[3304]  THE  COURT:  (Interposing)  Mr.  Lucas,  will  you  stop

A.  Has  never  been.  As  a matter  of fact,  the State  of Michigan
is probably  the  most  decentralized  in  its  authority  for curriculum
decision-making  of any  state  of the union.

THE  COURT:  Well,  do you  think  this accounts  for the  lack  of

ability  to read  and  write  among  our  high school  graduates?

A.  If it were  not  for the  fact  that  Michigan  experience  in this
regard  is comparable  to all other  states  in the union  which  do have
this  requirement.  But,  I doubt  it, because  it is [3305]  required,  as
a  matter  of fact,  by our  local  boards.  All  local  boards  do  require
English  for graduation.  it is not  a state  requirement.

THE  COURT:  It is one  for graduation?

A.  Yes,  to  the  best  of my  knowledge  every  local  board  does

require  it.

THE  COURT:  That  gives me a little  reassurance,  at least.

A.  We  have,  in  effect,  state  regulations  in  that  every  local
board  elects  to  do  those  things  as if they were  state  requirements.
But,  they  are  not  written,  and  they are  not  mandated  through  the
state.

[3309]  Q. You  mentioned  something  about  the  difficulties  in
Detroit  with regard  to  the  shortage  of  money,  is  this  a common
phenomenon  in  school  systems,  urban  and  otherwise,  throughout
the  country?

A.  Yes.

Q.  They  are  all  dealing with  those  problems  in  the  same  sort

of context,  are  they  not?

A.  Ican  only  speak  to  the  situation  in  Michigan  but  the
newspapers  would  make  it appear  as  if it is a generalized  national
problem.

Q.  Is the  situation  in  Detroit  somewhat  different  from  some
of  the  surrounding  school  districts  in  Wayne  County  which  you
may  be  more  familiar  with  because  of your  Intermediate  School
|
District  role  but  in  general  the  suburban  community  school?  Is  __
Detroit  in a more  difficult  position  in terms  of money?

A.  I suspect  it is, it has a more  difficult  problem  than  most  of
the  school  districts,  but  not  all  of them  in the  surrounding  area
because  some  of  the  school  districts  have  had relatively adequate
industrial  tax  basis  over a period  of years  and  have  not  experienced
_a@  great  deal  of  growth,  for  example.  Dearborn,  River  Rouge  and
Ecrose  would  be  examples  of  school  districts  which  have  had  a
long-time  industrial  tax  base  and  have  not  experienced  great
increases  in  school  population  so  they  are  relatively  well  off  by
way  of contrast.

Q.  In  terms  of  a  city  such  as  Detroit  and  the  needs  of the

between  Detroit  and  some  of the other  suburban  districts?

A.  That  would  be  my  opinion.  We  do  not  have,  in  my

the  state.

[3360]  THE  COURT:  I was  just  curious  about  the  function
of the  intermediate  schools  not  only  near  Detroit  but  elsewhere  in
the state.

Q.  (By  Mr.  Ritchie)  Along  that  line,  Doctor  Della-Dora,  the
City  of Detroit,  the  first-class  school  district  of the City of [3361]
Detroit  is  a member  district  within  the  Wayne  County  Intermedi-
ate  School  District?

A.  Itis.

Q.  How  many  other  districts  are  there  within  the  Wayne

County  Intermediate  School  District?

A.  I believe  there  are  a total  of 36 or  37.

Q.  The  Wayne  County  Intermediate  School  District,  does  it

have  its own  board?

A.  Correct.

Q.  With  regard  to  the authority  of the  board  then,  the  Wayne
County  Intermediate  Board,  has  that  board  to  your  knowledge  in
the  past  ever  exercised  any  supervisory  powers  over  the  City  of
Detroit  Board  of  Education  and  the  representatives  acting  under
them?

A.  It  would  be  in  connection  with,  for  example,  the  person
who  handles  child  counting.  He  operates  out  of the  Wayne  County
Intermediate  District  Office  and  does  it for  Detroit  as  well  as  for
all  other  districts.  That  is  a cooperative  matter,  however,  and  one
in which  all  of the  districts  have  concurred  that  they should  have
the  intermediate  district  perform  that  function  for  them.  It also
processes  the  reports  that  are  submitted  to the  State  Department.
It also  checks  with  the  State  Department  in some  instances  on  the
progress  made  with  regard  to  compliance  with  state  regulations,
and  so  in  those  [3362]  matters  it would.  With  regard  to  curricu-
lum,  it would  not  have,  any  more  than  it would  any  vane district
supervisory  functions.

A.  Yes.

There  were  two  ways  in  which  the  enrollment  of
black  students  at  the  elementary  level  was  overstated.  One  is that
all  of  the  Winterhalter  and  McKerrow  areas  which  were,  in  fact,
[3393]  an  optional  area  were  shown,  were  counted  in  the
Mackenzie  area,  but  in  addition  to  that  area  which  is as  optional,
there  was  another  band  between  there  and  Dexter  where  students
also  attended  McKerrow  and  Winterhalter  and  went  from
McKerrow  and  Winterhalter  to  Central,  but  these  were  counted  in
the tabulation as being  in  the  Mackenzie  constellation.  So that  the
number  of  students  at  the  elementary  level  in  the  Mackenzie
constellation  is  overstated.  That  was  the  first  point  of objection.

Q.  Let  me  take  up  that  point  right  now,  then  we  will  get  to
your  second  point.  The  Winterhalter  -  McKerrow  areas  that  you
are  talking  about  are  those  schools,  part  of  which  were  in  this
optional  area  between  Central  and  Mackenzie?

A.  Yes.

factor  of  this  belt  over  a  block  and  a  half  to  two  blocks.  wide
which  was  not  in  the  Mackenzie  area  at  all  where  students  did go
to  Central  High  School  and  this  area,  the  total  enrollment  of

[3394]  area?

A.  The  students  in  that  belt  along  Dexter  were  in the  Central

feeder  pattern,  yes.

Q.  What  was  the  other  factor?

A.  The  other  factor  is  that  in  the  Mackenzie  area  there  was
occurring  a movement  of young  families  whose  children  appeared
in  the  elementary  schools  who  did  not  have  older  children  in  the
secondary  schools,  that  is, there  was  a disproportionate  load  in the
elementary  schools  which  got  counted  here  and  tend  to  skew  the
whole  comparison.  The  formula  implies  that  all children  at all ages
are  equally  represented  in the  area  and  this  was  not  the case  in the

MacKenzie  area.  It is not  the  case  in any  area  where  young  families

Force  was  that  the  point  at which  transportation  should  be under-
taken  should  be  lowered  and  I think  in  order  to  maintain  a  class
size  not  to  exceed  32.  Actually,  I think  that  the  earlier  transpor-
tation  before  that  time,  transportation  had  not  usually  been
resorted  to  until  the  class  size  was  at a point  of 36 to 40.

[3396]Q.  In  1960  you  would  say  that  that  was  probably  the

standard  used?

A.  I think  this  was  generally  the  case;  that  an  area  was  not
red  flagged  for  emergency  until  it reached  class  size  in  the  neigh-
borhood  of 36.

was  of  talking

A.  This  was  to  indicate  that  the  senior  high enrollment  in the

enrollment  is  rising,  and  this  has  the  effect  of balancing  the  load

their  area  school.
