Q.  The  study  goes  on  to  describe  a  working  relationship
between  the  Department  of  Parks  and  Recreation  of the  City  of
Detroit  and  the  Board  of  Education.  It  indicates  that  this  joint
program  was  implemented  by a series  of  financial  arrangements
between  the  two  agencies  intended  to  be  a proper  allocation  of
costs.  Is this  same  procedure  going  forward  today?

A.  Yes, basically  it is.
[3515]  Q.  In  selection  of  sites,  Mr.  Henrickson,  isn’t  the
procedure  basically  that  you  .and  your  office  make  a
determination  of  asite  in  conjunction  with  whatever  is on  the
Master  Plan  as  it  presently  exists,  and if there are any deviations
from  that  existing  Master  Plan, you  consult  with  city planning  and
obtain  their  views  on  that  deviation?

.

A.  Well,  I think  I should  make clear initially that  where  they

were  talking  about  the  period  before  decentralization  there  —

Q.  Let’s  talk  about  before.

A.  There  are some  changes  in  procedures  in process  now.  The
procedure,  that  we  followed  over  the  decade  was  initiated
essentially  by  Doctor  Brownell  in the spring  of 1959  in which  he
transmitted  to  the Plan  Commission  a list of schools  which were  in
the ‘59  priority  list and asked  that a series of joint  conferences  be
set up and studies  be set  up between  ‘the two staffs  to  begin  to
determine  the sites,  and this  procedure  of joint  staff  conferences,
usually  between  the three  departments  and sometimes  with  other
agencies  of  the city,  has continued  over  the decade.  Now, in  most
cases  an initial  proposal  has come  from  my office,  and this  has
been  reviewed  for any  problems  that  it  might  raise  with  the
Department  of Streets  and Traffic  and street  closings  or any of the
agencies  that might be affected  by it.

{3516}  Q.  Is that for any program  or for particularly  schools

which do not fit the existing  master  plan of the City of Detroit?

A.  This has been a procedure  for all school sites.
Q.  Generally  speaking,  there have  been situations  where the
Park  Commission  purchased  acreage  or already  had ownership  of
acreage  and the School  Board  would then  in turn acquire  part of
the site for it. Conversely,  the School  Board  would have a site and

Ill  a  140

the  Park  Commission  would  acquire  acreage  prior to the  site  being
used?

A.  Yes.

Q.  There  has  been  an  expansion  of that  in  connection  with
recent  federal  programs,  has  there  not,  the Open  Spaces  Program?

A.  Well,  the  Open  Spaces  applications  have  been  processed  in
part  through  the  city,  but  we  have  not  had  joint  Open  Space
applications  with  the  Department  of  Parks  and  Recreation.  We
have  had our  own  applications.

Q.  But  your  Open  Spaces  has been  to add  open  land  adjacent

to existing  sites to expand  them,  is that  correct?

A.  Yes.

part  of the

original  site  for  new  construction  because  of the  additional  area

[3517]  Q.  How  does  the  relationship  between  your  office

contribution  than  there  has  been  actual  use  by  the  Housing
Division  of school  construction
of the
moneys.  The  provisions
housing law  is that  the  Housing  Commission  must  make a
one-third  contribution  to the  total  cost  of an urban  renewal
project  and  that  that  one-third  contribution  may  be  in  kind,
including  school  construction,  so  that  in  some  few  projects,  but
not nearly  as many  as has been  discussed,  the  Board has simply
furnished  the  Housing  Commission  with  some  certification  of the
expenditures  it  has  made  so  that  it can  in  turn  certify  that  as a
local  contribution  towards  the  one-third  contribution  toward  the
cost.

and  senior  high  school,  there  were  within  the  white  residential

THE  COURT:  I  think  not.  It  calls  for  an  educational

conclusion  which  the  witness  is qualified  to answer.

A.  As  I  have  indicated,  I  think  this  provides  the  white
students  with  an  integrated  experience  which  is  important  to
them.  I do not  profess  that  this  constitutes  total  desegregation.

Q.  (By  Mr.  Lucas)  Does  it constitute  a desegregated  school  in

the example  I gave  you?

[3531].  A.  I think  for the experience  of the  white  children  in

that  school  it certainly  does.

Q.  What  about  for the black  child?
A.  1  think  for  him  it  is  also  an  important  integrating
experience,  and  that  it is certainly  desegregation  in relation  to any
other  experience  he would  have  in the  inner  city.

Q.  In  terms  of  his  own  viewpoint  is  that  school  a
desegregated  school?  Would  you  make  any  difference  between  the
white  child  in that  situation  or  the black  child?

A.  I think  regarding  the  social  importance  of the  experience
of the  white  and  black  child,  it is more  important  that  the  white
child  have  the experience  than  the black  child  have  the experience.

Ill  a  150

Q.  Why is that,  Mr.  Henrickson?

A.  To  the  extent  that  the  patterns,  practices,  attitudes  of
segregation  in  our  society  are  problems  of the  white  society,  are
endemic  to  the  white  society.  It  is most  important  to  the  white
child  that  he  have  the  experience  of fellow  students  and  teachers
and  others  in the  community  who  are  black,  and  this  experience
can  be a constructive  experience.  I think  this is  the most  important
thing that  we  can do  in  the  City  of Detroit  at this  time.

Q.  In  terms  of  your  obvious  familiarity  with  desegregation,
Mr.  Henrickson,  is it generally  considered  that  this  type  of [3532]
experience,  whether  white  or  black,  that  it is most  important  that
it  come  at  the  elementary  level,  that  it  be  initiated  at  the
elementary  level?

A.  It is most  advantageous  if it can  start  early,  yes.

Q.  In the  forming  of attitudes  and  development?

A.  Yes.

Q.  Does  it  create  additional  tensions  and  additional
difficulties  when  children  come  from  racially  separate  elementary
schools  into  an  integrated  situation  for  the  first  time  at the junior
or  senior  high school  level?  Does  that  magnify  the  problem?

A.  I think  it certainly  has been  the experience  in Detroit  that
the  problems  have  been  greater  at  the  upper  secondary  levels
where  their  first  experience  has been  at that  age  level.

Q.  Mr.  Henrickson,  what  about  the  desegregated  experience
where  74  percent  of all black  students  in Detroit  attend  schools  in
1970  that  are  90  percent  or  more  black?  I think  you  referred  to it
as  the  central  city,  or  whatever.  What  about  their  integrated
experiences?

A.  Well,  we  don’t  have  enough  white  kids  to go around  unless

we  sit them  three  abreast  with  a white  kid  in the  middle.

Q.  How  much  do  you  consider  enough  to  go  around,  Mr.

Henrickson?

A.  Enough  to  go  around  in  this  case  would  be  three  abreast
with  a  white  kid  in  the  middle,  and  this  is not  a  proportion  that
can  give  a useful  constructive  experience  to white  or black  [3533]
kids  at  this  time.  I’m  afraid  I  have  come  mepett a y  to  this
conclusion  in the last  three  years.

;

Il  a  151

Q.  So,  you  are  saying  it  is  a  good  experience,  a  beneficial
experience  if  you  have  five,  10,  20  percent  black  in  the  white
schools  and  plus, is that  correct?  It is advantageous?

A.  I don’t  want  to  suggest  that  there  is a numerical  formula.
Q.  Not  necessarily.  I’m  asking  you  based  on  what  it is now.
You  say  you  think  that  is  an  advantage  that  the  white  children
have  this experience?

A.  Yes,  and  I  think  it  was  a  very  helpful  constructful
experience  for  my  daughter  to attend  Miller  Junior  High  when  she
was  virtually  the  only,  if not  the  only,  white  child  there.  But  that
was  about  four  years  ago.  I’m  not  optimistic  at this  point  that  she
could  have  the  same  constructive  experience  there  today  that  she
had  four  years  ago,  or  that  other  white  children  can.

A.  Could  you  restate  the  question?
[3534]  As  I understand  what  you  said,  you  thought  it was  _
good,  of  benefit,  it  was  needed  tohave  black  association  with

are  dealing  with  right  now,  that  it was  advantageous  to  them,  and
it  was  necessary,  and  I’m  asking  you  now  would  it  not  be
advantageous  in  terms  of  isolation  to  put  30  percent,  40 percent
white  in  black  schools,  all  of them?  I’m  not  talking  about  one  or
two  of isolated  instances.  I’m  talking  about  all of them  in terms  of
requiring  both  black  and  white  students,  the  black  in  a
predominantly  black  area  and  the  whites  in a predominantly  white
area,  to  be  required  to  learn  and  live  and  adjust  and  understand
each  other?

‘

A.  I’m  not  opposing  at this point  that  that  could  or  would  be

a constructive  experience.

[3535]  Q,  I  take  it  you  are  not  an  advocate  of  white

separatism?

then  the  Department  of Public  Instructions  in  the  capacity  more
or  less of the  finance  director.

_  membership  report  comes  in a  principal prepares  an_after-

grade;  two,  the  principal  may  or  may  not  have  some  estimate  from

anticipate  a  change  in  organization  or  change  in boundaries  which
would  affect  the enrollment.

pleted  we  —  well,  we  try  to  have  the  boundary  change  forms  all
completed  and  signed  and  announced  within  the  school  about  a

pleted  we  then  take  the  maps  and  make  the  mapping  revisions  and
try  to  have  a  revised  map  or  revised  boundary  guidebook  available

[3848]  Q.  Most  of  those  room  additions  are  additions  of
bases  which  are  not,  shall  we  say,  the  basic  educational  offerings

A.  About  50  per  cent  of the  instructional  stations  in  a high
school  currently  are  classrooms  in the traditional  sense,  and about
50 per  cent  of the  instructional  spaces  are  specialized  laboratories,
specialized  places  for  specialized  instruction.  I would  not  accept
the classification  of either  of those  as  being basic  or  not  basic.
__Q.  When  do youngsters  register  for school?  Or first  let me  ask
you:  How  do  you  assign  pupils  to  schools  for  the  forthcoming
school  year?

A.  The  high  school  students  or  students  who  are  graduating
from  a junior  high school,  are  visited  at their junior  high school  by
the  counselor  during  the  previous  semester  and  are  given  an
opportunity  to  indicate  course  preferences  where  they  are
electives  and  that  sort  of  thing.  And  then  their  records  are  for-
warded  to  the  high school  during  that  semester  before  the  close  of
the  semester  so  that  the  program  for  fall  can  be  made  up  at  the
end  of the term  so  the  programs  are  ready  for the  students  to start
in September.

Q.  When  do  you  have  a  good,  a  pretty  good  definite  idea  of

A.  Well,  -

Q.  By mid  summer?
A.  The  work  or  preparing  for  fall  is done  before  the close  of
school  in  spring.  There’s  not  normally  a  staff  on  hand  in  the
building  for  the  purpose  of  working  on  fall  programs  during  the
summer  so  that  the  counselors  work  in  scheduling  the  students.
The  department  heads  work  in making  up class  schedules  has to be
completed  during  the  last  month  of  school,  and  it  is  normally
handled  in that  way.

Now,  there  are  additional  students  received  at the opening  of
school. There  are  always some  enrollments  that  come  in special  for
them  as  over-the-counter  enrollments.  And  these  have  to be fitted

Ill  a  164

into  the program.  And  if there’s  such  a large number  of them,  or  if
there’s  a  large  number  of students  that  does  not  return,  then  the
programs  schedule  may  have  to  be  revised  in  that  school  in  the
fall.  But  basically  the organization  is done  in the  mene’ and  before
the teachers  leave  on  summer  vacation.

Q.  Do  you  find  most  of the  over-the-counter  enrollments,  as
you  refer  to  them,.occur  at  the  secondary  level  as opposed  to the
elementary  level?

A.  No.

[3850]  Q.  It occurs  at all levels?

A.  It  occurs  at  all  levels  because  they  are  from  —  they  are
from  a number  of sources.  And  one  of the  basic  sources  simply  are
the  moves  that  many  families  make  so  that  at  the  beginning  of the
school  year  there  may  be  a  large  number  of  families  whose
children  appear  of  those  families  that  moved  in  during  the

summer.

Q.  Do  you  have  a  registration  day  which  is  a  few  days
perhaps  in  advance  of  actual  school  opening  to  give  you  some
indication  of what  your  problems are going  to  be?  Do the  counter,
the  counter  enrollments  take  place  on  the  day school  opens  or  do
you  have  those  -  do you  have  a leeway  of a few  days there?

A.  Well,  let  me  —  let  me  say  at  this  point  that  this is not  one
of the  functions  of the  Schoolhousing  Division,  and  I am  speaking
to  the  best  of my  knewledgs, but  I’m  not  in any  of the schools  on
the opening  day.

{3851}  Q.  I  understand  that,  yes,  sir.  It  does  affect  the

Schoolhousing  Division?

A.  It  you  have  large  numbers  then  we  have  to  make  adjust-
ments  at  those  points.  Normally  there  is not,  to  the  best  of  my
knowledge,  there  is  not  an  advance  day  for  new  enrollment,  but
the  students,  all  of the  students  come  and  those  who  are  not,  have
not  previously  had  programs  made  out  go  to  the  counsellors  and
get  programs  and  they  catch  up  with  the  organization  of  the
school  as soon as they  can get a  program  made out.

Q.  Do  you  have  many  situations  where  you  have  an  un-

expected  amount  of opening  day enrollments?

A.  The  principal  situations  of  this  kind  are  the  situations
where  we  do  have  or  where  we  have  large  numbers  of  young

Ill  a  165

families  moving  into  a  new  neighborhood.  This  always  involves  a
considerable  accumulation  of these  families  over  the  summer  but
in those  neighborhoods  it is  a continuing  process  through  the year.
Every  Monday  morning  there  may  be some  new  student  arriving.

Q.  If  1 understand  what  you  are  suggesting,  you  find  that  in
the  area  of  let’s  say  changing  neighborhoods,  or  where  you  have
young  families  moving  in or  usually  where  you  have  black  families
move  in, is that  what  you  are  referring  to?

A.  Yes.

Q.  So  where  you  have  these  areas  of changing  on  the  1970
census[3852]  map  where  it  would  be  further  out  those  are  the
areas  you  run  into  a lot of problems?

A.  Yes.

Q.  Would  you  find  out  in the  white  areas  in the  northeast  and
northwest  your  predictions  are  usually  fairly  stable  aside  from  the

parochial  school;  problem,vhich  flucuates.  as  as  I  understand  it?

A’  In  the  outlying  areas  the  projections  are  more  apt  to  be
very  close  and  by very  close  I mean  within  a  half dozen  or  dozen
and  they  are  more  apt  to all arrive  on  the  first  day of school.

Q.  Within  a  week  before  school  opens  is  it  your  general
experience  that  you  would  know  pretty  well  what  is  going  to
happen  in those  schools?

A.  No.

Q.  From  information  from  the  principals  or  what-have-you?

A.  Not  the  week  before  school  opens.  There  is  no  one  on

hand  to know  what  is going  to happen.

Q.  The  region  superintendent  doesn’t  know?

A.  The  region  superintendent  doesn’t  have  any  way  to have  a

report  from  the  school  because  there  is no  one  at the school.

Q.  The  last  report  he would  have  from  the  school  would  be in

May?

A.  Normally.

Q.  Or June  at the  latest?

[3853]  A.  Yes.

Q.  The  April  7 plan,  as  I understand  it, or  perhaps  I should

EE  EEEEEEEEEEIEEIISSE''S2=S3—-~~-  rr

—e

Ill  a  166

ask  you  to  describe  it —  the  effect  of the  feeder  pattern  changes
was  a  change  of  boundary  line  on  an  east-west  direction?  Could
you  describe  the  feeder  pattern  changes  in  some  sort  of general
terms?

A.  Well,  it was  on  an  east-west  direction  in  most  cases.  With
Western  and  Southwestern  it was  a  north-south  direction  but  the
principal  point  here  is not  that  it was  east-west,  but  that  it was  a
change  of  boundaries  along  principal  transportation  lines  which
for  Northwest  Detroit  are  the  east-west  mile  roads  and  sometimes
the  half-mile  roads,  and  that  there  is much  better  transportation,
more  regular,  more  frequent  in  the  east-westerly  direction  than
there  is in the  north-south  direction.

Q.  I have just  place  up on  the  board  Plaintiffs’  Exhibit  143-A,
which  is  the  April  7,  1970  high  school  zones.  I understand  the
reasons  for  the  east-west  change  and  the effect  of it in appearance,
but  what  I am  interested  in  is the  mechanics  of the  feeder  pattern
schools  and  how  they  were  rearranged.  If you  could  just  give  me
an  example  of  how  you  rearranged  the  junior  high  school  feeder
pattern?

A.  It  was  in  the  Redford-Cooley  area  the  boundary  line  had
been  Southfield  Road  here,  which  left  students  having  a
considerable  amount  of north-south  transportation.  Nevertheless,
Redford  was  [3854]  on  McNichols  Road  here,  Cooley  was  on
Fenkell  here,  Fenkell  and  Hubbell,  so  they  were  both  right  on
east-west  transportation  lines.

The  Redford  High  School  service  area  under  April  7 would
have  extended  the  Redford  service  area  to  the  east  and  bring
students  in  along  the  Six  Mile  coach  all  the  way  from  Livernois
actually  and  at  the  same  time  it  would  have  exchanged  with
Cooley  to  bring  students  to  Cooley  along  the  Fenkell  coach  and
along  the  Schoolcraft  coach.

Q.  What  were  the  schools  involved  in  the  Cooley  area  in that

change?  Was  that  Cerveny  and  Cadillac?

A.  Cerveny,  Cadillac  and  Post.  In  all  cases  the  high  school
service  area  would  have  been  broken  to  serve  the  junior  high
school  service  pattern.  We  were  not  at least  at that  point  proposing
any  changes  in  the  junior  high  feeder  pattern  but  simply  dividing
the  students  on  a  geographic  basis  to  feed  into  the  senior  high
school.

:

Ill  a  167

Q.  If  I  understand  the  way  the  plan  worked,  for  example,

Burns  fed  into  what junior  high school?

A.  Burns  fed  into  Cadillac,  which  is directly  south  of it.

Q.  So it continued  to  feed  into  the  same  school?

A.  Yes,  and  it  would  have  continued  to  feed  under  any  cir-
cumstances.  All  of the  elementary  schools  feeding  into  the junior
high  would  have  continued  to  feed  on  the  same  basis.

A.  Yes.

ary  feeder  schools,  did  the  plan  not  work  so  that  part  of those

A.  Yes,  and  this  was  a  departure  from  the  plan that  we  were

working  on  earlier  in the  decade  as  I described  it.

A.  Yes.

A.  As  an  example  the  Burns,  Cadillac  and  Edison  students

the  present  time  they  all  proceed  together  from  Cadillac Junior
High into  Cooley  Senior  High.

Q.  So  the  April  7th  plan  was a  departure  from  at least that

approach  that  you  tried  to take  in the aia

A.  Yes.

places  throughout  the system  where  the  full  articulation  was  never
achieved  and  the  reasons  for  not  achieving  it, well,  there  are  a
variety  of  reasons  including  school  capacity  and  specialized
reasons  such  as  those  at the  Burton.

A.  That’s  right.

Brownell’s  administration  to  reconvert  it  to  a junior  high  school
sending  [3883]  the  senior  high  school  students  into  the  old
Eastern  High.

was  built  around  1920?

A.  Yes,  I believe  that  would  be correct.

Q.  It  was  built  as  an  intermediate  school  and  subsequently

you  say  it was  converted  to a high school?

A.  Yes.

A  letter  dated  June  22,  1971  was  marked  Joint  Exhibit  1 by

the  reporter  and  reads  as  follows:

[3889]  “DEPARTMENT  SERVICE”

SUBJECT:  Movement  of Transportables

FROM:  Department  of  Planning  and  Building  Studies,  per

Merle  Henrickson

TO:  Dr.  Alvin  G. Skelly,  Deputy  Superintendent

DATE:  June  22, 1971

The  following  proposed  transportable  moves  contain  issues

which  merit  further  examination  and  a  possible  appeal  for
exceptions  to  proceed.

1.  Farwell  to Cooley—2  transportables.

In  this  case  two  transportables  must  be  moved  from  the
Farwell  site  in  order  to  complete  the  grading  and  paving
portions  of  a  construction  contract  nearing  completion.  At
Cooley  there  is also  a  construction  contract  under  way  which
involves  taking  three  rooms  out  of  service.  The  two  trans-
portables  are  intended  to  maintain  the  level  of instructional
“spaces  during  contruction.

2.  Longfellow  to  Redford

Redford  high  school  has  an  anticipated  anrollment  for
September,  1971  of 4,062.  In  addition,  there  are  241  magnet
school  applications.  [3890]  The  additional  rooms  will

Il  a  191

seriously  affect  the  ability  of  Redford  to  accept  magnet
school  applications.

3.  Longfellow  to  Harding
Harding  will  be  approximately  150  over  capacity  in
September.  The  intention  of  the  portables  is  to  reduce  the
scheduled  instructional  use  of the  gymnasium  in order  to pro-
vide  a lunch  program  not  now  available.”

[3891]  Q.  Mr.  Henrickson,  I  believe  you  testified  on

A.  I think  that’s  correct.

A.  Yes.

and  Lafayette  Park?

Q.  Given  this  fact,  Doctor,  of a  significant  decrease  in white
pupil  population  over  the  10-year  period  and a significant  increase
in  black  pupil  population  to  the  point  where  today  or  at least  for
the  past  school  year  of  °70-71  the  pupil  population  census  shows
that  Detroit  enjoyed  a  65  percent  black  population  and  a  34
percent  white  population  and  given  the  additional  factor  that  the
predominant  economic  or  SES  condition  of the  kids  in this district
is poor  to  poverty  level,  what,  in your  opinion,  sir, can  be done  to
develop  a  delivery  [4132]  program  of educational  service  that  will
provide  both  higher  performance,  educational  performance
achievement  for  the  poor  or  poverty  child  and  to  provide a racially
integrated  educational  experience  for all of those  children?

A.  Given  the  facts  as  you  state*them  there  are  two  strategies  I
would  pursue  were  I a  decision  maker  in  this  instance.  I. suppose
the  most  obvious  is  to  begin  to  regard  the  boundaries  of the
Detroit  city  schools  as  little  more  than  arbitrarily  drawn  and
historical  artifacts.

Q.  By boundaries  of the  school,  what  do  you  mean?

A.  I mean  of the  gehool district.
Q.  School  district?

A.  Yes,  such  that  you  would  begin  to  consider  this  entire
metropolitan  "Detroit  area  as  the  basin  in which  school  children
were  located  and  you  began  to  establish  school  attendance  areas
which  did  not  take  school  district  boundaries  into  regard.  By so
doing  as  I understand  the  facts  you  have  given,  one  would  increase
the  likelihood  of gaining  not  only  racial  integration,  which  indeed
I  do  regard  as  important,  but  also  racial  and  social  class
integration,  which  I  think  is  advantageous  in  that  it  leads  to
increased  performance  on  the  part  of  black  students  and  better
attitudes  of  whites  and  so  forth.  That  is  one  strategy  I would
pursue  to  begin  to  disregard  school  discrict  boundaries  and  I
would  do  so  under  [4133]  the  rationale  insofar  as  | know  the
education  is a  state  function  and  the  state  can  draw  boundaries  as
it chooses.

Another  point  to bé considered  is that  I would  imagine  within
the  boundaries  of the  school  districts  of the  City  of Detroit  there
are  non-public  schools,  probably  the majority  of which  are Catholic,

[4330]  Q:  Mr.  Johnson,  would  that  be  true  also  of  the
curricular  and  extracurricular  materials  that  you  and  Mr.  Bushnell
discussed  after  the  recess,  the  radio  and  TV  materials?  You  would
support  their  dissemination  in  all  school  districts  from  the
standpoint  of educational  soundness,  would  you  not?

A.  I  would.  I  want  to  make  it  clear,  however,  that  |
emphasize  two  levels  of  need  or  two  types  of  need  here.
Historically  we  are  talking  about  a  large  segment  of  American
citizens  who  ‘happen  to  be  black  whose  racial  history  has not just
been  distorted  but  in  a  large  measure  denied.  Now, I think  it is of
primary  importance  that  those  students  and  their  families  get  to
know  themselves  and  that  they  are  able  to  reclaim  their  heritage.
That  is  essential.  | can’t  see  it  is  equally  important  to  a  white
student  that  he  knows  that  history.  It  is  important,  and  in  the
nature  of  our  society  I  think  it  is  essential  that  he  knows
something  about  that  experience  as  much  as  black  students.  But,
the  beginning  point  is  that  black  students  know  themselves  and
their  historical  heritage.

Q.  But  Mr.  Johnson,  putting  aside  cultural  self  respect  and
intercultural  respect,  simply  from  the  standpoint  of  an  accurate
presentation  and  educational  history  you  would  support  the
dissemination  of such  material  in all schools,  would  you  not?

A.  Yes,  but  I emphasize  again  you  have  two  problems  here,
on  the  one  hand  the  general  and  equal  distribution  of  history
materials  [4331]  that  are  fair,  and  that  is  to  the  good  of  all
students,  black  and  white,  and  then  in addition  to  that  we  must
recognize  that  we  have  a history  that  would  be something  more  to
black  students  than  white  students,  and  we  have  to  try  to  meet
that, and  that  is what  we  try to do, also.

Mr.  Johnson,  do I understand  your  testimony  to be that as Ex-
ecutive  Secretary  and  as  a member  of the Citizens’  Advisory  [4347]

said.

-

A.  Practices,  yes.

THE  COURT:  Are  there  in  existence  now  discriminatory

A.  I would  have  to  say  that  I think  we  have  the  results,  we
still  live  with  the  results  of  discriminatory  practices.  I am  not

MR.  FLANNERY:  That  is all  I have,  if it  [4348]  please  the

Court.

MR.  BUSHNELL:  No  redirect.
THE  COURT:  I have  one  question,  Mr.  Johnson,  and  I hope
it doesn’t  lead  to  a  lot  of  others:  based  on  your  background  of
age  and  experience  and  your  various  assignments  both  inside  the
School  System  and  outside  but  having  much  contact  with  them,
is  the  Detroit  School  System  a  segregated  system  or  integrated
system  or  neither?

A.  I would  say  it  is  what  we  would  have  to  describe  as  a

segregated  school  system.
THE  COURT:  Why?
A.  Because  you  have  a  large  clear  pattern  of concentration
of black  students  in  certain  schools  and  white  students  in  other
schools,  and  that  is  what  I  know  to  be  a  segregated  school
system.

[4349]  THE  COURT:  Without  indicating  where  you  stand
on  it would  you  say  that  the  drive  of perhaps  the  majority  or  at
least  some  of  the  black  community  of  Detroit  toward
decentralization  of  the  school  system  in  Detroit  had  its  focus  on
a goal  of control  of their  schools?

A.  I think  that  the  drive  for  school  decentralization,  your
Honor,  had  its  focus  on  something  slightly  different  or  more
than  control.  I think  that  it  was  a  drive  to  really  connect  the
black  community  with  the  controls  of  the  school  system  in  a
meaningful  way  to  such  extent  that  this  community  could  feel
that  it had  equal  stake  in  the  whole  system  and  equal  access  to
the power  of the system.  -
Now,  I  know  that  the  political  activist  who  made  the
Propaganda  campaign  about  community  control  talked  about
black  control  of  black  schools,  but  that  was  always  to  me  the
extreme  statement  of  the  politics  of  the  process  and  it did  not

III  a  230

—y

necessarily  represent  the  basic  aspirations  of  the  black
community.  As  I  tried  to  say  earlier, | think  it  was  important
that  historically  this  is  a community  that has  felt  and  lately  has
come  to  recognize  that  it really  had  little  access  to  the  power  of
the  system  and  did  not  share  the  power  in  the  controls  of
program  development,  mechanisms  equally  with  whites,  and  in
an  effort  to  remedy  this  and  to,  in  a  sense,  establish  or
re-establish  that  link  between  [4350]  community  life  and  school
life  the  community  moved  in  the  direction  of  decentralization.
In  that  sense  I  think  it  has  been  a  rather  valid  and  legitimate
process  of  renewal  of  the  relationship  that  should  exist  between
schools  and  the  community.

THE  COURT:  Well,  visualizing  ieeptioatienity  a  possible
metropolitan  system,  do  you  seen  any  inconsistencies  between
trying  to  achieve  integration  and  yet  holding  onto  community
control  of the  schools?

A.  Not  so  much  as  an  inconsistency  but  as  some  break  in
the  pattern  of  community  development  and  thinking  around  the
schools  at  the  present  time.  I suggest  that  I don’t  think  that  it is
totally  inconsistent,  because  I  would  assume  even  with  the
metropolitan  approach  if that  were  developed  that  with  it would
go  the  right  of  people  to  move  which  would  give  body  and
substance  to  this  beyond  just  the  movement  of  students.  I’m
talking  about  where  people  live.  But  there  are  some  hazards  in
this  for  school  decentralization,  and  whatever  people  may  have
in  mind,  when  we  talk  about  control  as  this  process  is  now
conceived  and  even  as  it  is  being  implemented  there  are  some
real  hazards  there,  and  frankly,  your  Honor,  I do  think  that  we
are  under  heavy  obligation  to  serve  both  needs.  There  is need  in
the  big  urban  school  systems  to  bring  schools  in  the  community
back  together  in  [4351]  a  way  that  they  have  not  been,  to
reduce  the  distance  and  to  increase  the  involvement  of  parents
in  the  community  in  the  education  process.  I do  think  that  is
essential  in  any  development  of  the  school  system.  Anything
that  tends  to  frustrafe  that  I think  imperils  the  development  of
a quality  program.

.

At  the  same  time  I believe  that  integration  is  required  to
have  a  truly  quality  educational  program  for  black  and  white.
:
students.

So, our  command  is to  find  a  way  to  do  both, and  I know  that  is
extremely  difficulty.

ate  School  District.  But,  as  the  Court  well  knows,  these  funds
[4621]  from  government  grants  have  to  be  funneled  through  a
designated  governmental  grantee  who  is the contracting  agent.  The
City  of Detroit,  for instance,  is the contracting  agent  for all Model
Neighborhood  funds,  even  though  those  funds  are  spent  by  the
Detroit  School  District  on  Projects  developed  by them.  There  has
been  no  testimony  from  this witness,  nor  has there  been  testimony
from  any  witness  that  this  was  indeed  a  project  of  the  Wayne
County  Intermediate  District,  or  that  the  Wayne  County  Inter-

system  that  we  —  that  I would  prefer  not  to give  such  a system
further  excuse  for  systematic  differentiation  paralleling  racial
lines.  So,  my  answer  is in Detroit  specifically  I’m  afraid  I would
have  to say  yes,  not  because  that  is an  ideal  educational  practice,
but because  I have observed  something  much  less than an ideal
educational  practice  in what  little  data  I have  seen  here.

Q.  So your  answer  to the question  is yes?
A.  Yes.
Q.  Thank  you.  Why  in  this  series  of exhibits  did  you  look

only at local  funds and  did you  take  out  federal  funds?

A.  I will  give  you  two answers.  First,  in Plaintiffs’  Exhibit  91

Ill a 240

only  Board  funds  were  given.  That  was  the  original  source
document  from  which  we worked.  But  more  than  that  that’s  what
I thought  ought  to  be  used,  and  I had  considerable  discussion
about  this  first.  Here  is why:  as  I understood,  the  purpose  of my
exhibit  was  to  give  a  fair  summary  of the  Detroit  School  Board's
educational  efforts  inasfar  as  those  efforts  could  be  simply
measured,  related  to  obviously  educationally  relevant  procedures
such  as  teaching  expenditures,  and  we  could  [4667]  examine  the
degree  to  which  they  did  or  did  not  parallel  differences  in  the
racial  composition  of  schools.  But  the  key  thrust  of the  issue  at
least  as  it  was  put  to  me  was  to  summarize  the  Board's  efforts.
Now  that,  it  seemed,  simply  ruled  out  mixing  federal  monies,
which  are  highly  targeted  in  their  many  forms  and  particularly
Title  1 which  are  highly  targeted,  from  the  Board  monies.  I think  it
may  be really  a  matter  of separate  interest  as  to where  the  federal
monies  go.

Q.  So,  the  purpose  of  using  Board  funds  was  to  determine
when  the  Detroit  Board  of  Education  was  acting  in  a
discriminatory  manner  and  not  whether  the  students  in  the
individual  schools  were  receiving  an  equal  educational
opportunity,  is that  right?

A.  Well,  not  in  bulk.  After  all,  the  Detroit  Public  School
doesn’t  control  the  whole  lives  of  the  children.  But  it was  only
those  parts  of the  measured  school  experience  of those  children
which  were  under  the  direct  control  of the  Detroit  Public  Schools
and  which  were  a  direct  measure  of  that  system’s  efforts  as  a
matter  of its own  policies.

Q.  But  you  are  in  agreement  that  the  federal  funds  also
to  the  educational  experience  which  children  in

contribure
individual  schools  experience  or  receive?

A.  Well, again  I’m  certainly  in agreement  with  the  idea  that  in
Detroit  the  federally-targeted  schools  are  concentrated  in  [4668]
black  districts.

Q.  No,  that  is not  the  question  I asked.  I’m  asking  whether  a
teacher  whose  salary  is paid  by federal  funds  teaches  children,  and
those  children’s  education  is affected  by that.  It seems  to me  that

A.  Sir,  you  shift away from  my  numbers  to  ask  me  whether

Il a 241

in general  isn’t  it a  fact  that  federal  effort  is expended  in such  a
way  that  it benefits  children.  I think  in general  it is.

Q.  No,  that  is  not  what  I’m  asking.  I’m asking  whether  a
teacher  who  conducts  a class  in a classroom  has come  effect  on  the
students  in that  classroom.

Q.  Has  some,  but  what  the  effect  is is going  to depend  upon

the character  of the  system  in which  they operate.

Q.  Of course.

A.  I do  not  have  evidence  here  that  allows  me  to testify  as  to
whether  any  of  these  resources  do  directly  benefit  one  child  or
another.  So,  I’m  just  trying  to  make  it  clear  when  you  ask  a
question  like  that  I will  ultimately  be  pressed  into  giving  you  my
opinion,  but  that  certainly  —  I don’t  have  evidence  on  it here.

Q.  Exhibit  163  and  164  include  a  figure  which  is  average

teacher  and  ESRP  annual  salary.

A.  Yes.

Q.  And  there  is  a  difference  between  the  0 to  10  percent
black  [4669]  schools  and  the  90 to 100 percent  black  schools.  The
figures  in  the  0 to  10 percent  schools  are  12,985,  and  in the  90 to
100, 11,661,  is that  correct?

A.  Yes.

Q.  What  accounts  for that  difference?

A.  I  can  only  tell  you  what  parallels  it,  and  you  must
remember  I have  only  the  School  Board’s  documents.  The  School
Board  does  not  account  for  this  difference.  I can,  of course,  tell
you  that  from  what  I observed  from  the  percentages  under  salary
class  6 or  over  salary  class  10  that  there  is a  strong  tendency  for
those  salary  classes  to  parallel  the  average  teacher  salary  in  the
“school.  So,  the  only  information  related  to  that  which  I have  was
to  read  status  of teachers  and  the  School  Board’s  category  known
as Salary  class.  Both  of those  certainly  parallel  this  figure.

Q.  What  does  salary  class represent,  do you  know?
A.  I do  not  know.  I have  asked  repeatedly  to  have  it broken

down  for me.

Q.  Would  it  be  reasonable  to  you  if  I told  you  that  that
represents  the  experience  of the  teachers,  and  that  there  are  two

lil a 242

factors  in the  teachers’  total  salary  in  that  one  is degree  level  and
the  other  is level  of experience  or  years  of experience?

A.  If  you  told  me  those  were  the  only  two,  I would  be
surprised.  But  those  are  the  predominant  two  in  almost  every
school.

[4670]  Q.  Would  you  also believe  if reasonable  if 1 told  you
that  previous  evidence  in this case  has indicated  that  85 percent  of
the  difference  in  teachers’  salaries  or  average  teacher  salaries  is
accounted  for  by differences  in  average  levels  of experience  or  in
position  in the salary  step?

A.  Well,  we  have  a $1200  difference.  You  mean  85 percent  of

1200  is accounted  for by experience?

Q.  Yes.
A.  | would  strongly  disagree.

Q.  And  how  would  you  account  for  it?

A.  I would  account  for  the  85  percent  in the  following  way:
I’m  willing  to  guess,  sir -  and  I’m  guessing,  now  —  that  someone
said  85  percent  of the  variance  is explained,  some  in  annual  rate
by some  factor.  The  percentage  of variance  has nothing  to do with
a  percent  of  a  difference.  There  is  a  $1300  difference  here.  85
percent  of variance  may  be explained  by a factor,  and  none  of this
difference  be  due  to  that  factor.  Those  are  two  completely
different  and  potentially  unrelated  things.

Q.  I will  ask  you  again  what  would  you  say  accounts  for  this

difference?

A.  Again  as  a  matter  of fact  I do  not  know.  I only  have  the

Board  expenditures  on  teachers.

Q.  You  have  no  reason  to  doubt  that  it might  be  accounted

for by [4671]  differences  in experience?

A.  Right,  experience  or  degrees,  both  contribute.

Q.  Thank  you.  The  class  size  figures  in those  two  exhibits,  do
they exclude  federal  funds  and  federal  teachers,  the  computations?
the computations?

A.  Class  size?

Q.  Yes.

HI a  243

A.  There  would  be no  federal  fund  in class size.
Q.  Well,  class  size  seems  to  me  dependent  on  the  number  of

teachers  in a school.
A.  Right.

Q.  And  if you  are  taking  out  federal  teachers  then  it is going

to have  an  effect.

A.  There  are  no  funds  involved  at  all  in  the  computation.
There  are  only numbers  of kids and  teachers,  and  these  numbers  in
my summary  sheets  do exclude  the  federal  teachers.

Q.  So  that  in  a  school  which  has  certain  class  size  in your
prior exhibit,  and  if half of the  teachers  in that  school  were  federal
teachers,  the  class  size  figures  in  your  new  exhibit  for that  school
would  be double  what  it was  before,  is that right?
A.  In your  hypothetical  example,  yes.
Q.  So  that  you  have  excluded  federal  teachers,  but  you
haven’t  excluded  the  students  who  are  taught  by  those  federal
teachers.

[4672]  A.  Again  the  new  table,  yes.  But,  that’s  why we have

the old, and  we  can  see  whether  there  is a large difference.

Q.  You  have  excluded  the  students?
A.  No, in the  new  table  -

Q.  So, you  have  —
MR.  CHACHKIN:  Let  him  finish  the answer,
A.  In  the  new  table  we  have  excluded  federal  teachers.  In the
old  table  we  have  included  the  federal  teachers.  So if you  want  to
see  it current  and  observable  but  not  attributable,  just observable
pupil teacher  ratio,  you  look  at the single  letter series.

Q.  Except  that  that  series,  I believe,  was  based  on  October

enrollment  figures,  wasn’t  it, and  February  teacher  figures?

A.  Yes,  it was.  But,  again  I think  there  has to be throughout
this such  small  differences  when  only enrollment  was  affected  that
it is very  unlikely  that  those  numbers  would  change  at all due  to
the  substitution  of one  enrollment  from  another.  The  fact  is that

OE  _  __  C

7

Ill a 244

children  drop  out  of  school  from  October  to  February.  But  it
would  be  a  very  harsh  indictment  of  the  Detroit  Public  School
System  in its own  right  to  argue  that  the  black  kids  are  dropping
out  at a far higher  rate  than  white  kids.
&

Q.  It would  be a harsh  indictment  if 6,000  children  graduated

in September?

[4673]  A.  Unequally,  only  whites  graduating  and  not  blacks,

MR.  LUCAS:  I have  certain  statements  of  Doctor  Drachler
and  Mr.  Arthur  Johnson  made  to  the  Committee  on  Equal
Educational  Opportunity  on  Tuesday,  May  12,  1970.  These
excerpts  have  been  shown  to  counsel,  and  I understand  he has no
objection  to my  reading  them  into  the record.

The  first  one  appears  on  page  316  of the  volume.  There  is a
statement  of Doctor  Drachler,  and  in part  from  that  statement  it
says:

“On  April  7, 1970,  the  Board  of Education  approved a plan
for seven  regions.  Within  this plan was  a recommendation  from  the
superintendent  to  change  the  feeder  patterns  of  eighteen  senior
high  schools  so  that  over  a period  of three  years  twelve  senior  high
schools  invoiving  35,000  students  would  achieve  greater
integration.

“The  plan  changed  the  boundary  lines  between  certain  high
schools  to  run  east  and  west  instead  of north  and south.  The  plan
had  the  support  of the  Michigan  [4675]  Civil  Rights Commission,
the  Interfaith  Council,  the  NAACP,  the  Urban  League,  the  United
States  Civil  Rights  Commission,  and  the  United  States  Commis-
sioner  of  Education.  But  nevertheless,  those  who  were  directly
involved,  particularly  the white  community,  showed  opposition  to

Ill a 245

the  extent  that  they  began  a  drive  to  recall  the  four  board
members  who  voted  for the  plan, and  their  impact  upon  the State
Legislature  was  so great  that  two days after the Board  adopted  the
plan  the  State  House  of  Representatives  amended  Act  244 so  that
the  city  would  be  divided  into  not  less  than  eleven  regions,
decreasing  opportunities  for  integration;  stated  that  the  high
school  boundaries  must  be  drawn  in  such  a  manner  that  each
student  that  attends  the  closest  high  school  to  his  home,  and
legislated  that  the  plan  when  adopted  must  be  approved  by the
people  in the referendum.

“The  Senate,  recognizing  that  the  House  bill  was  probably
unconstitutional,  simply  repealed  decentralization  on  the  next
day.  Thus  the  State  Senate  placed  the  total  burden  for  the  move
to integrate  upon  the  Detroit  Board  of Education.”

That  is  the  end  of the  part  I wish  to  read.  It does  go  on  to

refer that:

“Both  bills are  now  in committee.”
MR.  BUSHNELL:  Has  the  source  of  this  [4676]  beén

identified?

MR.  LUCAS:  Yes.
MR.  BUSHNELL:  Okay.

~Y

MR.  LUCAS:  The  next  is a  quote  from  Mr.  Arthur  Johnson,
Deputy  Superintendent,  under  the heading Equal  Educational Op-
portunity-Components,  and it is on  page  340 and  341.  It says:

“This  goes  all  the  way  from  the  question of the  number  of
dollars  that  may  be spent  on  the  education  of the children  to the.
matter  of achievement  scores,  to the question  of physical  facilities
as well  as the programs  that  we  can  provide  for them.

“So,  in a real  sense  we  are  faced  with  the problem,  I think,  of
bringing  to  a  system  like  Detroit  those  conditions  which  will  at
least  make  it possible  for poor  black  and  for  poor  white  to share
equally  in that  which  is available,  and  they are  not  now  able  to do
that.
“They  cannot  do it under  segregated  conditions  which  exist,
and  they  cannot  do it under  the terms of the  financial  aid  formula
which  exists  in the state.

:

Ill a 246

“I would  like  to go one  step  further,  and  my  concern  here  was ©
suggested  by a question  which  Senator  Hughes  raised.  It is enough  ~
to  be  a  victim  of  the  kind  of economic  segregation  conditions
which  exist  in  a community  [4677]  like  Detroit,  which  Doctor
Drachler  described  in the charts  and  maps.

“But  when  you  add  the  problem  of  racial  isolation,  which, ” 2
ly, in many  respects  makes  it impossible  for the  children,  even ~
of the disadvantaged  to  take  from  other  children  what  they might © ;
Ae able  to  receive  as  well  as  to  give,  then  the  problem  is
compounded.

“This  is, as  I see  it, a very  stiffling  condition in  any  attempt  to

achieve  quality  education,  by the  best  standards  we  can  describe
or  identify.”

The  last  item  of  Doctor  Drachler  appears  on  page  350.  rt

refers  to racial  isolation, and  black  self-image

“I might  add  that in  relation  to  the aie that  we  propose;

a group  of black  parents,  supporting  integration,  who  were  worried
that  their  children  will  not  be  able  to compete  educationally  with 4
the youngsters in  the new  school.

4

“| had  to  show  them  that  their children’s  scores  were  highe :

than  some  of  the  children  in  the  school  that  they  are  about  to”
enter.  The  self-image  that  they had  of themselves  is that  if a school
4g
is totally  black,  then  its students  are  not  doing as well.
“T do  not  blame  the parents.  Segregation,  [4678]  attitudes o!
whites,  instructional  materials  all contribute  to this image.”  4

[47]  PATRICK  A.  McDONALD,  resumed  the  stand  as  a
witness  herein,  and  having  been  previously  duly  sworn,  testified
as follows:
further

[50]...

eK

A.  Basically,  Plan  C talks  in terms  of  the  transporting  or
busing  of  children  certain  days  of  the  week  to  certain  paired
schools.  I don’t  think  that  that  would  accomplish  the  goal  of
either  integration  or  desegregation.
pe

[57]...
Q.(By  Mr.  Flannery)  Mr.  McDonald,  you  testified  that  the
concept  we  are  discussing  had  proved  in  the  pilot  program
relatively  unsuccessful  at the  high school  level,  is that  correct?

A.  Yes,  sir.

Q.  It is fair  to  say,  is it not,  on  the  basis  of the  November  |
report  on  progress  of the  magnet  school  program  at page  16 and  I
am  quoting:

“The  impact  of  student  move,  both  at  the  high  school  and
middle  school  level  on  the  racial  balance  of  identical  schools
throughout  the  city has generally  been  less  than  2 percent.”

That  was  the  conclusion,  am  I correct  in my  [58]  reading  of

the conclusion  at page  16 of the  November  | report?

A.  I don’t  think  ‘that  is a conclusion.  I think  it is one  sentence
in a multi-page  document.  I think  what  they are  indicating  there  is
the  fact  that  this  was  a  pilot  project,  that  it wasn’t  designed  to
affect  more  than  a great  number  of students  and  they  are  talking
about  the  actual  student  movement.  The  actual  number  of
students  affected,  of course,  is much  beyond  2 percent.

Q.  Can  you  tell  me,  sir,  at  the  high  school  level  how  many
white  students  chose  whiter  schools  than  those  they  were  attend-
ing?

A.  No,  sir,  I don’t  have  that  statistic  at my  fingertips.  It is in

the progress  report.

Q.  Would  it be far off to say  approximately  836?

A.  I really  don’t  know  what  the  number  would  be.  I would
point  out  when  you  say  whiter  schools,  I think  we  are  into  value
judgments  as  to what  is a whiter  school  or  not.

Q.  A higher  percentage  —

EE EEE_

es

|

A.  (Continuing)  What  isan  integrated  or  desegregated  school.

Q.  A  higher  percentage  of  white  students  than  the  school
from  which  they  were  moved  or  which  they  previously  attended?

A.  I don’t  know  the  exact  number  and  I don’t  think  the
number  is really  important,  because  if you  take  a white  student  at
a  school  which,  say,  had  300  black  students  and  200  [59]  white
students  and  one  white  student  left  to  go  to a school  where  there
were  more  white  students,  say  50  percent  white  and  50  percent
black,  you  might  say  that  is not  helping  the  integration  process.  |
would  submit  that  it is because  you  have  taken  a  student  from  a
segregated  situation  and  put  him  in an  integrated  situation  and  the
fact  whether  it is one  white  or  two  white  or  three  white  students
wouldn’t  really  make  a  great  deal  of difference  in  the  school  he
came  from.

The  point  we  are  trying  to  do  is really  to  have  an  integrated

situation  as  opposed  to a mere  1,2 or  3 percent  in a school.

_

Q.  Without  quarreling,  Mr.  McDonald,  with  the  value  judg-
ments,  would  you  turn  to  page  16 of the  report  to  which  I have
referred  and  read  aloud  for  the  Court  starting  at  the  second  full
paragraph  “‘White  students”  —?

(Reading)  “White  students,  on  the  other  hand,  did  not  move
into  majority  black  schools  in  any  large  numbers.  Only  nine  of
those  who  changed  schools  selected  a  curriculum  offered  in  a
majority  black  school.  (An  additional  85  chose  to remain  to their
neighborhood  schools  for  the  magnet  curriculum  even  though  the
school’s  racial  balance  left  them  in a relatively  small  minority.)

{60]  “Of  the  520  white  students  who  left  majority  black
schools  to enroll  in majority  white  schools,  326  came  from  schools
which  are  more  than  90 percent  black  in their  enrollment.  ”

I think  that  merely  re-enforces  what  I am  saying  that  if you
take  a  student,  a white  student,  from  a school  that  is well  over  the
90  percentile  in  black  students  already,  you  really  haven't
segregated  the  school.  It is already  segregated.

Q.  But,  neverthless,  were  those  the  data  that  you  had  in mind
when  you  testified  earlier  that  at  the  high  school  level  the  program
had  not  been  effective?

A.  I  think  my  testimony  is  that  it  has  been  relatively
ineffective.  It  has  been  effective  in  four  or  five  schools  and  being
of some  assistance,  but  it  is not  as  effective  as  it  should  be, and  I
am  certainly  not  satisfied  with  it.

1Va3

Q.  If  I understood  the  future  of  the  high  school  program
there  will  be  four  general  curriculum  high  schools,  is that  correct,
with  approximately  1500  kids  each?

A.  Yes,  sir, that  is correct.
Q.  And  they  will  be  balanced,  did  I understand  that  to  be

your testimony?

A.  Yes,  sir,  through  the  same  process  that  we  used  in  the
[61]  middle  schools,  by application,  keeping  that  balance  as  close
to 50-50  as  possible.

Q.  Well,  sir,  suppose  that  the  choice  system  that  you  plan  for
the  middle  schools  does  not  result  in the  four general  high schools
being  balanced,  does  the  system  have  a  mandatory  alternative  in
mind?

A.  Not  in contemplation  of this  particular  plan.
Q.  What  reason  do  you  have  to  believe  that  the  four  general

schools  will each  in fact  be approximately  50-50?

A.  First,  they  will  be  the  only  four  general  curriculum  high
schools  in the City of Detroit  and  the  school  district  of Detroit  has
on  the  high  school  level  about  70-some  thousand  students,  so  that
we  are  talking  about  6,000  out  of  a  possible  70,000  electing
general  curriculum.  Inasmuch  as  that  is the  usual  program  now  it
would  be  most  improbable  that  at  least  6,000  would  not  so  elect.
Secondly—Q.  (Interposing)  Excuse.  me.  May I interrupt  you
there.  Do  you  have  any  projections  by  race  concerning  which
curricula,  including  general,  high school  pupils do elect?

A.  Projections?
Q.  Yes, sir.
A.  No, sir.
Q.  I’m  sorry,  Continue  with  your  answer.

eee

{70}  Q.  Mr.  McDonald,  turning  to  an  unnumbered  page
one-fifth  of the  way  through  I would  ask  you  to  begin  at the  top
of  the  page,  if you  will,  and  read  that  page  aloud  for  the  record
through  the  first  full  paragraph  on  the  following  page.  I don’t
mean  to restrict  you  to that,  but  if you  will  do so.

A.  (Reading)

“First  introduced  in  September,  1957,  the  Science  and  Arts
Program  was  offered  at  Cass  Tech  and  Mumford  High
Schools.

IVa4

“The  program  operated  on  the  basis  of  invitations  to  the
top  5 percent  in academic  achievement  throughout  the  city.

“From  the  beginning,  this  program  attracted  large numbers
of  students  to  Cass  Tech  and  relatively  smaller  numbers  to
Mumford.  As  the  movement  of  black  families  into  the
Mumford  area  began  to  change  the  racial  composition,  it
affected  the  academic  reputation  of Mumford  in the  eyes  of
many  parents.  The  program  then  declined  rapidly  at Mumford
and  large numbers  of white  students  from  Mumford  elected  to
go to Cass.

“In  the  summer  of  1965  the  movement  of white  students
with  high  academic  achievement  from  Mumford  to  Cass
became  a  matter  of  serious  [71]  concern  to  the  parents’
organization  in  the  Mumford  area.  As  a  result  of  their
expression  of  their  concern  to  the  Board  of  Education  the
basis of the science  and arts program  was re-examined  and a
new  plan  announced  for  the  fall  of  1965.  Under  the  new
program  four  centers  for  Science  and  Arts  were  established  as
Cass,  Mumford,  Mackenzie  and  Pershing,  the  latter  three
schools  all  undergoing  racial  change  and  having  grave  fears
over  the  loss  of high-achieving  students  and decline  in general
levels  of achievement.

“With  the  introduction  of four centers  for Science  and  Arts
programs,  each  high  school  was  assigned  a  geographic  area
from  which  it could  draw  its  students.  The  Science  and  Arts
attendance  area  for  Cass  Tech  was  limited  principally  to  the
inner  city  while  the  other  three  schools  had  the  opportunity
of recruiting  from  outlying  white  high schools.

“The  revised  basis  for  the  Science  and  Arts  Program  was
further  supported  by a Title  I funded  program  for  improved
services  in the  three  schools.

“The  revised  basis  for the Science  and  Arts Program  failed  to
attract  a  significant  [72]  number  of  students  to  the  three
programs.  Simultaneously  most  of  these  students  who  |
strongly  decided  to  escape  from  Cass Tech  were  able  to do so
by enrolling  in other  specializations  which  the  school  offered.
“In  the  fall  of  1970  Science  and  Arts  Programs  will  be
continued  only  at  Cass  Tech  and  will  be  opened  only  to
students  from  the entire  city.”

Q.  Mr.  McDonald,  if I understood  your  testimony  correctly,
the  magnet  schools  would  be  in  your  judgment  of  significantly
enhanced  educational  quality?

A.  Yes, sir.
Q.  And  they would  involve  greater  per-pupil  expenditures?
A.  Yes, sir.
Q.  Mr.  McDonald,  what  would  happen  with  respect  to  the
per-pupil  expenditures  at  schools  attended  by  children  in  those
grades which  were  not  magnet?

A.  It  would  depend  upon  the  total  budget  of  the  school

system  and  what  funds are  available.

Q.  And  if  supplementary  special  funds  from  some  source
were  not  available  you  would  be  faced  with  the  prospect,  would
you  not,  apart  from  race,  of running  a dual  system?

A.  No.  We  would  be  faced  with  the  prospect  of expending

more  sums  in some  schools  and  less sums  in other  schools.
Q.  All right,  sir, I will accept  that  amendment.

funds  are  being  expended  or  to  a  magnet  where  he  knows  more
funds  are  being  expended  and  the  child  is going  to  engage  in an
integrated  experience.

because  we  would  run  out  of choices  when  we  ran  out  of white

Progress  report.

;

bus transportation  to any  great  extent?

vestigation  of school  transportation  problems.

Q.  (By  Mr.  Beer,  continuing)  Mr.  Kuthy,  Dr. Gordon  Foster,
who  Plaintiff has informed  me will testify  as their expert  in pre-
senting  the  Detroit  Plan  which  Plaintiffs  have  propounded
estimates  that  his  plan  would  require  the  transportation  of some
75,000  children  within  the  City  of  Detroit,  not  counting  those
who  ride  regularly-scheduled  bus  lines.  Would  you  have  a  pro-
fessional  opinion  as to how  many  buses  would  be required  to
transport  that  many  children?

A.  I would  estimate  that  approximately  750  buses  would  be
required  to  transport  some  75,000  students.  The  numbers  I have
arrived  at  for  that  particular  coefficient  is primarily  based  on  the
experience  in both Genesee  County  and  in Muskegon  County.

In  Genesee  County  almost  50,000  students  are  transported
with  a  fleet  of approximately  435  to  450  vehicles.  In Muskegon
County  some  193  vehicles  were  used  for  approximately  18,000
students  being  transported.  That  works  out  in  one  instance  to
about 90 students  per vehicle  and in the other  about  108 or 109
students  per vehicle.

I would  submit  that  on  the basis  of that  and  experience  I have
had  in  looking  at  other  school  situations  that  a  rough  test  of

IlVa9

approximately  100  students  per  vehicle  is  an  adequate  way  to
measure  your vehicle  requirements.

(123)  I  further  substantiate  that  on  the  basis  that  that
accommodates  not  only the precise  number  of vehicles  for student
carriage  plus,  includes  the  spare  vehicles  for  backup,  breakdown,
maintenance  and  in  addition  to  that,  as  all  averages,  it  acco-
mmodates  the  proposition  that  some  school  districts  in  the
County  are  of  long  distances  and  hence  long  runs  in  terms  of
mileage  and  in  terms  of  running  time.  It  accomodates  the  dif-
ferences  in  school  start  and  quit  times  and  the  spread  between
various  schools  start  and  quit  times  and  accommodates  vagaries  of
geography  and  the rest.

constitutes  more  than  one  run  per bus per day, is that  correct?

Q.  I  take  it  that  the  average  of  one  bus  per  100  pupils
A.  Yes.  Our  studies  in both  Genesee  and  Muskegon  Counties

showed  wide  variances  in the number  of runs  per bus.

when  all of these  average  factors  boil down  to the average  of each

per run?

A.  If I understand  that  question  correctly  you  would  like  me

Vall

to  compare  the  feasibility  of  school  busing  within  the  City  of
Detroit  as  opposed  to the  three—county  metropolitan  area?

Q.  Yes,  assuming  a  unified  basis  for  that  transportation

A.  Yes.  I think  probably  the  first  place  to  start in. analyzing
that particular  question  would  be to look  at it from  the standpoint
of short  term  implementation  and  the  longer—term  [133]  imple-
mentation  and  cost  of savings.

s

Q.  Fine.

A.  Initially  there  would  be  a  serious  question  in my  mind  if
the  Detroit  School  District  alone  were  to  have  a  school  busing
transportation  system  provided  here.  Initially  it  would  be  my
feeling  that  the  required  number  of buses  to be purchased,  should
they  be  public  transit  vehicles,  you  would  probably  not  be able  to
get 750  buses,  as  I understand  the  number,  for the  fall  term.

I base  my  answer  primarily  on  the  fact  that  the  production  of
diesel  transit  vehicles  is  primarily  in  two  manufacturers  in  the
country,  the  total  production  of one  of  those  manufacturers  last
year  was  only  about  four  times  the  number  of buses  that  we  are
talking  about  here.

In addition  to  that  1 would  assume——  I will  qualify  that  ——
the  Department  of  Street  Railways  in  Detroit  in  the  past  has
purchased,  in  the  past  few  years,  exclusively  all  of  its  vehicles
under  Federal  Government  grant  programs  through  the  Urban
Mass  Transportation  Administration.  I am  familiar  with  the
processing  time  to  obtain  such  grants  and  I would  feel  that  the
timing  would  not  be  sufficient  unless  some  emergency  procedure
were  made  other  than  which  is currently  followed  in  the  Urban
Mass Transportation  Administration.

The  processing  of capital  grants,  I have  [134]  processed  one
of  these  myself  and  participated  in  it in  the  City  of Flint,  Michi-
gan.  It takes  from  six months  to one  year.

In  addition  to  that  diesel  transit  vehicles  being  used  exclu-
sively  in  the  City  of  Detroit  would  require  the  Department  of
Street  Railways  to  either  purchase  additional  or  rent  additional
maintenance  facilities  other  than  that  which  they  presently  have,
unless  the  decision  were  made  to store  these  vehicles  outside  and  I

1Val2

—

have  already  recited  the  difficulties  with  that  particular  question.

From  a short  term  basis  it seems  to me  that  it would  be easier
and  more  implementable  to  look  at  this  on  a  tri—county  basis
where  there  already  is a  base  school  bus  transportation  system  in
all  three  of the  counties  in  the western  portion  of Wayne  County,
in  a  good  portion  of Oakland  and  Macomb  Counties.  There  they
have  school  bus repair facilities  and  servicing  facilities.  They  have  a
cadre  of school  bus drivers  and  they have  established  driver  educa-
tion  programs  and  in my  estimation  it would  be  easier  and  faster
to implement  this program  if it were  on  a metropolitan  basis.

The question  of vehicle  availability  I think  could  be answered
as  well.  There  are  six  principal  manufacturers  of  school  bus
equipment.  The  traditional  time  for  ordering  school  bus  equip-
ment  is in March  and  April  of a  [135]  calendar  year  for  delivery
for  the  fall  semester.  It  would  seem  to  me  more  feasible  at  this
time  to acquire  school  bus vehicles  certainly  new.  If decisions  were
made  to  acquire  used  vehicles,  that  would  be  a  different  story
altogether.

Another  compelling  reason,  it  strikes  me,  for  looking  at  the
metropolitan  solution  to  this  particular  problem  is based  on  the
work  my  organization  did  in both  Genesse  County  and  Muskegon
County  where  total  public  cost  savings  over  the  long  term  from
consolidating  public  school  operations  and  using  a  larger  base
realized  economies  of scale  that  are  extremely  important  both  in
the  short  term  and  in the  long term.  Capital  equipment  economies
are  realized  in this fashion  and operating  economies  are realized  in
this fashion.

Q.  Let  me  interrupt  you  just  a  minute.  When  you  talk  about
consolidation,  do  you  mean  consolidating  between  school  districts
or  are  you  referring  to  consolidation  between  school  districts  and
public  transit?

A.  I refer  to both  issues.

Q.  I see.

A.  If  these  economies  stand,  a  substantial  number  of
economies  result  from  the  consolidation  of all school  districts  and
another  order  of  economies  are  achieved  in  the  consolidation
[136]  of public  school  busing  with  public  mass  transit  operations.

IVa  13

I  further  submit  that  the  institutional  framework  to
accomplish  a  reasonable  busing  solution  is  here  in  terms  of  a
regional  transportation  body,  Southeastern  Michigan  Transporta-
tion  Authority,  so  that  the  framework  for  such  an  effort,  in my
opinion,  is extant  at the  present  time.

Q.  Does  the jurisdiction  of that  body  include  at least  Wayne,

Oakland  and  Macomb  Counties?

A.  Yes,  it  includes  six  counties,  the  three  you  mentioned,
Monroe  County,  I  believe  St.  Clair  County  and  Washtenaw
County.

Q.  In  your  professional  opinion,  Mr.  Kuthy,  would  there  be
any  other  available  administrative  structure  within  which  to
operate  such  a  coordinated  transportation  system?

A.  I think  not.
Q.  Mr.  Roumell  reminds  me  I left  one  fact  off  your  vita
which  I  would  note  for  the  record.  You  are  a  member  of  the
Oakland  County  Planning  Commission?

A.  Lam, sir.

A.  Yes.  The  Genesee  County  School  System,  I can’t  answer

but  I can’t  answer  with  certainty  the  total  number  of students  in
either  of those  counties.

Q.  Can  you  give  us  an  approximation,  sir, of the  numbers  of

of the total  numbers  of students  in the  systems  are  transported?

1Val4

A.  In  answer  to  the  number  of  students  transported,
approximately  50,000  in  Genesee  County  and  in  Muskegon
County  approximately  20,000  are  transported.

systems  I could  do the  percentages  easily  enough?

A.  Yes,  sir.

[138]  Q.  Could  you  estimate  for us,  Mr.  Kuthy,  with  respect

to those  systems,  the average  travel  time  per  trip of each  bus?

A.  Sir,  are  you  referring  to  the  average  running  time  in miles
per  hour  of the  vehicle  or  the  average  trip length  in terms  of time?

Q.  I would  like  to  elicit  an  average  of how  long  the  kids  are
on  the  buses  between  pickup  points  and  delivery  points?  Is that  a
meaningful  way  to put the  question?

A.  I think  that  is total  elapsed  trip time  from  commencement

of the  pickup  of the  student  to delivery  at the  school.

Q.  Yes, sir.
A.  That  varies, sir,  with  the  variables  I mentioned  earlier,  the
number  of stops,  the  length  of the  run  and,  well,  those  are  the  two
primary  variables.  I can’t  tell  you  that  with  certainty.  I could
approximate  and  say  about  a maximum  of one—half  hour.

Q.  What  mileage  would  that  be, the maximum  mileage?

A.  Again  I would  estimate  because  the  average  miles  per  hour
speed  in  the  rural  portions  of those  counties  is about  15 miles  per
hour  so  half  an  hour  would  make  the  average  run  about  seven  and
a half miles.

Q.  I just  want  to  make  sure  I understand.  The  average  run  is
seven  and  one—half  miles;  does  that  make  the  average  trip  a  half
hour  or  is that  the  longest  trip in the  system?

[139]  A.  No, there  are  longer  trips.

Q.  Do  you  recall  what  the  longest  trip  would  be  in terms of

time  and  mileage,  if you  will,  sir?

A.  No, sir, I cannot.
Q.  Do you  know,  Mr.  Kuthy,  in your  experience  that  trips on

the  order  of an  hour  are  not  unconventional?

A.  School  bus  trips  on  the  order  of an  hour  are  not  uncon-

I1Val5

ventional?

Q.  Yes,  sir.

A.  If you  are  again  referring  to  the  total  elapsed  time  of the
bus  from  the  first  pickup  point  to  the  dropoff  at  the  school,  I
would  say  that  that  is not  unconventional.  If you  are  referring  to
the  amount  of time  an  individual  student  would  spend  on  the  bus,
that  varies  enormously  again  with  the  character  of the  stops  and
the points along  the  route  that  the  student  is picked  up.

I personally  saw  one  school  bus run  where a student  lived  in a
very  unaccessible  rural  area  rather  far off the  route.  That  student
rode  the  longest  time,  of  course,  and  the  other  students  rode
proportionately  less  because  of  the  deviation  to  pick  up  this
particular  student.

Q.  Yes,  but  it  is  true,  isn’t  it  sir,  in  systems  all  over  the

United  States  there  are  trips per  student  of upwards  of an  hour?

A.  From  published  reports  that  I have  seen  I would  say  yes.
[140]  Q.  Mr.  Kuthy,  doyou  have  an  opinion,  sir,  whether
from  the  standpoint  of  traffic  hazards  and  weather  hazards,  do
you  have  an  opinion  as  to  whether  going  to  school  by bus  rather
than on  foot  is more  safe  or  less safe?

A.  With  the  two  qualifications  you  suggest  of  traffic  and
weather  hazards,  I would  conclude  that  traveling  to school  by bus
would  be safer  and more  pleasant  than walking,  in general.  If I am
assuming  by weather  conditions  you  mean  bad  weather  conditions
such  as  rain  or  snow  or  sleet  as  opposed  to  more  delightful
conditions  of sunshine  and  soforth.  The reason  I add  that  caveat  is
that  bus  ridership  on  public  systems  and  on  ‘school  bus systems
tends  to decline  in more  idyllic  weather.

Q.  That  is  an  interesting  statistic.  Mr.  Kuthy,  from  your
experience  in  school  bus  transportation  in  dealing  with  school
systems,  do  you  know,  sir, whether  it is the view  of school  admin-
istrators  that  transporting  pupils  to  school  by bus produces  better
results  with  respect  to  tardiness  and  absenteeism  than  were  the
same  students  to walk  to school?

A.  No,  sir,  I  cannot  answer  that.  That  is  beyond  my

experience.

IV a 16

=

Q.  Mr.  Kuthy,  do  you  know  what  the  experience  of school
systems  in  Michigan  has  been  with  respect  to  the  numbers  or
percentages  of pupils  transported  to  schools,  that  is, over  a  20 or
30  year  period?  Is  that  a  growing  phenomenon  or  static  [141]
phenomenon  or  Geclininc  phenomenon  and  would  you  tell  us
what  you  know  about  that  both  in  urban  systems  and  rural
systems.

A.  The  answer  will  have  to be complicated  to this extent,  sir.
In  growing,  unincorporated  areas,  because  of  the  nature  of  the
Michigan  State  Aid  Reimbursement  Formula  which  provides  State
Aid  Reimbursement  to  school  districts  of up  to 75 percent  of the
total  cost  of  school  bus  transportation.  It  only  goes  to  unincor-
porated  areas.

_  Accordingly,  in  the  development  or  maturation  cycle  of so—
called suburban  counties,  if I can be indulged  to  use that term
commonly,  when  the  area  is developing  and  is unincorporated  the
cost  and  the  number  of  students  and  the  size  of  school  busing
systems  goes  up.  When  the  area  becomes  incorporated  then
changes  take  place.

I have  witnessed  a shrinkage  in the several  instances;  I can cite
one  in  particular:  the  Mona  Shores  School  District  in Muskegon
County,  -through  the  fortuitous  circumstance  of  being  incor-
porated  lost  its  state  aid  reimbursement  and  hence  the  school
district  was  required  to  pay  the  entire  costs  of  transporting
students  and there  was  a  dramatic  shrinkage  in  the  amount  of
school  bus transportation  provided.

It is my  understanding  that  conversations  [142]  with  other
school  districts  administrators  that  in  those  instances  where
because  equipment  was  available  and  the  district  had  the  funds  to
do  so,  students  were  picked  up,  so—called  ineligible  students,  from
the  standpoint  of state  aid reimbursement  because  they live  within
a one  and one—half  mile  radius  of the school,  they were  picked  up
as  an  accommodation  and  it was  an  accommodation  apparently
approved  by the  people  in their  school  district,  at least  the  cost  of
so doing, and  so  there  is a  variance  depending  on  the circum-
stances,  I suggest.

As  to  whether  there  is a general  shrinking  or  a general  growth
I can  only  probably  conclude  there  has  been  a general  increase  in

IVal7

the  amount  of  school  bus  transportation  and  I think  the  gauge
that  I would  use  would  be the amount  of state  aid  reimbursement
dollars  over  the past  decade  which  has  been  steadily  climbing.

I guess  I cannot  be  totally  responsible  for  that  because  there
may  be  elements  of  inflation  that  get  into  that  picture  of  that
state  aid reimbursement  increasing,  sir.

Q.  Would  I be  correct  in understanding  that  on  the  basis  of
the  factors  known  to  you  there  has  been a sustained  increase  in
school  bus transportation?

A.  Yes,  sir.
[143]  Q.  Mr.  Kuthy,  I might  have  misunderstood  an  answer
you  gave  to  Mr.  Beer  and  I would  like  to  clarify  it for  my  own
mind,  if you  will.  Did  I understand  you  to say  that  from  the  stand-
point  of  your  recommendation  or  the  optimum  situation  there
should  be one  service  bay per seven  vehicles?

A.  Yes,  in  a  public  transit  system  that  is the  standard  in  the

industry.

Q.  So  that  for  700  buses  we  would  be  talking  about  100

service  bays?

A.  Yes,  sir.  That  is  standard  again  for  qualification  and  to
avoid  misunderstanding  is related  to  the  public  transit  system  as
opposed  to public  school  bus  systems.  I know  of no  such  standard,
at least  in this state.

Q.  For public  school  buses?
A.  For  public  school  buses,  yes.
Q.  Do you  know  what  the  practice  is?
A.  I could  only  gather  by inference  the  practice  seems  to be

one  service  bay for approximately  10 vehicles.

Q.  Thank  you.  I understood  in response  to  another  question
by Mr.  Beer  that  it would  be  difficult  to secure  750  public  transit
vehicles  by the  fall of 1972?

:

A.  Yes, sir.

Q.  Yes.  Let’s  assume  the  Court  approves  the  plaintiffs’  plan
and  Detroit  must  transport  75,000  children  in  September,  should

opinion?

A.  Let  me  start  my  answer  this  way:  There  are  two  real
questions  involved  there  —  certainly  one  is  cost.  The  gasoline
powered  large  size  traditional  66-passenger  yellow  school  bus costs
about  $10,000.  The  equivalent  size  in a diesel  transit  vehicle  which
would  be  a  53  passenger  bus  because  of  the  [156]  standee
capability  and  stanchions  and  the  like  for  such  capabilities  in  a
public  transportation  vehicle  that  are  not  available  in a school  bus
vehicle,  the  cost  is about  $32,000  to  $35,000  per vehicle.

[157].Q.  Are  you  suggesting  a gasoline  school  bus is unsafe?

A.  No, I’m  not,  sir.

Q.  You  are  simply  saying  the  diesel  bus is safer?

A.  Y-s, sir.

4.  I suggest  there  are  economies  of scale  over  the  long  term
in  using  that  approach.  Economies  again  are  relative  items  and  if
one  is to  talk  of  the  initial  cost  of  implementation  of a  plan  as
opposed  to  another  plan,  as  opposed  to another  plan,  those  trade-
offs  are  significantly  different  than  the  trade-offs  that  are  inherent
in a large scale  and  large based  operation  across  many  years.

Those  are  entirely  different  propositions  and  the  com-

parability  of those  issues  are  difficult  at best.

A.  The  route  down  the  John  Lodge?

Q.  Yes.

A.  Just  based  on  my  own  experience  I would  estimate  —  let

during  [170]  the  time  when  students  are  moving?

Q.  During  the usual  school  hours,  yes.

travel  characteristics  curve  of  a  major  freeway.  If  the  students
were  leaving  early  in  the  morning  in the so-called  pre-peak  hours  |
would  estimate  that  time  to  be  no  more  than  45 minutes.  If they
were  moving  during  the peak  hour  of highest  traffic  density  on  the
John  Lodge,  I would  estimate  that  travel  time  to  the  rim  of the
core  area  that  you  commended  to be.an  hour.

A.  Yes.

Q.  If  the  Court  were  to  approve  the  Detroit  metropolitan
plan  and  300,000  youngsters  were  bused  every  day,  would  such  a
system  be  more  economical  than  transporting  75,000  children
within  the City of Detroit?

A.  If by economical  you  mean  total  cost  of carrying  75,000
students  as  opposed  to  total  cost.  of  carrying  300,000  students,
[171]  if you do I suggest  that  is a rhetorical  proposition.

Q  It  would not be more economical?

A.  If  you are suggesting  it  would  cost  more  to  carry  more
kids  I suggest  the answer  is rhetorical  and yes,  more  buses  would
be  required,  more  drivers  will  be  required,  more  gasoline  will  be
and so on
consumed and  more  tire  wear  and more  maintenance
and so on, yes, sir.

Q.  If  there  is  any  economy  it’s  in  per  pupil  cost.  If you
transport  more children  you can lower  the per pupil cost,  is that
right?

.

A.  Yes, sir

Q.  As far as dollar  outlay  is concerned.

A.  In absolute  dollar  amounts  there  is. The  chief difference  is
whether  or  not  there  is  a  straight  line  relationship  between
carrying  a  smaller  amount  of  students  as  opposed  to  carrying  a
larger  amount  of students  and  my  comment  is that  the  larger  base
will provide  for a  lower unit cost  of  carriage  as opposed  to  the
smaller  base.

Q.  If there  is any  economy  it’s there.

A.  Yes, sir, in scale.

Q.  Finally,  are  you  aware  that  the  state  has  reimbursed  the
Flint  school  district  for  transportation  of  school  children  who
were  required  to leave  the City of Flint  to reach  their  schools?

[172] A. Yes,  sir, that  is the  so-called  In-and-Out  proposition.
