THE COURT:  Mr.  Ritchie.

.

CROSS  EXAMINATION  BY  MR.  RITCHIE:

Q.  The  last  question,  Mr.  Kuthy  —  what  is the  so-called  In-

and-Out  proposition?

A.  Well,  heretofore  the  traditional state  aid  reimbursement
and  I comment  on this not because  I am a state  aid reimbursement
expert  but  I  worked  with  this  material  in  preparation  of  the
transportation  plans  I  alluded  to  earlier.  The  state  aid
reimbursement  formula  is  exclusively  limited  to  transportation
eligible  students who  live  in  unincorporated
areas.  The City of
Flint  being an incorporated  area  like  a  number  of other cities and
villages  in this state  is not eligible  as a  school  district,  that school
district  which  comprises virtually  the City of  Flint  is not eligible
to receive  state  aid  reimbursement  for  transportation  costs.  That
formula  is a  complicated  formula  -that  essentially  produces  a  75
per  cent  reimbursement  of the  total  cost  of running  transportation
for such  transportation  eligible  students.

However,  there

is a proviso  that  if students  [173]  living  in  a
portion  of the incorporated  area must leave the incorporated  area

IVa  22

by  the  most  direct  route  to  get  to  the  school  within  their  school
district  and  go  into  an  unincorporated  area  and  hence  back  into
the  incorporated  area,  so-called  In-and-Out.

Apparently  the  theory  is  that  the  residual  taint  of  having
moved  outside  the  incorporated  area  into  an  unincorporated  area
attracts  the  state  aid  reimbursement.  In  any  event  those  trips  for
those  students  who  qualify  under  the ground  rules  I specified,  the
district  is eligible  to receive  reimbursement  for  those  costs  only.

Q.  You  mentioned  in  Genesee  County  there  is what  would

you  say,  approximately  50,000  students  being  transported?

A.  Yes, sir.

Q.  To  you  knowledge  how  many  of  those  would  be

transported  from  one  school  district  into  another  district?

A.  How  many  are  being  transported  from  one  school  district

into  another?

Q.  Yes.

A.  I  have  no  knowledge  of  the  specific  number.  I would
expect  it would  be a rather  nominal  amount  based  on  my  observa-
tion.

Q.  But  the  phenomenon  of cross-district  busing  does  exist  in
Genesee  County.  You  have  students  being  transported  form  one
school  district  into  another?

[174]  A.  The  only  thing  I am  reacting  to  is  the
characterization  of the  phenomenon  of cross-district  busing.  If it is
used  to functionally  describe  the  movement  of students  from  one
district  to  another  as  opposed  to  a  more  recently  acquired
parlance  I would  say  yes,  there  is  to  that  extent  cross-district
busing  in Genesee  County.

THE  COURT:
Do  you  have  an  opinion  as  to  whether  the  total  cost  of tran-
sportation  would  be  less  for  a comprehensive  metropolitan  trans-
portation  system  encompassing  the  three  counties  and  the City of
Detroit  than  the  present  cost  of transportation  in a tri-county  area
increased  by  the  cost  of  transportation  by  use  of  a  separate
transportation  system  in [181]  the City of Detroit?

A.  Yes,  sir.  The  two  studies  that  we  did  advanced  the  hypo-
thesis  that  the  consolidation  of  school  transportation  operation
with  public  transit  operations  would  result  in a  total  public  cost
reduction,  total  public  cost  reduction.

:

THE  COURT:  Was  that  a substantial  amount?
A.  Yes, sir.

of  the  schedule  and  so  that  you  may  be  aware  of  what  has

The  ruling  on  the  motion  of  the  Jefferson-[  182]  Chalmers

The  motions  of Allen  Park  Public  Schools,  et  al., the  Grosse
Pointe  Public  Schools,  the  School  District  of  the  City  of Royal
Oak  and  the  Southfield  Public  Schools  are  granted  under  Rule
24(a)2,  and  in the  alternative  under  Rule  24(b)2  under  conditions
which  my  ruling  specifies  in detail.

The  motion  of  Carrie  Green,  et  al.,  including  Tri-County

Citizens  for  Intervention  of  Federal  Court  Action  No.  35257  is.
granted  under  Rule  24(b)  2 under  conditions  specified.

is  granted  for  the  following  reasons:
1,  the  standing  of  the
National  Association  for  the  Advancement  of Colored  People  was
not  challenged  by  the  original  parties  to  this  action,  and  2, for
practical  purposes  the grant  of intervention  to the Carrie  Green,  et
al., is  a grant  of intervention  to said  organization.

propriety  of  either  the  standing  of  the  NAACP  nor  the
intervention  of the  citizens  group.

The  motion  of the City of Warren,  a municipal  corporation  of
the  State  of  Michigan,  to  intervene  is  denied  [183]  as  of  right
under  Rule  24(a)2  and  in  the  discretion  of the  Court  under  Rule
24(b)2.

The  motion  of Nancy  Bird,  et al., to intervene  is denied  under
Rule  24(a)2  and  under  Rule  24(b)2  in the discretion  of the Court
for  the  reason  that  their  interests  are  already  adequately  repre-
sented  by the parties,  including  those  to  whom  intervention  has
been  granted  here  today.

The  motion  of  Professional  Personnel  of  Van  Dyke  to
intervene  is denied  under  Rule  24(a)2  and  in the  discretion  of the
Court  under  Rule  24(b)2.

EUGENE  W.  KUTHY
resumed  the  stand  as  a  witness  herein,  and  having  been  previously
duly sworn,  testified  further  as  follows:

.

REDIRECT  EXAMINATION  (Continued)  BY  MR.  BEER:

Q.  Mr.  Kuthy,  I have  just  one  or  two  questions  based  on  the
question  the judge  asked  you  just before  the  recess.  Y ou  testified,
as  I recall,  that  there  would  be substantial  savings  in consolidation
of all transportation  in the  metropolitan  area,  is that  correct?

A.  Yes,  sir.

Q.  Would  those  savings  be realized  or some  of those  savings
be  realized  only  if school  transportation  were  consolidated  and
public  transportation  would  not?

A.  Yes,  they  would  be  based  on  the  work  we  did  in Genesee
County  and  Muskegon  and  particularly  in  that  latter  community.
One of the  tests or efforts  that  we  were  to undertake  was  to  [185]
examine  the  economic,  operational,  administrative  and  legal
feasibility  of  consolidating  school  bus  transportation  with  public
mass  transportation  in those  two  counties.

a

IV a 26

The  answer  came  out  as  to each  of those  issues.  Economically
it made  more  sense  because  of the  cost  savings.  Administratively  it
was  possible  and  legally  it  was  feasible  and  operationally  it pre-
sented  no  problems  at  the  conclusion  of our  study.

Collaterally  we  also  determined  that  if such  a consolidation  of
public  mass  transit  and  school  transportation  were  not  accomp-
lished  but  that  exclusively  school  bus  transportation  within  those
counties  among  the  varied  districts  within  the  counties  were  con-
solidated,  the  same  orders  of  feasibility  were  achievable  econ-
omically,  legitimately,  operationally,  administratively  feasible.

As  a  matter  of  fact,  the  cost  savings  showed  up  rather
dramatically.  As  I recall  in  the  case  of Muskegon  County  the  cost
savings  were  in  the  neighborhood  of some  10  to  20  per  cent  over
-
the  total  public  cost  of transportation  in that  county.

These  cost  savings  typically  occur  in  the  following  ways:
Instead  of  each  individual  school  district  purchasing  its  buses  as
the  requirements  occur  for  replacement  or  for  additional  bus
equipment  necessary  because  of  its  [186]  increased  student
passenger  carriage  and  paying  the  highest  dollar  for  the  acquisition
of  that  vehicle,  the  assumption  would  be  that  the  county—wide
purchase  of  all  of  the  bus  requirements  for  all  of  the  school  dis-
tricts  involved  would  realize  savings,  we  estimate,  in the  neighbor-
hood  of  13 per cent.

That  is based  on  empiric  evidence  from  my  own  county,  Oak-
land  County,  where  they  instituted  on  a  pioneer  basis  a  coopera-
tive  bus  purchasing  plan  among  some  as I recall,  25  cooperating
school  districts  who  purchased  alone  school  bus  chassis,  school  bus
bodies,  not  the  entire  bus.  As you  know  they  are  bought  separate-
ly.  The  cooperative  purchase  in  1970  of  the  25  school  districts
accumulated  by,  I don’t  recall  the  exact  number,  I think  it was
something  like  40  school  bus  bodies  came  out  at  a  unit  cost  of
$3700  per  body.

The  average  cost  in  Muskegon  County  for  individual  body

buys by district  was  $4425.

Q.  Let  me  interrupt  you  at  that  point,  Mr.  Kuthy.  Would
these  economies  of purchasing  volume  still  be  realized  when  you
are  dealing  with  very  large  numbers,  for instance,  the  difference  of
buying  say  750  buses  and  1500  buses?

1Va27

A.  I would  expect  even  more  dramatic  economies,  primarily
because  the  intensity  of competition  among  bus manufacturers  to
secure  [187]  an  order  of  that  size  would  make  a  very  dramatic
impact.

I  might  add  that  these  economies  spread  not  only  across
equipment  but  spare  parts  and  in  addition  to  that  there  is some
very  interesting  economies  in  the  purchase  of insurance  which  is a
very  important  item  particularly  in school  bus operations.

We  were  informed  after  a discussion  of a number  of insurance
brokers  that  the  technique  used  in  the  insurance  business  to  sell
so—called  fleet  insurance  to  bus  operators  such  as  the  school  dis-
trict  is to  take  into  consideration  the  number  of vehicles  covered
and  as  the  number  of vehicle  increases  the  premium  decreases,  the
premium  dollars  do  because  there  is a  volume  discount  based  on
vehicle  numbers.

In addition  to  that  there  is a  discount  that  is incident  to  the
risk  pooling.  If a  particular  school  district  had  a  particularly  bad
accident,  assuming  for  a moment  that  a  child  were  killed  or  very
badly  injured  or  had  another  accident  record  of  equally  bad
character,  that  school  district  might  be  paying  a  very  high  pre-
mium  dollar  as  opposed  to  another  school  district  that  had  a
better  accident  record.

The.  pooling  of  the  risks  tends  to  lower  the  overall  cost  of

insurance  once  again  based  on  fleet  size.

Q.  I have  one  final  question.  Using  your  best  professional
[188]  judgment  and  assuming  optimum  cooperation,  availability
of funds  and  utilization  of equipment  how  long,  in your judgment,
would  it take  t plan,  schedule  and  organize  bus  transportation  of
the  magnitude  suggested  for  the  City  of  Detroit  alone  in  the
plaintiffs’  plan?

A.  If I may  also  take  the  latitude  of making  assumptions  as
you  have,  Mr.  Beer,  if I could  assume  concurrence  of a number  of
key  activities  such  as  initiation  of  bus  acquisition  from  the  first
day  that  a “go ahead”  was  given  in  such  a  program  so  that  bus
acquisition  could  be pursued  independently  and  concurrently  with
route  preparation  and  routing  and  scheduling  and  planning  and  if I
could  assume  further  that  sufficient  staff  were  available  and  I
believe  that  appears  under  your  hypothesis  to  begin  the  site

IVa  28

acquisition,  whether  it be  by purchase  or  lease  or  similar  activity
for  maintenance  facilities  and  assume  further  the  staff  and  the
ability  to  concurrently  initiate  the  administrative  requirements
such  as  acquisition  of  insurance  and  cadre  staff  and  this  kind  of
thing,  with  all  of  those  assumptions  I would  assume  it would  be
possible  under  the  City  of Detroit  plan  to prepare  and  have  a plan
available  for  inspection  by  appropriate  authorities  within  some
five  months  subsequent  to go ahead.

that

assume

I  would

in  my  opinion

it  would  be
possible  some  nine  months  hence,  assuming  another  four
for  driver  training,
months  [189]
for  running  of  routes  and  the  myriad  of  detail  incident  to

for  operational

Q.  Is  there  any  significant  difference  in that  time  between
doing  what  you  have  just  described  and  doing  that  for  the  metro-
politan  area,  making  the  same  assumptions?

A.  Not a significant  difference.  | would  estimate  perhaps  an
additional  month  of effort  to  prepare  the  plan  and  the  same  kind
of time,  it would  be a little  more  strain,  perhaps  three  months  to
operationally  debug  and  test  and  train  drivers  and  do  all  those
kind  of. things  necessary  and  incident  to  an  operational  beginning
some  nine  months  hence  from  go ahead.

RECROSS  EXAMINATION  BY MR.  FLANNERY:

Q.  Mr.  Kuthy,  do  you  know  the  annual  perpupil  operational
expenditures  for  transportation  in  the  Detroit  metropolitan  area?

A.  Per  pupil  expenditure  of  students  in  the  Detroit  metr-

politan  area?

{190}  Q.  Yes.  What  is the operational  cost,  putting  aside  capi-
tal  costs,  what  is the  annual  dollar  figure  for pupil  transportation,
do you  know?

A.  I can  not  answer  that. I can only  answer  to  some numbers
that  are  most  recently  available  in  the  Michigan  Pupil Transports

IV a 29

tion  Handbook  prepared  by  the  Michigan  Department  of Educa-
tion  which  indicates  in  a  review  of the  state  aid  reimbursent  act
that  the approximate  per  pupil  cost,total  transportation  cost  in the
1969-1970  year  period  was——I  can’t  remember  the  exact
number,  I believe  $47.92  per  pupil,  a state—wide  figure.
Q.  Would  you  tell  me  again  the source  of that?
A.  The  Michigan  Pupil Transportation  Handbook  prepared  by
the  Michigan  Department  of  Education.  I  make  no  claim  to
expertise  in  their  techniques  or  derivation  of those  numbers,  I am
just reporting  what  they are.

Q.  Thank  you.  If  I understood  your  testimony  earlier,  Mr.
Kuthy,  it was  to the effect  that  the  utilization  of buses  in Genesee
and Muskegon  ranged  from  1.1  per  trip to 3.56?

A.  Yes, sir.
Q.  Do  you  know  what  the utilization,  comparable  utilization

data are  for other  districts  in the Detroit  metropolitan  area?

A.  No, sir, I do not.
Q.  So  that  in  terms  of  utilization,  if  a  school  district
immediately  [191]  adjacent  to  Detroit  used  pupil  transportation
you  are  not  in a position  as  I understand  it to  tell  us  whether  or
not  such  districts  are  relatively  speaking  under—utilizing  their
buses  or over—utilizing  their  buses?

A.  Without  investigating  those  numbers  it  would  not  be
possible  for  me  to  do  so.  The only comment  I can  make  to that  is
that  an  attempt  was  made  in both  of the  studies  I referred  to  to
try  and  determine  if  there  is  a  single  universal  operational
efficiency  index  by which  you  can  rate  individual  school  systems
relative  to one  another.

It was  the conclusion  of my staff that  it was  not  possible  with
what  we  knew  or  perhaps  the  limits  of  our  Own  expertise  to
develop  such a single  operational  efficiency  index.

As  a  consequence  of that  we  used  a  variety  of other  indicies
to  measure  operational  efficiency:  trips  per  bus,  pupils  per  bus,
loading  factors,  miles  per  run  and  a number  of other  such  indicies
were used and that was the basis for at least getting, if I can put it,
a  handle  on  one  district  as  opposed  to  another’s  ability  to
efficiently  handle  its student  load.

——

IV a  30

On  the  aggregate  of  these  indicies  or  a  comparative  look  at
four  or  five  indicies,  would  at  least  show  the  worst  case  and  the
best  case.  The  middle  grouping  of  some  [192]  of  these  school
districts  made  for  a  high  order  of  invidious  comparison  and  not
terribly  reliable  within  that  middle  range.

Q.  Putting  aside  legal  consideration,  if you  will,  are  there  any
impediments  to  District  A  using  District  B’s  equipment  to
transport  District  A’s  pupils  if District  B is adjacent  to  District  A
and  is  substantially  under—utilizing  its  own  transportation
equipment  by the  factors  you  mentioned?

A.  I’m  sorry,  I didn’t  get  that.

Q.  Putting  aside  legal  consideration,  two  districts  side  by side

A.  Yes.

KOK

{19S}...
A.  The  answer  is yes  and  I think  I answered  it yes earlier  in my
conversation  because  that  was  one  of the  findings  we  made  in the
Genesee  and  Muskegon  County  studies  that  collaterally,  although
our  central  hypothesis  was  to  look  for  economic,  administrative,
operational  and  legal  feasibility  of  consolidation  of  public  and
school  bus transportation  we  concurred  or  recommended  that  that
was  so  but  in additon  to that,  collaterally  we  noted  that  if no  such
consolidation  between  public  transportation  and  school  bus
transportation  in  the  [196]  individual  districts  were  made  a
benefit  would  flow  simply  from  the  consolidation  of  all  of  the
individual  districts,  that  stood  on  its  own  feet.  That  was  not
something  we  sought  in  the  study,  it appeared  as  a  result  of our
efforts.

So the answer  again  is Yes  to  your  question.

Q.  I believe  you  testified  that  a  certain  bulletin  put  out  by

the  state  indicated  a cost  of $47.92?

A.  Yes,  sir.

A.  As  I understand  it and  you  will  have  to accept  my  reading
of the  Michigan  Pupil  Transportation  Handbook  and  I don’t  recall

the  exact  page  where  this  formulation  was  prepared  but  it

tion.

It may  be at best  a modestly  understated  figure  only  from  the
standpoint  as  I perceive  in  reading  that  particular  formulation  in
that  only  transportation  eligible  students  were  factored  into  the

ROGER  H. MARZ,
was  called  as a witness  by Defendant  Detroit  Board,  and  being  first

THE COURT:  Your  full  name,  sir?

IV a 32

THE  WITNESS:  Roger  Henry  Marz.

[198]  DIRECT  EXAMINATION  BY  MR.  BEER:
Q.  Mr.  Marz, how  are  you  employed,  please?

A.  I am  a  Professor  of  Political  Science  at  Oakland  Univer-

sity.

Q.  Do you  hold  any  other  consulting  positions?

A.  I am a visiting  professor  in  graduate  studies  at  Michigan
State  University,  visiting  on  a  parttime  basis  there.  I  am  an
occasional  consultant  on  a  regular  basis  with  the  Metropolitan
Fund  which  is a  regional  non—profit  research  organization  focus-
ing  on  Detroit  problems.  I consult  with  them  in  their  Inter—
Governmental  Relations  Division.

I an  a  consultant  on  inter—gonvernmental  relations  and  par-
ticularly  problems  of state  and  local  governments  to  the  Systems
Evaluation  Division  of  the  Office  of  Emergency  Preparedness  of
the  Executive  Office  of the  President  of the  United  States,  which
is essentially  a civil  domestic  problem  version  of cost  effectiveness,
cost  benefit  analysis  in the civil  area.

We  do  work  for  other  governmental  agencies.  This  is more  of

an  in—house  think  tank.

Q.  What  is  it  you  taught  or  teach  at  Michigan  State,  Dr.

Marz?  _

;

A.  I  was  asked  to  come  up  there  especially  to  teach  a
graduate  level course  in  public  administration  with  special  empha-
sis on  local  and  metropolitan  problems  and  a course  on  the  [199]
governmental  politics  of the  metropolitan  area  at a graduate  level.

Q.  Do  you  teach  any  courses  relating  to  metropolitan  prob-

lems  in your regular work  at Oakland  University?

A.  Yes.  I teach  politics  of the  local  community,  metropolitan
problems,  political,  socio—economic  aspects  of  the  environment
which  tends  to  center  around  disposal,  in my  interests,  disposal of
human  wastes  and  trying  to  engage  in  comprehensive  planning  in
metropolitan  areas.  I  teach  local  government  courses,  public
administration.

Q.  Dr.  Marz,  with  regard  to  the  Metropolitan  Fund,  you  char

acterize  it as dealing  with  Detroit  problems,  is that  right?

IVa  33

A.  Yes,  it’s  specifically  a  Detroit—based  organization.  Its
board  of  directors  include  people  like  vice  presidents  from  the
Ford Motor Company  ——

Q.  What  I had  reference  to, do  you  mean  it focuses  only  on

the City of Detroit?

A.  No,  it’s  a Detroit  metropolitan  area  and  the Detroit  region
is the  area  of concern  of the  Metropolitan  Fund.  In fact, its focus
is specifically  regional  problems,  problems  that  reach  both within
the  central  city  and  beyond  its boundaries  into  the  metropolitan

C—4, Dr. Marz  vita.

THE COURT:  It may  be received.
Q.  (By  Mr.  Beer)  Dr.  Marz,  in  your  professional  opinion  is
there  a single most  appropriate  geographical  frame of reference  for
viewing  or  studying  or  solving  social,  political  and  economic
problems  in a metropolitan  area?

A. There  is  a  standard  base  from  which  I would  start if I
didn’t  know  what  kind  of problem  you  were  talking about.  There
might  be  some  differences  depending  upon  the  specific  problems
but if all other  things  being  equal  I would  start  with  the base,  the

1V.a34

United  States  Federal  Government  Bureau  of Census  [201]  Divis-
ion  of  standard  metropolitan  statistical  areas  which  consist  of
Wayne,  Oakland  and  Macomb  Counties.

Q.  With  regard  to Detroit?

A.  With  regard  to  any  problem  that  was  perceived  as  having

any  implications  reaching  Detroit  or its hinterlands.

Q.  You  say  that  is a standard  definition,  sometimes  standards
mean  arbitrary  or  artificial.  In your  professional  opinion  that  isa
real  definition  or  viable  one  or  simply  an  administrative  conven-
ience?

A.  It  was  very,  very  carefully  designed.  The  reason  | would

use  it is not  simply  because  it is conventional  but  because  it is in
fact  in  general  a  very  useful  definition.  Various  agencies  of  the
Federal  Government  began  to  recognize  in  the  1930s  that  you
could  not  attend  to  or  understand  the  problems  of cities  without
also  being  away  of the things  that  went  on  beyond  their  immediate
political  boundaries.

So  independently  the  census  of  manufacturers  and  employ-
ment  people  and  the  labor  department  and  people  in  the  fore-
runners  of  housing  and  urban  development,  housing  and  home
finance  agencies  and  so  on  began  to  develop  definitions  of what  a
metropolitan  area  was  and  each  of them  had  slightly  different  def-
initions  so  you  might  find  a  particular  metropolitan  area  would
have  one  line  drawn  on  it as  far as  labor  was  concerned  on  the  map
and  another  line  drawn  around  [202]  it as  far  as  the  department
of manufacturers  was  concerned.  So the drive  which  culminated  in
the  creation  of the  standard  metropolitan  area  which  was  since  re-
named  the  Standard  Metropolitan  Statistical  Area  in  1950,  the
drive  was  to  get  a  definition  which  caught  the  essentials  as  far as
all  of  these  agencies  and  all  other  users  of the  census  were  con-
cerned  as  best  they  could.

The  essential  characteristics  of what  a  metropolitan  area  was
and  what  it  meant,  it was  very carefully  worked  out  not  just  by
government  agencies  but  also  by committees  of professional  con-
sultants  around  the  county.  I wasn’t  old  enough  to  be  a  pro
fessional  consultant  then  but  I am  familiar  with  the  work  they did.
They  were  very  careful.

IV a 35

Q.  What  are  the  criteria  used  in framing  this  definition?

A.  There  are several  and they are sort of used  in order,  that is
if you  don’t  meet  the  first  criteria  then  go  on  and  inspect  the
second  one  and  so  on.  Would  it be convenient  to answer  that  ques-
tion  with  particular  reference  let’s  say  to Detroit?

Q.  Yes,  that’s  fine.

A.  The  first  criteria  is that  there  be  a  central  city  of at  least
50,000  population  or  a  clutch  of  smaller  areas  so  inextricably
welded  together  they  total  50,000  even  though  there.is  not  any
one.  Detroit  meets  that  criteria  so  it will  have  some  kind  of stan-
dard  metropolitan  statistical  area  [203]  around  it.

The  second  criteria  is that  the  boundaries  of the  SMSA  will  be
county  boundaries.  This  is for  administrative  purposes  largely  be-
cause  the  Federal  Government  works  well  that  way  and  because
most  other  agencies  want  to  have  some  kind  of  standardized
boundary.  There  is  an  exception  to  this  made  in  New  England
where  counties  do  not  exist,  there  the  boundaries  are  town  and
city, township  boundaries.

after  you  define

The  second
either

criteria
it  is  a  central
a  county  or  county  surrounding  it,  is
city  and
that  it  be  of metropolitan  character.  The  population  of this  area
ceases  where  the  metropolitan  character  of  the  area  ceases.  There
is a very  careful  definition  of what  that  means.

The  definition  of metropolitan  character  is that  there  has to
‘
be  an  unbroken  chain  of  minor  civil  divisions.  If  we  went  out
Woodward  the  minor  civil  divisions  would  be say,  Ferndale,  Royal
Oak,  Birmingham,  Bloomfield  Hills,  Bloomfield  Township,
Pontiac,  Waterford  Township,  Pontiac  Township,  et  cetera,  each
of  these  divisions  must  have  a  population  density  of at  least  150
persons  per  square  mile.

When you get to  a  minor civil division  along  a  line that has a
population  density  of less  than  that  then  that  signals  the  beginning
of  the  end  of  the  metropolitan  area.  The  metropolitan  area  will
end  at  the  end  of the  county  that  [204]  contains  the  last  of those
minor  civil  divisions.

A  population  density  of  150  square  miles  was  chosen  as  a
rather  conservative  definition  of  a  population  that  sustains  an

~

ae

IV a 36

urban  life  still.  For  example,  in a rural  area  the  old  township  areas
you  typically  had  160 acre farms.  There  would  be four of those  to
a  square  mile.  If a  farm  family  had  10 people  on  it then  a  rural
population  density  would  be about  40 per square  mile.

Just  to  be  on  the safe  side  and  not  to have  metropolitan  areas
that  spread arbitrarily  across  the landscape  like ink spilled  out of  a
pot,  they  picked  a  figure  for  determining  when  urban  density
stops at about  four  times  that  density.

The  last  criteria  is  that  even  if  you  got  this  metropolitan
density  that  the  county  should  form  a social  and  economically  in-
tegrated  community  with  the  county  forming  the  central  city and
then  there  are a  whole string  of contingent  criteria  which  are used
to  determine  whether  or  not  this  is the  case.  The  prime  criteria  is
the occupation,  the place of occupation  of the  labor  force.

Q. Now  let’s  if we  may  go  int&a  bit more  detail  on  the  qual-
ity of  metropolitan  communities. Could  you  list specifically  what
the criteria  is with  regard  to  work  force and also  state  some of the
other  criteria?

[205]A.  Yes,  I can.  May I read  from  this.

Q.  You  may  if you tell me  what it is.

A.  It’s  from  the  User’s  Guide  of  the  1960  United  States
Census  and it tells you what  the criteria  are.  I xeroded  it off be:
cause  of  my  memory  is not that great.  It’s  one of these things  that
you are supposed  to  know but you usually end up  looking up  be-
fore every lecture;  at least I do.

Q.  Go ahead.

ed

A.  The  criteria  of metropolitan  character,  I have  already  de-
scribed  it.  Let  me  go  to  criteria  of  integration.  A  county  is re-
as  integrated  with  the  county  or  counties  containing  the
central  cities  of the  area  if either  of the  following  criteria  is met.
The  first  criteria
living in the
is that 15  per cent of  the workers
county  —  we are now talking  about  an outlying  county  like  Oak-
land  —  15 per  cent  of the  workers  living  in that  county  work  in the
county  or  counties  containing  the  central  city or  cities  of the  area.

So  it’s  the  percentage,  if 1S  per  cent  of the  workers  living  in
Oakland  County  were  to  work  in Wayne  County  it would  be  de-
fined  as  meeting  this criteria,  being  socially  and  economically  in-

IVa  37

tegrated  with  Wayne  County  and,  therefore,  by inference  with  its
central  city.  The  same  test  would  be  applied  to  Macomb  and  to
Washtenaw.

In  fact,  Oakland  County  in  the  1960  census,  [206]  33.8  per
cent  of  the  workers  in  all  walks  of life  living  in Oakland  County
were  employed  in Wayne  County.

Q.  One-third  of the  work  force  and  this  was  in 1960?

A.  Yes, one-third  of the  work  force  in 1960.

Q.  All right.

A.  In  Macomb  County  the  other  counties  or  county  which
forms  the  standard  metropolitan  statistical  area  with  Wayne  and
Oakland,  in  Macomb  County,  44.2  per  cent  of  the  work  force
were  employed,  people  who  lived  in  Macomb  County  and  were  in
the  labor  force  worked  in  Wayne  County.  That  is the  prime  cri-
teria,  that  is the  one  that  the  census  looks  at  first  in order  to deter-
mine  the  boundaries  of an  area.

There  is a backup  criteria  and  I did  not  bother  to calculate  the
statistics  on  this  one.  I know  that  Oakland  and  Macomb  County
met  it but  Idon’t  have  the  exact  numbers.  They  have  to be extract-
ed from  the  census.

Q:  Tell  us  what  the  criteria  is, please.

[208]...

Q.  By  “our  school”  I suppose  you  mean  Oakland  University?

A.  Yes  I’m  possessive  about  that, I forget.

Q.  We  have  some  schools  too.

A.  Yes,  I’m  sorry  about  that.  There  is one  in Ann  Arbor  but  I
keep  forgetting  its  name.  As a political  scientist  I tend  to  look  at
governmental  institutions  as  indicaters  of  a  recognition  of  the
existence  of  a  metropolitan  area.  In  other  words,  has  there  been
some  kind  of public  or  political  legal  collective  judgment  that  you
should  meet  some  governmental  problem  on  a  metropolitan  basis
and  I can  think  in the  Detroit  metropolitan  area  right  offhand  of
three  such  examples  of such  regional  governmental  institutions.

Q.  What  are  they?

oe

1Va38

A.  The  HuronClinton  Metropolitan  Authority  is the  oldest.
It was  founded  by the  state  in 1939  and  it was  a direct  recognition
of  the  fact  that  no  one  community  nor  indeed  any  one  [209]
county  in  the  state  could  provide  adequate  recreational,  educa-
tional  and  cultural  facilities  and  they  were  also  chartered  to  pro-
vide  transportation  facilities  to  get  to  those,  out  of  its  own  re-
sources,  within  its own  limits.

So the  Huron-Clinton  Metropolitan  Authority  was  deliberately

chartered  to meet  a regional  need  of the  metropolitan  area.

A  second,  also  created  by the  state  legislature,  is the  South-
east  Michigan  Transit  Authority.  That  has  a  lot  of power.  I have
the  impression  it has  not  very  much  money  but a lot  of authority
to  get  the  net  collective  transportation  needs  of  the  southeast
Michigan  region.

The  third  is an  interesting  one  because  it was  never  imposed
from  without  but grew  and  evolved  very  naturally  from  within  and
in  fact  it  makes  Detroit  one  of the  envies  of other  metropolitan
areas  throughout  the  county  and  that  is the  Detroit  Metropolitan
Water  and  Sewer  System.

Through  supply  contracts,  through  mutual  bonding,  through
agreements  reached  in the  tightest  kind  of bargaining  but  all con-
trolled  largely  by  the  perception  of a  collective  interest  of all  of
the  parties  the  Detroit  metropolitan  area  is literally  knit  together
by webs  of steel  pipe  and  concrete  sewer  pipe  such  with  so  many
overlapping  responsibilities,  so  many  overlapping jurisdictions  and
physical  [210]  responsibilities  that  you  couldn’t  disaggregate  this
into  a separate  community  if you  wanted  to.

There  is literally  no  way  to pull  the  metropolitan  region  apart
with  respect  to  water  and  sewage  supply.  It’s  integrated.  It will
always  be  integrated  even  if we  would  like  to  pretend  that  it is
not,  we  couldn’t.  We  owe  each  other  bills.  We  have  collective  res-
ponsibilities  for  bond  issues.  It’s  really  quite  a remarkable  system.

Q.  You  say  this  simply  happened  as  a manifestation  of needs

as  recognized  by discreet  entities  within  that  area?

A.  Yes,  they  bargained  with  one  another,  reached  mutually
agreeable  contract;  Detroit  found  it was  efficient  to  sell  water  to
suburban  communities.  The  suburban  communities  found  it was

IV a 39

more  efficient  to  buy  from  Detroit  and  share  with  Detroit  in  in-
creasing  its  capacities.  The  same  was  true  in  sewage  disposal
systems.

Not  every  community  in the  three  —  I guess  about  four  or  five
county  region  now  belongs  to  it  but  by  far  the  bulk  of  them  do
and  most  of them  do  so  because  it is, frankly,  in their  interests  to
belong.  It  is  the  best  way  to  solve  a  problem  on  a  metropolitan
basis  and  you  didn’t  need  a  state  law,  you  didn’t  need  directive
action.  It  was  perceived  by  the  local  people  themselves  that  this
was  the  best  way  to do  it.

Q.  I see.

A.  [211]  It’s very  pervasive.

Q.  Dr.  Marz,  I seem  to  have  a  blind  spot  today  about  plan-
ning  commissions.  I believe  I neglected  to  mention  you  are,  as  I
recall,  a member  of the  Oakland  County  Planning  Commission?

A.  lam  a  member  and  secretary  of the Oakland  County  Plan-

ning Commission.

Q.  What  is the  particular  function  of that  commission?
A.  It’s  to  give  policy,  direction,  and  supervision  to  the  plan-
ning  staff of the County  of Oakland,  to consider  the  needs  of Oak-
land  County  in  planning  in  the  fields  of  transportation,  open
space,  land  use,  local  governmental  structures  and  the  like  and  to
coordinate  our  activities  as  best  we  can  with  the  constituent  local
bodies  within  Oakland  County  and  the  other  relevant  local  bodies
whose  decision  outside  impinge  on  Oakland  County.

Q.  Dr.  Marz,  in  terms  of both  your  professional  opinion  and
your judgement,  as  you  exercise  it as  a member  and  officer  of that
body,  is it conceivable  to  you  that  those  planning  functions  could
be  performed  without  consideration  of  the  City  of  Detroit
or the geographic  area  it represents?

A.  No.

Q.  I didn’t  hear  you?
A.  No,  it’s  not  conceivable  to  me.  It is indeed  inconceivable.
We  are  part  of the Detroit  metropolitan  area  and  we  have  to make
(212]  our  plans accordingly.

IV a 40

Q.  Dr.  Marz,  do  you  have  a  professional  opinion  as  to
whether  the  concept  of metropolitan  community,  which  you  have
explained  and  delineated  in  your  testimony  exists  as  a  subjective
perception  of the  people  who  live  within  that  metropolitan  com-
munity?

A.  Yes,  I do.  In the  proper  context  if you  go up  to somebody
cold  and  and  ask  him,“‘Are  you  a  member  of  the  Detroit  metro-
politan  community?”  The  response  will  probably  be “Huh?”  That
is, people  tend  to be aware  of the  fact  that  they  live  in Bloomfield
Township  although  that  sometimes  won’t  do  them  very  much
good  because  if they  live  in  Bloomfield  Township  they  might  still
live  in  any  one  of  five  school  districts  which  serve  Bloomfield
Township.

To  get  a  sense  of  a  community  in  a  metropolitan  area  like
Detroit  it is very  tough  to  do  on a local  basis  because  there  are  so
many  overlapping  jurisdictions,  but  I think  the  test  of whether  a
person  has  such  a  sense  of community  comes  in  the  question  of
how  he  acts  when  his  metropolitan  area  is indeed  what  is relevant
and  what  I do in school  when  I am  trying  to get this  idea  across,  is
if you  are  in another  part  of the  country  or  if you  are  in a foreign
country  and  you  are  trying  to  tell somebody  where  you  are  from,
how  do  you  do  it?  You  say  you  are  from  Detroit  or
you  say  you  are  from  [213]  Detroit  and  its  environs  and  it
is in  that  sense  that  the  sense  of a  metropolitan  area  is very  real
and  that  is the  sense  that  it is relevant.

That  is the  sense  in  which  it is relevant  as  compared  to other
metropolitan  areas.  It doesn’t  preclude  you  knowing  the  fact  that

you  live  in  Bloomfield  Township  means  you  don’t  live  in
Birmingham.  You  can  have  lots  of senses  of community  and  they
nest  but  I would  say  the  metropolitan  sense  is a real  one  too  in its
proper  context,  just as  real.

MR.  BEER:  Thank  you  very  much.  I have  nothing  further,

your  Honor.

MR.  FLANNERY:  Nothing,  your  Honor.

CROSS  EXAMINATION  BY  MR.  KRASICKY:

Q.  The  concept  of a  metropolitan  area  in essense  is a conven-

ience  for the  Federal  Government,  is it not?

IVa  41

A.  No,  sir,  I would  say  not.  I would  say  it is an  essential  ne-
cessity  for  anybody  who  wants  to.  think  about  any  kind  of an
urban  problem.  What  was  convenient  for the  Federal  Government
and  for  everybody  else  working  on  urban  problems  was  to develop
a set  of criteria  so that  everybody  would  be  using  the  concept  in
the  same  way  but  they  didn’t  invent  the  concept  [214]  for  their
convenience.  The  concept  was  invented  kind  of  independently
over  and  over  again  by all  kinds  of people  who  wanted  to look  at
urban  problems.

Q.  But  your  basic  definition  of the  metropolitan  area  is the

Federal  Government’s  definition,  is it not?

A.  Yes,  sir,  because  that  is the  one  that  has become  the  con-
ventional  one.  to  be  used  by all  professional  workers  in  the  field.
It’s one  you  may  depart  from  on  certain  occasions  but  you  depart
from  it  knowing  that  there  is  a  conventional  definition  and  it is
there  for a reason.

Q.  Are  you  familiar  at  all  with  the  area  that  is known as Ar-

mada,  Michigan?

A.  Armada?  Yes,  sir,  I know  where  it is.

Q.  Do  you  think  that  the  people  in Armada  perceive  they are

part of Detroit  in any  sense?

A.  I would  answer  that  question  I guess  the  way  I answered  it
with  Mr.  Beer.  In the  proper  context  I am  sure  they  do.  If a person
from  Armada  were  in Puerto  Rico  and  somebody  asked  him  where
he was  from  I think  he  would  say  he was  from  the  Detroit  metro-
politan  area.  I can’t  prove  it.

Q.  You  think  he would?
A.  I think  he  would,  yes.  I use  this  definition  in  my  classes
over  and  over  again  and I find  that  it is readily  recognized  as  an
appropriate  definition.

Q.  You  described  certain  governmental  institutions  such  as

.  O, yes,  sir.

>
>O>OP>rO

.  No,  sir,  I don’t  think  there  is a true  metropolitan  area.  Mr.
Beer’s  question  to  me  was  all other  things  being  equal  what  would
I consider  a  metropolitan  area.  At least  that  is how I interpreted  it
and  like  any  other  professional  in  the  field  all  other  things  being

gested  that  professionals  in  the  field  will  depart  from  the  standard

Huron-Clinton  Metropolitan  Authority  would  be an  example  of an

THE  COURT:  Let  me  ask  you  this  question,  Professor.  Do
you  take  into  account  in exploring  this  subject  the  perception  of
outsiders?

A.  In  a sense  that  is exactly  what  controls  the  perception  of a
person  in  another  area.  That  is  when  he  is  trying  to  explain  to
somebody  where  he  is from,  he  responds  to  what  he  thinks  they
know  about  the  region  so  he  centers  on  the  important  aspects  of
the  region  and  develops  his  definition  of himself  for somebody  in
those  terms  so  that  somebody  who  comes  from  an  area  whose
socio-economic  life  is  centered  on  Detroit  will  name  Detroit  be-
cause  he  knows  that  the  outsider  perceives  Detroit  but  perhaps
does  not  perceive  Pleasant  Ridge  or  Warren  or  something  else  as a
separate  entity.

When  he  is talking  with  somebody  who  is a  near-outsider  he

can  make  finer  distinctions.

‘THE  COURT:  I would  expect  that  you  would  say  that  the
farther  removed  the  stranger  is  the  larger  the  [219]  community
becomes.

,

A.  I’m  sure  that  is true.

Q.  Could  you  list  for the  Court  if you  would  some  of  the

A.  I was  appointed  as  a  probationary  teacher  at  the  Linge-
mann  [224]  School  in  February  of  1942.  I was  promoted  to  a
teaching  assistant  principalship  at  the  Monteith  School  in 1950; to

to  a  principalship  at  the  Crosman  School  in  1960.  I was  laterally
transferred  to the  Herman  School  in  1965,  and  last  September I was
“appointed  the  Principal  of the  Region  3 Magnet  Middle  School.

Q.  That  is the  position  you  now  hold?
A.  Yes,  sir.
Q.  In regard  to  the  position  that you  now  hold  was  this  a pos-
ition  that  you  were  asked  to assume  or  is this  the  position  you  vol-
unteered  for?

A.  I was  invited  to  accept  the  responsibility  and  was  named
the  administrator  on  March  15,  1971,  and  from  that  time  to May,
I  was  still  the  principal  at  the  Herman  School,  was  released

year  ago  this  spring,  to  begin  the  formulation  of  the  initial
proposals  and  the  program  for  the  middle  school  which  was
opened  the  following  September,  this  last  September.

IVa  47

application  if they  would  like  to  take  the  job.  At  that  time  we
were  in  the  process  of  formulating  a  program  at  the  Herman

program  and  I was  reluctant  to  make  application  for anything  un-
til  we  had  expended  that  money  and  formulated  a  creative  pro-
gram  at the Herman  School.

On  request  of our  region  superintendent  —  that  I would  con-

one  operation  I did  submit  a  letter  stating  under  certain  circum-
stances  I would  accept  such  a position.

Q.  Would  you  tell  the  Court,  please,  exactly  what  you  did  be-
ginning  on  May  6,  in  terms  of  developing  the  Region  3 middle
magnet  school?

A.  We  began  prior  to  May 6, to,  with  an  understanding  that
May  6, being  a  Monday,  it’s  always  a very  good  day  to start doing
something  but  prior  to  that  we  worked  through  our  entire  spring
vacation  period  which  was  in  April  of that  year  to  [228]  formu-
late  plans  that  we  would  present  to  our  region  board,  they  would.
be  returned  to  us,  these  were  largely  curriculum  in  nature,  end
goals,  products,  the  direction  in which  the  magnet  program  would
take,  what  our  end  product  of such  a  school  program  would  be,
what  innovative  measures  would  be taken.  We  began  the  process  of
description  of  staff  and  staffing  for  the  school  and  all At this
before  the  school  was  named  or  the  site  located.

Q»  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  I take  it that  the  development  of the  mag-

net  middle  school  was  done  under  your  direction?

A.  Yes,  sir.

:

Q.  In  terms  of obtaining  students  for  the  school  did  you  en-

gage  in a recruitment  program?

A.  It  was  not  a  recruitman  program  as  such.  It was  more  of
an  interpretive  program.  A  program  to interpret  some  of the  aims,
goals, designs  of the  curriculum.

Q.  What  did  you  do?

A.  We  were  contacted  by  parents  groups  and  we  made  the
church  meetings  on  Sundays  and  held  open  house  on  Sundays,
went  to  a  variety  of schools  in our  region  to  give publicity  to  the
fact  that  this  was  something  new,  something  different,  something
creative  and  something  that  would  be  of  a  high  quality  for  our
young  people.

Q.  In  other  words,  would  it  be  fair  to  say,  Dr.  Ritzenhein,
what  [229]  you  and  your  staff  did  was  go  out  and  tell  the com-
munity  about  the  magnet  middle  schools?

}

A.  That  would  be correct.
Q.  Your  community  was  the community  known  as  Region  3?

1V a 50

A.  Yes,  sir.

Q.  It has about  35,000  school  children  in it?

A.  That’s  correct.

Q.  Did  you  accept  applications  then  from  students  to attend

Region  3?

A.  The  application  process  was  from  the  Central  magnet
school  office.  The  applications  were  designed  by  the  Central  ma
gnet  school  office.  The  applications  were  distributed  to  each
school  in  the  City  of Detroit  and  I will  speak  for  Region 3 only at
this  point.  The  applications  were  sent  to  the  schools  where  parents
could  pick  them  up  and  return  them  to  the  region  office  where
upon  arrival,  upon  receipt,  they  were  stamped  with  a  number  1,2,
on  up,  and  the date  of arrival.

Upon  the  collection  of these  applications  at  the  region  office |

was  there  almost  daily  picking  them  up,  arranging  them  in  some
kind  of order  and  following  the  receipt  of applications  it became
apparent  to  us  quite  early  that  the  number  of places  we  had  for
young  people  was  quickly  diminished  and  so  the  region
superintendent  and  staff  decided  on  the  4th  of  June  that  they
would  accept  no  more  applications.

In  a  school  [230]  that  would  house  and  could  physically
house  approximately  400  to 450  youngsters  we  had  already  had in
receipt  over  800  applications.

QQ.  Were  these  applications  from  both  black  and  white

«
students?

A.  Yes,  sir.

Q.  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  after  you  got  the  800  applications  did  you

and  your  staff do any  interviewing?

A.  The  problem  of  deciding  how  the  youngsters  were  to be
assigned  to  the  school  reached  some  very  critical  stages.  No.  |, if
we  took  everyone  numerically  as  they  arrived  at  the  region  office
--we  would  be  discriminating  against  those  youngsters  whose  par
ents  wanted  to  apply  early  but  the  school  was  out  of applications
and,  consequently,  came  in late.

1Va5l

We  decided  then  that  we  would  establish  as  nearly  as possible
the  quota  of the  number  of youngsters  by Grade  5, 6, 7 and  so  on.

Q.  When  you  said  “quota”  what  did  you  mean?
A.  By  taking  a  larger  percentage  of  the  younger  youngsters
over  a  four-year  period  of time  within  a  program  their  evaluation
would  be  more  statistically  significant  than  taking  a  few  number
of youngsters  at  the  eighth  grade  level  because  they  would  be with
us  for  a  very  short  period  of time;  taking  only  those  youngsters  at
the  upper  level  by grade  whose  younger  siblings  were  accepted  in-
to  the  program  for  the  adjustment  [231]  during  the  first  year  or
so.  The  quota  also  was  based  on  sex.  You  couldn’t  put all boys  in
the  school  or  all  girls,  nor  could  you  have  more  boys  in the  sixth
grade  and  all  girls  in  the  seventh  grade,  so  it was  established  we
would  try  to  select  those  youngsters  by race,  by sex  and  by grade
to get an  even  balance  to  the  school.

Q.  I want  to  ask  you  about  race.  What  was  the aim  establish-

ed in terms  of race?

A.  We  tried  to  establish  the  balance  at  50 per  cent  black  and

50 per cent  white.

Q.  At  the  present  time  how  many  students  do  you  have  in

the Region  3 middle  school?

A.  400.  I haven’t  been  there  in two  days.
Q.  Well,  racially  what  is the  percentage  of  black  and  what  is

the percentage  of white?

A.  50 per cent  black  and  50 per  cent  white.  It’s within  .4 of  1

per cent  of being precisely  racially  balanced.

Q.  Dr,-Ritzenhein,  did  you  interview  any  potential  pupils  for

the school?

A.  We  had  several  other  things  happen  prior  to  the  interview-
ing process.  We  had  letters  from  psychologists,  from  psychiatrists,
from  school  social  workers,  school  administrators,  from  ministers
within  the  basic  parish,  priests  and  so  forth,  who  made  strong
appeals  for  certain  youngsters  to  be  placed.  [232]  in  the  school
based  on  either  a  very  poor  previous  school  record,  a  history  of
truancy,  a  history  of  grade  failure,  a  history  of  perhaps  poor
school  adjustment:  I  know  we  received  youngsters  from  Haw-
thorne  Center  for  the  Emotionally  Disturbed  whose  director
specifically  stated  to return  the  youngster  to  the  school  where  the

1V.a  52

instructional  damage  syndrome had  really  done  something  to mold
the  kid  in  that  his  chance  for  school  success  was  greater  in the
environment  of the  new  setting  of the  middle  school.

Upon  receipt  of  those  letters  certain  notes  were  made  on  the
youngsters  application  forms  and  then  in  June  before  school
closing  I visited  several  of the  elementary  schools  having  notified
the  administrators  in advance,  having  the  parents  of those  young-
sters  who  made  application  at  te school  be  interviewed  at  the
school.

Q.  You  interviewed  the  parents  and  the  child?

A.  Yes,  the  child  was  not  seen  without  the  parents.  During
the  summer  those  interviews  not  completed  during  the  school  year
were  completed  during  July  and  August.  All  fifth,  sixth  and
seventh  grade  applicants  were  interviewed.  The  eighth  grade  appli-
cants  were  not,  having  previously  stated  they  only  were  invited  to
come  to  the  school  upon  their  application  if a  younger  sibling
were  there.

The  interview  process  did  seve

ZO
.  It  [233]  made  the
school,  No.  1, a very,  very  important  and  impressive  kind  of place
to  be  because  a  parent  had  never  been  interviewed  in  a  Detroit
public  school  before  about  the  youngsters  assignment  to  the
school.

Another  thing  that  was significant:  youngsters  do not  normal
ly make  application  to go to a Detroit  public  school.  This  put us in
a  separate  class,  let’s  say,  temporarily.  The  interviewers  were  to
determine  the  degree  of interest  the  child  had  or  lack  of interest,
the  degree  of interest  on  the  part  of the  parent,  an  explanation  on
the  part  of  the  parent  about  the  kind  of problem  the  youngster
had,  what  kind  of interest  the  child  had  in  terms  of his schooling
and  another  significant  question,  there  were  two  final  significant
questions,  one  had  to do with  how  do you  feel  about  the  reporting
system  in  the  Detroit  public  schools.  I refer  to  the  “report  card
syndrome”  and  the  feeling  that  the  youngster  and  parent  had  to
ward  the  integrative  prospect  of  the  program,  remembering  that
the  middle  school  had  the  two-fold  thrust,  one  was  for  quality
education  and  one  was  for the  integration  experience  for children.

THE  COURT:  I am  reminded  of  an  occasion  when  I had  a
golf date  at  1:00  o’clock.  We  were  running  summer  hours—  this
was  not  while  I was  on  the  Federal  bench,  it was  another  bench.
Some  high-powered  attorneys  presented a difficult  problem  which
I solved  before  they  rose  and  it was  about  five  minutes  to  1:00.  Of
course  as  you  will  notice  about  this  courtroom  the  clock  is always
in the  wrong  place.  It’s  not  placed  so  that  the  attorneys  can  see  it,
it’s always  placed  so  the judge  can  see  it.

We  were  supposed  to  recess  at  1:00  and  it was  five  to  1:00
and  I was  due  on  the  tee  at  that  time  by prior  commitment.  I said
to  counsel,  “I  assume  you  don’t  want  to  argue,  you  waive  argu-
ment?”  One  lawyer  said  he  would  and  the  other  fellow  said-  he
would  like  to  argue  it.  I said,  ‘Well,  you  go ahead  and  argue  it and
take  all  the  time  you  want  so  long as  you  finish  by  1:00  o’clock.”

So  -

Q.  Give  us  one  example.
A. I believe  it’s a fact  that  one  of the  features  of our  program
is youngsters  have  the  opportunity  to  select,  to  build  their  own
programs  and  to  make  choices  about  their  school  day,  to  [238]
have  input  into  what  happens  to  them  on  the  educational  setting
and  to  the  point  of  where  they  might  select  the  things  of  the
greatest  interest  to  them,  that  they  are  not  bound  by a time  period
in  the  completion  of  a  project  and  that  there  is  nothing  sacred
about  a bell  ringing  every  40 or  45 minutes.

Q.  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  the  program  has  been  operating  since  the

opening  of school,  that  is obvious.

A.  Yes.
Q.  What  type  of response  have  you  received  from  the  parents

IV a 56

during  this  time  about  the  program?

A.  We  have  had  an  overwhelmingly  favorable  response.  When
new  programs  open  there  is  generally  some  apprehension  on  the
part  of everybody.  We  experienced  the  same  apprehension.  It was
quickly  dissipated  however  with  the  smooth  arrival  of our  buses,
the  smooth  transportation  of our  youngsters,  there  was  a concern
of  parents  especially  the  parents  in  the  far  north  area  and  west
area  traveling  toward  predominantly  the  black  end  of  our  com-
munity.  There  was  apprehension  on  the  part  of these  parents.

This  was  very  rapidly  dissipated.  The  parents  who  used  to fol-
low  the  buses  in  their  cars  just  to  be  certain  the  kids  arrived  on
time  —  within  a  week,  no  more.  It wasn’t  necessary.  Then  when
the  youngsters  started  to  talk  more  and  more  about  their  school,
you  know  the  old  cliche,  a  [239]  youngster  comes  home  from
school  and  mother  says,  “What  did  you  do  today?”  and  the  kid
says,  “Nothing”  but  the  kid  has  been  gone  for  six  hours  and
obviously  something  had  to  happen.  We  heard  none  of  that.  Our
parents  have  reported  that  the  youngsters  have  been  extremely  res-
ponsive,  are  dragging  their  parents  all over  the  city to dig up  things
they need  for their  school  program  and  so  forth.

Q.  You  do have  PTA  meetings?

A.  We  have  school  community  council.

Q.  What  type  of response  have  you  got  to  meetings  of your

school  community  council?

A.  We  introduced  to  the  school  community  council  the  fact
we  would  have  parent  workshops  to  help devise  the  new  reporting
system  that  would  be  used  for  our  youngsters.  We  had  more  re-
sponses  from  parents  who  wanted  to  be  part  of an  all  day  Satur-
day workshop  than  we  could  accommodate.

That  is an  example.

Q.  Are  these  interracial  groups?

A.  Yes,  they  are.

Q.  In  your  observation  in  the  five  or  six  months  that  you

have  had  the  program  are  children  of both  races  mixing?

A.  Yes,  they  are.

Q.  Are  they  doing  projects  together?

A.  Yes,  they  are.

IVa  57

Q.  [240]  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  do  you  believe  that  this  particular

program  had  desegregated  —

A.  For  the  youngsters  I speak  for,  Region  3, it has  been  an
excellent  example  of desegregation,  an  excellent  example  of child-
ren  playing  in  string  quartets  together  and  in concert  bands,  glee
clubs,  working  on  projects  and  basketball  teams  and  the  cheer
leaders.  The  total  experience  of our  youngsters  always  appear  to
end up just representative  of the  population  of our  school.

Q.  Do  you  believe,  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  that  this  program  as  it

now  operates  can  desegregate  the  entire  system?

A.  No, sir.

Q.  Why?
A.  The  ingredients  are  not  present  in  the  City  of Detroit  to

make  for meaningful  desegregation  for  the  total  City of Detroit.

Q.  When  you  say  the  ingredients,  will  you  tell  the Court  what

you  mean  by the  ingredients.

A.  The  numbers  of our  young  people  of each  race.
Q.  Do  you  believe  that  a  plan  that  puts  65  per  cent  black
children  and  35  per  cent  white  children  in each  school  would  de-
segregate  Detroit?

A:  It would  not.

Q.  Why?
A.  It would  result  in a resegregated  situation.  My administrat-  .
ive  [241]  experience  in  the  past  has  been  and I will  use  the  Her-
man  School  as  a  base  statistic.  I came  to  the  Herman  School  in
1960.  It was  11  per cent  black.  Now  in the  intervening  years  as  the
race  change  came  into  the  community,  when  it  came  past  the
point  of tipping  the  balance  then  we  found  that  the  per  cent  then
continued  to escalate  for the  black  students  and  de-escalate  for  the
white  students.  I understand  from  the  present  administratory  now
—  this  has  to  go  back  to  when I left  in  May  —  that  the  school
population  now  is approximately  60 per cent  black.

Q.  You  used  the  word  “tipping  the  balance”  what  do you  be-

lieve  was  the  tipping?

A.  I think  that  when  you  go through  a resegregation  cycle  for
a  while  a  school  is desegregated  because  there  are  white  children

IVa  58

—,

still  on  the  campus  and  as  the  black  population  escalates  we  find
that  there  is  a  rapid  de-escalation  of the  white  population.  So it
reaches  a  point  somewhere  between  50 per  cent  and  55  per  cent. |
think  it’s  going  to  vary  depending  upon  the  community.  It will  be
more  rapid  in some  communities  and a little  slower  in others.

Q.  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  referring  to this  magnet  middle  school,  the
Region  3 magnet  middle  school,  has  that  school  adopted a visita-
tion  program  with  a  middle  school  outside  of the City  of Detroit?

{242]  A.  In  our  attempt  to  broaden  our  youngsters  horizons
we  began  some  correspondence,  not  just  with  the  city,  I won’t  go
outside  of the  city, just  for communication  purposes,  we  also  com-
municated  with  the  West  Bloomfield  middle  school.  We  had  seen
pictures  of  the  new  high  school  in  our  Sunday  magazines  in the.
News  and  so  forth.

Q.  And  the  Free  Press?

A.  Or the  Free  Press,  was  it?  *

Q.  In both  papers.
A.  It was  in  both  papers.  I will  be kind  to say  I did  not  see  it
in the Christian  Science  Journal.  The  eighth  grade  history  class and
one  of our  city  classes  corresponded.  They  were  interested  in the
eighth  grade West  Bloomfield  eighth  grade  history  class;  they were
interested  in  exploring  the  busing  situation,  integration  or  desegre-
gation  or  as  it existed  in Detroit,  what  were  the  youngsters  feeling
being  in  a middle  school  and  how  was  the  process  of desegregation
affecting  them.

Q.  Let  me  make  clear  for  the  record  the  West  Bloomfield

middle  school  is in the  West  Bloomfield  school  district?

A.  Yes.

Q.  Fine.  What  did  you  do?

A.  We  invited  them.  We  thought  the  firsthand  experience,  pr-
mary  experiences  are  much  better  than  secondary  experience,
which  is correspondence.  A couple  carloads  of youngsters  came  to
[243]  visit  our  school.  They  were  at our  school  for,  from  about
8:30  for a little  briefing  session  prior  to  our  start  of our  original
session  at  quarter  to 9:00.  We  selected  a group  of our  eighth  grade
youngsters  to  be  their  escorts,  their  pals  as  it  were  for  the  day
through  the  noonhour  and  until  dismissal  when  the  group  left. We

IV a 59

continued  our  correspondence  and  were  invited  out  to  the  West
Bloomfield  area  to  meet  the  same  friends  that  had  been  establish-
ed.

Our  plans  call  now  for continuing  our  exchange  visits  through
the  rest  of this spring.  One  of the  suggestions  made  is that  we  start
with  the  younger  group  of  youngsters  next  year  instead  of  the
eighth  grade  so  they  would  have  a longer  period  of time,  a  four-
year  block  of  time  for  better  acquaintanceship,  a  better  sharing
experience  and  perhaps  eventually  some  other  kind  of  relation-
ships,  I don’t  know.

Q.  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  the  group  that  you  took  out  to  the
Bloomfield,  West  Bloomfield  middle  school  was  that a racially
mixed  school?

A.  It was  representative  of our  school.

Q.  50-50?

A.  Right.
Q.  Just  out  of curiosity  do  you  recall  how  long it took,  trans-
portationwise,  how  long  it  took  the  children  to  get  out  to  West
Bloomfield  school?

.

[244]  A.  Being  pilot  and  also-navigator  of one  of the  cars  I
ought  to  tell  you  something.  I got  lost  only  once  myself,  going
past  a turn  which  meant  coming  back,  getting  back  on  the  freeway  .
and  getting  back  down  to make  the  turn.  It was  about  a 25  minute
ride  from  West  Bloomfield  from  school.

Q.  A 25 minute  ride  and  you  were  lost?
A.  Yes,  sir.
Q.  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  do  you  have  any  idea  how  far  the  West.

Bloomfield  middle  school  was  from  your  school?

A.  Unfortunately  I did  not  check  the  mileage.
Q.  Dr.  Ritzenhein  —
THE  COURT:  You  use  the  term  middle  school  as  applied  to
the  West  Bloomfield  school.  Am  I going  to  give  it  the  same
meaning  as  the  middle  school  in Region  3?

MR.  ROUMELL: I will  let Dr.  Ritzenhein  answer  that.
A.  West  Bloomfield  is going  toward  the  direction  of middle
schools  and  this  is  the  new  West  Bloomfield  middle  school  en-
compassing  the  same  grades  that  we  encompass  in  our  regions  in

Detroit.

THE  COURT:  It has the  same  type  of approach  to education?

A.  Their  approach  is a little  different.  You  see,  just  being a
middle  school  is encompassing  younger  kids  10 to  14 is one  [245]
part  of  the  program.  They  are  unique  in  that  they  have a brand
new  plant  designed  for  large  group  assemblies  and  so  forth.  We
made  do with  much  less.  There  are  parts of their  program  without
going  into  too  many  details  that  our  youngsters  thought  were  a
little  stale.

There  is a little  more  electricity  in the  Region  3 middle  school
because  they  are  sort  of an  electric  bunch  of eels  —  real good  kids.

Q.  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  you  indicated,  I believe,  in response  to my

also  indicated  you  did  not  believe  a 65-35  plan would  desegregate

person  who  spent  30  years  of  your  life  in  the  Detroit  system,
could  do an  effective  desegregation  job?

A.  The  only  plan  I  can  see  ultimately  would  be  the

metropolitan  plan.

Q.  Do  you  believe  or  would  you  favor  the  institution  of an

A.  An interim  plan would  tend  to  —

THE  COURT:  It  reminds  me  of  another  instance  where  we
had  a  condemnation  proceeding  for  the  municipal  center  for  the
City  of Flint.  A matter  of perhaps  150  parcels  were  taken  care  of
by agreement  of  the  parties  with  respect  to  value  and  about  17
parcels  were  left  for  determination  by  the jury.  We  respect  to one
of the  parcels,  two  or  three  high-powered  appraisers  who  said  they
had  taken  correspondence  [247]  courses  in  appraisals  and  also
attended  so-called  seminars,  which  I think  were  conventions,  were
over  on  one  side  and  on  the  other  side  one  of the  best  lawyers  in
this  state  offered  as  his witness  a man  from  the  field  who  had  been
buying  and  selling  houses.

In qualifying  him  he  asked  him  if he had  ever  enrolled  in one
of these  correspondence  schools  and  he said  no,  he hadn’t,  and  he
asked  him  whether  he  had  attended  any  of these  seminars  and  he
admitted  he  had  not.  And  he  said,  “How  do  you  feel  you  are
qualified  to  express  an  opinion  about  the  value  of Parcel  17?”  He
said,  “Well,  I have  been  buying  and  selling  houses  like  that  for  30
years  and just last  week  I sold  three just like  it.”

I accepted  him  as qualified.  I think  the  witness  is well  qual-

ified.

Objection  overruled.

Q.  (By Mr.  Roumell)  Do you  recall  the  question,  Doctor?

A.  Would  you  please  restate  the  question,  Mr.  Roumell?

Q.  Thank  you,  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  do  you  believe  based  upon
your  statement  that  you  believe  the  solution  is  a  metropolitan
plan and  that  you participated  in a desegregation  plan  in the city of
Detroit,  a  brand  new  desegregation  plan,  do  you  believe  that
between  the magnet  plan and  the adoption  of a metropolitan  plan
that  there  should  be adopted  an  interim  plan?

[248] A. I do not.

Q.  Would  you  tell us  why?

1V a 62

A.  I believe  that  if we  are  going  to  move  forward  towards
meaningful  desegregation  of  our  youngsters,  if we  are  going  to
have  the  fullest  respect  for  young  and  for  their  self-development
and  responsibility  that  the  shifting  and  opening  of  schools  and
closing  of schools  and  most  of the children  around  the  city  prior
to  such  a  time  would  be  confused,  it would  be  confusing  to  our
staff,  confusing  to our  community  and  certainly  not  educationally
very  sound.

Q.  Do  you  believe  that  the  magnet  middle  school  program
_that  you  participated  in  can  aid  in  the  implementation  of  a
metropolitan  plan?

it might  be
A.  I agree  that  it  could  very  much  so.  I believe
even  the  catalyst  through  which  some  of these  changes  could  be
made.  If we  can  prove  that  education  in Detroit  can  be  superior
even  to  education  in  a  higher  priced  area,  if we  can  prove  our
school  is an  attractive  qualifying  situation  institution  for youth  to
come  and  participate  in,  if we  can  convince  parents  throughout
our  metropolitan  area  that  these  kind  of programs  exist  and  can  be
developed  within  our  school  I think  our  chances  to have  meaning-
ful  integration  experiences,  desegregation  experiences  for  our
youngsters  is far more  easily  attained.

{249}Q.  Am  I  correct,  Dr.  Ritzenhein,  you  began  to  see

ticipated  in?

other  magnet  schools  may  feel  when  you  take  a look  at  the  size  of

the  size  of, and  I am  thinking  of a residential  area,  larger  than  the
city of Ann  Arbor.

These  youngsters,  many  are  coming  back  and  forth  to school,
they  are  not  missing  days in attendance.  There  is enthusiasm  diffi-
cult  to  describe,  it would  be hearsay  and  merely  my  perceptions  as
1 am  recording  them  to  you  now. I think  it is, well,  we  are  sort  of
miniature  metropolitan  plan  right  now  all by ourselves.

A.  Yes,

as Plan  A submitted  by the  Defendant  Detroit  School  Board?

Detroit?

A.  In my opinion  it would  not.

IV a 66

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  do  you  have  an  opinion  as  to  whether  Plan  A
as  testified  to  would  accomplish  the  greatest  possible  degree  of
actual  desegregation  for  the  Detroit  System,  and  if you  have  an
opinion  would  you  tell  us  what  it is and  the  basis  for it.

A.  Well,  is using  the  word  “greatest,”  I think  you  would  have
to compare  it with  something  else.  In terms  of the  Plaintiffs  plan |
do  not  think  it would  approach  in any  way  the  amount  of desegre-
gation  involved  in that  plan.  First  of all, I think  it is  a misnomer  to
call  it a  desegregation  plan.  Mr.  McDonald  himself  testified  to that
effect.

Secondly,  as  I understand  it the  task  at  hand  was  to provide,
to  come  forth  with  a  plan  that  woulddesegregate  the  Detroit
schools  in  the  fall  of  1972.  I don‘t  think  even  the  adherents  of
Plan  A  agree  that  it  would  do  that.  For  one  thing  the  plan  is
basically,  offers  unequal  educational  opportunity  which  I think  is
not  appropriate  and  it  sort  of sets  up  a  private  school  operation
within  the  framework  of a public  school  system  by taking  applica-
tions  for  admission  and  this  sort  of thing,  and  it seems  to me  it is
inherently  unequal  in  setting  up  so—called  quality  education  for
some  children  and  limiting  it  to  certain  children  who  would  be
accepted  on  application.

[295]  Idon’t  think  there isany

promise  that  it  would
desegregate  the  Detroit  System  in the  fall  of  1972.  Its track  record
for  the  current  year  is  certainly  far  below  the  expectations  that
were  held  for it. Admittedly  it has been  a failure  at the  high school
level  and  in  the  middle  school  operation  where  it has  had  some
measure  of  success,  if  you  relate  the  total  amount  of  children
involved  directly  in the  operation,  something  under  4,000  children
and  compare  it to  the  middle  school  operation  it is a  drop  in the
bucket.

If in  fact  the  projections  which  were  made  for  the  fall  were
followed  out,  that  is  to  say  40  schools  wéuld  be  involved  as
described  in  the  presentation  both  vertically  and  horizontally,  this
would  still  only  affect  20,000  children,  which  would  leave a vast
‘number  of children  still  in  segregated  situations  and  not  affected.

At  the  high  school  level  it  seems  to  me  that  the  plan  runs
against  current  feelings  in  education  about  what  high  schools

1V  a67

should  be.  To  the  best  of my  knowledge  there  has been  no  strong
counter  proposal  to  Dr.  James  Conant’s  philosophy  on  the
American  high  school  which  has  been  presented  for quite  a while
now  but  his  position  is that  the  best  American  high school  educa-
tion  is  offered  in  comprehensive  high  schools.  The  specialized
program  envisioned  by  Plan  A  would  run  contrary  to  this  [296]
approach.

Certainly  at  the  elementary  and  middie  school  levels  the plan
is primarily  what  we  would  call  an  open  enroliment,  freedom  of
choice  plan,  or  certainly  a  modified  one  at  best,  and  our  exper-
ience  in Southern  desegregation  has  been  pretty clear that  freedom
of  choice  plans  do  not  work,  and  this  is  all.seen  in historical
perspective  at  this  point at least in  the  South.

The  proposed  provision  for  a  majority  to ‘minority-  tratisfer
role  again  seems  to  offer  no  relief  because  again historically  in
desegregated  situations  it has  been  quite  clear  that  whites  will  not
volunteer  to  attend  black  schools,  while  initially,  under  freedom
of choice  plans in the  South,  there  were  a few  blacks  who opted  to
go  to  white  schools,  this  died  out  fairly  rapidly.  No large  numbers
of black  children  in any  attempt  opted  to make this kind  of trans-
fer.

The  plan.  would ‘be very  costly  for a system  that is apparently
already  in  financial  problems.  It would  seem  to.me  inappropriate
in light  of Serrano-type  decisions in  school finance to attempt  to
minimize  expenditures  among  school  districts  to have  an intra-
district
expenditure  where’  you expended  considerably  more
amounts  of  money  at  some  schools  than  others:  Short  ‘of  a
desegregation  plan that:  [297]  is  supposed'to  work  ‘ow and  to
promise  relief,  it seems  to  me  somewhat  akin  to oe a band
aid and  aspirin  to adying  man.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,

I would  like to  ask you, sir,  to describe  for us
the substance  of the plan that  you have  prepared  and in so doing,
sir,  | ask not that  you,  with respect  to every school  or every  grade
level, that  you explore [324] the plan in infinite  detail,  but if you
would,  sir,  please  state the steps involved  in your  operation,  the
data relied  upon  in preparing  those steps,  and would  you in the
course  of  your  description  illustrate  on the basis  of  the exhibit
now  evident  your  manner  of preparation  of  the  plan  and the
results  secured?

A.  First  of all, the data  which  I used in the plan have  been
submitted  as exhibits.  The capacity  and enrollment  by high  school
constellations  for September  of "71,  the breakdown  of schools  by
grade  by race  made  in October  of ‘71,  the various  maps  that  have
been  involved  in the  case  prior  to this,  and then  finally  the
material  prepared  by  Mr.  Henrickson  which  was for all practical
purposes  the base of our plan and our plan.

I think  it  might  be helpful  to  go briefly  through  one cluster
’  to  illustrate  how the plan  works.  Perhaps  we won't need to do that
for every cluster  because  it  would  be pretty  time-consuming  but
the  same  principle  would  go from  cluster  to cluster  in terms  of
how  we proceeded.

First of  all we did have some criteria  associated  with  the plan  —
development.  One  is that, of course,  both  majority  group  members
and minority  group  members  would  be treated  equally  in terms  of
assignment,  transportation,  whatever  the  niceties  of  the  plan
involved  as far as pupils  were  concerned.  I think  this has largely
been  followed.

[325]  For example,|]  think  in  terms of transportation it
involved  pupils
in Cluster  1.  There  is a  very  slight  difference
between  the total number  of blacks  required  under this  manner  to
be transported  and the total number  of  whites.  We also asked  the
Board  to  prepare  estimates  of transportation  required.  These were
to  be placed  on a limit  of approximately  one mile for elementary
students  and one mile and  a half for junior  high school  students.
We  asked  that  the feeder  pattern  in the Detroit  System  in the

1Va75

high school  constellations  be  reconstructed  in such  a manner  that
the  flow  from  elementary  schools  to  middle  schools  or junior high
schools  would  result  in junior  high schools  that  were  substantially  —
proportionate  in  racial  composition  and the flow from junior  high
schools  to  senior  high  schools  would  again  result  in substantially
proportionate  racial  makeup  at senior  high schools.

Then  at  the  elementary  schools  it remained  simply  to  work
out  a  system  of pairing  and  clustering, changing grade levels in such
manner®  that  the  racial  makeup  of the  elementary  schools  would
also be roughly  proportionate.

Initially  the  clusters  were  developed  so  that  the  five  clusters
were  themselves  of an  approximate  racial  makeup  and I think  the
percentage  of  white  and  black  did  not  vary  more  than  perhaps  2
percent  among  the  five  clusters.

So  another  suggestion  we  made  was  that  [326]  initially  at
least  the  Kindergarten  children  be  assigned  to  the  present  base
until  at  least  such  time  as  we  could  see  how  the  plan  would
develop  and  what  would  be  required  in  terms  of  transportation
time  and  distances  and  how  the enrollment  patterns  would  fit into
capecities  of buildings  and  so  forth.  So these  were  left  aside.

One  other  requirement  we  asked  of the  plan’s  development
was  that  in  pairing  or  clustering  elementary  schools,  historically  in
desegregation  plans  in  some  cities  there  has  been  a  tendency  for
formerly  black  schools  or  predominantly  black  schools  to  be
assigned  upper  level  pupils  at  the  elementary  grade,  that  is to say
grades 4 to 6 and  grades  3 to  6, grades  5 to  6;  what we call the
intermediate  grades  would  be  assigned  to  the  formerly  black
schools  and  the  primary  grades  would  be assigned  to the  formerly
white  schools.

We  requested  that this not be done in the plan, but that there
be sort of  an  indiscriminate  chance  distribution  so there would  be
a  large  number  of  primary  pupils  assigned  to formerly  black
schools  as well as to  formerly  white schools.  I think that this has
been  done  very  effectively  in the plan,  to the best  of my
knowledge.

A.  I think  it could,  yes,  sir.
Q.  Dr.  Foster,  do  you  have  an  opinion,  sir,  whether  a  plan
embodying  the  principles  you  have  described  implemented  in  the
fall  of  1972  would  be  or  could  be  synchronized  with  the  metro-
politan  approaches  to  desegregation?

A.  You  are  asking  this question  assuming  that  a metropolitan
plan  might  take  effect  a  year  afterwards  or  two  years  [349] after-
wards  or  something  like  that?

Q.  Yes,  sir,  just  as  an  assumption  for  the  purposes  of  the

question.

A.  I think  I stated  in  the  narrative  that  the plan  was not
designed  with  metro  in  mind.  However,  under  deposition  with
Attorney  Beer  I  think  I also  stated  in  a  configuration  sense  it
seems  to  me  that  a  plan  like  this  would  be involved  in any  metro-
politan  plan  that  was  developed.

I haven’t  gone  into  them  extensively,  but  it seems  to me  any
metropolitan  plan that  is developed  is going  to have  to cut  into  the
City of Detroit  in some  sort  of pie,  wedge-shaped  pieces,  in order
to pick up Detroit  students.

IVa  78

In  a  configuration  ‘sense  these  five  clusters  that  have  been

ment  with  some  changes.  I think in that sense  the plan  could  be
over to a metropolitan  plan.
transposed

Q.  Do  you  have  an  opinion,  sir,  whether  a  plan  embodying
these  principles  would  be more  compatible  or  less  compatible,  or
equally  compatible  than  the magnet  Plan  A  would  be with  a
metropolitan  desegregation  approach?

A. I think  that is sort of difficult  to answer  because  the
promises of the magnet  plan are only  promises  and  I [350]  don’t
think  there  is any  way we can estimate  what will actually  happen
to  the  extent  that  the  magnet  plan,  Plan  A, does  not  actually
result  in any  appreciable  desegregation.  Presumably  then  a metro-
politan  plan  coming  later  would  simply  take Detroit  as it is now
and  build  on  that  base to  the  extent  that  this  plan  would  in  fact
desegregate  at all levels

of the City of Detroit.

Then  there  would  have  to be more  changes  just on  that basis
alone,  assuming  then  that a metropolitan  plan came  later.  Basically
I don’t  think  that either  plan is geared  towards  translation  into  the
metropolitan  arrangement  specifically.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  assuming  that  implementation  of a  plan  such

occasion  greater  inconvenience  to  the  system  than  permanent

whether  integration  would  be  more  harmful  to  the  pupils  of
Detroit  than  the  greater  inconvenience  resulting  from  two  changes
in a short  period  of time?

A.  Well,  I think  educationally  the  adoption  of the  proposed
plan  would  be advantageous  if for no  other  reason  than  the  fact  it
would  simply  happen  and  a  bird  in  the  hand  [351]  is always
worth  more than  a bird in the bush.
