A.  A  plan  that desegregates.  This is, I think,  taken from  court
parlance  which  says  the  court  wants a plan  that  works
and works
now,  works  realistically  and  works  now,  something  to that  effect.

THE  COURT:  Do you  know  what  they  meant?

A.  Well,  I think  in  terms  of what  Mr.  Beer was referring  to as
a desegregation  process  is what  they  meant  and  the  court  wasn’t
involved  in  the  educational  niceties  but  the constitutional  require-
ments.

as a layman  would
because  some  courts as I have  read decisions
include  in their  conception  of a plan that  works,  this matter.

pormotes  stability.

just as you  use  the  term  “work”

Q.  How  about  the  question  of  educational  benefit  derived

A.  I would  hope that any desegregation  plan  would be educa-

tionally  beneficial,  yes.

Q.  But  is it possible,  sir,  that  you  could  have  a desegregation
plan  that  works  as  you  use  the  terms  that  does  not  have  an
educational  benefit?

A.  I don’t think  that you can guarantee  this in recommending
a  plan  for implementation  that  it  is going  to,  in all cases,  be
educationally  beneficial.  We  go  on  the  assumption  that  is what  we
are doing  it for in the first  place and if it is. carried  out with
prioriety  and respect  that it will be educationally  beneficial.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  in your  comments  and what  I believe  is PC  2
the  original  document,  you  do refer  to  the  purpose of the  plan as
being  to  eliminate  racially  identifiable  schools  so  that  the
community  does  not  have  black  schools  or  white  schools  but just
schools.  I hope this will be the last definition  I ask for. What  do
you  mean  by a racially  identifiable  school?

A.  I think

a racially  identifiable  school,  the  term has to be
taken  in  context  to  the  system  that  is  under  consideration  and
that  you can  basically  assign  the description  of racially  identifiable
to  any  given  school  until  you  do consider  the  school  within  the
context  of the total  system.

[359] Technically  I think  racially  identifiable  school  is one
that  is disproportionate  in  racial  makeup  to  the other  schools  in
that system.  I think  I stated  in my deposition  with  you  that in my
opinion  there  is also  a  psychological  factor  involved  and  that  I
am  not  sure  but  I believe  I  mentioned  the  Tampa  system  in
Florida  which  is a  metropolitan  system  as all counties  are in
Florida  since  they are  county  districts.

But  there  are,  under  Tampa’s  present  plan  either  two  or
perhaps  three  schools.that  from  a  numbers  standpoint  would  be
racially  identifiable  because  they are  either  close  to being  all white
or black  or  they are,  I don’t  remember  exactly  but within  the total

IVa8l

context  of the  Hillsborough  County  or Tampa  system,  I would  not
say  they  are  racially  identifiable  because  they  are  isolated  schools
and  for  certain  reasons  the  community  I don’t  believe  perceives
them  as being racially  identifiable  or as segregated  schools.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  let me  ask  you  this, in your judgment  would  a
school  in Detroit  today,  right now,  which  has a student population
that  was  65 per  cent  black  and  35  per  cent  white  be  a racially
identifiable  school?

ba

A.  I suppose  by  my  definition  in  a  sense  it would  be com-
pletely  disproportionate  to  most  schools  in  the  system  if it were
65  per  cent  black  and  35  per  cent  white,  so  in that  strict  sense
[360]  it would  be racially  identifiable.

In  terms  of Detroit  as  a  desegregated  system  where  the racial

mix  is 65-35  then,  of course,  it wouldn’t  be.

Q.  You  are  saying  that under  the  current  system  it would  be

racially  identifiable  under  the system  as it is now?

A.  In  a  technical  sense  because  it would  be  so  different  to
most  of the  other  schools  in the  system  which  are  either  all black
or all white  or nearly  so  for the most  part,

of the  schools  are  65-35  it loses  its identity.  I take  it its  identity
would  be  as  a  black  school  now,  is  that  what  you  suggest  cur-
rently?

A.  It would  be in the sense  that you put it I think  as a school
that  was  racially  different  in makeup  from  most  of the schools  in
the system.

A.  It  obviously  wouldn’t  be  identified  as  a  white  school  I

suppose  what  —

Is that  what  you  mean?

reaches

IV a 84

A.  I think  it does  so  in a variety  of ways.  One  certainly  would
be in his own  self-concept  in knowing that the city of Detroit  and
the Detroit  school  system  was  offering him the same  opportunities
that  it offered  all of the  other  children  in  Detroit in a rough  way,
so  that  he  would  not  feel  that  he  was,  shall  we  say,  racially
isolated  or contained  in a segregated  situation.

I think  this  would  be very  effective  also  on  the  expectations
that  were  made  for  him  by particularly  teachers  in  terms  of such
things  as  a  self-fulfilling  prophecy  and  also  parents  and  others
whose  expectations  for  children  in  ghetto  schools  or  racially
isolated  schools  seem  to be considerable  different  than  for those  in
suburban  type  schools  or  predominantly  middle  class  or  white
schools.

I think  the  chances  in  a  city  like  Detroit  of  his  obtaining
benefit  from  what  we  might  call  delivery  of [365]  services  would
be  greatly  enhanced  by  this  desegregation  process  because  there
seems  to  be a  tendency  for  desegregation  to more  equally  balance
such  delivery  of services.

Q.  Would  you  perceive  those  same  benefits  occurring  in the
same  fashion  where  the  racial  proportion  of  the  Detroit  school
system  as  a  whole  80 per cent  black  and  20 per cent  white  so  that
the  average  child  would  be in a school  that  was  80 per cent  black?
A.  Insofar  as  the  city  of  Detroit  was  concerned  I think  so,
yes.  Insofar  as  something  like  the delivery  of services,  for example,
were  affected  by an  all black  school  district  surrounded  by mostly
all  white  districts  in the  total  context  this  might  affect  the  situa
tion.

Q.  It would  have  a greater  affect  at  80  per  cent  than  65  per

cent?

A.  Well,  I think  the  chances  of  something  like  delivery  of
services  being  equal  to  an  all  black  city  school  population  or
nearly  all  black  in  a  ey  that  is  80  per  cent  white  would  be
lessened.

This  is  one  of  the  reasons  why  I have  spoken  in  behalf of

metro.

Q.  How  about  a  perception  of  himself  as  being  racially

even  though  the  rest  of the  schools  were  80  per  cent  black  there

of?

[366]  A.  If  the  metropolitan  portion  surrounding  the
Detroit  system  has  its makeup  of 95 per cent  white  or  whatever  it
is, as  the child  becomes  more  aware  of his larger environment  then
Iam  sure  he would  be aware  he is in a city and  goes  to city schools
that are  almost  all black.

Q.  Would  that  same  phenomenon  occur  in a  school  that  was

not 65 per cent  black?

A.  I think  so  far as  his  position  in the  city  is concerned  the

important  thing is equality  in respect  to the  rest  of the city.

Q.  Doctor,  I have  this  problem,  we  talk  about  the  case at 80
per cent,  we  seem  to find  it necessary  to discuss  the suburbs.  When
we  are  talking  about  the  65  per  cent  it  seems  to  be possible  to
discuss  it in terms  of the  framework  of the  city  alone.  What  I am
wondering  about  is what  is different  between  the  two  cases?  What
are  the  processes  that  occur  in  terms  of  the  child’s  frame  of
reference  that  are  different  at 65 per cent  than  at 80 per cent?

A.  I don’t  think  I intended  for  the  interpretation  to  be  that
different.  | would  also  state  that  in a 65 per cent  system  the child
as  he becomes  more  aware  of the surrounding  environment  would
also  be  aware  he  was  in  a  city  school,  a  school  system  that  was
majority  black  as  compared  to  the  suburbs  that  were  majority
white  to an  even  greater  degree.

Q.  So he  would  then  even  at 65  per  cent  have some  sense  of

racial  [367]  isolation,  is that  correct?

A.  Not  within  the  context  of the  city  but  in relation  to  the

metropolitan  area.  He would  at both  levels  yes.

Q.  Doctor,  let me  pose  this hypothetical:  Suppose  you  have  a
student  in a  school  in Detroit  now  which  is 50 per cent  black  and
50 per  cent  white  and  that  student  is moved  to  a  school  which  is
between  65  and  70  per  cent  black,  what  will  that  do  to  that
particular  student’s  sense  of racial  isolation  or  racial identifiability,
in your judgment?

;

A.  Well,  I  think  the  important  determinant  in  any  inter-
pretation  of a person’s  situation  such  as school  children,  would  be
how  he is being  treated  in respect  to the other  children  and if, for

of what  can  or  can  not  be done

opinion?

A.  In Detroit?

Q.  Generally.

IV a97

A.  That  move  from  the  innercity  to  —

Q.  That just move.

A:  In the United  States?

Q.  Yes,  the  United  States.
A.  I think  the  book  “Future  Shock”  which  is  widely  read
these  days  states  the  figure  of  20  per  cent  mobility  of  the
American  population.  The  figure  I quoted  in the  Memphis  thing
‘you are  talking  about  —

Q.  That  was  20 per cent,  was  it not?

A.  Yes.

Q.  Would  you  tell  the  Court  what  re-entry  is?  I believe  you

used  the  term  “re-entry.”

A.  Out  of context  I don’t  remember  using it.

Q.  Let  me  put  it another  way.  Can  we  reasonably  expect  in

your  opinion  should  the  Court  approve  your  plan  that  after  a
passage  of time  that  there  would  be movement  of people  back  into

A.  I see  what  you  are  saying.
[390]  Q.  Yes.  «

A.  One  of  the phenomenon  that  is  exercised  here,  in  the
south  particularly,  is the  business  and  I guess  it is not  peculiar  to
the  south,  is  private  schools;  so  it’s  not  so  much  physical
movement  in  and  out  of  the  city  as  many  times  just  changing
schools  so  that  the  experience  in  many  places  in  the  south,  for
example,  would  indicate  that  following  a  fairly  extensive  de-
segregation  plan  there  is considerable  movement  to private  schools
which  are  set  up  for  the  most  part  by  church  groups  and  for
various  reasons  after  a  period  of beginning  the  following  year  and
then  continuing  there  is a  gradual  re-entry  of a  large  number  of
these  students.

Q.  Are  there  any  statistics  on  the  amount  of  re-entry  that

could  be expected?

A.  I think  there  are  some  but  I’m not  familiar  with  them.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  in  your  opinion,  your  proposed  plan  to  de-
segregate  the  Detroit  school  district  is a sound  educational  plan, is
that correct?

A.  Yes.

rounded  by  water,  much  like  Beaver  Island,  I don’t  know  if you

A.  No.

any  [391]  doubt  as to the soundness  of the  plan you  have  submit-
ted  to the Court  for the desegregation  of Detroit?

A.  I would  think  not.

identifiable  schools?

A.  Is this the reason  I think  it is educationally  sound?

would  provide  for equal treatment  of children,  would  it not?

A.  I think  so,  yes.  I perceive  it as  non-discriminatory  in that

regard.

Q.  In  your  opinion  this  would  improve  the  educational  op-

portunity  of the children  of Detroit?

A.  Yes.

CROSS  EXAMINATION  BY  MR.  RITCHIE:

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  at  the  beginning  of your  direct  examination  in
response  to  a  question  by Mr.  Beer,  you  said  that  compared  to the
plan  submitted  by  the  Detroit  Board  of  Education  this  plan
worked  and  it  was  my  understanding  from  your  statement  you
were  addressing  yourself  to  the  problem  of functionally  desegre-
gating  the  pupils  within  the  school  system  of the  City of Detroit.
Was  I correct  in making  such  an  assumption?

A.  I think  so.  I perceived  that  as  the  primary  task  of the  plan.

Q.  Mr.  Krasicky  also  asked  if this  was  a  sound  educational
plan  in his  direct  examination  and  you  said  yes.  I would  offer  this
suggestion  to  you  that  the  plan  you  proposed  to  the  Court  only
addresses  itself  to  the  numbers  but  with  regard  to  the  educational
plan  of Detroit  it really  makes  no  basic  changes  in the  number of
schools  or  the  curriculum  that  the  students  are  using  and  other
matters  related  to  the educational  plan  itself.  We  are  talking  about
desegregating  in  terms  of  numbers  in  this  plan  of  yours  not  in
terms  of the  educational  plan of Detroit  itself.

A.  Well,  that  is  true  except  the  desegregation  in  itself  will
[398]  lead  to  educational  change  and  certainly  I would  anticipate
change  in  the  educational  program  as  a  result  of  the  Plan’s
adoption.

Q.  Were  you  aware  tha-  two  weeks  ago  the  State  of Michigan
published  a  comparative  survey  of  the  test  course  of the  various
schools  in  the  State  of  Michigan  and  on  a  comparison  basis  the
City  of  Detroit  ran  I believe  in  the  lower  one  percent  of  the
schools  in  the  state?  If you  have  any  opinion  on  it would  your
plan  make  any  appreciable  change  in those  scores?  Do you  think  it
would?

A.  I would  hope  that  it would  over  a  period  of time.  If the

plan were  accompanied  by sound  implementation,  yes.

*

IV a 103

Q.  Is  it not  a  fact  that  there  are  educational  problems  in
Detroit  which  while  they  may  include  the  racial  problems  are  also
educational  problems  of  deep  and  long  standing  which  require  a
good deal  to correct?

A.  I think  so,  yes.

Q.  Mr.  Krasicky  asked  you  a  hypothetical  question  with
regard  to  I believe  the  constitutionality  of  the  plan,  making  the
assumption  that  Detroit  was  an  educational  island,  self -  contained
surrounded  by a  sea  and  you said  it  would be a  sound  plan. Now I
asked  you  to  look  at  it  realistically  as  an  educational  island  sur-
rounding  by a  sea  whose  suburban  community  is basically  white.
Without  addressing  yourself  to  the  problem  [399]  of correcting
the  obligations  of the  Detroit  school  system  but  going  up a level  in
our  responsibility  to  the  problems  of the  state  itself,  taking  your
plan  and  assuming  that  it is completely  effective  and  results  in a
school  district  as  you  have  proposed,  would  you  not  have  looking
at  your  plan  with  the  suburban  communities,  the  State  of
Michigan  maintaining  a  plenary  school  system,  namely  a  school
system  in Detroit  which  is identifiable  as  racially  black  surrounded
by others  which  are  identifiable  as  racially  white?

A.  I think  that  is a legal question.

Q.  Would  you  care  to  give  us  an  answer,  you  have  on  other

legal matters.  I hear  no  objection  from  counsel.

A.  Well,  I think  I have  inferred  already  that  Detroit,  taken  in
the  context  of the  metropolitan  area,  at least  technically  would  be
racially  identifiable.

Q.  Now,  with  regard  to  the  proposition  of the  stability  of the
plan  itself,  you  hoped,  you  said,  that  this  plan  would  provide
stability.  I think  we  all  recognize  in  the  courtroom  that  to  effect
an  integration  problem  the  element  of compulsion  is required  in
this plan and  it assumes  such  a proposition.

Is it not  correct  and  I think  you  may  have  stated  so  that  the
white  flight  occurs  when  middle  class  whites  attempt  to avoid  the
integration  experience  in  the  neighborhood  schools  they  live  near
and  they  leave.  In  your  [400]  direct  examination  and  sub-
sequently  you  stated  that  there  was  the  factor  of economics,  there
was  the  factor  of economics  that  was  a  restraint.  I submit  to you

IV a 104

=

not  only  is economics  a  factor  but  the  availability  of schools  and
other  neighborhoods  to  go  to  is also  a  factor  in white  flight,  is it
not?

A.  Yes.

Q.  So  that  in the  event  there  is a  factor  of instability  in this
plan  the  people  most  likely  to  leave  would  be  the  middle  class
families  and  the  most  prosperous  families  would  remain  in  the
Detroit  community.

A.  Well,  I don’t  think  that  is completely  so  by any  manner  or
means  because  some  people  have  considerable  income.  In any  city
people  have  a stake  in where they  are and will not flee  in this sort
of situation  but  other  factors  being equal  I would  agree  with  your
proposition.

MR.  RITCHIE:  Thank  you.  I would  have  not  further  ques-

tions,  your  Honor.

Q.  Am I correct  that was pursuant  to an  Alfred  North  White-

head  Postdoctoral  Study?

A.  Yes.

class  and  race  and  completed  a  book  entitled  “Schools  and

Q.  You  mentioned  a  book  called  “Schools  and  Inequality.”

Institute  of Technology  Press,  1971?

A.  That  is correct.
Q.  Have  you  published  any  other  books?
A.  I have  edited  another  book  and  I have  authored  several

articles.

EER aa

IV a 106

Q.  The  book  called  “Schools  and  Inequality,”  can  you  tell us

what  the general  subject  matter  of that  was?

A.  The  thesis  of the  volume  is that  the  quality  of the  school
[446]  services  distributed  in  this  nation  is a function  of the  social
class  and that  could  be interpreted  as race also in some sections.  It
is a  function  of the social  class of the child and the quality  of the
school  service  the  child receives  has a bearing  on  his school  perfor-
mance  and  his performance  in schools  subsequent  has a bearing on
his post-school  opportunity.

Q.  Doctor  Guthrie,  am  I correct  that  you  have  taught  at the

Q.  Yes,  very  briefly.  Now,  let  me  ask  you  this.  It part  of it

A.  In my  mind  that  is part  of the explanation,  a large part.

Q.  In  terms  of  black  and  white  would  it be  fair  to  say  that

you  have  found  low  SES  among  whites?

[455] A. Without  any  question.  Most  of  the  poor  people  in

this nation  are  still  white  in absolute  terms.

Q.  Have  you  found  high SES  among  black  students?

A.  Yes, that  is correct.  There  are  some  of those.

Q.  Would  that  be the  middle  class  of black?

A.  Middle  or  above  in some  instances,  rare  instances.

Q.  Would  it be fair to say  that  a number  of black  children  are

poor and  have  low  SES  for that  reason?

A.  The  large  majority  of  them  are  poor  and  would  be

characterized  as  being low  SES.

Q.  Doctor,  I would  like to explore  another  term  I have  heard.
I have  heard  the  term  “racial  isolation.”  Have  you  heard  that  term
before?

A.  Yes,  I have.

—

IVa  108

Q.  Have  you  heard  that  term  in regard  to  education  among

our  public  school  children?

A.  Yes,  I have.

Q.  When  I  speak  here  on  education  on  this  record  I am
talking  about  primary  and  secondary  grades.  Could  you  explain
what  that  term  means?

A.  Racial  isolation  refers  to  the  degree  to  which  a  child,
regardless  of his  own  race,  goes  to  school  only  with  members  of
his own  race.
QQ.  Can you  give us some  examples?

[456] A. An  all-white  school  or  all-black  school,  either  of
which  could  be  characterized  as  being  a  situation  where  there is
racial  isolation.

Q.  Suppose,  bei: we  have  a  school  that  80  percent  white
and  20 percent  black  are  contained  in it. Would  you  consider  that
a  racially  isolated  situation?

A.  No,  I  would  not.  Those  percentages  approximate  the

proportion  of black  and  white  people  in this  nation.

Q.  Is it for that  reason  you  state  it is not  racially  isolated?

A.  That  is correct.

Q.  Suppose  a  black  child  going  to  a  school  that  has  90  per-

cent  black  —  would  you  consider  that  racially-  isolated?

A.  I would  not  consider  it racially-isolated.  I would  begin  to
consider  it  racially-identifiable  perhaps  —  excuse  me  —  it was  90
percent  black?

Q.  Yes.

A.  I’m sorry.

Q.  What  did you think  I said?
A.  I was  thinking  90  percent  white.  Well,  that  begins  to  be
racially  isolated  in  my  eyes.  A  black  child  who  sees  hinself in a
school  which  is 90 percent  black  must  have  some  understanding  of
the  fact  that  that  is out  of proportion  to his numberin  this society.
He  must  begin  to  get  a  feeling  there  is something  different  about
that  school  and  about  this society  [457]  at large.

IV a 109

Q.  Let  me  ask  you  would  he  get  that  feeling  if it  was  75

percent  black?

A.  My suspicion  is that  he also  would  still  have  that  feeling.  It
is complicated  and  I don’t  know  where  to draw  the  line  exactly
between  90 percent  black  and  75 percent  black,  but still  when  one
considers  the  fact  that  some  11  or  12 or  13 percent of all  of the
people  in the  nation  are  black  if that child  should  see  himself in a
school  which  is  75  percent  black,  acknowledging  there  are  some
white  students  there,  but 75 percent  black,  he must  begin  to think
why  is it my  school  is that  way?  What  has  society  done  that  has
placed  me  in this  situation?  What  is there  about  me  that  I should
be  with  so  many  other  black  children  in  such  a  low  proportion
relatively  of white  children?

Q.  What  about  65 percent,  would  you  have  the  same  feeling?
A.  I would  have  the  feeling.  Anytime  one  begins  to  move
beyond  the  proportion  of blacks  in society,  beyond  that  the black
child  must  begin  to  wonder  what  kind  of factors  are  in operation
which  are  aggregating  him  there  in  a sense  in a disproportionate
way.

Q.  And  have  you  ever  heard  the  term  “tipping  point”?
A.  Yes.

Q.  What  does  that  term  mean  to you?
[458] A. It is asserted  to be that percentage  point after which
the  —  percentage  point  between  the  proportion  of black  and  white
after  which  when  the  number  of blacks  exceed  that  point  whites
begin  to leave.

Q.  Where  do you  think  that  point  is?
A.  I would  be  most  reluctant  to  be specific  about  it. I would

be willing  to place  it in a band.
Q.  Give  us  the  band.
A.  Somewhere  between  35 and  50 or  55 percent  black.  When
a school  is more  than  that,  then  somewhere  in that  band  whites
become  fearful  for  some  rational  and  some  irrational  reasons  and
_  When  can  they leave.

Q.  Doctor,  you  were  discussing  the  black  child  in  a  65
percent  black  school  and  a 75 percent  black  school  perceiving  that
his school  is not  in keeping  with  the  population  in society?

IVali0

a

A.  That’s  correct.
Q.  Now,  where  would  he get the  conception  that  society  was

placing  him  in isolation?

:

A.  In order  for him  to get that  conception  he has to have  two
opposing  pieces  of information  constantly  or  sometimes  crossing
his  mind.  One  set  of  information  is that  he  goes  to  school  with
children  who  are  primarily  black  or  lives  in a neighborhood  which
is primarily  black,  yet  his  picture  of the  world  which  is presented
to  him  by the  newspapers,  by television,  by  [459]  advertising,  is
that  it  is primarily  a  white  world.  Yet  he  sees  himself  living  in
almost  totally  or  a proportionately  black  world.

He:  has  two  conflicting  pieces  of  information  crossing  his
mind.  Also,  if  he has  the  ability  to travel  at all, he will see outside
of  his  neighborhood  that  it is  a  white  world,  particularly  if he
should  get  out  to  the  suburbs  he  will  see  it is  a non-black  world
out  there.

Q.  Are  you  familiar  with  the  television  program  called

“Room  222”?

A.  I  have  watched  it  and  I  have  watched  my  children

watching  it.

Q.  Does  that  program  depict  an  American  high school  do you

recall?

A.  It  attempts  to  depict  an  American  high  school,  a  typical

American  high school.

A.  Well,  I do  for  a  strange  reason.  Apparently in the  mind’s
eye  of the producer  of that  show  the typical  American  high school
has  a  minority  proportion  of  black  students  in it, so  [460]  about

That  came  to  my  attention  because  my  own  children,  who  go  to

.  40 percent  white  and 40 percent  black.

Q.  Dr.  Guthrie,  have  you  had  an  opportunity  to examine  the

plan presented  by the Plaintiffs  to the Court?

A.  Yes,  I have.
Q.  I show  you  what  has  been  marked  and  received  ‘as PC-2

presented  by the Plaintiffs.  Is that  the plan you  have  seen?

[461]  A.  This is the plan I have seen.
Q.  Dr.  Guthrie,  is it correct  my office  sent  you  a copy  of that

plan about  a week  or  10 days ago?

A.  That  is correct.  I received  it approximately  a week  ago.
Q.  You have  examined  that  plan?
A.  That  is correct,  I have  read  it.
Q.  Today  is it  correct  that  my  office  advised  you  of  some

changes  that  were  made  in the plan?

A.  That  is right.  I understand  that  the  Plaintiffs’  have. sug-

gested  some  changes or  made  changes  in it.

.

Q.  Dr.  Guthrie,  would  you  recommend  to  this  honorable

Court  that  it should  adopt  this plan for the City of Detroit?

IVali2

A.  I would  not.

Q.  Would  you  tell the Court  why?

A.  I believe  that  there  are  substantially  better  educational
alternatives  available  to  the  Court.  I believe  that  this plan,  should
it be  implemented,  has  the  possibility  of educational  disadvantage
for  the  children  in Detroit.  That  is, it involves  risk  for  them.  The
combination  of  those  two  events,  two  conditions,  lead  me  to
believe  that this plan is not a worthy one.

Q.  Could  you  be specific  and  tell me  what  in your judgement

is objectionable  to the plan?

A.  The  objectionable  parts  are  1, the  instability  upon  which

it{ 462]  is based  in my view.

Q.  Would  you  explain  that  for the Court,  please?

A.  Yes.  If the  effort  is to  eliminate  a segregated  school  sys-
tem  for  whatever  reason,  if that  is the  intent  then  it is my  belief
that  this  will  not  eliminate  a segregated  school  system,  but rather
will lead  only  to greater  segregation  in the City of Detroit.

Q.  Would  you  explain  what  you  mean  by that, please?

A.  Yes.  Certainly  starting  already,  perhaps  starting  already
with  the  greater  assuredness  should  such  a desegregation  plan  be
announced,  and  every  year  thereafter  when  it is implemented,  my
prediction  would  be  that  the  number  of white  persons  residing  in
the  City  of Detroit  and  the  number  of white  children  available  to
attend  its  schools  would  decrease.  I based  that  prediction  on  the
behavior  of whites  in other  desegregated  situations,  desegregating
situations,  primarily  in  the  South  but  also  in  the  North  of  this
nation.

that their children  will be
When  faced  with  the  possibility
attending  schools  which  have  a  large  proportion  of  blacks  and
whites,  frequently,  very  frequently,  they  will  leave.  Now,  in part
that  is a  consequence  of racial  intolerance,  racial  prejudice  in sub-
stantial  part.  There  are also some  real  fears,  that is realistic  fears,
of  whites  in  that  situation.  For  example,  it  has  been  a  long
tradition[463]  in this nation  to allocate  a lower  proportion  of our
school  resources  to  schools  which  are  predominantly  black  and
whites  fear  that if  the school  becomes  characterized  as being black
the quality  of school  service  will depreciate  in it.

IVa  1l3

I say  that  is a realistic fear because  frequently  it  has hap-
pened.  Those  individuals  who  are  in a position  to  make  decisions
about  such  things  frequently  when  a  school  is  in  some  heavy
proportion  black  will  discriminate  in  the  allocation  of  the
resources  to it.

Q.  Dr.  Guthrie,  would  a  65  percent  school,  a school having
65 percent  black  students,  be considered  a black  school  as you  are
considering  the  term?

A.  Yes.  I believe  so.
Q.  Would  a  system  having  65  percent  black  in  it  be

considered  a black  system?

A.  I believe  that  the  white  parents  living in it would  have  the
same  fears  when  it was  65  percent  black  as  if it were  90 percent.
For them  it is becoming  a  black  system.  Again  I acknowledge  the
ratio of prejudice  involved.

I also  want  to  emphasize  the  point  that  knowing  that  it  is
becoming  more  than  50  percent  black  or  it is becoming  a heavy
percent  black,  white  parents  fear  “Where  will  the  white  teachers
go? Where  will  the  money  come  from  to make  it run  now  that we
are being discriminated  against?”

[464]  Making  these  calculations  in  their  own  mind,  they
leave  for  a setting  where  they think  the quality  of schools  is better
insured,  namely  the suburbs.

So  an  objection  I have  to  this  plan  is that  if implemented  at
best,  at  best  —  and  I fear  this  wouldn’t  even  occur  —  but  at best  it
would  be a short-run  solution,  at very  best.  At worst  —  and  I think
more  likely  you  would  have  a  segregated,a  more  strongly  segre-
gated situation  after one,  two,  three  or  four  years.

Q.  When  would  this flight  begin,  in your opinion?
A.  As  I tried  to  make  clear,  I suspect  that  simply  given  the
publicity  that  accompanies  a court  case  such  as this that  the white
flight may  have  been  intensified  over  that  which  it was  before  the
court  case.

Should  a  Detroit  based  desegregation  decision  be  given  it is
my  prediction  that  that  in itself  would  intensify  the  white  flight
and  then  upon  implementation  of  the  plan  it  perhaps  would
continue.

=

1Vall4

Q.  In  the  white  flight  you  are  speaking  of,  in  terms  of SES,
which  you  spoke  of before,  what  group  of white  parents  or  white
families  would  you  expect  to flee?

A.  It  is the  higher  social  and  economic  status  families  who
flee  first  for  several  reasons.  Most  frequently  they  have  —  they are
the  ones  that  have  the  economic  resources  which  enable
them[465]  to  flee.  Secondly,  these  are  frequently  the people  who
have  the most  concern  for the quality  of their  children’s  schooling,
and  they  flee.  The poorer parents  —  incidently,  the  ones  who  social
science  findings  have  revealed  to  be  the  most  prejudiced  and
intolerant  typically  —  poorer  families  don’t  have  the  means  as  fre-
quently  to  flee  even  when  they  want  to,  and  so  they are  the ones
who  are  left in the  city, the  whites  left  in the  city.

Q.  So  then  based  upon  what  you  told  the  Court,  the  whites
who  are  left  in the  city  would  produce  the  ingredients  of a  poor
SES mix  for the  black  students  and  the  white  students  left?

A.  That  is  correct.  Not  only  would  you  not  be  able  to  have
the  social  and  economic  advantages  of racial  desegregation  in that
instance,  you  are  also  stirring  a  pot  which  has  substantially  more
volatility  to  it.  You  are  putting  students  together  for whom  racial
intolerance  is typically  the  highest  and  so  you  are  running  the risk
at  least  in the  initial  years  of such  desegregation  of increased  stu-
dent  violence  in such  schools,  increased  over  that  which  it would
be  if  you  could  desegregate  across  social  economic  status
boundaries.

Q.  What  effect  would  it have  in putting  a  number  of low or
medium  low  white  SES  students  into  a  school  where  the  black
students  range  from  middle  SES  on  top  the  scale?  What  effect
would  it have  on  the achievement  of the  white  students?

[466]  A.  On the white  students?

Q.  Yes.

A.  My  prediction  would  be  that  probably  their  school

achievement  would  be elevated.

Q.  What  about  black  children?
A.  There  is so  little  evidence  on  this  is just raw  speculation  on
on  my  part  now  because  that  kind  of  desegregation  is so  infre
quent  as  almost  never  to  have  been  studied.  But  if  the  same

1Vall5

principles  apply  as  apply  when  the  proportions  are  reversed,  then
the  achievement  of the  black  students  in that  situation  should  not
suffer.  It should  maintain  the same  level.

Q.  What  would  be  the effect  of putting  in low white  SES

students  with  low  black  SES  students,  achievement  students?

A.  I would  expect  achievement  not  to  change  and  possibly
given  a  greater  possibility  of friction  and  tension  between  those
students,  achievement  might  suffer.

Q.  Would  it be your  position,  Dr.  Guthrie,  that in any deseg-

regation  one  must  consider  a mixture  of SES?

A.  In  any  desegregation  decision  which  is concerned  about
performance  of children  and  what  they learn  and what  schools  do
for  them I think  it is imperative  to  consider  social  and  economic
circumstances  of the students  involved.  Not  to do so  is to make  an
assumption  that  there  is some  magic  about  a  white  person  sitting
next  to  a  black  person.  It  is  to  make  an[467]  assumption  that
black people can learn simply  by being in close proximity  to
whites  by  some  magical  osmotic  process  for  which  there  is no
evidence.  Whereas,  if you consider  that there can be a transfer  of
values across  SES boundaries  the revitalization  of a school  environ-
ment  across  SES  boundaries,  that  does  give  us  some  explanatory
modal  for what  happens.

It enables  us  to  explain  why  it is a  low  SES  black  child  can
learn  more  in  an  integrated  environment  where  he  is placed  near
high SES whites.

Q.  For that  matter,  near  high SES  blacks?
A.  That’s  right.  I agree  to  that,  but  there  may  be some  other
advantages  that  are  not  gained  in that  situation.  That  is that  one
doesn’t  necessarily  learn  greater  racial  tolerance  in  the  situation
you  have just described.

the Plaintiffs’  plan. Assuming

A.  I  would  judge,  I feel  safe  in judging  that  a  typical

perception[468]
Q.  If  I  mention  Washington,  D.C.,  to  you,  educationally,

A.  That is a black system.

Q.  You  mentioned  stability  as one of the objections  you had

A.  I do have  the objection  as we have  discussed  peripherally
that  it  doesn’t  maximize  the educational  advantages  of the SES
integration.  On the contrary,  it runs the risk of the worst kind  of
SES integration,  that is, low SES whites  with low SES blacks.

In  a  situation  as  I said  before  like  that  it  would  be
extraordinarily  volatile,  dangerous  physically  for both  black  and
white  children  and as a  consequence  of the tensions  created  in
such  situation  can  result  in a  lowering  or decrease  in  the
of students.
achievement

Q.  What  effect  would  it  have,  in  your  opinion,  on

achievement?

A.  The  Plaintiffs’  plan,  what  effect  would  it  have  on

achievement?

Q.  Yes.
[469]  A.  It  is  difficult,  it  has  no  possibility,  very  low

students,  and runs the risk of decreasing  the achievement  of both.
Q.  Do you  find  any  other  objections  to the plan?  You

mentioned  stability,  education  and achievement.

A.  A third  objection  I have,  or another  objection,  is purely  a
practical  one.  Desegregation  is what  a  sociologist  calls  a
systemwide  change.  You cannot  simply  desegregate  and expect
nothing  else  to change  in that  system.  The  way in which  you
employ  people,  the way in which you allocate  resources,  the way

IVall7

in which  you  make  decisions,  the way  in which  the board
members  and  superintendent  interact,  everything  else  in the
district  is touched  when  you  desegregate  it.

There  is the high  probability  of tremendous  turmoil.  Just
when  one  considers  the logistics  of it, when  one considers  the
administrative  decisions
the human  time
that  had to take  place,
and effort  and money  involved  in a  desegregation  decision  of this
magnitude,  to  do  it once,  to  desegregate  in  the  City  of  Detroit
counts  for  a  tremendous  expenditure  of  effort  and  commotion.
Having  done  that,  if the  whites  flee,  which  is predictable,  then  you
have  a  segregated  or  more  segregated  system,  and  then  you  say,
“Well,  in  order  to  desegregate  we  must  integrate  with  the
surrounding  metropolitan  community.”  To do that  again and  call
forth  all  [470]  that  administrative  effort  again  seems  to me  to be
wasteful  at  best  and  perhaps  ridiculous  at  the  extreme,  and
unnecessary.

that  the  Plaintiffs’  plan  which  gives  basically  a  65-35  ratio
would these
proportion  and  in  some  cases as high as 75  percent,
make  these  schools  racially  identifiable,  in your  opinion?

A.  Yes, it would.

A.  Yes, I do.

A.  I believe  that  it will  have  the  greatest  stability  over  time.

desegregation
over time.  It will last longer  for one. Secondly,  and

Q.  Let  me  ask  you  this:  I believe  you  stated  to  the  Court  a
very  few  minutes  ago  that  you  found  objection  to  the  Plaintiffs’
plan  because  of  the  administrative  problems.  I believe  that  was
your  testimony  and  you  explained  it.  Let  me  ask  you  this:
Suppose  the  Court  was  to order  Plaintiffs’  plan  to be implemented
next  fall,  for  example,  and  then  the  subsequent  fall  ordered  a
metropolitan  plan.  Would  you  favor  such  an  approach?

A.  No, I would  not.

Q.  Would  you  tell  the Court  why,  please?

A.  Well,  in  addition  to  the  repetitive  administrative  costs  and
turmoil  which  I already  discussed  and  which  I would  find  undesir-
able,  in  addition  to  that  you  got  the  cost  to  the  [472]  children
themselves  and  their  parents.  Any  time  you  uproot  children  and
move  them  out  of  the  norm,  out  of  an  expected  pattern  of
schooling,  you  are  disturbing  all  kinds  of  relationships  between
parents  and  school,  between  students  and  schools  and  between
students  and  students.  A child  who  moves  into  a  school  makes  a
new  group  of friends,  begins  to  find  the  stability  that  they  afford
him  and  the comfort that that gives
him such that  he  or she can
focus  on  his or  her schoolwork  and  then  the  subsequent  year  in an
unexpected  pattern  —  one  where  the  child  is uprooted  and  placed
in another  new  social  environment  —  the  child  must  get used to it
and  the  parent  has  to  get used  to it, and  whatever  relationship  has
been  built  up  between  parent  and  school  must  now  be  built  up
again.  So  in my  mind  it is excessive  dislocation  that  is necessary  in
a two-step  desegregation  procedure  such  as you just illustrated.

Q.  Educationally  what  effect  might  that  have  on  the  child if

we  did  it by two  steps?

IVali9

A.  Well,  in  the  first  year  of  it  within  the  boundaries  of
Detroit  as  I have  already  discussed  it  is  not  likely  that  in  those
circumstances  you  will  get  the  optimum  SES  desegregation
necessary  for learning.

So  in  the  first  year  of such  a  two-step  operation  it is possible
‘that  achievement  will  suffer.  It  is  [473]  possible.  It  is  highly
probable  that  the  amount  of  conflict  will  be  intensified  if one
would  move  immediately  to a metropolitan  solution.

Q.  Explain  to  the Court  what  you  mean.
A.  By conflict,  I mean  both students and students,  again banking
on  my  knowledge  of  the  levels  of  prejudice  typically  embedded
in both  low  SES  blacks  and  whites  that  are  going  to run  a greater
risk  of  conflict  when  placing  them  in  contact  with  one  another
than when  you  integrate  over  SES  boundaries.

Q.  Do  you  see  any  other  disadvantage  to  the  children  in  a

two-step  process?

A.  No,  not  that  I don’t  think  we  have  touched  upon  in  one

sense  or  another  already.

Q.  Would  it  be  your  opinion,  based  upon  your  testimony,
that  just  placing  students  number  by number  has any  educational
advantage?

A.  None  that  has ever  been  demonstrated.

Q.  Is  this  your  opinion  based  upon  your  testimony  that  in
fact  placing  students  number  by  number  may  have  educational
disadvantages?

A.  Within  the  context  of Detroit  it would  appear  to  have  or
‘would  appear  to run  the grave  risk  of educational  disadvantage. -

A.  No.

he would  in effect  be more
situation  into  a  majority  situation,
than  reassured  by the  knowledge  that  everybody  else  found

A.  I find  that  very strange  logic in at  least two ways. One, it
assumes  that  the child  has knowledge  that  everyone  else is in the
same situation  and to  me that is debatable  that  children  do carry

IVa  121

that  knowledge  with  them.  But,  secondly,  I think  that  I could
pursue  that  line  of reasoning  to  say  [476]  that  if the  black  child
would  move  from  a  point  where  it was  40  percent  black  and  he
was moved  to a place  where  it  was 100 percent  black he  would
find  comfort  in  saying,  “Well,  everyone  is the  same  as  I am.” I
don’t believe  that  either.

He  would  find  himself  in a racially-identifiable  school.  It’s  a
black  school.  He  must  be  torn  between  the  tension  of knowing
that that is a school  which is a  majority  black and society  or the
surrounding  area  isn’t.  What  is he  to  say  to  himself,  “It’s all right
that  they grouped  me  this  way.  It’s all right  because  they grouped
other  people  that  way.”  I personally  don’t  find  that  persuasive,
nor  logical.

Q.  How  about  moving  a  black  child  from  a  90 percent  black
school  to  a 75 percent  black  school?  Would  he find  comfort  in the
fact -  would  your  answer  be the same?

A.  My  answer  would  substantially  be  the  same.  If he  should
find  comfort  in  that  he  would  have  to  have  an  extraordinarily
sensitively  calibrated  comfort  meter  inside  of him.

A.  The  tipping  point  concept  is a  band  that  slips  back  and
forth.  To  me  it  is important  to  consider  in  terms  of a  desire  to
achieve  a  stable  solution,  whatever  it  is.  That  is  why  I would
consider  the tipping  point  concept.

Q.  Yes,  but  the question  is am  I correct  in understanding  that
that  affects  your  thinking  of how  one  goes  about  desegregation?
A.  No.  If  I understand  you,  not  only  would  I have  to  take
into  account  how  one  goes  about  desegregating,  but  I would  want
to know  what  one  got at the end.

Q.  I understand,  but  if the  tipping  point  factor  could  not  be
controlled  as  it can  be  if I understood  your  earlier  testimony  in a
metropolitan  situation  as  that  obtaining  in  Detroit,  if  for  any
reason  the  tipping  point  factor  could  not  be controlled  would  you
maintain  racially  segregated  schools?

A.  I would  not  maintain  affirmative  action,  that  is,  I would
not  retain  racially  segregated  schools  by  affirmative  action.
[494]  Neither  would  I move  to desegregate  them  unless  I thought
I could  accomplish  socio-economic  integration  at  the  same  time.
Q.  Doctor,  would  you  agree  with  the following  statement:
“Although  the  question  of  housing  is beyond  the  scope  of
the  Commission’s  charge,  we  are  well  aware  that  the
separation  of racial  and  ethnic  groups  in cities  and  suburbs  is
attributable  to  many  complex  factors,  including  housing
policies  and  practices  by private  as  well  as public  agencies.  In
order  for  our  society  to  achieve  true  integration  many
institutions  in addition  to schools  will  have  to be involved.

desegregation  and  integration  of our  society.”

ought  to  be put  in a  metropolitan  context  and  others  need  not  be

worst,  but  I  do  not  think  that  under  the  hypothetical

A.  I’m in favor  of a metropolitan  desegregation  solution.

attendance  pattern  between  Detroit  and  its  surrounding  suburbs,

Socio-economic  mix  which  would  place  middle  and  upper
socio-economic  status  children  in the majority.

Q.  Dr.  Guthrie,  you  gave  some  reasons  why  the Court  should
not  adopt  this  plan  as  an  interim  plan  if  it  may  adopt  a
metropolitan  plan.  One  of  the  reasons  you  gave  was  the
tremendous  turmoil  administratively—

A.  If I may  borrow a term,  sir, excessive  turmoil.

Q.  I wrote  down  tremendous  turmoil,  trying  to  write  what
you  said.  If  the  Court  were  to  adopt  a  metropolitan  plan  there
[516]  would  also  be turmoil,  would  there  not?

excessive  turmoil.  There  will  be  “turmoil”  in  any  desegregation

Q.  Whether  it  is  done  on  intra-district  or  a  metropolitan

basis?

A.  That  is my  belief.

Q.  Let’s  assume  the  Court  adopts  some  kind  of  a  metro-
politan  plan,  in  your  opinion,  would  there  be  flight  from  the
metropolitan  area?

A.  Probably  less.  Where  can  one  go.  There  are  a  limited
number  of  prople  who  have  national  mobility  at  their  fingertips.

than  that  there  would  be less flight,  is this  what  you  are  saying?

2. Dr. ‘Guthrie, in the Detrpit system there are some 25 or 30
[S18], schools with a  ratio  proportion  of between  25  and  50  per
cent  black. Do you bélieve  that ‘chahging  the  racial  situation or
balance in the  Setiool to a Balance where'theré would be 70  pe
cent black’ woulld have''a ‘negative ot _ Positive ‘effect ‘on ‘these
schoots?  ©

=

 _-£S

1Va  131

A.  If in moving  from  25  to  50 per cent  black  to 70 per cent
black,  if  one altered  the SES nature  of the school,  that is, if one
moved  to  a  situation  where  there  were  a  larger  concentration  of
higher  SES  blacks  it  might  actually,  it  could  improve  the
achievement  of the  black  children.

However,  I think  the  probability  is high that in increasing the
proportion  of blacks  in the school  you would also increase  the
proportion
of children  from  low  income  families.
If that were
done  my belief  is that at  best the achievement  of  the performance
of those  students  would  remain  stable.  At worst  it could  go down.
There is a risk that it  would decrease.

Q.  Thank  you.  On  the  subject  of  the  allocation  of
educational  resources  to  a child,  it  was suggested  last night and  I
don’t  know if it has ever been suggested  in this court before  but it
may,  assuming  we  have  formal  hearings  on  a  metropolitan  plan,
that the  solution  to  I think what we all agree are the admitted
achievement. problems  in  the  Detroit  Public  School  System  could
be solved  not  by the  imposition  of a so-called  [519]  court-forced
busing  plan  on  the school  system but in  the alternative by the sole
delivery  of academic  resources  through  increased  Federal  funding
alone; do you,  based  on  your  knowledge as an educator  and expert
and  your  knowledge  of Detroit  believe  that  the mere  allocation  of
substantial  funds  of  money  for  whatever  academic  purpose  the
school  board would see fit to use, would  that  solve  the problems
of Detroit?

Let  me  give  you  this  as  further  assistance  in answering  that
question:  There  are  287,000  students  in  Detroit  in  the  first  class
school district  of which  you  know  65 per cent are black and of
which  I understand  and certainly  other  counsel  here can correct
me if I'm  wrong in my figure  of  some 68,000  based on state tests,
perform  on  the  very lowest level.  I think the figure  was given to
me on  a  performance  level  of  between  80 and 85,  some  68,000
students  approximately  in Detroit;  of the 287,000
who performed
on that level we have  schools  I  know  in my  own region  -  I’m a
board member
in Region 7, where 65  of the students  in the school
have a performance  on  that  level
of 80 to 85, where
the state  and
national  norms
are upward  of slightly  over  100.

Would  the mere delivery  of services  alone solve the problem?
A.  I think  the  probability  that  it would  is small.

THE  COURT:  I just  have  a  matter  of curiosity  and  perhaps
you  can  satisfy  it.  Have  you  seen  or have  you had an opportunity
to  become  familiar  with or perhaps  you had some  part in the
preparation  of follow-up  studies  and analyses  made  based  on  data
collected  by  or  supplied  to  the  Coleman  Commission  which
recently  was  publicized?

A.  Yes,  I believe  you  refer  to  the  book  edited  by Mostheller

and  Daniel  Moynihan.

THx  COURT:  A Random  House  publication?
A.  Yes.
THE COURT:  Is that  available  now?
A.  Yes.
COURT:  How bi has it been  available?
A.  To reviewers  for approximately  two  weeks.
THE  COURT:  Have you  read  it?
A.  Yes, I have.
‘THE  COURT:  In your  estimation  does  that in  any  way  shake
the  general  conclusions  reached  by  the  so-called  Coleman
Commission?

A.  I believe  that  it shakes one  conclusion  greatly  and  I believe —

that  it  does not reaffirm  another  one at all even  though  it claims
to.  Let  me  be specific.  The  first  one  that  I will  comment  upon  is
perhaps at least  relevant  to the Court,  but  [521]  it may  be relevant
so  I will repeat  it.

One  of  the Coleman  report  findings  or assertions  is that the
quality  of  school  services  measured  typically  in  the  amount  of
money  spent per child does not influence  the performance  of the
child  independent  of the social  class.  The  criticisms  of  that
findings  in the Coleman  report  I believe  still hold even after these®
reanalyses,  despite  what  those  authors  say,  Mostheller  and
Moynihan.  I do not believe  that finding  has been  reaffirmed  and
though  if it  becomes  important  I would  try  to  explain  why in
detail.

What  I think  the relevant  finding  is that  has been  exploded  by
the  Coleman  report  held  that  the  racial

their  reanalysis,

composition  of a child's  classroom  had  a strong effect  on  a child’s
achievement  and  they  published  tables  to  try  and  demonstrate

reading and  arithmetic,  does  it not?

Q.  Phat  does  relate  to  achievement  test  scores  on  verbal
A.  That’s correct.
Q.  That  reinterpretation  or  new  finding  would  not  affect

Q.  Not  only  that  there  are  other  reasons  as  I believe  the

test scores.

IV a 134

A.  That’s  correct.

Q.  Nor  is  there  any  dispute  with  your  earlier  analysis  as  to
the  correlation  in fact  between  low  SES  and  race  in  this  country
on  account  of prior discrimination?

A.  That’s  correct.

Q.  Are  you  familiar  in  that  volume  with  a  chapter  by

Christopher  Jenks?

A.  I  am  moderately  familiar  with  it,  particularly  the
reanalysis  about  dollars  in schools  as  you  may  know  where I sort
of played  more  than  other  areas.  I do  know  that  part pretty  well.

Q.  Does  your  familiarity  at  the  moment  permit  you  to agree
with  me  to  the  effect  that  the  general,  returning
to the
implications  of Mr.  Ritchie’s  question,  the  approach  of additional
dollars  without  more would seem  on the basis  of the reanalysis as
well  as  the  other  data  to  which  you  referred,  to  be  inadequate in
terms  of the problem?

A.  My response  to  Mr.  Ritchie’s  question  was  not  based  very
much  on  the Coleman  report,  rather  it was based  on  my  work  with
Senator  Mondale’s  select  committee  on  equal  education  [524]
opportunity  where  I have  come  to see  almost  every  effort  we  have
made  at  putting  additional  dollars  on  the  head  of poor  children
has  somehow  never  occurred  because  we  never  have  gotten  the
additional  dollars  there.  As Senator  Mondale  says,  “Every  time  we
try  to  help  poor  children  in  this  nation  someone  robs  the  train  on
the  way.”

That  seems  to  be  what  happens  when  you  look  at  actual
delivery  of  Title  1 dollars  to  poor  children,  it  often  doesn’t  get
there  and  a  volume  which  has  been  mentioned  here,  Schools  and
Inequality,  for  the  State  of Michigan  myself and  colleagues  found
a  negative  relationship  between  the child’s  income  and  the amount
of federal  money  being  spent  on  him.  Well,  it wasn’t  a negative  it
was  a  positive  relationship,  poor  children  were  not  having  money
spent  on  them  in Michigan  the way  it was  alleged  to be the case.

Q.  If  the  dollars  got  there  but  nothing  else  was  changed,
including  social  SES  composition  and  racial  composition,  would
you  be optimistic  about  the dollars  spent?

A.  No,  I would  not  be  optimistic  even  if the  dollars  were

changed  by thousands.

THE  COURT:  I have  a couple  more  questions  but  I am  fearful
of asking  them  because  my  experience  usually  is it’s followed  by a
barrage  of counsel  questions.

A.  Perhaps  we  could  take  a walk.

THE  COURT:  Perhaps  I could  accompany  you  to the airport.

MR.  ROUMELL:  You  notice  I have  kept  quiet,  your  Honor.

Go right ahead.

THE  COURT:  I may not see you again and there are a  couple
matters  which  have  troubled  me  throughout  this  case  and  it  is
troubling  me  now  and  because  of my  regard  for your  expertise  and
your  experience  and  background  I would  like  to  have  the benefit
of your thoughts  even  if  perhaps  they  are out of  order.

Have  you had occasion  recently  to  read, I believe  an article  by
James  S. Coleman  which  in effect  says  that  our  educational  system
as Organized  and  conducted  in this country  today  is something  like
30 or 40 years  behind  the times?

A.  That  is it was  designed  for an  information-poor  era.

‘THE  COURT:  Yes,  and  an  action-rich  era  which  is quite  the

reverse  of what  we  have  now.  Do you  agree  with  [526]  that?

A.  I find  it a  brilliant  thesis and I agree with  it  for the  middle
and high SES  child.  For  poor  children  it is still, relatively  speaking,
it  is  still  not  an  information-rich  world  relative  to  their  more
advantaged  peers.  So  for  one  to  say,  and  here  I will  take  some
liberties  with  this  statement,  for  him  to say  the schools  should  no
longer  worry  about  teaching  information  and  cognitive
development  and  just  worry  about  attitudes  and  things  might  be
very  fine  if all  of the  children  were  advantaged,  but  I think  to do
so  at  this  time  and  point  in history  when  there  are  still  plenty  of
poor  children  who  don’t  have  access  to  information  the  way  the

ee

es

IV a 136

middle  class  child  does  would  be  to  deprive  them  of  an  equal
opportunity.

The  schools  in effect  would  be  denying  them  something  they

need.

THE  COURT:  Do  you  agree  with  his  observation  that

generally  speaking  we  are  out  of tune  with  times  in our  needs?

A.  Ido.

THE  COURT:  Let  me  ask  you  another  question  and  it has
been  touched  upon  already,  I  believe.  Do  you  think  that  in
attempting  to  solve  our  problems  in the  field  of education  that we
tend  to  think  in  terms  of  the  usual  [527]  American,  if  it  is
American  way  of  solving  things  and  that  is,  “Let’s  put  some
dollars  into  it.”

A.  That  is  too  frequently  the  fallback  position  without  any
consideration  to  how  to  restructure  the  spending  of the  dollars.  |
concur  with  that  observation.

THE  COURT:  As I recall,  both  the  Coleman  report  and  also
the  more  recent  analysis  from  data  gathered  by  the  Coleman
Commission  the  general  conclusion  was  that  the  amount  of effort
and  money  that  went  into  supplementary  support  and  resources
for education  just hasn’t  paid off.

A.  No,  sir,  your  Honor;  in point  of fact  that  is not  a Coleman

report  finding.

THE  COURT:  It isn’t?

A.  No, sir.

THE  COURT:  Isn’t  that  suggested  by Brother  Moynihan  and

his group?

A.  Yes,  it is suggested  by  them.  The  Coleman  report  finding
was  that  they  could  find  no  relationship  between  amount  of
dollars  spent  and  the  performance  of the  child  but  subsequently
scholars  have  reanalyzed  that  data  and  found  that  the  analyses
themselves  are  invalid.  I will  give you  one  simple  reason  why:

The  measure  of  dollars  per  child  was  a  district-wide  average
and  we  know  that  in  most  districts,  [528]  particularly  large ones,
the  amount  of  money  spent  on  a child,  on  every  child  in  the
district  is not  an  average,  some  children  have  $100,  $200  or  $300
more  spent  on  them  in  that  district  than  others,  at  least.  It’s  an

a

IV a 137

average  dollar  per  pupil  in  the  district.  What  they  should  have
done  is  found  the  number  of  dollars  spent  on  that  child  in  that
school  and  then  given  other  proper  controls  they  might  have  had
such,  or justified  such  a finding.

If your  Honor  wishes  I would  be  happy  to  make  available  to
you  these  criticisms  of  the  Coleman  report  findings  on  “Do
Dollars  Make  a Difference”  such  that  you  could  evaluate  them.

THE  COURT:  They  will  have  to  come  to  me  through
channels  of  these  gentlemen  here.  I  want  to  thank  you  for
indulging  me  my  matter  of curiosity  along  these  lines.

A.  I  wish  J  could  explain  it  better.  It  is  extraordinarily
complicated.  There  are  people  at  Harvard  and  the  University  of
California  at Berkeley,  such  as myself,  that  teach  whole  courses  on
trying  to  discern  the  difference  that  dollars  make  and  I am  sure  I
don’t  capsulate  the  problem  very  well  in a few  minutes.
THE  COURT:  I think  you  have  done  very  well.
| take  it there  are  no  more  questions?

STUART  C.  RANKIN,  was  called  as  a  witness  by Defendant
Detroit  Board,  and  being  first  duly sworn  by the Court,  testified  as
follows:

[606]...

Q.  Dr.  Rankin,  doesn’t  this  in  essence  set  up  a  system
whereby  you  have  quality  integrated  education  for  all  white  and
black  children  and  some  different  level  of  education  is

maintained  for the  overflow  black  surplus?

IV a 138

A.  No,  sir,  it does  not.  I believe  you  said  quality  education is

maintained  for  all  white  and  some  black.  I would  say  no  because
of that  phrase.

Q.  May  I  amend  that  to  a  greater  percentage  of  white
children  and  a  lesser  percentage  of black  children  based  simply  on
the  percentages  in the  city?

[607] A. Because  of the  present  complexity  of the  sentence |

will  have  to  respond  by uttering  another  one  instead  of saying  yes
or  no,  sir.

Q.  Please  do.

A.  It  is  clear  to  me  that  we  would  be  expending  a
significantly  greater  amount  of  funds  and  resources  for  some  of
our  youngsters  than  we  were  for  others.  Some  of those  are  white
and some  of those  are  black.

Q.  Yes,  but  proportionately,  Dr.  Rankif,  more  would  be

white  because  their  availability  sets  the  ceiling,  is that  right?

A.  If by  “proportionate”  you  mean  there  would  be a greater
percentage  of  the  white  children  in  our  schools  who  would  be
allowed  to  participate  then  the  percentage  of black  children  in the
school,  then  you  are  correct.

If,  on  the  other  hand,  you  mean  just  the  sheer  number  of

students  then  you  are  incorrect.

Q.  I thought  I said  proportions.

THE  COURT:  May I interject  a question?

MR.  FLANNERY:  Please,  your  Honor.

THE  COURT:  A  prior  witness  indicated-I

think  Mr.
McDonald  did—that  it  is  anticipated  that  the  magnet  program
would  directly  affect  about  20 or  21-thousand  students.

A. I believe,  sir, that  is slightly  low  an  estimate.  If we  were  to
count  the  present  8, the  new  16,  add  the  middles  and  the  [608]
new  16  elementaries,  we  have  40  schools  at  the  elementary  and
middle  level  at  500  students  each,  which  would  be  20,000  and in
addition  to  that  we  should  add  the  students  at  the academic  high
schools  and  according  to  our  projections  we  would  put  at  least
1200  in  each  of  those  so  that  would  be  an  additional  4800
students  bringing  it to about  24,800.

ee

IV a 139

THE  COURT:  Let’s  take  it  as  modified  by  you  then,  say
25,000.  What  is the  future  of the  program  in terms  of the  number
it will  affect?  Is that  a constant  figure  if the  student  population  in
the city remains  constant?

A.  For  that  part  of  the  program  I believe  it  would  be
constant  unless  we  were  to  add  even  further  numbers  of schools.
This  does  not  include  what  students  might  choose  the  majority  to
minority  option  and  it does  not  include  what  possible  affect  the
magnet  high  school  specialty  curriculum  might  have  but  for those
we  are  sure  of I think  the  figure  would  be fairly  constant.

THE  COURT:  Now,  if the  program  would  affect  directly  let’s
say  25,000  students  under  the  magnet  plan wouldn’t  that  intensify
the racial  imbalance  in the  remainder  of the  school  population?

A.  It would.

[612]...

2%

tly  black  schools  to  less  black  schools  under  the

[648]...

Q.  Dr.  Rankin,  do  you  agree  with  Dr. Guthrie  and  Dr.  Foster

that  [649]  with  respect  to desegregation  the  earlier  the  better?

1 to  13, no  problem?

No problem.

1 to 3 preferable?

.  Yes, in my opinion.

Why  is K to 2 omitted  from  Plan  A?

>OPrPO>re
.  The  Board  in its  wisdom  suggested  that  plan should  begin

A.  No, sir.

IV a 142

[1] Proceedings  had  in  the  above-entitled  matter  re:  Metro-
politan  Plans,  before  Honorable  Stephen  J.  Roth,  United  States

tok

[4]  THE  COURT:  Gentlemen,  we have  not  had  an  opportun-
ity  for  a  pre-trial  conference.  We  arranged  the  schedule  of these
hearings  and  I would  expect  the  best  way  to proceed  on  this  phase
of the  case,  that  is, a  consideration  of the  metropolitan  plans  for
desegregation  would  be to receive  the  proofs  from  the  State  Board
of  Education  first  and  then  the  proofs  of  the  Detroit  Board  of
Education  followed  by  proffs  of  Intervening  Defendant  Mag-
dowski,  et al and  proofs  of the  plaintiffs.

Some  of the newly  intervening  parties  have  filed  objections  to
the  conditions  of intervention  which  I have  not  given  full  consider-
ation  to.  Presently,  I believe  all  the  Court  need  say  is  that  the
conditions  are  subject  to  modification  and  change  as  justice  may
require  as  the  proceedings  progress.

At  this  point  of  these  proceedings  I see  no  possible  harm  or
prejudice  to  the  intervenors  by  the  conditions  I previously  im-
posed.  So  We  may  proceed  in  an  orderly  fashion  with  a  sense  of
expedition.  Let  me  first  [5]  inquire  whether  counsel  for  the  new
intervenors  have  selected  lead  counsel?

pending  a  determination  on  the  objections  we  filed  because  some

will  speak  in connection  with  individual  witnesses.

We  do  not  understand  your  order  to  be  so  broad  that  at  the
outset  of the  case  we  had  to  select  one  counsel  among  all of us  to
continue  to  act  throughout  the  trial.  If we  misread  your  order  we
apologize.  We  did  not  read  it to have  that  kind  of breadth.

We  had  hoped  perhaps  the  Court  might  reconsider  the  con-
striction  at  least  insofar  as  an  opportunity  of counsel  to represent

ity endeavor  to  select  a  spokesman.  We  would  like  some  clarifica-

BY MR.  RITCHIE:

A.  I'm  an  Associate  Professor  of Education  and Chairman  of

the  Department  of  Education  at  the  University  of  Michigan's
Dearborn  Campus.

psychology  from  Wayne  State  University  and  a  Doctor  of Educa:
tion  and  Philosophy  of History  and  Education  from  Wayne  State
University.

A. I reside  in Detroit,  10416  Sommerset.

Detroit?

A.  About  all of my  life  with  the exception  of several  months,

as an  early  infant.

Q.  Do you  have  a family?

A.  Yes,  I.  have.  I  have  a  wife  and  two  daughters,  both

attending  Detroit  Public  Schools.

background  and experience?

A.  A  number  of years  ago  I was  a substitute  teacher  at  the

Pointe  Public  Schools.  I taught  school  in  Grosse  Pointe
Public  Schools.  I have  moved  on  and  was  an  instructor  in  philo
sophy  and  psychology  and sociology  at Highland  Park  Community  |
College.  I then  moved  on  and  was  a part  time  faculty  member  at
[15]  Wayne  State  University  for  a number  of years  in the College
of Education.

part  time  basis.  After  that  I became  a  full  time  faculty  member,

at the  University.  That’s  Dearborn  Campus.

Q.  (By  Mr.  Saxton)  How  did  you  determine,  Dr.  Morshead,

percent;  what  yardstick  did  you  use  to determine  that?

considerations:  The  20 percent  figure  was  the  figure  that  the  black

members  of  the  community  in  terms  of their  professional  exper-

group  within  the population.

And  also  we  had  to  take  into  consideration  another  fac#or,

Q.  You  mentioned  something  about  concentrating  certain

Department  of  Health,  Education  and  Welfare  on  the  success  or
failure  of compensatory  education,  are  you  not?

A.  Yes,  sir.

Q.  And  the  general  conclusion  was  that  compensatory
education,  some  eight  or  nine  million  dollars,  had  been a failure  in
terms  of the effort  expended  and  the  product?

A.  There  is some  contravening  evidence  that  I believe  I made
reference  to  that  would  be  available  from  the  Detroit  Board  of
Education  but in the  main  your  report  is correct.

Q.  There  may  be  some  isolated  instances  where  it helped  but
[213]  in  general  the  compensatory  education  has  been a failure?

A.  Yes, sir.

Q.  Are  you  familiar  with  the  study  Racial  Isolation  in Public

Schools?

A.  Yes,  sir.

Q.  I would  like  to  read  to you  a statement  from  that  and  ask

you  a question  about  it.

.

‘“‘Another  important  factor  in  successful  school
desegregation  is  the  involvement  of  all  schools  in  the
community.  Where  desegregation  affects  only  part  of  the
community,  the  affected  parents  may  feel  resentful  at  being
required  to  contribute  to  the  solution  of  a  problem  which
other  parts of the community  remain  free  to ignore.”

Do you  agree  with  that?

A.  I agree  with  the  principle  that  I interpret  underlying  that
all  people  engaged  in  public  —  all  people  whose  children  are
partaking  of public  education  ought  to be involved  in this  process.

Q.  Do  you  think  that  is  true  of  the  K  through  4  parents,
parents  of children  in  K through  4?  Do  you  think  they  should  be
involved?

IV a 154

[214] A.  They  will  be  involved, sir, under  the  auspices  of this
plan  when  their  youngster  enters  the  fifth  grade  and  their  young-
sters  will  be involved  in the  integration  aspects  of this  proposal.

:

Q.  Isn’t  one  of the  crucial  periods  of time  when  desegregation
first  occurs  is when  some  of the  mythology  about  what  is going to
happen  is solved  by finding  out  that  the  world  does  not  come  to
an  end  because  you  desegregate.  Isn’t  it  important  that  that
particular  period  that  all  people  in  the  community  share  the
responsibility  not  only  for  dealing  with  the  problem  but  solving
the  problem?

A.  Yes,  sir.

ed

[218}Q.  And  if  the  Court  is  to  decide  that  perhaps  some-

‘thing  like —

“3

Are  you  familiar  with  the  State’s  Plan  3, which  is the  metro-

politan  plan?  Have  you  looked  at it?

A.  Yes,  I have  looked  at it.

Q.  What  they  call  the  I.0.Z.,  six  regions,  this  sort  of thing?

A.  Yes.  It’s pretty  fuzzy  in my  memory  right  now  though.

Q.  Assuming  such  a  plan  developed  which  did  not  retain  the
existing  school  districts  as  a  separate  entity,  would  you  think  it
possible,  assuming  that  your  goal was  to come  up with,  rather  than
multiply  years  of  administration,  a  single  administrative  unit  but
perhaps  some  sub-unit,  as  Detroit  has,  regional  boards?  Would  you
think  it  feasible  to  have,  as  its  first  year  of  development,  a
retention  of  the  existing  school  districts  while  some  of  the
mechanics  of the  merger  of school  districts  were  worked  out?

A.  This  is  entirely  feasible.  And  it  was  under  consideration
for  this  plan.  And  one  of  the  options  that  was  before  the  com-
mittee  was  that  the  retention  of  the  local  school  districts  be an
interim  detention,  and  the  boroughs  themselves  would  end  up
being the equivalent  of the school  districts.

[219]It  was  finally  concluded  that  we  would  go  with  this
particular  format  but  the  other  format  was  considered  and  it is
workable.

Q.  Assuming  an  approximate  20 percent  minority  population
in the  metropolitan  area,  or  at least  the  part  that’s  most  urbanized,

IV a 155

’  would  you  describe  your  plan  as  an  effort  to  achieve  maximum

desegregation  within  limits  of feasibility  of transportation?

A.  Yes,  sir.

Q.  One  other  point.  I think  you  testified  that  more  than  45
minutes  would  be,  in  your  opinion,  too  long for an  early  element-
ary  child,  or  I think  you  used  the  term  “primary  grade  child”.  Up
to 45 is okay,  within  that  parameter  but  much  more  than  that  you
would  think  counter-productive?

A.  I believe  this  to be the  case.

Q.  Did  you  not  also  say,  Doctor,  on  your  direct  examination,
that  the  maximum  estimated  time  for  transportation  under  the
plan that  you  have  proposed  is 45  minutes?

A.  This  was  our  estimate.  It may  be in error  because  it is only

an  estimate.

Q.  All  right.  Similarly,  the  45  minutes  is  an  estimate  and  a
variable  depending  on  the  child,  his  age  and  his  grade,  naturally?

[220] A.  Yes.  Yes,  oh, yes,  yes.

Q.  So  within  your  own  estimate  and  your  own  criteria  there
is no  justification  in terms  of time  or  distance  —  time  being  a more
important  factor  —  for  not  including  the  elementary  grades  or  the  .
primary  grades;  is that  correct?

A.  Smaller  youngsters,  in  my  estimation,  could  be  bused  for
shorter  distances,  and  the  fatigue  factor  that  I alluded  to  would
not  be debilitative  to  their  education.

STUART  RANKIN,
resumed  the  stand  as  a  witness  herein,  and  having  been  previously
duly sworn  testified  further  as  follows:

[411]...

eee

Q.  I believe  you  previously  have  testified  that  one  of  the
problems  that  you  had or do have  within  the city limits  of Detroit,
is that  you  have  a large  number  of black  students  who  are  poor,
and this results  in low SES.
ks
Am I paraphrasing  —

A.  (Interposing)
Yes, sir.
Qa. And  on  the other  hand we have  a  number  of middle  class
hg suburbs  who,  it would  be  fair  to  say  have a higher  [412]

A.  Yes, sir.
Q.  Now,  Doctor,  I think  you have mentioned

a target  area; is

that correct?

IV a 166

A.  Yes, sir.

Q.  Would  you  explain  to  the  Court  what  you  mean  by a

target  area?

,  A.  Well,  if one  is going  to  have  a  metropolitan  desegregation
plan  one  needs  to  decide  what  the  bounds  of that  metropolitan
area  are.  And  we  as  others  have  done  struggled  with  where  that
boundary  should  be  drawn.  We  felt  that  it  should  be  drawn  far
enough  out  so  that  we  can  provide  an  adequate  balance  -for
socio-economiic  status,  but  we  also  felt  that  we  should  draw  it far
enough  out  so  that  we  could  avoid  having  racially  identifiable
schools.  We  felt  that  we  should  not  draw  it  so  far  as  to  make
unreasonable  transportation  problems;  we  felt  that  we  should
draw  it  far  enough  out so  that  it  should  at  least  inhibit  white
people  —  although  I am sure  that  it can’t  totally  prevent it — so
that  all  of us share  in  the  desegregation  plan  and  that  we  have no
racial  isolation  in the general  area,  either  black  or  white.

And  in  pursuing  that  we  decided  that  [413]  the  Oakland,
Macomb  and  Wayne  County  area  was  a logical  one  to choose,  and

Q.  I see.  Doctor,  as  far  as  white  racially  identifiable  [414]

Q.  Now,  white  flight:  Is there  any  significance  to white  flight

in education  in terms  of SES?

A.  Yes,  sir.  Generally,  the  average  —  let  us  assume a scale  of
socio-economic  status  running  from  low  to  high.  The  average
socio-economic  status  of  black  families  is  considerably  lower  on
that scale  than  the average  socio-economic  status  of white  families.
And  when  the  white  families  leave  a neighborhood,  then  they take
with.them  that  higher  socio-economic  status  and  that  lowers  the
socio-economic  status  in  the place  where  they leave  And  that  is an
example  of what  occurs.

Sk:  gunk: th Maite  ie  tit  when. dee  nee
downtown  portion  of  Detroit  and  we  were  making  combination

to  Southeastern  High  “School  and  its  constellation  would  be

would  be  with  Detroit  Osborn  and  Van  Dyke,  Fitzgerald,

minimize  the  total distance.

ible  organization.  And  as  I spoke  before,  that  we  believe

We  believe  the  Intermediate  School  Districts  have  a  key  role
also.  They  at  present  are,  they  are  more  of service  agencies  to the
local  school  districts  than  they are  enforcement  arms  of the  State.
Many of us hope  that  that  remains  the  case.  But in any  event  the
Intermediate  School  District  will  often  provide  programs  for the
handicapped  where  it’s  not  possible  for a small  local  district  to do
that.  Because  they  don’t  have  enough  students  to run  the  program.

And  in other  ways  they  provide  experts  in  various  areas.  The
Oakland  County  Intermediate  School  District  has  a  rather  well

established  computer  facility  and  they
for  their  districts.  The  Wayne  Coanty  Intermediate  School

service,
so that  as the cluster begins to work through the details of

Districts.  So  they  would  continue  to  play  the  kind  of  role  that

_
The  local  school  districts  have  also  been  the  key  of  the
educational  program,  the  heart  of  it.  They  are  the  ones  who  are
responsible;  they  are  the  ones  who  conducted  the  programs.  And
that would remain  the case in this situation.  They would be in a
new  situation  where  it was  necessary  with  regard  to desegregation,
and that would  take  them  into  other  afleas,  working  in cooperation
with  some  other  districts.  And  that  will  cause  them  to  play some
new roles.

.

But  we  believe  the  responsibility  for the specific  design,  the
mechanism  of assigning  peoples,  the  decision  as  to which  grades
are  housed  in  which  schools,  the  transportation  mechanisms,  the
in-service  education  programs,  the  plans  for  making  [443]  sure
that  we  prize  the  dignity  of every  student,  the  plans  as  to  insure
equal  quality,  to  insure  increased  quality,  to  provide  equal
opportunity;  all  of  those  demands  fall  on  that  cluster  of  local
school  districts  which  would  have  to  operate  in  a  team  fashion.
And in  my  judgement  and  in our judgement,  the  best  way  would
be for  them  to  respond,  given  a date  and  a deadline,  to  respond
with a specific  plan.

I believe they can do it far better  than anyone else;  they  know
their  own  school  plants,  they  know  where  laboratories  are,  they
know  where  cafeterias,  lunch  rooms  are,  they  know  their  own
transportational  capability,  they  know  the  needs  of  their
individual  pupils better  than  anyone.  In our judgment  the specifics
of the plan should  be worked  out  by the  participants,  by cluster.

A.  Well,  the  school  districts  in many  cases  cooperate  with  the
Intermediate  School  District  doing  this,  and  certainly  there  ar
intra-district  cooperation  endeavors.  I wouldn’t  say  that  we  havea
lot  of them,  but  surely  there  are  examples,  yes,  now  going  on,
where  school  districts  are  cooperating  in endeavors.

Q.  And  the  cooperation  in special  education  is done  through

the Intermediate  School  Districts?

A.  Generally.

Q.  And  is  there  any  reason  why  that  cooperation  can’t  be
done  in the area  of desegregation,  through  the  Intermediate  [445]
School  Districts?

A.  None  that  I know  of.

understand  that  we  don’t  need  computers  to assign students?

A.  Well,  I think  that  the  people  who  work  with  the children
and youth  in those  districts  could  create  plans without  the use  ofa
computer.  Although  it  may  be  useful  for  certain  functions,  it is
just  a  tool  to  help  you  do  some  things  more  rapidly.  And if there
were a lot  of  things  that  had to  be done  it  might be easier to
computerize  part of it, but it is not necessary.

Q.  In  fact,  wasn’t  that  one  of  the  considerations  in making

smaller,  manageable  units?

A.  Yes, sir.
