that  school,  transporting  the  children  who  live  the  furthest
distance  from  the  school.  Would  that  not  have  a  tendency  to
reduce  some  of your  transportation  times?

[765]  A.  It  would  reduce  the  transportation  times  in  that
end  of  the  school  district  where  you  had  children  coming  great
distances  to the school.

Q.  You  might,  for instance,  instead  of drawing circles  around
the  school  simply  say  all  children  who  live in this quadrant  equals
the  number  you  need  and  would  attend  thé  school  and  then  the

IV a 201

children  who  lived  closest  to  the  perimeter  in  the  direction  they
were  going would  attend  the  other  schools,  is that  correct?

A.  Yes.
Q.  There  are  many  variations  and  shorter  techniques?
A.  That  is  the  fashion  we  have  normally  transported  for
reasons  of  overcrowding,  selecting  a  geographic  area  in
transporting  all children  in all grades.

Q.  In  some  other  context  other  than  my  diagram  it  is

sometimes  called  an  “island  zone”’  is that  correct?

A.  Yes.
Q.  In  the  examples,  illustrations  which  you  have  done,  is it
not  the  technique  simply  to  take  a  group  of  schools  in  the
particular  area  or  cluster  and  look  at  all  of  those  schools  and
determine  what  is  the  best  and  most  practical  manner  of
reassinging  the  pupils  in  order  to  change  the racial  patterns  of the
schools;  isn’t  that  in gross  what  you  do?

[766]  A.  Yes,  to  select  a  grouping  that  will  yield  the
percentage  composition  we  want  and  then  apply  an  organization
that  will  achieve  that  and  this organization  at the elementary  level
is what  is  sometimes  referred  to  as  the  Princeton  Plan  at  the
elementary  level.

Q.  I  am  referring  particularly  to  Mr.  Krasicky’s  question
about  Chrysler  School.  You  are  not  limiting  it  to  the  children
going  to  Chrysler  now.  They  may  go there  for many  reasons,  they
may  not  live  physically  closer  to  Chrysler  but  live  closer  to  some
other  neighborhood  school  but  happen  to  be  there  at  Chrysler
right now?

A.  There  are  some  at  Chrysler  right  now  who  do  not  live
there  who  have  been  admitted  under  various  policy  bases  over  the
years.

Q.  Essentially  what  you  are  doing  is you  wipe  the slate  clean,
you  have  X  number  of  school  buildings  with  various  capacities,
various  equipment  and  so  forth  and  you  have  a certain  number  of
students  and  you  make  an  assignment  plan.

A.  Yes
Q.  For  practical  reasons  you  sometimes  use  the existing zone
and  divide  it  up  but  that  is  not  necessary  either.  You  can

IV a 202

sometimes  achieve  more  practical  results  by disregarding  existing
zone  boundaries,  is that  correct?

A.  That  is possible,  yes.

[767]  Q.  So  it  is  not  a  question  of  reassigning  Chrysler
School  which  is  desegregated  in  some  artificial  sense  and
surrounded  by  a  number  of  other  schools  which  are  almost  all
black,  it is simply  taking  X number  of black  children  in  the  King
constellation  and  there  are  some  whites  in  that  constellation  and
dealing  with  the  white  students  in  the  Grosse  Pointe  Schools  and
coming  up  with  an  equitable  desegregation  plan,  isn’t  that  the
basic  outline?

A.  Yes.  There  are  several  of  the  Detroit  constellations  that
have  varying  numbers  of white  students  in them  although  they are
predominantly  black.  Where  those  situations  occur  the  plan should
certainly  show  some  way  of  dealing  with  them  so  they  wind up
with  a racial  composition  of the  objective.

Q.  I take  it your  predicate  is no  child,  no  parent  on  behalf of
his child has  a  particular right  to  go to  Chrysler  or Joy  or any
other  particular  school.  The  School  Board  has  the  responsibility
for assigning them  to  schools,

is that correct?

A.  Yes, this is correct.  This  plan  does  as we have illustrated
does  permit  students  for  some  years of their  education  or  assumes
they  will  be attending  the schools  to  which  they are now  assigned
assuming  that  we  don’t  have  to  make  changes  in those  boundaries
for other  reasons.

[768]  Q.  Would  you  say  from  your  experience,  Mr.
Henrickson,  that  construction  policies  have  a substantial  effect  on
the  process  of  desegregation  and  the  ease  by  which  it  might  be
done  or  undone?

A.  Yes,  the  availability  of buildings  in given  locations  can  be
very  helpful  or  can  be  detrimental  depending  on  the eee
of the  immediately  surrounding  area.

Q.  Let’s  look  first  of  all  at Detroit  and  having  in  mind  the
Court’s  findings  about  excess  capacity  within  certain  Detroit
schools  and  the  concentration  of  that  capacity  in  a  number  of
predominantly  black  schools  or  innercity  schools.  Let’s  assume
you  use  your  present  capacity  figures  per  classroom.  Would  this

IV a 203

not  reduce  the  number  of black  students  that  might  be required  to
be transported  out  in some  instances?

Let  me  give  you  an  example.  Suppose  you  have  a  school  a
thousand  capacity  and  you  have  500  empty  spaces  in that  school.
In terms  of  desegregating  that  school  you  would  not  be  bringing
out  as  many  black  children  as  if it had  1000  in it, it would  simply
be  bringing  in  enough  white  children  and  taking  out  a  smaller
percentage  of black.

A.  In  desegregating  you  are  dealing  with  children  and  the
number  of children  you  have  in a given  situation,  not  in [769]  the
first instance  with  the capacity  of the school.

Q.  You  have  numbers  of children  but  you  would  be bringing
in substantial  numbers  of white  children  to  that  school  and  you
would have space  for them already  in that school  in a  larger
number.  You  wouldn’t  have  to  move  out  to  make  room  for them
in other  words.

A.  That’s  right
Q.  Mr.  Henrickson,  the  base  plan  or  outline  submitted
by the Detroit  Board calls  for some  regions  that  do not
take  any  part  of  existing  Detroit  School  System  students,  is
that  correct?  I  think  it’s  Number  18  and  Number  12,  is  that
correct?

A.  Yes.
Q.  Could  not  some  of the  problems  which  were  foreseen  by
the  Board  in  terms  of creation  of a  set  of schools
to attract  white
students  away  from  integrated  schools  be  controlled  to  some
extent  by restrictions  on  construction  policy?  In other  words,  if
you  did  not  permit  construction  of a ring of white  schools  outside
of whatever  perimeter  the  Court  chooses,  would  this  not  have  a
tendency  to limit  the movement  of whites  out of the system?

A.  To  the  extent  that  people  move  to  what  they  consider
good  schools  and  there  were  not  good schools  available  this would
certainly  tend,  any  restriction  on  construction  of  [770]  schools
would  certainly  tend  to limit  movement.

Q.  Suppose  you  applied  a  uniform  method  of  determining
capacity  of  school  buildings  throughout  the  metropolitan  area,
where  would  there  be  a  need  for  school  construction  in  terms  of
geography,  what  area  would  have  construction  needs?

—

IV  a 204

A.  The.  Detroit  schools  have  classrooms  sufficient  to
accommodate  students  at  a  level  of  31  to  32  students  average
overall.  The  other  school  systems  average  overall  of  classrooms
sufficient  to  accommodate  students  at a level  of 24 or  25 students
in a class.  This  may  not  be  true  individually  of all  systems  but on
the  average  the  number  of  classrooms  in  the  metropolitan  area
indicate  that  capability.

I  would  draw  from  this  the  conclusion  that  in  order  to
equalize  class  size  over  the  metropolitan  area  the  greatest
construction  need  would  be within  the  City of Detroit  and  I know
from  my  personal  knowledge  that  the  greatest  need  would  be  at
the secondary  level  within  the  Detroit  school  system.

Q.  The  .establishment  of  new  facilities,  if  you  leveled  out
throughout  the  metropolitan  area  would  be in Detroit  rather  than
building  a  ring  of  schools  around  Detroit  or  in  the  outermost
reaches,  you  would  tend  to  establish  more  [771]  capacity  within
the  city, is that  correct?

A. If your  objective is to equalize  class size this is where the

capacity  would  need  to be.

Q.  If you  used  some  care  in locating  those  schools  you  could
tend  to  minimize  travel  distances  by  putting  them,  say,
equi-distant  between  the  innercity  downtown  and  the  outer-most
suburban  ring?

A.  That is true.

ker) Be

MR.  SAXTON:  May I suggest  to  the  Court  then  perhaps  we
can  get some  guidance  before  a meeting  of Intervenors  counsel  this
evening  or  tomorrow.  One  line  of  evidence  we  are  considering
proposing  to  the  Court,  let  me  state  it by way  of background,  we
understood  really  what  we  are  all  about  here  and  perhaps  we  are
wrong  but this our  understand  of what  we  are  all about  is trying to
bring  about  equality  of  educational  opportunity  for  all  children
and  the  question  is then  now  one  of remedial  effect.  What  is the
best  means  to  do  that.

The  Court  found  the ill of de jure segregation  exists  in Detroit
and  I understand  what  we  are  about  is trying  to  do  something  to
bring  about  equality  of  educational  opportunity  and  that  may
take  various  forms  as  the  Court  knows  and  the  higher  court
suggested  busing  is merely  a remedial  tool.

I would  suggest  and  appreciate  the  Court’s  viewpoint,  one, in
line  of  testimony  that  we  were  considering  offering  was  expert
testimony  based  on  studies  of  the  effects  of  systems  wher
desegregation  through  busing  has  been  employed,  the  results  of
those  studies  being  to  show  that  desegregation  accomplished
through  busing  has not  resulted  in any  measurable  achievement  for
minority  group  [793]  people  and  has  in  fact  resulted  in  some
social  disadvantages.

If the Court  feels  that  type  of testimony  would  not  be helpful
to  it  or  runs  contrary  to  previous  rulings  we  would  nonetheless
like  to  have  stated  on  the  record  at some  point  we  have  attempted
to offer  it.

IV a 207

I  think  it  does,  your  Honor,  quite  frankly,  challenge  the
efficacy  of a metropolitan  plan accomplished  through  the  medium
of busing.  It may  be the Court  feels differently.

THE  COURT:  I understand  your  position  and  you  have  a
perfect  right  to stand  on  that  position.  But you  see  I don’t  confuse
one  objective  with  the  other  although  I want  to pursue  both.  You
try to  put  it in  the  alternative  that  what  we  ought  to  be  about  is
achieving  educational  excellence  and  I am  for  that  but  I am  also
under  command  to  do  something  else  and  that  is  accomplish
desegregation.  Hopefully,  if you  do one  you  will achieve  the  other.
I know  the  tests  results  differ  and  there  are  different  philosophies
and  viewpoints  on  this  but  why  don’t  you  consult  with  your
fellow  counsel  and  take  your  position  and  make  your  offer  of
proof and  until  you  present  it I am  not going  to rule  on  it. I made
some  suggestions  which  may  be of some  help and  may  not.

DIRECT  EXAMINATION

BY MR.  LUCAS:

Q.  You  were  served  with  a subpoena  by me  this  morning;  is

[891]  that  correct,  Mr.  Flynn?

A.  Yes, sir.

__A.  I am  the  Director  of  Inter-Group  Relations,  which  is a
oe ggg  in School Community  Relations,  Detroit  Public

IV a 208

Q.  Mr.  Flynn,  is  that  the  same  position  that  you  held  when

you  last  testified  in this case?

A.  Yes, it is.

Q.  And  your  duties  are  essentially  the  same;  is that  correct?
A.  That  is true.

Q.  Is  there  any  addition  to  your  vita,  such  as  recent

publications  that  should  be noted  to update  the  record?

A.  A  publication  which  is  a  staff  journal  of  the  Detroit
Schools  has  an  article  written  by  me  and  my  associate,  Dr.
Rosenberg,  in the  Winter  Issue  of this  year.
Q.  What  is the  title  of the article?

A.  The  article  deals  with  the  problem  of textbooks  and  the

adequacy  of textbooks  for urban  city school  programs.

Q.  You  are  listed,  sir,  as  one  of the  persons  responsible  for

Dearborn.

Are  you  that  same  Freeman  Flynn  that  [892]  is listed  in that

document?

A.  Yes,  lam.

Q.  Mr.  Flynn,  how  long  did  it take  to  prepare  the  proposal
which  was  submitted  to the  Court?  Not  particular  days if you  will,
but  simply  the  time  span.

A.  Roughly  two  months,  ten  weeks.  The  first  portion  of that,

between  the  professionals  who  were  involved  in  the  group  as  to

numbers  perhaps  took  as short  a time  as  two  weeks.

Q.  Once  you  decided  whether  or  not  and  how  far out,  then it

took  two  weeks  to put  it all  together?

A.  That  is true.

[896]...
Q.  Would  you  say,  then,  that  insofar  as  this  boundary  and  its
concern,  that  there  was  in  your  opinion,  or  the  consensus,  an
orientation  more  toward  Detroit,  or  a  greater  community  of
interest  inward  than  outward?

A.  I think  I would  accept  that,  that  language,  yes.
Q.  And  although  there  may  be  some  tendency,  community
of  interest  or  orientation  towards  Detroit  further  out,  it  is  a
matter  of degree.

A.  Yes.

Q.  Is that  fair?
A.  Yes.  I think  that  any  time  —
Q.  (Interposing)  I am  pointing  to the  west,  but  I really  meant

A.  I would  think  that  any  examination  of this  kind,  and  the

have  to  be  a  matter  of judgment  and  I don’t  think  that  you  would
get  complete  agreement.  The[897]  best  you  can  hope  for  is

Q.  And  would  population  density  play a part  in this?
A.  Yes.
Q.  Are  there  any  different  criteria  or  perhaps  more  magnified

court  here.

Q.  Well,  you  adopted  in  particular,  by  agreement  or

perhaps  time  and  distance,  that.  there  were  differences  to  the
northeast  between  the areas  excluded  and  those  included.

A.  I think  so.

IV a 210

There  is  another  dimension.  We  are  having  enough  difficulty

Q.  What  is  your  view  on  that?  |  think  you  perhaps  have
expressed  it  in  an  earlier  hearing.  How  important  are  the  earlier
grades, vis-a-vis  what  happens  to the  child  in a student  grade?

A.  I think  all  the  evidence  indicates  that  children  who  are
brought  together  at  an  early  age  can  be  more  —  sorry,  I tangle  up
my verb  on  that  sentence.

I think  all the evidence  indicates  that  it is appropriate  to bring
young  children  together  to  deal  with  the  problems  which  have  to
do with  prejudice,  with  stereotypes,  with  institutional  racism, with
all kinds  of things  which  our  present  separation  finds  itself  unable
to deal  with.

Q. Then,  sir,  do  I hear  you  in  dissent  from  that  limitation

[901]  on  that proposal?

A.  Yes.  And I think  that  it  is  appropriate  to  say  that  that
committee,  again,  achieved  a report  which  it felt it could  support,
and  I do support  it.  As  a  proposal,  I felt at the  time  that  we  were
Preparing  it, that  all  parts  of it would  be  examined  carefully  and
that  it  would  be  tested  against  other  proposals,  and  that  those

IVa  212

areas  which  under  close  scrutiny  and  examination  could  not  stand
up,  would  be,  would  either  be discarded  or  would  be not  included
in the Court  Order.

[904]  My  objective  and  my  feeling  about  the  matter  is that
the  black  community  is  entitled  to  have  a  relatively  large
representation  in  any  matter  which  affects  them,  and  that  the
black  student  in a school  needs  to  have  a significant  number  of his
fellows  in the  school  in order  to  protect  his feeling  of self-identity
and  belonging  necessary  to a group  which  has dignity.

Q.  Is  this  particularly  true  in  a  transition  period  where  you
are  going  from  a  situation  where  you  have  had  segregated  schools,
to  a  desegregation  process,  hopefully  leading  to  meaningful
integration?  Is your  answer  particularly  true  in that  context?

A.  Let  me  answer  that  by giving  you  an  example:

The  Ford  High  School  in  northwest  Detroit  had  some

difficulty  a while  back.

Q.  That  is a predominantly  white  school,  is it?

A.  It's  31  percent  black,  and  most  of the  black  children  come
from  the  east  side  of  the  district.  And  the  area  immediately
around  the  school  is all white.

We  have  seen  this  same  phenomena  at  the  Finney  High
School,  at  Mackenzie  some  ‘years  ago,  at  Cooley,  a number  of
schools.  And  when  there  is racial  tension  and  when  there  is anger
between  the  two  groups,  one  of  the  problems  is  how  does  the
youngster  [905]  who  is  black  get  in  and  out  of  that  white
community  safely  and  securely  without  being  subjected  to  the
irritation  and  the  anger  of those  youngsters  who  are  temporarily  at
oddes  with  him.

At  the  Ford  School  the  youngsters  who  were  black  had  to get
on  the  bus  to  leave  the  community,  under  the  taunts  of  white
youngsters  who  were  saying  “This  is  our  community”.  And  the
black  kids  had  great  difficulty  getting  on  the  bus  and  leaving,
because  their  sense  of dignity  was  offended,  the  manhood  of the
young  men  was  offended.  And  it did  create  and  is a problem,  and
it must  be dealt  with  when  we  do execute  any  such  plan  as  this.

IVa  213

The  schools  have  got  to  devise  programs  and  ways  to  deal
with  the  feelings  of  young  people,  and  the  schools  have  got  to
provide  ways  for  the  youngsters  to  learn  how  to  get  along  with
each  other  and  they’ve  got  to  set  up  deliberate  programs  to make
sure  that  this  happens.

And  to  have  too  few  blacks  moved  into  a white  community  is

| think  something  that  I personally  would  not  wish  to see.

Q.  This  is  one  of  the  curative  factors  or  one  of the  factors
tending  to  solve,  to  mitigate  the  problem;  the  fact  that  [906]  the
proposals  that  have  seriously  been  considered  here  are  all two-way
plans  rather  than  just  black  children  being  brought  into  white
school?

A.  Absolutely.

Q.  And  students  perceive  a  difference,  do  they  not,  sir, when
it is only  black  kids  being  brought  out,  and  white  children  are  not
being  brought  into  the  schools  in the  inner  city?

A. I believe  that  that  is an  expressed  and  basic  factor  which  I

would  have  #0 argue  for and  advocate.

Q.  And  aren't  there  studies,  particularly  dealing  with  violence
situations,  tension  situations  at  the  high  school  level  which
indicate  problems  with  desegregation  or  with  black  and  white
children  in  the  same  school  often  are  a  product  of the  fact  that
they  came  from  all  black  elementary  schools  as  a  group,  and  all
white  elementary  schools  as  a  group,  and  were  placed  together  in
that high school  setting  for the  first  time,  in a desegregated  setting,
together  with  all  the  other  adolescent  problems  that  children  face
at that  age?

A.  There  are  a great  many  studies  that  indicate  this.

{907}  Q.  Is it true,  Doctor,  that  they  come  in with  not  only
the  identification  of  race  but  some  old  school  loyalties,  “‘We  are
the  kids  from  Jones  Elementary”  and  if those  kids  are  all  black
and the  rivalries  that  result  that  the  children  from  Smith who are
all  white  make  the  cleavage  automatically  on  racial  grounds  or
racial  lines, doesn’t  it?

A.  I think  Dr.  Rankin  used  the  term  “host  and  guest”  at one
point  which  is  perhaps  somewhat  a  fancy  term  but  the  problem
really  is that.  It is a  question  of our school  versus  you  who are not
really  members  of our  school.

IVa  214

oan

One  principal  was  so  unfortunate  I think  to  use  the  phrase
“resident  and  non-resident  students.”  We  have  got  to  deal  with
that.

Q.  So  if  you  had  segregated  K through  5  or  K  through 4

schools  and  you  brought  the  children  out  of their  all black  and all
white  schools  and  put  them  in  a  desegregation  situation  for  the
first  time  in  some  respects  you  are  compounding  your  problems
political  and otherwise,  are  you  not?

A.  Yes.  I think  again  if  we were  to begin  by  introducing
youngsters  at  the  high  school  level  as  we  have  done  in  Detroit  in
several  instances  we  are  setting  up  a  number  of specific  problems
which  we  know a great  deal  about,  hopefully  we  would  be  smart
enough  to  deal  with  if we  were  [908]  to  initiate  such  a  plan  but
the  fact  is  that  we  are  bringing  youngsters  together  who  have
through  the  school  system  and  their  communities  learned  some
things  about  each  other  which  are  erroneous  and  which  cause  great
difficulty.

Q.  Let’s  talk  a  little  bit  about  some  of  the  practices  and
effects,  Doctor.  Let’s  take  a  factual  example.  Suppose  we  have  a
grade  structure  in a  set  of  schools  of  K6  and  suppose  we only
desegregate  Grades  3 through  6.  Suppose  we  put  25 percent  black
children, 25 per  cent  of the  children  in Grades  3, 4 and 5 in that
school,  they  in fact  will  not  be 25 per  cent  of that  school  building,
will they?

+  A.  That  is true.

;

Q.  They  will  be in a very  small  minority,  will  they  not?

A.  That  is true.

Q.  Do you  see  some  problems  there,  Doctor?

A.  Well,  I see  some  problems  but  the  problems  are  not  nearly.
the  same,  however,  as  they  would  be  if we  were  doing  the  same
thing in high school  at the  high school  grade  level.

Q.  Would  it  not  in dealing  with  the  desegregation  process  if
there  should  be  any  stairstep  approach  at  all, I take  it you  would
agree  first  of all  that  it should  not  be the  [909]  high  schools  only
as a start,  that  it should  start  in the lower  grades,  is that  right?

A.  That’s  correct.

IVa  215

Q.  Both  administrative  and  in  terms  of presenting  a  realistic
picture  of  what  a  desegregated  school  looks  like,  wouldn’t  it be
more  rationale  and  sound  to  do  so  utilizing  the  entire  grade
structure  pattern  of the service  school?

A.  If we  could  do it in the  fashion  which  you  have  described,

I would  argue  we  should.

Q.  In  other  words,  if  the  majority  of  the  schools  in  the
suburban  districts  are  K6  it would  be  or  have  many  advantages  to
doing  that  entire  building,  the  K6  unit  as  a  unit  when  you  do  it,
isn’t that  right?

A.  Yes.  I would  like  to  point  out  something  else,  too.  I am
not  sure  whether  this  is responsive  to  your  question.  But in those
areas  where  we  have  bused  children  in,  the  elementary  levels  in
Detroit  other  than  the  fact  that  the schools  have  some  problems  in
terms  of  individual  teachers  or  some  minor  problems  of
adjustment  in  the  community,  my  experience  and  the  evidence  |
have  available  to  me  indicates  that  this  does  not  disturb  the
community  to  any  considerable  amount  whether  you  bring
children  in at  the  ten  per  cent  or  the  15  or  20 or the  25 even  the
30 per cent  level  in the school.

[910]  Where  we  have a great  deal  of difficulty  and  emotion  in
the  matter  is  when  the  community  is  undergoing  a  community
change,  a  housing  pattern  change.  Then  we  run  into  problems  that
we  don’t  seem  to  be able  to deal  with  but  the  schools  where  we  —
Q.  Excuse  me,  is that  a function  in part  do you  think  there  is
a housing  change  going  on  in a  particular  area  and  then  there  is a
school  change  at the same  time?

-  A.  Right.
Q.  Is it in  part  a  function  of the  fact  that  that  change  is not

occurring  somewhere  else?

A.  I think  so.
Q.  Go on,  I’m sorry  to interrupt.
A.  The  only  point  I was  making  is  that  as  I think  about  it
now  and,  by  the  way.  you  asked  whether  or  not  the  function  of
my  office  had  changed,  it  has  changed  somewhat  under
decentralization,  our  role  is  more  consultative  and  supportive
father  than  a  role  which  gets involved  in distinct  fire-fighting  kind

1Va216

of experiences  and  so  forth  and  so  on.  But  as  | think  back  about
it,  where  we  have  had  great  difficulty  and  where  there  are  racial
tensions  they  have  to do with  housing  patterns,  not  bused  children
in  those  schools  and  not  at  the  elementary  level  in  almost  any
case.

{911}  Q.  Isn’t  some  of  the  hostility  run  into  a  function  of
parents  complaining,  ‘““Why  did  you  pick our  school  to  bring  them
in?”

CROSS  EXAMINATION  BY MR  .  KRASICKY:

Q.  Dr.  Flynn,  you  have  testified  that  it is important  for black
youngsters  to  retain  their  self-identity  and  as  a  necessary  element
of  this  there  must  be  other  black  children  in  attendance  as  a
supportive  thing for it, is that  correct?

A.  Yes, sir.

Q.  Now,  would  you  tell  us  in  your  opinion  what  percentage
of youngsters  in  a  particular  school  classroom  give this  support  so
that  black  youngsters  may  retain  their  self-identity,  if you  can?

A.  Again  this  is a  matter  of personal  judgment.  I don’t  think
there  is any study  or that there  is a supporting
data I may use to
prove  my  point  but  I would  argue  that  [919]  somewhere in a 20
per cent  minimum  would  be my  personal  limit.

IV  a217

Q.  A minimum  of 20 per cent?

A.  I would  like  to  move  in that  direction,  yes,  at  least.
Q.  So  that  in  your  opinion  the  clusters  that  might  have  8 per
cent  black  children  really  are  not  subject  to  movement  of children
because  you  would  eliminate  the  self-identify  of black  youngsters
if you  diffuse  them?

A.  I think  I might join  you  in that  argument,  yes.
Q.  Would  this  be  your  opinion?  Would  you  be  opposed  to
movement  of  youngsters  in  a  cluster  that  have  only  8  per  cent
black  children  in them?

A.  Perhaps  I better  back  away  and  look  at  your  question  a
little  more  carefully.  If you  are  talking  about  8 per  cent  children
in one  school  and  we  are  talking  about  dividing  that  8 per cent  up
even  to  a  smaller  degree  well,  then  I would  oppose  that.  If you  are
talking  about  20  or  30  per  cent  youngsters  in  one  school  and  no
children  in  another  school  and a division  of that  20 or  30 per  cent
into  10 or  15  per  cent  in two  schools  I might  want  to take  a good
long  look  and  say  whether  or  not  and  discuss  whether  or  not  that
is reasonable.

Q.  Would  you  look  at Cluster  18 in Exhibit  M14,  which  is the

Detroit  Metropolitan  Plan.

[920]  A.  I don’t  have  a copy  of that  in front  of me.
MR.  KRASICKY:  Can  someone  give him  a copy,  please.
(Handing  the  witness  a document.  )

Q.  Look  at page  5.

A.  I have  it.
Q.  You  will  note  that  by clustering  the  seven  school  districts
together  there  is an  average  or  could  be an  average  of 8.6  minority
members?

A.  Yes, sir.
Q.  Is that  right?

A.  That  is true.
Q.  There  are  two  school  districts  that  have  22  per  cent

minority  members?

1Va218

A.  That’s  true.

Q.  This  is close  to your  minimum,  isn’t  it?

A.  Yes, it is.

Q.  Each  of  the  school  districts  have  at  least  some  minority

members,  don’t  they?

A.  Yes, they  do.

Q.  Would  it  be  your  recommendation  that  youngsters  be
moved  from  the  two  districts  that  have  22  per  cent  minority
members  in  order  to  diffuse  them  so  that  each  of these  districts
would  have  8.6  members?

[921]  Q.  You  pose  an  interesting  conundrum,  Mr.  Krasicky.
The  question  is  whether  or  not  a  school  district  with  nine  black
children  out  of 1850  ought  to continue  in that  fashion,  whether or
not  those  children  who  are  truly  in isolation  should  continue  to be
that  isolated.  The  other  question  is whether  or  not  the  1850  ought
to  be  isolated  as  whites  without  any  contact  with  black  children.
You  are  posing a question  which  is  a Solomon-type  question.  Am]
going  to  cut  my  child  down  the  middle  in order  that  each  parent
may  have  a portion.

I don’t  think  I know  the  answer  to  that.  I do know  that  I do
not  believe  a  white  community  and  white  school  districts,  as I
understand  them  and  know  them,  are  adequately  preparing  either
their  white  or  black  children  if  they  have  small  proportions  of
black  children  in that  district.

I feel  this  very  strongly.  I think  there  is a great  deal  of data to
support  that.  The  question  I think  really  needs  to be examined as
to  whether  or  not  that  district  should  divide  up  the  Mt.  Clemens
and  New  Haven  children  into  the  other  districts  now  or  whether
they  should  be  divided  over  a  period  of the  next  several  years  or
whether  or  not  the  change  in  that  community  would  mean  that
that  cluster  ought  to  be  brought  into  the  [922]  metropolitan
Detroit  area where  there  are  more  black  kids  who  could  be shared
with  them.  I want  to  remind  you  that  there  are  three  basic  plans
here.  The  common  thing  to  those  plans  seem  to  be  more
important  than  those  things  which  the  minor  areas  are  deficient  in.

Q.  I am  not  suggesting  you  pick  apart  the  plans,  I want to

understand  your  testimony  about  the  importance  of  black

IVa  219

youngsters  maintaining  their  black  identity.  I realize  we  have  some
competing  interests  and  I want  to understand  is it more  important
to  the  391  youngsters  at  New  Haven  and  the  1530 youngsters  at
Mt..  Clemens  for  them  to  retain  their  minority  identity  by
retaining  the  group  without  change  or  is it  more  important  to
diffuse  them  so  as  to  take  into  consideration  the  interest  of
perhaps  the 300  other  youngsters  in the other  districts.
How  do you  evaluate  these  competing  interests?
A.  Well,  I think one  of  the things  we might do if you  are
asking  for  alternatives  to  those  that  have  been  proposed,  an
alternative  might  be  to  examine  those  districts  very  carefully  and
to  withhold  the  position  which  would  dictate  that  they  do  the
distribution.  If we  were  to  have  gone  into  Livingston  County  or if
we  were  to  use  some  phraseology  that  was  used  earlier  there  was
some  discussion  of the  [923]  Mackinaw  Bridge.  At some  point  we
have  come  to  a  limitation  I grant  you  and  I would  point  out  that
No.  18 is putting  us  out  to the  limitations  but  I do want  to remind
you  that  I have  been  talking  about  are  we  going think  about  where
are  we  going  to  be  20  years  from  now;  the  Doxiadis  study,  the
growth  of the  metropolitan  area,  the  question  of a metropolitan
community  spreading  from  Port  Huron  leads  me  to  believe  this
question  which  you  are  posing  for me  is one  which  time  is going  to
have  some  considerable  impact  on  and  that  perhaps  it is wise  to set
up the structure  at this  point  in order  to deal  with  it.

Q.  I wonder  if I could  summarize  what  you  are  saying  this
way  that  while  you  are  concerned  with  the  minority  youngsters  in
the  five  school  districts  that  after  a  very  small  enrollment  of
minority  youngsters  you  would  not necessarily  solve their problem
by  moving  youngsters  from  the  other  two  districts  with  the
resultant  loss of identity  those  youngsters  have?

A.  If this  puts  me  in  conflict  with  the  other  members  of my

committee,  my  answer  to you  is, yes,  whether  it does  or  not.

[939]  FREEMAN  FLYNN,

resumed  the  stand  as  a  witness  herein,  and  having  been  previously
duly sworn,  testified  further  as  follows:

CROSS  EXAMINATION  (Continued)  BY  MR.  ROUMELL:

Q.  Dr.  Flynn,  I believe  we  came  to  the  end  of  yesterday”
where  we  were  talking  about  the  difference  in  the  perimeter
between  the  Board  of  Education’s  proposal  and  the  CCBE
proposal,  do you recall  that?

A.  Yes, sir.

Q.  To  refresh  our  memories  the  CCBE  proposal  is outlined  in
green  and  the  Board  of Education  is in orange  except  that  it leaves
out  the  two  clusters,  I believe  12 and  18?

A.  That  is correct.

[945]...

eke

Are you  aware  that  he  is no  longer  a member  of the  Board  of

Education?

A.  No,  sir,  he  is  no  longer  a  member  of  the  Board  of
Education.  He  is,  however,  an  influential  and  important  member
of our  community.

Q.  And  are  you  aware that  according  to  the  record  at  Page

13, that  he lived  at 5340  Kellen  Lanes,  Bloomfield  Hills?

A.  I know  that  Mr.  Stephens  lived  there,  yes,  sir.

Q.  Now,  I call your  attention  again  to PM-6,  and  I am  looking
at  the  upper  right-hand  corner  of  the  perimeters,  and I see  that
between  the  CBE  proposal  and  the  Detroit  Board,  minus  the
Mount  Clemens  Cluster,  there  is an  area  called  Utica  and  an  area
called  Shelby.  Is that  correct?

A.  Yes,  I see  it.

Q.  Now, did  the CBE  proposal  include  Utica?

A.  No, it did not.

Q.  Do you  believe  Utica  should  be included?

A.  Yes,  I do.

Would  you  like for me  to explain  why it was not  included?

IV a 223

Q.  Yes,  I would.

[946]  A.  The  CCBE  plan  started  with  a  premise  that  there
ought  to  be  an  outside  limit,  and  because  the  group  that  was
preparing  this  had  not  had  previous  experience  in  this  or  because
we  had  not  done  this  particular  kind  of analysis  before,  we  took
an  arbitrary  limit,  made  a  circle  from  the  center  of  the
community.  And  as  that  circle  extended  itself  we,  rather  than
making  an  arbitrary  line, of course,  we  followed  the district  which
existed.  Therefore,  we  get  that  puzzle  kind  of  character  of  the
outer  limits.  But  if you  do examine  that  line  you  will  see  that  it is
roughly  equidistant  at most  points.

Q.  It is roughly  what?
A.  It  is  roughly  equidistant  from  the  center  of  the  city  at

most  points.

And  in  the  distinction  between  that  plan  and  between  the
Detroit  plan,  as  we  went  further  and  as  individuals  familiarized
themselves  with  the  various  problems  that  occur  in trying 'to build
up such  a  plan,  it became  obvious  that  there  were  other  factors  —
which  had  been included  in the  CCBE  plan.  For instance,  location
of the  expressways  and  surface  routes  and  so  forth  —  and  we
introduced  the  concept  of  the  socio-economic  status,  the
combination  of  the  actual  [947]  demography  and  the
socio-economic  status  dictated  that  Utica  should  be included.

MR.  LUCAS  (Interposing):  I would  object  to  any  further
questions  along  this  line.  It goes  to the question  of whether  or  not
Mr.  Saxton  thinks  there  should  be  desegregation,  whether  he
thinks  there  would  be  any  benefit  to  it.  I think  that  the  issue  has
been  litigated  to  the  Supreme  Court  of the  United  States,  and  the
question  here  is  merely  a  matter  of plan,  not  whether  or  not  he
thinks  there  is any  benefits  to it.

[974]  MR.  SAXTON:  Excuse  me.  Your  Honor,  may  I note
that  on  October  4th  this  Court  held  a  meeting  between  various
counsel,  and  at that time  the  intervenors  were  not  in the  case.  And
a transcript  was  made  at the meeting  héld with  counsel.

During  the  course  of that  meeting  this Court  said, and  I quote

at Page  10 in the  transcript  taken  at that  meeting:

—

IV a 224

“I  would  remind  counsel,  as  I keep  reminding  myself in this
case,  that  our  objective  is quality.  education  for all children. |
am  not  concerned  about  the parties litigant  here.”

Now,  that  is the end of the  quote.

I have  assumed,  your  Honor,  as  counsel  for  the  client  that  |
represent,  that  that  is  the  primary  concern  of  this  Court  and  it
should  be  the  primary  concern  of all of us  as  citizens  of this  State
and  citizens  of  this  country.  Our  primary  concern  should  not  be
putting  children  on  the  bus  to  give  them  a  ride;  our  primary
concern  should  be  are  we  going  to  give  them  a quality  education.

In  that  regard,  what  we  are  attempting  to  do  is to  point  out
things  on  this  record  which  the  [975]  Court  may  well  take  into
account  in  measuring  whether  or  not  any  eventual  remedy
adopted  does  lead  to  a  quality  education.  If that  is no  longer  the
primary  concern  in  this  case,  and  it is so  indicated,  we  shall  stop
pursuing  questions  that  relate  to  the  quality  of  education  and
discontinue  that  line  of examination.

To  this  date  I have  not  seen  any  evidence  that  this  Court  has
retreated  from  its  statement  of  October  4th  and  its  real  and
abiding  concern  is  the  quality  of education  to  be  afforded  to  all
schools,  regardless  of their  color,  in this  country.

I think  these  questions  go to that  point.

MR.  LUCAS:  Your  Honor,  may  I respond?

That  is a  strawman  raised  by  counsel,  and  he  injects  the  new
political  curseword  of busing.  The  objective  here  is not  busing,  but
desegregation  of  the  schools,  ending  the  racial  segregation  of the
schools  and  the  community.  And  how  the  children  get to school  is
merely  a  matter  of providing  the transportation  if it is too  far  to
walk.

I think  he  betrays  his  interest  by referring  to busing  as  if that

is some  magical  thing  in  itself.  No  one  that  I know  of  has madea  ~
serious  brief,  [976]  although  there  is some  comment  that  busing  is
not  particularly  harmful,  that  it does  expose  people  to  do things,
but  that  that  is the  educational  tool,  it is really  the  vehicle  to get
there  by.  And  I think  everyone  has  agreed  that  that  is what  is at
the end  of the  line.

IV a 225

But  if  the  differential  is  that  desegregation  does  not  equal
what  counsel  may  deem  to  be  quality  education,  then  we  think  it
is  not  relevant.  He  is  trying  to  relitigate  Brown  v.  Board  of
Education.  I think  that  integration,  desegregation  of the  schools  is
the  first  requisite,  and  that  is the  legal  basis  for  this  case.  We  are
not  in  here  because  of  some  theoretical  basis.  And  arguments
about  SES  and  all  those  other  things  are  interesting,  but  the  legal
basis  for  this  Court’s  authority  and  for  our  Complaint  here  is the
segregation  of  racial  separation  of  students.

attending  a  school  district  on  account  of their a Now,  that  is
basically  what  desegregation  is.  We  are  now  talking  here  about

equality  of educational  opportunity  for  all  children  and  a quality
education.

[1085]  ROBERT  L. SMITH,

was called  as a witness  by Plaintiffs,  and being first  duly sworn  by

THE COURT:  Your  full  name,  sir?

THE  WITNESS:  Robert  L. Smith.

DIRECT  EXAMINATION  BY MR.  LUCAS:
Q.  Mr.  Smith,  what  is your  educational  background?
A.  I have  a  master’s  degree  from  the  University  of Michigan
in Civil Engineering,  specializing  in transportation  planning  and
have  completed  all  of  the  requirements  except  dessertation  of a
PhD degree.

Q.  In what  field?

A.  Civil  Engineering  and  specializing  in transportation.
Q.  Where  are  you  employed?

A.  I am  employed  with  the  Southeast  Michigan  Council  of

Governments.

Q.  Are  you  familiar  with  the TALUS  Study?
A.  Yes.  I am  a former  employee  of that  study.
Q.  I will  show  you  a  document  which  has  been  identified  as
Plaintiffs’  Exhibit  M  8 which  purports  to  be a  travel  time  taken
from  the  TALUS  Study.  Can  you  tell us  generally  what  travel  time
studies  are  as  used  in organizations  such  as  yours?  What  is it?  How
do  you  go  about  making  one  and  how  is  it  represented  on  the
chart  generally?

A.  Basically  for  the  purposes  of  regional  transportation
studies  we  develop  computer  representations  of  the  regional
highway  network  which  include  freeways,  major  arterials,  minor
arterials,  and even  local  streets  and  then  as  part of the developing  a
long  range  transportation  plan  we  utilize  home  interview  surveys
and  surveys  of  travel  speeds  on  the  existing  highway  network  to
come  up with  plans  for the  future  highway  plan.

Qa  Is  this a  commonly  accepted  tool in planning  in  general?
A.  That’s  right.
Q.  City  planning  agencies,  regional  planning  agencies  use  this

technique  as a regular  practice?

A.  That’s  right.

CROSS  EXAMINATION  BY  MR.  KRASICKY:

Q.  Was  the  survey  made  in the  spring  of  1969  or  fall  of  1969?

A.  Are  you  referring  to  the TALUS  Survey?

A.  The  TALUS  Study  came  up,  it was  done,  the  survey  home  -

interview  survey  was  made  in  1965  over  a  six  or  eight  month
period  and  then  a  transportation  network  was  developed,  a
highway  network  was  developed  representing  1965  and  another
one  was  developed  using  essentially  the  same  base  data  for  1975,
The  same  base  speeds  that  were  developed  in  1965  were  utilized
for  1975  with  the  inclusion  of the  additional  freeway  facilities  and
some  additional  major arterial  facilities.

were  taken  and studies  made  was  done  in  1965,  is that  correct?

district.

“Bring  with  you  all  records  for the past  two  years concerning
incidents  involving  damaged  property,  safety  of the  pupil  or  staff
whether  perpetuated  by  other  pupil  staffs  or  outsiders,  criminal
activities  or  fires  in  or  on  school  property  as  regard  each  school  in
the Detroit  Public  School  System.”

Mr.  Wells  has  responded  to  the  supoena.  He  is  in  court

presently.  Mr.  Wells  is sitting  in the second  row,  your  Honor.

However,  at  this  time  we  would  like  to  move  to  quash  this
subpoena  on  behalf  of  the  Board  of  Education  for  the  City  of
Detroit,  of Mr.  Wells.

Now, I will  be very  glad to hand  a copy  of the subpoena  up to

the Court,  if I may,  please.

store,  we  think  that  part  of the  subpoena  should  in

{1142]  But  I point  out  to the  Court  that  one  of the  things |

that’s  favorable  to  that  conclusion.  I submit  that’s  a  rather  novel

to  take  care  of  such  things  as  safety  problems,  community
relations  and so  forth.

Q.  Can  you  explain  in  sort  of  a  detailed  way,  Doctor,  how
you  went  about  making  the  clusters  that  are  on  the  exhibit.  Can
you  tell  us if you  tried  to follow  in any  degree  the clustering,  both
technique  and  the  actual  clusters,  suggested  in the  Detroit  Board
plan?

:

A.  My  major  objectives  were  to  take  into  account  the  time
[1166]  and  distance  involved  in  the  clusters,  of  transportation,
and  to  try  to  lessen  that  if  possible  and  achieve  the  greatest
desegregation  at  the  same  time,  the  greatest  amount  of
desegregation.

I started  out,  essentially  I think,  using the  Board’s  technique
of sort  of non-contiguous,  sometimes  contiguous  or  whatever  you
want  to  call  them,  skip  zone  clusters.  As I understand  the  Board’s
plan,  it was  developed  as  much  as  possible  along  thoroughfares  in
the  metropolitan  area  and/or  interstate  highways,  and  it  was
developed  with  this  in  mind  in an attempt  to make  maximum  use
of available  freeways  and  other  large  thoroughfares.

;

Q.  Is  skip  zoning  a  common  technique  in  desegregation
plans?  Although  this happens  to include  districts,  is it  a commonly
used device?

A.  Yes.  If you  have  a certain  geographical  region  that  you  are
trying  to  desegregate,  you  usually  have  a  buffer.  area  between
white  and  black  residents,  which  may  fall  anywhere  in  the  area.
But usually  in drawing  up  a  plan  you  start  with  this and  then  you
work  your way  out.  It’s sort  of difficult  to  describe,  but you  can  .
start in the center  and work  your  way  out equally  and arrive  at the
-extremes  of  both  ends,  or  you  can  do what  I think  [1167]  this
would be called, skip clustering,  and  keep  your  distance  the same
as you work your way out each side.

Q.  Doctor,  except  in this  case,  using districts  and  high school
constellations,  is  this  not generally  characterized  non-coritiguous
zoning?

,

A.  Yes.

wh

use  them  in  the  perimeters?

A.  Well,  except  for  the  Mount  Clemens  —

May  I step  to the  map?

went  around  the  perimeter  of  the  city  in  a  counterclockwise
*  rotation,  and  did  use  the  School  Board’s  plan  of clusters  as  sort  of

a base.

.

would  increase  it or  decrease  it.

A.  That’s  correct.  For  example,  to  illustrate  this,  a  distance
noted  from  Joy  Junior  High  School  to  Chippewa  Valley  School,
which  is just  short  of  the  40-minute  blue  line,  was  noted  as  5?
minutes  of travel  time;  21  miles.  On  the  basis  that  the  inclusion  or
exclusion  of  Chippewa  Valley  would  not  serious  impinge  on  our
desegregation  effort,  we  made  a decision  to exclude  it.

The  same  is  true  of  the  district  immediately  to  the  west  of

as  16 miles  from  Utica  to Kettering  and  31  minutes  —  but I believe
that’s  maybe  a  misprint,  because  on  the  next  page  the  same  run  is

40-minute  TALUS  line.

And  on  this  basis  Utica  was  excluded  from  our  proposal.

I might  give  two  more  examples  to  the  immediate  west,  of
Utica,  where  you  have  Bloomfield,  West  Bloomfield  and  Avondale.

Q.  That  would  be Cluster  8.
A.  Right.  Only  Avondale  is  excluded,  and  Bloomfield  and
West  Bloomfield  are  included.  This  was  a  matter  of judgment  on
our  part.  The  blue  40-minute  line  from  TALUS  runs  pretty  much
through  the  center  of the  West  Bloomfield  area  and  to  the  north
of Bloomfield.  However,  if you  will  look  at Cluster  8, without  the
inclusion  of  Bloomfield  Hills  and  West  Bloomfield  the  cluster
would  be  disproportionate  in  percentage  black  to  our  total
metropolitan  area.  And  so  on  that  basis  we  decided  to  include
those  two  districts.

A.  Yes.

A.  That is correct.

around  the  city  with  the same  sort  of judgment  either  including or

[1177]  Q.  Thot  was  the  substance  of  your  testimony  with

‘

gespect  to the  Detroit  plan we  presented,  is that  correct?

A. I believe  it was,  yes,  sir.

Q.  Doctor,  the  CCBE  plan  excludes,  I gather,  more  or  less
permanently  Grades  K  through  4  from  a  desegregation  point  of
view.  Would  you  support  that  position?

A.  I think  I have  testified  previously  that  I would  not  and
‘  research  indicates  that  the  inclusion  of  early  grades  is  very

important  in the  total  desegregation  effort.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  do  you  know  of any  administrative  obstacles
to implementation  of K through  6 in September  of the proposal  as
suggested  to the Court,  M-10  and  M-11,  that  is PM-10  and  11?

A.  Well,  there  are  obviously  administrative  to  anything  you
do.  I think  it  is  a  question  of judgment  on  whether  or  not  an
operation  like  this  could  be  foreclosed  by  September.  In  my
opinion  | think  it  probably  could  without  examining  in  close
detail  some  of  the  transportation  facilities  available  and  this  sort
of  thing.  I would  guess  roughly  this  would  involve,  I believe,
something  like  400,000  or 420,000  children.

Q.  That  would  be the  total  enrollment  in grades  K through  6

in this grouping?

A.  Very  roughly,  yes.  This  would  be  simply  taking a [1178]
division  of the  total  number  into  the  grade  and  presumedly  if we
used  a  ball  park  figure  which  seems  to work  out rather
consistently  in  city  desegregation  of around  40  percent  plus  or
minus  transportation,  this  would  run  out  at  in  the  neighborhood
of  160,000  to  170,000  children  who  would  need  transportation.
I haven’t  examined  the  capability  of the  metropolitan  area  to

support  this sort  of transportation  by September.  This  factor  —

Q.  You  have  not  examined  that  factor  at  all,  is that  correct?
A.  That  is  right.  Assuming  that  this  factor  could  be  taken

not be taken  care  of by September.

A.  Yes.

Q.  Have  you  ever  worked  out,  before,  Dr.  Foster,  in  your
professional  capacity,  any  kind  of  an  inter-district  plan  where
there  was  a  desegregation  plan  that  involved  two  previously
separate  school  districts?

A.

Well,  I have  worked  on  many  reorganization  plans  but
none  involving  desegregation  as  a  major  factor;  I have  worked  on
some  plans  in  Pennsylvania  in  the  last  five  [1245]  years  in which
desegegration  was  one  factor  but  not  the  major  factor.

Q.  Were  those  plans  that  involved  a  multitude  of  school

districts?

A.  In  some  cases  they  involved  districts  joining  or  being  what
we  call  school  district  reorganization,  where  several  districts  would
join  into  one  new  district,  this  sort  of thing.

[1268]  Q.  You  think  that  makes  a difference.

*e*

And  if you  were  working  on  a desegregation  plan,  would  that
be  one  of the  factors  you  would  want  to look  at before  you  made
your  ultimate  determination  as  to  where  pupils  were  to  wind  up?

A.  Well,  I think  the  three  plans  I reviewed  —  I did  not  go into

finance  at all in considering  our  proposal,  plaintiff’s  proposal.

As  I remember,  the  three  plans  that  I have  reviewed  did go
into  this,  not  in  complete  detail  but  in  some  general  suggestions.

For  example,  some  of  them,  I believe,  suggested  that  the
millage  rate  for  the  total  metropolitan  area  that  is affected  by a
plan  should  approach  the  highest  millage  rate  of  any  of  the
systems,  I believe  is the  way I read  it.

I think  another  possibility,  as  an  interim  measure  for finance,
might  be  something  like  a charge  of so  much  per  student,  so  much
per  head.  I think  there  are  various  combinations  of things.

Q.  Would  it be a voucher-type  system  or  -

A.  (Interposing):  No,  not  exactly.  No.  But  the  sort  of system
you  have  when  you  send  a  pupil  across  district  lines to  ——
city or  something  and  you  pay  his  [1269]  tuition.

IV a 253

Q.  Well,  you  treat  him:  the  same  way  you  would  as a tuition

pupil,  in other  words?

A.  Yes.

be considered,  yes,  sir.

Q.  Do  you  think  it  would  be  necessary  to  work  those  out

before  you  could  actually  implement  a plan?

A.  I think  this  would  be  not  so  much  a  problem  of  the  plan
maker,  as  of  the  governing  boards  of  the  area  that  was  set  up.
Whenever  the  final  decision  was  about  what  the  nature  of  the
governing  board  would  be,  this  would  be,  I think,  one  of  their
tasks.

which  certainly  [1270]  would  be a factor.

Q.  Dr.  Foster,  Mr.  Saxton  made  mention  of the  fact  that  no
one  has  ever  done  a  plan  for  a  district,  desegregation  plan  for

time in school  systems  for groups  of  students  that size?

not  with  a  700,000  odd  children  plan,  but  you  are  doing it cluster
by cluster,  yes.

proposal,  CCBE  proposal  or  the  Plaintiff’s  proposal  to  involve

clusters  or zones,  and  would  have  the same  general  situation.

involved  with  the number  of pupils and  the number  of buildings in

has been
no  significant  achievement  [1469]  for  black  minority  people  as a

orders.

metropolitan  districts  in the tricounty  area were  created  and

One,  is the  Court’s  statement  in  connection  with  its ruling,
that  it would  not  adopt  an  intra-Detroit  plan,  wherein  the  Court
noted  that if the present  school  districts  or boundaries  were  to be
[1470]  drawn  today  the Court  did not think  that  they could
withstand  constitutional  scrutiny.  That,  coupled  with the fact that
in the case  of Spencer  v.  Kugler,  which  was affirmed  by the
Supreme  Court  in January  of  this year,  the three-judge  panel  in
Federal  District  Court  noted  in that  case that  it  was a  matter  of
some  relevance  that all of the school  districts  there involved  were
established  long  before  the racial  patterns  had  changed  that
resulted  in the predominance  of black  citizens  in  a  certain
community.  We  think,  in  view  of  this  Court’s  statement  and in
view  of  the  finding  in  that  case,  that  information  would be
relevant.

We also  propose,  your  Honor,  to submit  —  and again  we think
this could be by way of written  proof  in  the form  of affidavits a
exhibits  —  information  as to the classroom  capacity  presently
existing  in the various  metropolitan  districts  and the number  of
students  occupying  that  classroom  capacity.  We  think  that is not
relevant  from  the standpoint  of  our legal argument,  but  from th
standpoint  of  making  sure that the Court does have all of the facts
in front  of it before  it makes  whatever  ruling  it intends  to make.

Lastly,  your Honor, is the matter of the subpoena  which we

Rights  Act  of  1964  and  43  U.S.C.  1983,  the  statutes  by virtue of
which
we are before  the  Court  and have  been  since  August  of
1970,  are  not  statutes  that  authorize  this Court  to sit in judgment
of sound  educational  quality  as  perceived  by  Dr.  Armor  of Mr.
Saxton  or  their  other  proffered  witnesses.  The  remedy  for  illegal
racial  school  segregation,  is school  desegregation.  The  amount  and
nature  of  school  desegregation  does  not  turn  on  non-legal
educational  considerations  concerning  the  racial  mixtures  that Dr.
Armor  or  others  of his associates  may  find  suitable  for themselves.
It  is a  legal  requirement,  your  Honor,  which  Mr.  Lucas,  if I read
the transcript  correctly,  has repeatedly  asserted  and  been  sustained
with  respect  to in this court.

Lastly,  your  Honor,  we  think  that  the  offer  of  proof  with

respect  to  the  incidents  of violence  in  Detroit  is not  worthy of
judicial  cognizance.  If  I understood  Mr.  Saxton  correctly,  th
incidents  of  violence  in  Detroit  make  such  schools  sone
i
suitable  for children in  Detroit  and  not  elsewhere.

The  point,  your  Honor,  is  that  this  [1475]  Court  has
previously  found that the illegal  segregation  within and of Detroit
requires  Fourteenth  Amendment  remedies and we  think  that is the
end of the inquiry.

For those  reasons,  your  Honor,  as indicated,  we  object  to Mr.

data or forego  the opportunity.

[1486]

arises  from the evidence

Our decision  to proceed  with  the metropolitan  rem
Sup

in this case  and from  what  the

IV a  275

Court  has  said  and  done  in school  desegregation  cases  that  it has
considered.

What  we  have  said is not  to be taken  to mean  that our view of
the law is infallible.  We do not  claim  to have  perfect  legal vision.  If
we  have erred  in our  view  of the law  those  who  are  opposed  to our
exposition  of it already,  through  their  pleadings,  have  the  benefit
of it.

Our  appellate  procedures  are  designed  to deliver  litigants  from
the evil  of trial  court  error  whenever  it occurs.  Parenthetically  we
might  note  that  in  looking  to  a  metropolitan  solution  for  our
school  problems  we  are  doing  nothing  new  and  nothing  more  than
the people  in the  greater  Detroit  area  have  done  for years  and  are
doing now.

Local  units  of government  in the  metropolitan  area  have  not
hesitated  to  joint  together  for  the  purpose  of  providing  better
solutions  to problems  confronting  them.  In such  instances various
units  of government  have  either  disregarded  local  boundaries  or
have  concluded  that  the  problems  were  such  as  to  call  for  a
metropolitan  solution.  In  some  cases  [1487]  they  have  created
overlay  organizations.

An  example  of such  is SEMCOG  and  recreational  authorities
come  to  mind.  Only  recently  the  press  carried  reports  of  a
tri-county  sewage  system  whose  current  expansion  is  being
concentrated  in the counties  of Wayne,  Oakland  and  Macomb.  It is
expected  that  it will  eventually  be  expanded  to  part  of Monroe,
Washtenaw  and  St.  Clair  Counties.  It  is  reported  that  when
completed  the  project  will  serve  72 communities  and  more  than  3
million  people.

The  Detroit  Water  System,  for  example,  is a  metropolitan
system  providing  service  to  municipalities  beyond  the  City  of
Flint.

In the  field  of health  the  Emergency  Care Commission  of the
Greater  Detroit  Area  Hospital  Council  has  lately  presented  a plan
calling  for  32  area  hospitals  to  provide  service  in emergencies  in
the metropolitan  area.

These  steps  for  meeting  metropolitan  problems  by  calling
upon  metropolitan  resources  in  instances  mentioned  where  it
does not  appear  that  the  protection  of basic  constitutional  rights

US.,  at pages  178 and  179.

“We  think  the  following  principles  have been established by
them  and have  become  settled  doctrines  of this court,  to be
acted  [1489]  upon  wherever  they  are  applicable.  Municipal
corporat-ons  are  political  subdivisions  of the State,  created as
convenient  agencies  for  exercising  such  of  the governmental
powers  of  the State  as may be entrusted  to them. For  the
purpose  of  executing  these  powers  properly  and  efficiently
they  usually  are given the  power  to acquire,  hold,  and manage
personal  and real property.  The number,  nature  and duration
of the powers  conferred  upon  these corporations  and the
territory  over  which  they  shall  be exercised  rests in th
absolute  discretion
their charters,  nor
of the State. Neither
any law conferring  governmental  powers, or vesting in them
purposes,  or authorizing
property  to be used for governmental
them  to  hold or manage  such property,  or exempting them
from  taxation  upon it, constitutes  a  contract  with the State
within  the meaning  of the Federal Constitution.  The State,
or withdraw  all such
therefore,  at  its pleasure  may  modify

IV a 277

All  this  may  be  done,  conditionally

powers,  may  take  without  compensation  such  property,  hold
it  itself,  or  vest  it  in  other  agencies,  expand  or  contract
{1490}  the  territorial  area,  unite  the  whole  or  a  part  of  it
with  another  municipality,  repeal  the  charter  and destroy  the
or
corporation.
unconditionally,  with  or  without  the  consent  of the  citizens,
or even  against  their  protest.  In all  these  respects  the State  is
supreme,  and  its legislative  body,  conforming  its action  to the
state  constitution  may  do  as  it  will,  unrestrained  by  any
provision  of the  Constitution  of the  United  States.  Although
the  inhabitants  and  property  owners  may  by  such  changes
suffer  inconvenience,  and  their  property  may  be lessened  in
value  by  the  burden  of increased  taxation,  or  for  any  other
reason,  they  have  no  right  by contract  or  otherwise  in  the
unaltered  or  continued  existence  of  the  corporation  or  its
powers,  and  there  is nothing in the Federal  Constitution  which
protects  them  from  these  injurious  consequences.  The  power
is in the  State  and  those  who  legislate  for  the State  are  alone
responsible  for any  unjust  or  oppressive  exercise  of it.”
As  I indicated  earlier  in this  set  [1491]  of hearings  what  we
are  about  is  drafting  and  implementation  of  a  plan  of
desegregation  in  the  metropolitan  area.  Now,  more  particularly
and  specifically  to  the offer of proof I make  the  following  rulings:
With  respect  to  the  proffered  or  offered  testimony  of David
Armor,  his deposition  may  be  taken  at  a  place  to be agreed  upon
by the parties,  absent  which  it  is to  be taken  in the  City  of
Detroit.

Upon  the  Court’s  perusal  of  his  testimony  if  it  becomes
evident  that his testimony  should  be taken  orally  in court  on  those
aspects  of his  deposition  as  may  be  pertinent  to  our  proceeding
the Court  will order it done.

The  intervenors  may  present  affidavits  as  they have  suggested
they  would  like  to  do  on  the  history  of  the  various  possibly
affected  suburban  school  districts,  the  capacity  data  they
mentioned  and  the  historical  and  statistical  data having  to do with
transportation.

By doing  this  the  new  intervenors  will  have  the  benefit  of a
record  in  support  of  their  contentions  and  it will  provide  a  base

IV a 278

for  them  upon  which  to  ask  the  Court  ot  make  appropriate
findings  and  conclusions.

[1492]  The  motion  to  quash  the  subpoena  is granted  and |

would  like  to  have  your  views,  gentlemen,  on  how  much  time you
want  in  which  to  prepare  proposed  findings  and  conclusions  and
proposed  order  of desegregation.  Perhaps  the  Plaintiffs  can  speak
first.

MR.  FLANNERY:  Thank  you,  your  Honor.  Not  longer  than
15 days  after  the  close  of the record  and  we  would  like  to suggest
that  if the  record  does  not  close  tomorrow  for  the  reason  that
affidavits  may  be  presented  thereafter  or  that  Dr.  Armor’s
deposition  may  be  taken  some  place  that  a  terminal  date  as  brief
and  as  fast  as practicable  can  be set by the  Court  for the  closing of
the record.

In  behalf  of  Plaintiffs  we  would  ask  for  no  longer  than  15

-
days after  such  a date.

THE COURT:  Mr.  Roumell?

MR.  ROUMELL:  We have  no  objection,  15 days is fine.

THE COURT:  Mr.  Krasicky?
MR.  KRASICKY:  We  would  ask  for a minimum  of 20 days.
The  Court  is  under  great  pressure  from  the  Sixth  Circuit,  we
appreciate  this.  We have  done  this  at a great  speed  and  we  must do
this  throughtfully  and  we  ask for a minimum  of 20 days.

[1493]  THE  COURT:  Mr.  Sachs?

MR.  SACHS:  Fifteen  days  suggested  by  Mr.  Flannery  from
the  point  he indicates  would  be agreeable.  We would  also  ask leave
either  for  oral  argument  or  for  the  submission  of  briefs  o
memorandum  as well.

THE COURT:  Mr.  Saxton?

MR.  SAXTON:  We  would  concur  in  either  of the  proposals

either  made  by Mr.  Krasicky  or  Mr.  Flannery.

THE  COURT:  Tell  me  this  on  that  point,  do  you  think the |

deposition  of David  Armor  can  be taken in  a week?

MR.  SAXTON:  I do  not  and  may  I explain  why  not? We !

would  propose  to  take  the  deposition  in, I believe  it is Cambridge
,
or  Boston.  The  reason  it cannot,  in having  spoken with Dr.

IV a 279

his  study  is recently  concluded.  He  is under  a commitment  with
the publisher  who  is going  to publish  that  that  it will  not  be made
public  until  they  put  it in manuscript  form  which  will  not  be for
several  days..He  is under  a  contractural  agreement  with  them  and
has been  approached  by the  United  States  Senate  to testify  before
their  committee and cannot  do  so  immediately  for  the  same
[1494]  reason.

As soon as he is released  from  that  and  he  indicated
to us he
thought  that  would  be  about  the  first week  in May then  he is free
to reveal  the  information.  We would  not  suggest  to this Court  that
you  delay  anything  that  you  feel  is  necessary  to  expedite  this
proceeding  in order  for us  to take  the deposition.

We  would  request  that  we  be permitted  to file the deposition
when taken.  We would  take  it at the very  first opportunity and we
would  be  happy  to  submit  an  affidavit  from  Dr.  Armor  as  to  his
availability.  He  is physically  available  but  he  is under  a legal  and
contractual  commitment  which  precludes  him  revealing  the
information  which  we  would  like  to take  in the  deposition  until
his  publishers  have  had  an  opportunity  to  put  it  in  manuscript
form.

THE  COURT:  So  far  as  the  affidavits  on  the matters  we have
mentioned  is there  any  reason  why you  couldn’t  have  those  within
a week?

MR.  SAXTON:  I would  hope  we  could  have  those  within  a

week.

THE COURT:  Mr. Condit?
MR.  CONDIT:  I think  we  can  comply  with  [1495]  the  time
limits  discussed.  I did  ask  Dr.  Armor  to  put  a  paragraph  in  his
letter  relative  to  timing  and  this  letter  has  been  attached  to  our

There  is one  other  point  Mr.  Saxton  did not mention.  Before

that must  be  received  from  the  people  that  conducted  these  five
Hot and they just can’t  be obtained  until  perhaps  the early part

y.
MR.  WEST:  We  would  have  no  difficulty  meeting  the  15 day

proposed  period.

Q.  Mr.  Henrickson,  have  you had an opportunity  to review

Plaintiff's  proposal  for a metropolitan plan?

plan.

point  of view

whether  you have any objection  to the Plaintiffs’  clustering plan’

A. The clustering  plan as presented  by the Plaintiff  is in many

IV a%81

[1521]  ...  MERLE E. HENDRICKSON

Q.  Whether  the Court  adopts  the Plaintiff's  plan  or the
Detroit  plan, if students  are going to be moved in school  buses  on
expressways,  interchanges  in rush periods,  this is going  to cause
delays regardless  of which plan is adopted?

[1522]  A.  Unless  they can be scheduled

to avoid  the rush

periods,  yes.

Thank  you.
MR. KRASICKY:

MR.  SAXTON:  Your  Honor,  that  provokes  one  question  on

my part,  if I may ask it from this point.  I’m  sorry.

What  did you consider  the rush periods,  Mr. Henrickson?

A. I was  speaking  principally  of  the  morning,  thinking
principally  of  the morning  rush  periods,  that  period  when the
traffic  coming  into Detroit  to arrive  at  8:00  or 8:30 or at  9:00
backs  up  along some  of the freeways  and thoroughfares  coming
into the city.

The evening  rush  hour traffic  would  generally  not affect  the
school  transportation,  because  the  school  transportation  would
normally  be completed  before  the evening  rush hour traffic.

MR.  SAXTON:  Did you ever drive  on  the north  bound  Lodge

Expressway  at three o’clock  in the afternoon?

A.  Yes, I have.

MR.  SAXTON:  What  happens  at the Ford interchange  and the
Davison  interchange  at three  o’clock  in the afternoon?  Pretty
crowded,  isn’t it?

{1525}...
Q.  Mr.  Henrickson,  you were  here  when Dr. Rankin  testified,

were you not?

A.  Yes.

Q.  I take  it, then, in your response  to Judge  Roth,  you
believe  that Grosse  Ile and Trenton  should  be included,  for the
same reason  as Dr. Rankin?

A. Yes, I do.

>

IV a 282

Q.  And  do  you  take  that  view  on  Plymouth,  the  Plymouth

School  District?

A.  It’s important  but  not  as critical  as  it is for Trenton-Grosge
Ile.  There  is a very  critical  problem  of finding  school  districts  of
high  SES in the downriver  community.  This is one of the  current
problems.

Q.  What  would  you  estimate  the distance  from  Southwester

High School  to Trenton  would  be, as you  have  it?

A.  The distance  is something  like in the range of  10,  12 miles,

and  it is along a freeway.

MR.  ROUMELL:  Fine.  Thank  you  very  much.

MR.  SAXTON:  Yes.  Pursuant  to  the  direction  of this  Court
on  April  13, we  did  on  May  22, take  the  testimony  by deposition

State  defendants.  The  deposition  was  completed,  has  been  filed

that deposition.

Mr.  Beer  for  the Detroit  Board  of Education  was  also present.
We  propose  to offer  that  deposition  in evidence  at  such  time

as permitted  by the Court.

evidence?

make  our position  clear.

having  to  do  with  the  offer  of  proof  the  deposition  will  be
received  on  a separate  record  of the  intervening  defendant.

MR.  LUCAS:  As an  offer  of proof?
THE COURT:  Yes.
MR.  LUCAS:  We have  no  objection  as an  offer  of proof.
THE  COURT:  That  is the manner  in which  it is received.
MR.  CONDIT:  If  the  Court  please  we  would  join  in  Mr.
Saxton’s  motion  relative  to  Dr.  Armour’s  deposition.  We  have  no
other  matters  to bring before  the Court.

THE COURT:-Mr.  West?
MR. WEST:  We will  follow  the  same  course.

'

[1723]  MR.  SAXTON:  We  would  like  to  note  for the record  |

an  exception  to the  Court’s  refusal  to  receive  the  deposition  jn  _
evidence  and in the alternative  receiving  it in the nature  of an offer
of proof.  Not  knowing  when  the  transcript  and  the accompanying

classified

as induced  or forced  integration.

We  have  had already

in this case
a great  deal of  testimony
from  expert  witnesses  called  on  behalf  of  the  plaintiffs  and
educational  experts  called  on  behalf  of the  Detroit  Board  as to the

deposition,  well  qualified  as  an  expert.  This  testimony  is the same
ilk  and  goes  to  the  same  general  subject  matter  as  that  already

does  not  take  away  from  its relevance.

We  submit  it  is  error  not  to  receive  it  in  evidence  and  we

would  object  to  the  limitation  that  it  is received  as  an  offer of
proof.

THE COURT:  Your position  is noted.

Act No. 306  of  the  Public  Acts  of  1919,  as  amended  by  Act  No.  387,  P.  A.  of  1921,
Act  No.  111  P.  A.  of  1929,  Act  No.  128,  P.  A.  of  1931,  Act  No,  188,  P.  A.  of  19387,
Act  No.  268,  P.  A.  of  1939  and  Act  No.  57,  P.  A.  of  1948;  entitled:
“An  act  to  define,  regulate  and  license  real  estate  brokers,  real  estate  salesmen,
including  within  the  term  real  estate  broker.  also  those  who  act  as  real  estate
appraisers,  real  estate  mortgage  brokers,  building  job  brokers,  business
chance  brokers,  and  those  who,  as  owners  or  otherwise,  engage  in  the
sale  of  real  estate  as  a  principal  vocation,  and  persons  employed  by
any  of  them  as  salesmen,  and  to  provide  a  penalty  for  a  viola-
tion  of  the  provisions  hereof.”

CODE  OF  ETHICS

mange ng tag law  and  the  rules  and  regulations  promul-

should  be  made  in  writing  and  should  be  complete  and  exact.
Article  34.  A  realtor  should  never  be  instrumental  in  introducing
into  a  neighborhood  a  character  of  property  or  occupancy,  members  of
any  race  or  nationality,  or  any  individuals  whose  presence  will  clearly
be detrimental  to  property  values  in  that  neighborhood.
Article  35.  No  instructions  nor  inducements  from  any  client  or  cus-
tomer  relieve  the  realtor  from  his  responsibility  strictly  to  observe  this
Code  of Ethics.

INTRODUCTION

This  bulletin  is prepared  and  published  by the  Michigan
Department  of Education  as directed  by Act  306, Public  Acts
of  1937,  as  amended.  It  is  the  intent  *of  this  bulletin  to
furnish  school  plant  planners  with  a source  of information  as
to  the  planning  process  and  the  necessary  procedural
methods  for processing  school  projects.

The  bulletin,  as  developed,  concerns  a resume of school
building  planning:  recommendations  of  the  Department  of
Education  as  to  site,  general  building  design,  and  to  specific
instructional  areas;  and,  the  approval  standards  for  review  by
health  and  fire safety  authorities.

It  is  hoped  that  this  presentation  format  will  make
necessary  information  most  readily  available  to  both  public
and  non-public  school  authorities.

John  W.  Porter

Acting  Superintendent

of Public  Instruction

[1xa280]

Care  in  site  location  must  be  taken  if  a
serious  transportation  problem  exists  or
if  housing  patterns  in  an  area  would  re~-
sult  in  a  school  largely  segregated  on
racial,  ethnic,  or  socio-economic  lines.

Va22

935.  Natural  Physical  Protection.

The  geographical
position  of a  location  may afford reliable  protection  from adverse
influences.  If a  location lies in the middle of an  area well developed
with a uniform  type of residential  properties, and if the  location is
be used for business
away  from main arteries  which would logically
purposes,  probability  of a change in type, use, or occupancy  of prop-
offered  to
erties at this location is remote.  ‘The degree of immunity
a  location  because  of its  geographical  position  within  the  city is to
be considered.  Natural  or  artificially  established  barriers  will  prove
a neighborhood  and  the  locations  within  it
effective  in  protecting
from adverse  influences.  Usually the protection  from adverse in-
fluences afforded by these  means  includes  prevention  of the  infiltra-
tion of business and industrial  uses, lower class occupancy,  and in-
racial groups,  A location close to a public park or area
harmonious
of similar nature is usually well protected  from infiltration  of busi-
ness and lower social occupancy  coming  from that direction.  Hills
and ravines  and other peculiarities  of  topography  often  make en-
croachment  of inharmonious  uses so difficult  that protection is af-
forded.  A high speed traffic artery or a  wide street parkway  may
prevent  the expansion  of  inharmonious  uses to a  location on the
opposite  side of the street.  However,  if a  high speed traffic artery
passes directly through a  desirable neighborhood  area with similar
development  on each side of the artery,  the noise and attendant
danger constitute an adverse  influence, rather than a  protection.
The same holds true for the presence  of railroads,  elevated or surface
lines, and other means  of transportation.

=

_
f

e

951.  Quality  and  Accessibility  of Schools,  When  con-

sidering  the question of schools, distances  to the schools should be
related  to the public or private means  of  transportation  available
from the location to  the schools.  The social class of  the parents of
children  at the  school  will  in many  instances  have  a direct  bearing.
of a neighborhood  area  may  be favor-
Thus, physical surroundings
able and conducive  to enjoyable,  pleasant  living in its location.  How-
ever,  if the children  of people  living  in such  an  area  are  compelled
to  attend  school  where  the  majority  or  a  considerable  number  of
the pupils represent  o  far lower level of society  or an incompatible
racial  element,  the  neighborhood  under  consideration  will  prove  far
less  stable  and  desirable  than  if this  condition  did  not  exist.  Fre-
quently, upon payment  of a fee, children in such an  area could  attend
another  school  with  pupils  of  their  same  social  class.  However,
desirability  of the  neighborhood,  when  compared  with  competitive
locations,  might  be adversely  affected  by the  additional  expense.  In
many instances where a  school has earned «  prestige through the
of instruction  and  adequacy  of  facilities,  it  will  be  found
that  such  attributes  will  be  an  element  in  maintaining  the  desir-
ability  of the entire area comprising  the school district.  In cases
where schools  are not immediately  present.  consideration  is given to
convenience  and  cost  of required  transportation.

ity

Va23

980 (3). Recorded  restrictive  covenants  should strengthen
and  supplement  zoning  ordinances  and  to  be  really  effective  should
The  restrictions  should  be  re-
include  the  provisions  listed  below.
\  corded  with  the  plat,  or  imposed  as  a  blanket  encumbrance  against
all  lots  in the  subdivision,  and  should  run  for  a  period  of  at  least
twenty-five  to  thirty  years.  Recommended  restrictions  should  in-
clude  provision  for  the  following:

a. Allocation  of definite  areas for specific  uses such as single
or two-family  houses,  apartments,  and business structures
so they will have  adequate  light
and air with assurance  of  a  space  of at least  ten feet
between  buildings

b.  The placement  of buildings

c.  Prohibition  of the  resubdivision  of lots
d.  Prohibition  of the erection  of  more than one dwelling  per

lot

¢.  Control  of  the  design  of  all  buildings,  by requiring  their
approval  by  a  qualified  committee,  and  by  appropriate
cost  limitations  or  minimum  square  foot ground floor areas

980

f. Prohibition  of  nuisances  or  undesirable  buildings  such  as
pig pens, temporary  dwellings,
g.  Prohibition  of  the occupancy  of  properties  except  by the

stables,

and high fences

race for which  they are intended

Boundary  Adjustment

A  third  generai  means  frequently  suggested  for  integrating  schools  is  to
change  boundaries  in  order  to  achieve  more  inclusive  districts  with
present  schools.
and  maintained  by
1
assumed  that  the  reverse  effects  can  equally  be  accomplished  by  boundary

On  the  assumption  that  segregation  has  been  achieved

of  district  boundaries,  it  is

tinui

tion,

ad

d

hi

es  of  integration  have  generally  recognized  that
Mae
much  of  the  present  de  facto  segregation  is  inherent  in  present  residential
patterns  in  Detroit.
There  are  a  few  marginal  communities  where  school
district  boundaries  can  in  some  measure  affect  the  enrollment  of  a  school.
Possibilities  of  creating  inclusive  districts  are  somewhat  better  on  the
secondary  school  level  than  on  the  clementary.

Since  much  of  the  local  public  transportation  system  operates  in  long  runs
particularly  on  the  east-west  mile  roads,  it  is  possible  to  develop  a  plan
for  secondary  schools  to  serve  students  from  greatly  elongated  districts
along  these  coach  lines.
The  relative  success  of  the  Murphy,  the  Coffey
and  the  Mettetal  junior  high  schools  in  attracting  students  under  the  “Open
Schools"  plan  is  due  to  their  convenient  locations  near  the  Fenkell  and
Seven  Mile  coaches.

There  are,  consequently,  a  few  situations  in  Detroit  where  creation  of
elongated  senior  high  school  districts  along  major  public  transportation
achi
lines  would  serve  effectively  to
a  better  balanced  student  com-

position  than  at  the  present  time.

Southwestern  enrollment  now.

in  setting  up  elongated  senior  high  districts  for  Cody  and  Mackenzie
along  the  coach  lines.
