at tons
Recoomend

1.

The  committee  recommends  first  that  while  gondiscriminstory  policy  of  the
Board  has  been  in  existence  since  1955,  the  Board  has  the  obligation  to
clarify  for  the  public  as  well  as  its  administrative  staff  that  all  phases
of  personnel,  either  "recruitment,  assignment,  transfer,  or  promotion  of
any  end  all  employees  of  the  Board  will  be  accomplished  without  racial
discrimination.”
Secondly,  that  the  Board  “accept  its  obligation  not  only  to  have  non~-
discriminatory  policies  and  procedures  but  affirm  that  it  will  do  every-
thing  necessary  and  possible  to  achieve  equal  employment  opportunity  for
all  personnel.”

(The fact that the Superintendent  continued until June,  1963 the practice  of
offering  a  choice  of  three  schools  for  the  assignment  of  new  teachers  is  indices
tive  of  the  latitude  permitted  by  the  previous  policy.)

:

\

ae

Va50

The  committee  recommends  that  “assignment  .
must  take  priority  over  any  other  consideration"  and  that  the  Superintendent
and  the  personnel  division  clarify  the  policy  and  standard  by which  all
personnel  transactions  will  be  made  and  judged.

.  without  regard  to  race

.

(The  Superintendent  has  acknowledged  that  in  inner  city  schools  the  previous
“three  year  new  teacher  assignment  rule"  has  frequently  been  waived  in  the
interest  of  "stability.”)
The  committee  recommends  that  “assignments  be  made  on  the  basis  of  need  at
the  convenience  and  judgment  of  the  Board,  and  that  factors  unrelated  to
preparation  such  as  residency  or  personal  preference  which  would  deny
Opportunity  based  on  race  cannot  be  given  priority  in  decisions  relating
to  assignment.

in  view  of  the  magnitude  of  personnel  transactions  involving  separations,

If  the  work  of  the  Commission  on  Community  Relations  is  to  procead,  then  an
honest  effort  must  be  made  to effect  attitudes  of  Detroiters  towards  each
other.
The  fact  that  44,000  elementary  school  children  can  have  a  public
school  learning  experience  that  offers  no  opportunity  to  see,  to  meet,  to
work  with,  or be taugit  by a  teacher  who  happens  to be of  another
race,  makes
@  travesty  out  of  th  learning  process.

the  development  of  democratic  attitudes  as  no  other  action  could.

community  would  have  to  come  to  grips  with  reality  in  «  school  system  where
a  Negro  could  teach  anywhere.

days  after  service  of  this  request,  admit  that  the  state-

ments  set  forth  below  aré  true  for  the  purpose  of  this

action  only.

A.

Within  the  City  of  Detroit  in  1940  there  were

369  census  tracts;  in  1950  369  census  tracts;

and  in  1970  420  census  tracts.

[In  1970  there

were  more  than  2  census  tracts  without  a  single

non-white.

In  1970  35  census  tracts  (or  8.33%

of  the  total  tracts)  had  no  blacks  and  93  census

tracts  (22.14%)  had  9  or  fewer  blacks.

For  1970

and  other  non-whites  in  census  tracts  with  less

than  5%  non-white  population  shows:

in  the  approxi-

population,  other  non-whites  outnumber  blacks  in  97

census  trects  (60.24%);  only  2,050  blacks  (.27%  of

the  total  black  population  in  the  city)  appear  in

these  approximately  161  census  tracts  compared  with

2,919  other  non-whites  (23.97%  of  the  total  non-white

other  population  in  the  city).

Thus,  although  blacks

constitute  98.25%  of  the  city-wide  non-white  popule-

tion,  in  the  epproximately  161  less  than  5%  non-white

census  tracts  blacks  constitute  only  41.25%  of  the

non-white  population.

Purthermore,  in  1960  of  the

tracts  (35%)  had  no  blecks,  while  83  other  census

tracts  hed  less  than  10  blacks.

Similarly  in  1950

of  the  369  census  tracts  in  the  city  39  (10.56%)

had  no  blacks,  while  another  123  census  tracts  hed

9  blacks  or  less.

®.

The  “point  system"  is  wholly  immoral.

b.  Under  its  screening  process  prospective  purchasers

¢.

Under  the  “point”  system  all  prospective  purchasers

are  required  to  live  up  to  a  certain  level,  but  much

A  Pole  is  required  to  have  5  additional  points.

and  Commissioner  Gubow  ordered  the  following:

any  other  screening  system  based  upon  discrimination

because  of  rece,  color  or  creed;

of  any  interchange  of  screening  information  by  the

of  new  rules  and  regulation  as  to  unlawful  activities

by  brokers  under  any  screening  system  based  upon

@iscrimination  in  regard  to  race,  color,  or  creed.

Commissioner  Gubow  proposed,  on  May  27,  1960,  Rule  9  to

define  particularly  what  would  be  considered  “unfair

Gealing"  under  Sec.  8  and  13(k)  of  Act  306,  P.A.  1919,

as  amended:

The  Michigan  Corporation  and  Securities  Commission

by  authority  of  Section  8,

No.  306,  P.A.  1919

(C.L.  1948,  Section  451.201  et  seq.),  amends  its

Real  Estate  Division  Rules  and  Regulations  by  adding

Rule  9  under  the  preamble  reading  as  follows:

“Any  broker  or  salesman  who  fails  or  neglects

to  abide  by  the  following  rules  and  regulations

adopted  by  the  Michigan  Corporation  and  Securities

Commission  shall  be  presumed  to  be  guilty  of  unfair

a

R  501.309

9.

A  broker  or  salesman,  acting  individually  or

jointly  with  others,  shell  not  refuse  to  sell

or offer

for  sale,  or  to buy  or  offer  to  buy,

or  to  appraise,  or  to  list,  or  to  negotiate  the

purchase,  exchange  or  mortgage  of  real  estate,

or  to  negotiate  for  the  construction  of  buildings

thereon,  or  to  lease  or  offer  for  lease,  or  to

rent  or  offer  for  rent,  any  real  estate  or  the

improvements  thereon,  or  any  other  service

performed  as  broker  or  salesman,  because  of  the

race,  color,  religion,  national  origin  or  ancestry

of  any  person  or  persons.

A  broker  or  salesman,  acting  individually

or  jointly  with  others,  shall  not  refuse  to  sell

or  offer  to  sell,  or  to  buy  or  offer  to  buy,  or

to  receive  an  offer  to  sell  or  to  buy,  or  to

lease  or  offer  for  lease,  or  to  negotiate  the

purchase,  sale  or  exchange  of  a  business,  business

opportunity  or  the  good  will  of  an  existing  business,

or  any  other  service  performed  as  broker  or  salesman,

because  of  the  race,  color,  religion,  national

origin  or  ancestry  of  any  person  or  persons.

6.

Subservuently  hearings  were  held  in  connection  with  the

proposed  Rule  9  by  Commissioner  Gubow  at  which  time  evidence

was  recorded  concerning  real  estate  marketing  prectices  in  other

@rcas  of  the  State.

The  Commission  found  with  respect  to

Giscrimination  practices  that  they  included  the  following:

Va74

(1)  "Gentleman's  agreements”  between  property  owners

and  real-estate  brokers  not  to  sell  or  rent  to  Negroes,

Mexicans,  Orientals,  Jews,  and  other  so-called  un-

Gesirables  outside  of  specifically  defined  areas

(such  agreements  also  exist  between  brokers  or

between  real  estate  boards);  (2)  prectices  on  the

part  of  real  estate  brokers  and  salesmen  of  showing

prospective  purchasers  whom  they  label  “undesirable”

houses  not  in the  price  range  for which  they  asked,

but  either  in a  much  lower  or such  higher  priced

area,  or  houses  too  large,  unkempt,  etc.;

(3)

brokers  telling  prospective  purchasers  (a)

they  have  no  houses  available  for  them  (b)  the  owner

Goesn't  want  to  sell,  (c)  the  house  listed  has

(4)  after  making  an  appointment  to  meet  the  prospec-

tive  purchaser  at  the  listed  house  the  broker  or

salesman  fails  to  show,  and  then  either  does  not

Now  come  William  G.  Milliken,  Governor  of  the  State  of

Michigan,  Frank  J.  Kelley,  Attorney  General,  John  W.  Porter,

Superintendent  of  Public  Instruction,  and  the  State  Board  of

the  plaintiffs’  request  for  admissions  number  two  through  seven

and  plaintiffs'  request  to  admit  the  genuineness  of  certain

documents,  as  follows:

1.

In  response  to  paragraph  2(1)  of  plaintiffs’  request

for  admissions,  the  state  defendants  admit  that  in  1960  the  Michigan

Corporation  and  Securities  Commission  investigated  the  Grosse  Pointe

"point"  system  and  deny  that  in  1960  the  Michigan  Corporation  and

Securities  Commission  investigated  other  real  estate  marketing

practices  in  the  State  of  Michigan.

2.

In  response  to  paragraph  2(2),  a  and  b  of  plaintiffs’

request  for  admissions,  the  state  defendants  admit  that  the  Grosse

Pointe  Brokers  Association  and  the  Grosse  Pointe  Property  Owners

Association  established  and  maintained  a  screening  system  to  w

”

Va77

would-be  purchasers  who  were  "undesirable."

Further,  the

tate  defendants  admit  that  the  screening  f.  oe was  a  device

through  which  prospective  purchasers  of  homes  in  the  Grosse  Pointe

area  were  investigated  by  a  private  detective  agency  and  then  graded

by  such  factors  as  their  national  origin,  whether  their  way  of  living

was  “typically  American,"  their  church  and  club,  membership,  whether

their  friends  were  predominantly  "typical,"  their  swarthiness,  nature

of  speech,  occupation  or  position,  source  of  income,  dress,  and  whether

their  names  were  “typically  American."

Further,  the  state  defendants

admit  that  a  committee  of  three  brokers,  who  were  able  to  operate  as

brokers  solely  because  they  held  a  State  license,  graded  the  above

information  when  received  and  the  grades  were  then  used  to  determine

whether  the  prospective  purchasers  were  to  be  admitted  to  the  Grosse

Pointe  residential  area.

Further,  the  state  defendants  admit  that

the  Grosse  Pointe  “point”  system  started  in  1945.

Further,  the  state

defendants  deny  that  the  Grosse  Pointe  "point"  system  grew  from  or

as  a  result  of  the  Code  of  Ethics  as  published  in  the  1943  Real

Estate  Licensing  Handbook,  hereafter  referred  to  as  the  "Red  Book."

“All  of  this  material  has  only  served  to  confirm

“Under  the  screening  process  prospective  purchasers
are  condemned  because  of  their  race,  color  or  creed
based  upon  a  summary  $10.00  investigation  by  a
private  detective.
The  detective  uses  pretexts
of  interview  the  neighbors  of  the  person  under
investigation--not  the  person  himself.

"We  in  America  believe  that  a  man  has  a  right  to
face  his  accusers  and  to  know  that  of  which  he  has
been  accused.
Rather  the  utmost
secrecy  is  preserved.

This  is  not  done.

"While  all  people  are  required  to  measure  up  to
certain  standards,  much  higher  standards  are
exacted  from  certain  persons.

"A  Pole  is  required  to  have  5  additional  points.

"An  Italian,  Yugoslav,  Creek,  Syrian,  Lebanese,
Armenian,  Maltese,  Rumanian,  or  other  Southern
European  is  required  to  have  15  additional  points.

"A  Jew  is  required  to  have  35  additional  points  and
his  points  are  more  difficult  to  achieve  because
of  penalties  in  a  special  marking  system  for  Jews.

"Orientals  and  Negroes  are  not  considered  at  all.

"I  am  satisfied  many  of  the  Grosse  Pointers  them-
selves  had  not  the  slightest  idea  as  to  the  nature
of  the  system  which  is  being  used  in  Grosse  Pointe
or  the  enormity  of  its  conception  and  execution.
Under  the  leadership  of  the  ministers  and  priests
of  that  community,  a  great  surge  of  moral  indigna-
tion  and  revulsion  against  the  system  has  been
steadily  rising.

"But  first  let  me  turn  to  the  legal  problems
involved.
Since  this  matter  came  to  our  attention
we  have  given  careful  consideration  to  what  legal
steps  might  be  taken  to  do  away  with  the  system.
Analysis  of  the  activities  of  the  various
indicated  to  us  the  possibility  of  prosecution  of
certain  corporations  and  individuals  for  violation
of  the  Corporation  &  Securities  Blue  Sky  Laws,  the
possibility  of  quo  warranto  proceedings  aimed  at
the  destruction  of  the  corporate  organizations
used  to  carry  out  this  scheme  and  restrain  competition,
the  possibility  that  Commissioner  Gubow  might  take
steps  to  revoke  the  licenses  of  those  real  estate
brokers  engaged  in  the  perpetration  of  the  scheme.

"Commissioner  Gubow  and  I,  and  in  fact  ell  of  the
people  of  the  State  of  Michigan,  are  faced  here  with
@  situation  that  is  primarily  a  moral  problem.
We
are  faced  with  the  question  as  to  whether  or  not  we
honestly  and  truly  believe  in  the  words  of  our
veclaration  of  Independence  that  all  men  are  created

Va82

As  one  minister  put  the  problem,  ‘We

equal.
should  seek  communities  made  all-inclusive  by
love  ratner  than  exclusive  by  pride.’

“Commissioner  Gubow  and  I  mirht  pursue  this
investigation  for  many  more  days,  but  we  are
convinced  that  this  is  unnecessary.
kather,
wnat  is  needed  is  the  conversion  of  all  of  us
to  a  way  of  life  that  is  closer  to  our  American
ideal."

f~

Purther,  in  response  to  paragraph  4  (c)(1)  of  plaintiffs’

request  for  admissions,  the  state  defendants  admit  that  the  State

Attorney  General  Adams  and  the  Commissioner  of  the  Corporation  and

Securities  Commission  Gubow,  did  make  the  following  joint  statement:

"Under  these  circumstances  we  announce  tié  following
program:

J

"1.
There  must  be  a  complete  and  absolute
abandonment  of  the  screening  and  reporting
system  and  an  absolute  pledge  and  assurance
by  all  persons  involved  that  such  a  system
based  upon  discrimination  because  of  race,  color
or  creed  will  never  again  become  a  part  of  their
activities.

"To  accomplish  this  end  we  will  require  the  Grosse
Pointe  Property  Owners  Association  to  take  appro-
priate  action  to  repudiate  the  screening  regulations
and  all  of  the  screening  practices  in  which  it  or
any  of  its  members  have  participated.

"We  will  further  require  that  the  Grosse  Pointe
Brokers  Association  take  formal  action  to  amend
its  by-laws  to  delete  therefrom  any  reference  to
the  screenins  regulations,  to  compliance  therewith
and  to  any  other  of  the  screening  system  practices.
We  will  require  that  these  two  corporations  com-
pletely  separate  themselves  from  any  co-ordinate
or  cooperative  effort  to  enrare  in  the  screening  process
or  to  interchanre  screeniny  information.

"io  accomplish  these  objectives  in  a  manner  satis-
factory  to  us  will  take  a  reasonable  period  of  time.
Therefore,  we  are  adjourning  the  present  hearing
subject  to  being  reconvened  at  the  end  of  30  days,
upon  notification  by  i'r.  Gubow.
we  stand  ready  to  work  witn  the  two  corporations,
the  individual  ourckers,  tne  property  owners  and  the
attorneys,  to  accomplish  these  objectives  and  to
implement  the  required  corporate  action,
faith  efforts  are  not  made  to  these  ends,  we  shall
be  compelled  to  impose  all  sanctions  against  these
corporations  permitted  under  the  law  for  tne  purpose
of  mandatorily  requiring  the  outlawing  and  abandonment
of  the  discriminatory  screeninr  process.

Durinr  this  neriod

If  good

Va83

The  information  which  has  been  received  into

"2.
the  record  in  this  proceeding  has  made  it  clear
that  additional  rules  and  regulations  are  needed
governing  the  activities  of  real  estate  brokers
as  to  unlawful  activities  by  brokers  under  a
screening  system  based  upon  discrimination  in
regard  to  race,  color,  or  creed.
&  Securities  Commission  will  immediately  undertake
the  preparation  and  promulgation  of  an  adequate  rule.

The  Corporation

"3.
It  clearly  appears  from  the  information
received  in  this  hearing  that  there  have  been  vio-
lations  of  the  Blue  Sky  Law  in  connection  with  the
issuance  and  transfer  of  the  stock  of  Grosse  Pointe
Properties,  Inc.
immediately  by  the  Corporation  &  Securities  Com-
mission  prohibiting  further  sales  or  trading  in

A  stop  order  will  be  issued

"4,
%If  further  or  additional  action  is  indicated,

of  the  statements  or  program  announced  herein."

Further,  the  state  defendants  admit  that  subsequent  to

May  13,  1960,  the  Commissioner  of  the  Corporation  and  Securities

Commission  did  not  receive  any  additional  complaints  concerning

the  Grosse  Pointe  "point"  system.

Further,  the  state  defendants  admit  no  more.

5.

In  answer  to  paragraph  5  of  plaintiffs'  request  for

admissions,  the  state  defendants  admit  that  Rule  9,  as  adopted  and

promulgated,  read  as  follows:

"A  broker  or  salesman,  acting  individually  or
jointly  with  others,  shall  not  refuse  to  sell  or
offer  for  sale,  or  to  buy  or  offer  to  buy,  or
to  receive  an  offer  to  sell  or  to  buy,  or  to
appraise,  or  to  list,  or  to  negotiate  the  purchase,
sale,  exchange  or  mortgage  of  real  estate,  or  to
negotiate  for  the  construction  of  buildings  thereon,
or  to  lease  or  offer  for  lease,  or  to  rent  or  offer
for  rent,  any  real  estate  or  the  improvements  thereon,
or  any  other  service  performed  as  broker  or  salesman,
because  of  the  race,  color,  religion,  national  origin
or  ancestry  of  any  person  or  persons.

"A  broker  or  salesman,  acting  individualy  or
jointly  with  others,  shall  not  refuse  to  sell  or
offer  to  sell,  or  to  buy  or  offer  to  buy,  or  to
offer’to  sell  or  to  buy,  or  to  lease
receive
or  offer
sale  or  exchange  of a  business,  business  opportunity,

or  to  negotiate  the  purchase,

for  lease,

Va84

or  the  good  will  of  an  existing  business,  or  any
other  service  performed  as  broker  or  salesman,
because  of  the  race,  color,  religion,  national
origin  or  ancestry  of  any  person  or  persons."

Purther,  the  state  defendants  admit  no  more.

6.

In  response  to  paragraph  6  of  plaintiffs'  request  for

admissions,  the  state  defendants  admit  that  Commissioner  Gubow  held

hearings  on  proposed  Rule  9  and  deny  that  evidence  was  recorded

concerning,  real  estate  marketing  practices  in  other  areas  of  the

State  from  which  the  Commission  made  findings  with  respect  to

discrimination  practices.

Further,  the  state  defendants  admit  that

on  becember  14,  1960  Commissioner  Gubow  filed  a  statement  with  the

United  States  Commission  on  Civil  Rights  which  contained  within  it

a  listing  of  the  five  practices  set  forth  on  pages  5  and  6  of

plaintiffs  request  for  admissions.

Further,  the  state  defendants

admit  that  Commissioner  Gubow's  sources  of  information  concerning

practices  one  through  four  included  the  investigation  into  the

Grosse  Pointe  “point”  system,  the  unsworn  statements  of  persons  not

subject  to  cross-examination  at  the  hearings  on  proposed  Rule  9,

and  conversations,  telephone  calls  and  letters  involving  Commissioner

Gubow  which  occurred  or  were  received  by  Commissioner  Gubow  in  his

Lansing  office  and  in  his  travels  throughout  the  State.

Further,

the  state  defendants  admit  that  Commissioner  Gubow's  source  of

information  for  the  fifth  practice  was  a  hearing  tefore  the  Michigan

Corporation  and  Securities  Commission.

Purther,  the  state  defendants  admit  no  more.

7.

In  response  to  paragraph  7  of  plaintiffs"  request  for

admissions,  tne  state  defendants  admit  that  the  Supreme  Court  of

Michigan,  in  McKibbin  v  Corporation  and  Securities  Commission,  369

Mich  69  (196§),  held  that  the  defendant  Commission  had  exceeded  its

statutory  authority  by  promulgating  Rule  9  and  affirmed  the  injunction

Vass

issued  by  the  Circuit  Court  against  the  enforcement  of  Rule  9  by

the  defendant  Commission.
the  plaintiffs  in  that  case  were  duly  licensed  real  estate  brokers,

Further,  the  state  defendants  admit  that

either  as  individuals  or  corporate  brokers.
defendants  admit  tnat  in  that  case  there  was  no  trial  before  the

Further,  the  state

Circuit  Court  and  the  case  was  decided  solely  on  questions  of  law.

Further,  the  state  defendants  admit  the  genuineness  of  each  of  the

three  documents  listed  in  paragraph  7  of  plaintiffs‘  request  for

admissions.

Dates  of  promulgation  and  adoption  are  admitted.

Further,  the  state  defendants  admit  no  more.

5.

In  addition  to  the  answer  already  filed  by  the  state  ~

defendants  to  paragraph  5  of  plaintiffs'  request  for  admissions,

the  state  defendants  admit  that  on  May  27,  1960  Commissioner  Gubow

proposed  Rule  9  to  define  “unfair  dealing"  under  Act  306,  PA  1919,

as  amended,  and  that  the  proposed  rule  contained  the  following
-  introduction:

"The  Michigan  Corporation  and  Securities  Commission
by  authority  of  Section  8,  Act  No.  306,  P.A.  1919
(C.L.  1988,  Section  %51.201  et  seq),  amends  its
Real  Estate  Division  Rules  and  Regulations  by  adding
Rule  9  under  the  preamble  reading  as  follows:

"*‘any  broker  or  salesman  who  fails  or  neglects  to
abide  by  the  following  rules  and  regulations  adopted
by  the  Michigan  Corporation  and  Securities  Commission
shall  be  presumed  to  be  guilty  of  unfair  dealing.'"

The  State  Legislature  has  full  power  to  pass  legislation  mandating  the

reorganization  of  school  districts  across  city  and  even  county  boundaries.

Answers  to  Subpoenas  or  Interrogatories  supplied  by  Local

School  Districts  (1970-71  or  1971-72  Data).
An  asterisk  (*)  indicates  that  the  current  source  of

information  was  used.

Key.

Buses:

includes  chartered,  leased  and  owned  vehicles  with

-capacity  of  31  or  more  passengers.

Vans:

includes  chartered,  leased  and  owned  vehicles  with
These  figures  are

less  than  31  passenger  capacity.
indicated  by  parentheses.

Number  after  the  hyphen(-)  represents  capacity  of  each

vehicle.

Total  Students  Transported:  excludes  all  children

by  private  car  pools,  privately  leased  buses,  or
public  transit  authorities;  except  the  figures
for  Detroit  and  Grosse  Pointe  include  estimates
of  riders  by  public  transit.

Total  Cost:

(annual  total  cost)  excludes  out-of-pocket

expenses  by  private  individuals  to  pay  for  bus
fares,  the  costs  of  private  car  or  private  charter
bus  transportation,  and  in  many  instances,  annual
depreciation  costs.

Note:  Two  school  districts  (Woodhaven  and  Mt.  aarerst.

Sec.  36.  A district  receiving  moneys  under  section  31  may  use  these
in  any  manner  which,  in  the  judgment  of  its  board  and  its  staff,  contri
significantly  toward  substantial  improvements  in the  basic  cognitive  skills of
pupils.  These  uses  may  include,  but  are  not  limited  to,  the  following:

CHAPTER  4

Sec.  41.  From  the  amount  appropriated  in  section  11,  $500,000.00  shall  be
allocated  for  grants  to  districts  to  enter  into  performance  contracts  for  instruc-
tional purposes.  The  department  shall  establish  and  supervise  the  contracts.
Sec.  42.  From  the  amount  appropriated  in  section  11,  $250,000.00  shall  be
allocated  to  continue  contractual  arrangements  for  a  statewide  program  of
oriented  mathematics  utilizing  the  discovery  method  to
abstract  conceptually
the  basic  skills of  educationally  needy  children  attending  elementary
schools,  The  department  shall  evaluate  the  effectiveness  of  the  programa  and
submit  its  findings  to  the  legislature.
Sec.  43.  Districts  offering  learning  disabilities  programs  approved  by  the
department  are  entitled  to  75%  of  the  actual  cost  of  the  salary,  not  to  exceed  —
$8,100.00  for any  individual  salary  of a  learning  disabilities  teacher  approved  by
the department.  The  state  board  may  provide  by rules  for the  maximum  number
of pupils per  teacher  to  be  counted.  From  the  totalsappropriated  in  section  11,
there  is allocated  not  to  exceed  $1,650,000.00  for  learning  disabilities  programs
to be used  for  teachers’  salaries  at  the  grades  K-3  levels  only.  A  school  district
funded  under  section  31  shall  not  receive  funds  under  this  section  for  any
learning  disabilities  program  except  that  cach  district  shall  receive  under  this
and  section  44  an  amount  equal  to  the  remedial  reading  funds  received

Va  167

EMERGENCY  APPROPRIATIONS

P.A.1937,  No.  120, Imd. Eff. June 26

AN ACT to provide  for the releasing  of appropriations  for emer-
gency purposes;  to create  a special  commission  on appropriations,
and to prescribe  its powers  and duties;  and to repeal all acts or parts
of acts inconsistent  with the provisions of this act.

The People of the State of Michigan enact:

$.1

members
Special  commission  on appropriations;

Sec.  1.  A special  commission  for the release  of  sums from
the appropriations  made  from time  to time  for  the use of such
is hereby  created  to consist  of the governor,  the
commission
speaker  of the house  of  representatives  and the members  of the
ways  and  means  committee  of  the house  of  representatives, and
the president  of  the senate  and the  members  of the finance  and
appropriations  committee  of the  senate,  of the state  legislature,  in
which  commission  shall  be vested  the authority and administration
of this act.
of the provisions

Sec. 2. Whenever  it  shall seem  necessary  for  the commission
to  consider,  pass  upon  and  release  appropriations  for  emergency
purposes,  contingency  or  supplementary  appropriations  for  state
departments,  institutions,  boards  or commissions,
or special  state
purposes,  out  of funds  appropriated  by law  for use  by such  special
commission,  the  governor,  as chairman  of such commission shall
give due  and reasonable  notice  to the members  of  the commission.
The  commission  shall  not  have  the  authority  to  appropriate
money  for any  purpose  that  could  have  been  anticipated  and  made
while  the  legislature  was in session,  nor for  any purpose  that  has
been considered
The commission  is
and denied  by the legislature.
authorized  to  transfer  appropriations  as  between  institutions  and
activities  under  the jurisdiction  of the  same  administrative  depart-
ment,  board  or commission.  A majority  of  the commission shall
constitute  a quorum  for  the  transaction  of business
and no action
shall  be taken  on  release  of funds  from  the  appropriation  made  for
the  use  of  the  commission,  except  that  it  be  approved  by  a

Va  168

EMERGENCY  APPROPRIATIONS

$3

That the
majority  of the members  of  the commission:  Provided,
governor  may veto any action of the commission  at any time with.
in  5 days of such action; and shall so notify  the speaker  of the
house  of representatives
of such veto: Provided  further, That the
speaker  of the house  of representatives  shall immediately  call a
to assemble  in the governor's office
meeting  of said commission
of such veto,  and at
within  14  calendar  days after  notification
such meeting  the commission  shall vote upon the said veto by the
governor,  and upon  a 2/3 vote of  the  members  of the commission
the veto of the governor  shall not prevail.  Each member of  the
commission,  including  the governor,  shall be entitled  to  a vote
upon all questions  having to do with the release of appropriations.

Sec. 3. The expenses  of  the members  of  the commission  in
attending  any meeting  of the commission  shall be audited  by the
auditor  general  and  paid  from  the  emergency  appropriation  fund.

Sec. 4. The governor  shall be chairman  of  the commission.
The commission  shall prescribe rules and regulations  for  carrying
out  the  provisions  of  this act.  All  requests  for  funds  from  any
appropriations  made  for the use of  the commission  shall be sub-
in writing,  set-
and to the  budget  director
mitted  to  the governor
ting forth clearly  the necessity  for such  funds  and the emergency
nature
of the request.

5.5

Appropriation  for emergency  purposes

Sec.  5.  There  is  hereby  appropriated  from  the  general  fund
for  the  fiscal  year  ending  June  30,  1959,  and  for  each  fiscal  year
thereafter,  the sum  of $100,000.00  for emergency  purposes  and to
carry  out  the provisions  of this act.

NORMAN  DRACHLER

(13)  A.  Right,  and it’s still true  to a  very large  degree  in

1970. At least their policy is to  go in the other direction.

Q.  When  was the Carver  District in existence  as a  separate

entity?

A.  The Carver  District?  The  Carver  is not  in Detroit.
Q. Is  it  a  separate  school district  whose students  attended

some  Detroit  high schools,  in particular  Northern?

A.  Oh,  I see  what  you're  referring  to.  I am  told  that  back  in
‘57, ‘58, at  that  time  I was  not  in Central  Office,  there  were  some
students  from  Carver  District  who  did  not  have  a  place  for
adequate  high  school  facilities.  An  arrangement  was  made  with
Detroit  for the Carver  students  to  come  in on buses  and go to
Northern  High  School.  Now,  the nearest  school  to Carver  was
Mumford  at  the  time.  And  they  did  go  past  Mumford  towards
Northern.

Q.  Is Carver  a black  district?

A.  Yes,  black  and  very  poor.

Q.  Has Carver  District  subsequently  merged  with  Detroit?

A.  Oak  Park.

Q.  With  Oak  Park?

A.  That's  right.
Q.  And  at that  time  the  transportation  was  terminated?

A.  That’s  right.  By  the  way,  as  a  result  of those  youngsters
coming,  there  was  a  rumor  spread  that  Detroit  children[14]  were
being  bussed,  say,  from  the  Higginbotham,  which  is  north  —
Higginbotham area which  is north  of  Mumford  High  School  area
but  in  Detroit,  that  they  were  being  bussed  to  Northern,  too,
because  they  were  black  students,  people  saw  black  students  from
the  Eight  Mile  area  coming  down.  But  to  the  best  of  my
knowledge  these  were  outside  students.

Q.  There  were  black  children  being  bussed  to  Higginbotham,

weren't  they?

A.  There  were  black  children  being  bussed  to Higginbotham.

Q.  From  Angell?

Va  182

A.  From  Angell  past  some  white  schools.  And  when  the  issue
was  brought  to  Doctor  Brownell’s  attention  by me  in about  ‘59 or
‘60  —  there  were  a series  of instances like  that.  There  was the
Angell,  there  was  from  the  military  for in  the  southwest,  they
were  bussing  their  own  children  up  to  the  Noble,  and  Doctor
Brownell,  as soon  as it was  brought  to his attention,  abolished  that
as well  as  the optional  areas.

Q.  Was this so-called

intact bussing,  that  is a  class being

as a unit?
brought

A.  Generally  speaking,  yes.  That  policy  of  changing  to
geographic  bussing  occurred  about  °62-’63  as a result  of the  Equal
Education  Opportunities  Committee.

Q.  Was all of  the bussing  done  in the City of Detroit of an

[15]  intact  nature  until  the  Equal  Opportunities  study?

A. To  the best  of my  knowledge  it  was.  | know  when  my

children  were  being bussed,  they  were  bussed  intact.

Q.  Were  they  kep  separated  within  the  school  with  respect  to
other  activities  as  well  as  the  classroom?  I’m  not  necessarily
speaking  intentionally,  I’m talking  about  as a practical  —

A.  They  very  often  took,  let’s  say,  a  section  that  was  froma
school  that  was  overcrowded  and  moved  that  classroom  intact,
say,  to  the  Clinton  School  and  there  they  went  to  school  with
their  former  classmates.  This  was  during  the  later  40's  and  early
$0’s  when  the  northwest  and  the  northeast  was  being  just
overwhelmed  with  new  houses  and  school  buildings  were  not  up.

.Q.  They  were  bussed  back  into  the  central  city?

Yes, they  were  bussed  southward.

Would  this  be mostly  white  students  at that  time?

Oh, yes.  Oh, yes.

Were  they  bussed  mostly  to white  schools?

Oh, yes.  Without  exception  as  far as  I know.

QPrOreo-p

.  Was  there  some  feeling  in  the  administration  in  the  60's
egninat bussing  children,  I think  the  phrase  is used,  back  into  the
neighborhoods  they  moved  out  of?

A.  Definitely.

Vail8s3

ota}...

DR.  NORMAN  DRACHLER:

Called  as  a witness  by defendant  Detroit  School  Board,  and  being
first duly sworn  by the  Notary  Public,  testified  as  follows:

[30]  ...

In  1940  Detroit  was  69  per  cent  of  the  metropolitan
population  but  notice  we  dropped  8 points  by  1950.  This  area  had
a bigger  drop  between  ‘SO  and  ‘60.  It dropped  down  to 44 and  in
1970  we  are  about  36  per  cent.  So  there  has  been  a  steady
migration  evidently,  or  change  in  the  shift  of population  because
we  have  dropped  about  400,000  from  1950  to  1970  or  350,000  in
population.

Q.  Doctor,  for  the  record  this  chart  that  you  now  display  to
all of us  reflects  that  the  Detroit  population  comprises  69.5  per
cent  of the  population  in  the  Detroit  metropolitan  area  in  1940?

A.  Right.

MR.  LUCAS:  Can  I understand  whether  he  means  as  defined

by the census  when  he says  metropolitan?
MR.  BUSHNELL:  Good  question.

Q.  What  do you  mean  by the  Detroit  metroplitan  area,  sir?

{31]  A.  I used  the  federal  standard  metropolitan  area  which

involved  several  counties.

MR.  LUCAS:  Thank  you.

Q.  Follow  that  through,  the  correlative  figure  is  30.5  per
cent  in  1940  of so-called  suburban  population,  that  is, outside  of
Detroit?

A., Right.

Q.  By  1970,  however,  the  Detroit  population  constituted  but
36 per  cent  of the  metropolitan  area  and  the  suburban  population
64 per cent?

A.  Right.

Q.  I  think  it  is  also  interesting,  Doctor,  to  show  the

population  figures,  Detroit's  population  in ‘40 was  1,623,000.

A.  1,850,000  in  1950.

Q. ,In 1950?

Va  184

A.  1,850,000  but  in  1970,  1,511,000.  The  suburban
population  has  gone  from  714,000  in  1940  to  2,692,000  in  1979,

Q.  The  Detroit  population  in  the  30-year  period  has

decreased  by but  112,000?

A.  Correct.

[45]  Q.  There  was a chart  in that  report,  was  there  not?
A.  Yes.  The  explanation  that  Personnel  used  to give was  that
teachers  were  assigned  to  areas  closest  to  their  home  and  this
accounted  for the  places  where  teachers  taught.

This  chart  which  is in  the  report  opposite  Page  78, is entitled

Racial  Distribution  of Teachers  and  Pupils.

Q.  That  report  is an  exhibit.

MR.  LUCAS:  Plaintiffs’  Exhibit  3.

MR.  BUSHNELL:  Thank  you,  sir.
A.  That  demonstrated  that  by  and  large  an  all  white  staff
meant  predominantly,  or  an  all  white  student  body  I mean  meant
an  almost  in  all  instances  an  all  white  staff.  On  the  other  hand,
below  100  per  cent  there  was  the  distribution  of black  teachers
which  was,  generally  speaking,  in  relation  to  the  high  percentage
of black  students.

The  committee  finally  came  in with  its recommendation  that,
and  although  there  are  various  sections,  Mr.  Bushnell,  in  this
report,  the  one  on  staffing  occupied  the  major  portion  of the
committee  and  was  the  chief  thrust  of  the  discussion  when  the
committee  made  its  presentation  to  the  Board  although  there  are
chapters  dealing  with  curriculum  and  instructional  improvement
organization,  administration  and  so  on.

[46]  The  other  major;recommendation  in  this  committee's
report  was  that  bussing  to relieve  overcrowding  should  not  be ona
grade  basis  as  it was  done  at that  time  but on  a geographic  basis so
that  the  youngsters  that  are  bussed  can  be  integrated  more
successfully  in the school  which  is receiving  them.

Q.  Let  me  interrupt  at  this  point,  Doctor.  There  has  been
testimony  in  this  case  about  various  types  of  bussing,  intact
bussing,  bussing  by grade  and  geographic  bussing.  Will  you  please

Vai85

;

9

tell'us  what  those  terms  mean  and  what  the district  was  doing  and
when’  If | understand  intact  bussin  it referred  to  the  kind  of
pussing  that  was prior to Dr. Bownell’s recommendation  that we bus
to  the  nearest  school.  It  was the kind  of bussing  that  went  on
between  1940  and  1956  where  a  school  was  not  yet  built  or  was
overcrowded.  It was  generally  a  predominantly  white  school  or
sometimes a predominantly  back school.

The children in a given  classroom  were  moved  in to to a school
that  had  space.  Sometimes  they  were  even  moved  with  their
teacher.  This  is what  I refer  to as  intact  bussing.

Q.  What  happened  to  them  when  they  got  to  the  receiving

school?

A.  At  the  receiving  school  they  were  in  a  homeroom  by
themselves  [47]  went  to  special  classes.  The  only  time  they  came
in contact  with  children  of the  receiving  school  might  Have  been
during  a  gym  class  or  an  auditorium  class  where  there  was  more
than  one  section,  for instance,  well,  I can  be  personal  —  my  son
was  moved  from  the  Vernor  to  the  Clinton.  The  Vernor  wasn’t
finished  yet,  so  the  whole  classroom  was  moved  and  the
homeroom  teacher  they  had  went  with  them  or  the  one  they  were
to have  rather.  Then  they  were  brought  back.  The  Vernor  at that
time  was  an  all white  school  but  it was  the  socio-economic  status  I
am  sure  which  concerned  the  parents,  the  lower  socio-economic
status.

Q.  At the Clinton?

A.  At  the  Clinton,  yes.  So  there  was  the  fear  and  the  charge

that  the  children  couldn’t  be  challenged  if  they  were  just
distributed  in  the  schools.  Then  came  the  bussing  to  the  nearest
school.

Q.  Why was  that  practice  of intact  bussing  discontinued?

A.  Well,  I don’t  know  of  any  direct  policy.  When  that  was
done  or carried  on  I was not in the central  administration  or
involved
as a principal.  I do  know  that  there  was a  charge  made
about  the  Angell  School  where  the  children  were  black  and  they
went  by  a  number  of  white  schools  all  the  way  to  the
Higginbotham  near  Eight  Mile  where  there  was  space.  Whether
that  was  intact  or  not  I don’t  know  but  it  [48]  was  black  with
black.

:

——

Va  186

“ee

At  Northern  High  School  where  we had space  some  black
students  were  brought  in under  a  lease  from,  the Carver  School
District.  That was before Carver  became  a  part bf Oak Park.

Q.  Or Royal  Oak  Township?

A.  Or  Royal  Oak  Township,  that’s  right.  Because  they  went
by  Mumford  down  Wyoming  and  there  were  some  who  thought
that  we  were  bussing  Detroit  black  students  from  the  Mumford
area  all  the  way  to Northern.  To my  knowledge  that  was  not  true.
The  contract  for  Carver,  if  I am  not  mistaken,  had  been  started
when  it was  not  necessarily  with  Brownell’s  involvement  but when
he became  aware  of it he asked  that  it be discontinued.

The  rationale  for  not  doing  it  at  Mumford  which  was  the
nearest  school  was  simply  that  Mumford  was  much  more  crowded,
from  what  I understand,  than  was  Northern  and  that  is the  reason
they  were  being  bussed  there.  But  nevertheless,  that  was
discontinued  and  that problem  was  resolved.

.

[143]  ...
Q.  Doctor,  you  have  described  what  happened  to  education,
what  happened  to  the  pupil  population  of the city, you  described
socio-economic  factors  that  afflict  a  significant  portion  of  the
pupil  population  of this  district,  you  have  also  indicated  that  the
Title  | experience  would  show  that  if there  is increased  educational
service  and  intensified  educational  service  there  can  be
improvement  in  the  student’s  performance  and  achievement.  You
also  told  us  the  non-Title  | schools  in the  last  year  or so  have  been
going  down  in achievement  level.  I assume,  though  you  didn’t say
so,  because  of  the  proliferation  of  lower  income  and  lower  SES
pupils  throughout  the city  being  concentrated  as they  were.

“All of  this  you  demonstrated  in  the  last  hour  or  so  that  we
have  been  talking  together.  There  is  testimony  in  this  case  that
given  the  poverty  of  this  district  or  poorness  of  a  substantial
number  of pupils  in  the  district  [144]  and  given  the low  SES ofa
substantial  number  of  pupils  in  the  district  that  the  best
educational  result:  can  only  be  achieved  if  those  students  ar
exposed  to  higher  SES  students  in  a  ratio  of  approximately,
according  to  one  witness,  60  per  cent  high,  40  per  cent  low orat
least  50/50.

Vail87

It  has  also  been  suggested  that  the  one  effective  way  of
achieving this  educational  or  making  such  educational  progress  and
enhancing  the  educational  program  delivered  to  the  children  of
this  district  is  to  enlarge  the  district,  eliminate  the  conventional
city  boundaries  of  the  district,  and  incorporate  higher  SES  kids
and by almost  assuming  a definition,  more  white  kids in  a omen
metropolitan  school  system.

Do  you  have  any  comment  that  you  would  wish  to  make

about  that  suggestion?

A.  First,  I believe  in  the  need  for  metropolitanism  from  the
s@ndpoint  that  even  as  late  as  last  week  our  Board  involved  in
appointing  a  selection  committee  for  the  superintendency  of the
city was  debating  the  issue  as  to  whether  or  not  you  have  on  that
selection  committee  people  who  live  outside  the  city  or  not.
Because  the  professional  and  business  leadership  of this  city  work
within  the  city  but  reside  in the  suburbs,  that  it makes  them  much
less  effective  leaders  for  bringing  about  the  change  in  the  central
city  than  in  the  areas  where  [145]  they  live.  Most  studies  of
millage  elections  indicate  that  Jack  Jones  doesn’t  decide  how  to
vote  on  millage  on  what  the  president  of one  of the  Big Three  says
but  what  his  next  door  neighbor  tells  him,  so  that  if central  cities
are  to  survive  for  financial,  for  political  and  leadership  roles  we
must  have  closer  ties  with  the  metropolitan  area.

Secondly,  I  believe,* I would  like  to  believe,  that  the
metropolitan  area  can’t  survive  if  central  city  is  allowed  to
deteriorate  or  decay.  They  are  very  interdependent.

Thirdly,  based  on  studies  made  of suburban  communities  and
children,  if the  democratic  values  of America  are  to survive,  some
bridges  and  linkages  have  to  be  made  and  they  would  have  to  be
done  between  the  central  city and  the  suburban  area.

It isn’t  going  to  be  done  just  by a group  of volunteers  coming
in  from  the  suburbs  and  helping  out  with  tutorial  programs  as
much  as  I appreciate  it.  People  can  communicate  best  only  when
they  share  experiences  that  are  rough  and  meaningful  as  well  as
pleasurable.  So  that  I  do  believe  there  is  something  about
metropolitanism  and  something  about  the  whole  area  of
integration  that  is  not  completely  the  same  but  has  some
comparisons  with  the  past  50  to  75  years  of efforts  by  the  rural
areas  to bring about  school  consolidation.

Va  188

[146] The rural  farmer,  not  any  different  than  the  poor  black,
believed  that  his youngster  would  get a better  education  through a
consolidated  school  system  which  embraced  a larger  area  but
which  also  would  bring  his  kids  in  contact  with  city-folk  kids and
a broader  area  of SES  participation.  It had  nothing  to do  with  race
but  it was  opportunity.

This  country  not  only  encouraged  it but  even  offered  awards
for  consolidation.  States  held  out  large  sums  of money  to  school
districts.  Whether  it  is  politically  feasible  today  in  a  sense,  even
possible  or  desirable,  to  force  parents  in  suburbia  to  bus  back  to
the  innercity  I would  be of the  opinion  it is as difficult  to imagine
as  to  ask  the  black  parents  in  Mumford  to  go  back  to  the  King
School  area  because  we  want  to  provide  them a better  SES,  you
know.

Possibly  we  will  have  to suffer  many  more  defeats  in the state
legislature  before  the  blacks  and  the  whites  in  suburbia  will  even.
want  to  talk  about  joint  efforts  because  at  present  I am  sure  the
blacks  will  say,  “Just  when  we  have  reached  the  stage  where  we
can  about  elect  our  own  mayor  and  our  own  city  council  and our
own  superintendent  of schools  you  say  to us,  “Let’s  unite.”  They
will  say,  “Why  didn’t  you  say  it 20 years  ago  when  we  wanted  to.”

So  I think  it’s  a  very  difficult  political  problem  right  now. I

believe  that  both  suburbia  and  the  central  [147]  cities  are  going to
have  to  suffer.  Suburbia  is beginning  to  suffer  —  they  will  have to
suffer  for  some  time  before  they  realize  the common  interest  that
has  to  hold  them  together  against  a  domination  of  a  rural
legislature  and  a  rural  congress,  then  they  may  unite  and  begin
talking  about  educational  joint  facilities  for  common  causes  as
they  have  talked  about  highways,  the  water  supply  and  as  they are
going  to talk  about  ecology  in the  next  few years.

So  I do  think  metropolitanism  ultimately  will  have  to  be an
answer  but  I think  if and  when  it comes  it will  not, just as  the idea
will  be  novel,  I think  the  solutions  to it may  be creative  ones  that
do  not  involve  our  present  pattern  of  how  we  think  education
could  be  improved.  I think  there  might  be  centers  running  along
Eight  Mile  Road  just  as  the  shopping  malls  maybe  and  I don’t
want  to  call  it  an  educational  park,  there  may  be  some  other
measure.  There  may  be a system  of a halfday  of school  experience
and  other halfdays  in  other  areas  or  it  may  be  on  a  12-month
basis.

Val89

1 think  and  I am  no  expert,  I am  just  guessing  like  anyone
else,  I think  basically  the  partners  in  education  have  reneged.  We
originally  thought  of  a  system  where  it  would  be  local
control-state  support  and,  frankly,  the  conditions  today  indicate
that  the  public  has  reneged,  the  state  legislatures  have  reneged, -
they  are  not  meeting  their  [148]  responsibilities.  The  conditions,
however,  in the  country  are  so  serious  in  terms  of education  and
other welfare  needs  that  I don’t  believe  that  this country  can  allow
fifty  states  to  muddle  through  the  solutions  to  the  educational
problems  that  we  have  and  I don’t  think  we  have  the talen  or  the
resources  in this state.

I think  the  only  one  that  has  that  talent  and  that  resource  is
the  Federal  Government.  When  the  Federal  Government  will
finally  come  to  the  fore  on  this  and  I think  it will  in the next  10
or  20  years  then  inevitably  metropolitanism  will  be  one  of  the
conditions  and  integration,  in my  opinion,  will  be  one  of the
conditions  or  prerequisites  that  they  will  set  as  some  of  the
solutions  towards  our  educational  problems.

I think  America  is  pragmatic  enough  as  a  people  to  retape
what  we  have  called  local  control,  which  I believe  is  a myth,  and
to squeeze  out  from  federal  planning  and  thinking  and  resources
the  most  pragmatic  kind  of  plan  which  will  on  the  one  hand
enable  the  schools  to  continue  to  operate  in  accordance  with
American  democratic  values,  and  at  the  same  time  leave  enough
control  at  the  local  level  which  will  be  more  meaningful  control
than  the  kind  of  local  control  we  thought  we  had  for  40  or  50
years.

My  belief  is that  it is necessary,  that  it  [149]  will  not  come
voluntarily  but  it  will  come  about  as  a  result  of  the  financial
bankruptcy  and  moral  bankruptcy  that  the  American  school
systems  are  facing  in  the  next  decade  that  they  will  have  to throw
themselves  almost  on  the  mercy  of  some  national  planning  and
within  this  national  planning  there  may  come  some  metropolitan
grandiose  operations  which  will  answer  some  of the  questions  that
are  being raised.

I don’t  think  we  have  suffered  yet  enough  to  do  it  on  a

voluntary  basis.

Q.  Doctor,  in  giving  this  answer,  I  assume  that  you  are
supportive  of  the  opinions  of  Dr.  James  Guthrie  and  Dr.  Stuart

Vail90

Rankin  that  the  best  educational  service  can  be  provided  those
educational  situations  where  there  is a  preponderance  or  majority
of  higher  SES  kids  and  a  minority  of  low  SES  kids  and as an  |
incident  to  that  racial  and  cultural  and  religious  integration
wherever  possible.  Is that  a fair assumption?

A.  I didn’t  hear  Mr.  Guthrie  nor  read  his  statement.  | do
know  Stuart  Rankin.  When  we  speak  of SES I think  it is assumed
that  we  think  of  it  in  terms  of merely  dollars  that  parents  have,
When  I speak  of SES  I think  of the  difficulty  and  handicaps  and
conditions  that  low  SES  places  upon  children.

Q.  I think  there  is  substantial  agreement.

A.  Basically  it  is  this  difficulty  that  when  one  sees  one  just
marvels  at  the  number  of children  that  can  overcome  it and  [150]
still, survive  and  if these  conditions  obviously  were  improved,  if
they  were  intermingled  I think  we  would  have  some  improvement
but  we  have  to  remember  that  there  is still  individual  differences
between  children  as  well  as  between  teachers  and  that  within  a
single  family.  Not  all  children  achieve  the  same  way  but  when  we
look  at  the  low  SES  areas  then  we  know  that  as  a large  group  we
are  failing.  It’s  not  the  exception  and  the  failure—well, I discussed
that  before.  I think  part  of  it is due  to  the  school  shortcomings
and  a  major  portion  of  it  is  society  too,  that  has  to  assume  a
responsibility.

could  this  district  accomplish  that  metropolitan  system  on  its own
and, if not,  what  help would  it have  to have?

A.  Obviously  this  district  does  not  have  any  authority  over
the  metropolitan  area.  Unless  the  courts  were  to  order-I  am
assuming  you  mean  they  would  order  the  metropolitan  district as
well  as  us.  ©

|

Q.  Your  answer  is  we  couldn’t  do  it  ourselves  without

intervention  of others?

[151]  A.  Definitely  not.  Within  the  city  I am  concerned  that

unless the district  were  reinforced  by an  amount  of educational  ser  |

Val9l

vices that would almost be double of what we have now we could not
reassure  black  middleclass  or  white  middleclass  parents  that  the
quality of education  would  remain,  would  not suffer.

[have  a feeling  that  the  majority  of parents  are  not  bigots. I

think  there  is a  minority  of bigots,  sure.  I think  the  majority  are
honestly  fearful  about  safety,  about  the  stereotypes  they  have  of
black  students  which  are  sometimes  very  often  false.

Q.  And  of white  students?
A.  Yes,  the  same  way,  less  so  because  remember  5000  to
7000  black  students  have  chosen  open  enrollment  schools  but  not
50 white  students  have  chosen  black  open  enrollment  schools.  In
the  very  fact  they  haven’t  chosen  it  I feel  integration  is  a  need
because  they  haven’t  chosen  it  on  account  of  historic  patterns
about  black  and  white  which  have  been  engrained  into  them,  into
their  parents  lives  and  so  on  and  this  is the sadness  about  the black
youngster  feeling  that  he  is unwanted  because  wherever  he  turns
seems  to  tell  him that, so  that within the city we have  greater
"limitations  unless  we  would  have  really  the  kind  of resources  that
would  definitely  provide  the  services.

Remember,  Detroit  unlike  New  York  and  Washington,  [152].
D.C., is one  of the  few  predominantly  black  cities,  black  majority,
as  far  as  schools  where  the  black  middle  class  has  not  yet  left.
Probably  the  housing  noose  around  Detroit  is much  stronger  than
it has  been  in  other  areas,  I don’t  know,  but  for  one  reason  or
another  the  black  middle  class  is still  here.  The  black  middle  class
has  the  same  worries  as  the  white  middle  class  about  their  child’s
education.

In order  not  to  have  them  leave  the  school  system  or  the  city
we  would  have  to have  some  very  outstanding  showcase  education
programs  in order  to convince  both  black  and  white  that  this court
order  means  not  merely  integration  and quality education but  as  a
result  of the  services  and  as  a  result  of integration  it will  even  be
better  education  than  you  ever  had  before.  Without  that  you  can’t
quarantine  people  in  the  city  and  tell  them  they  have  to  stay
there.

I will  give  you  an  example  of that:  An issue  was  raised  in the
state  legislature  recently  about  passing  a  law  that  said  all teachers
must  live  in  Detroit.  That  was  bandied  around  last  week  at a state

Val92

legislative  commission  where  I had to appear  and  this question  was
raised.  The  observation  was  made,  “Look,  we  are  just  about
reaching the stage  where  Detroit  has 50 per cent  black  teachers.  Up
until  we  were  50 per  cent  you  said  a  teacher  in Detroit  could  live
anywhere  they  pleased,  but  now  that  finally  black  teachers  [153]
have  made  it are  you  going  to  say  to  us  they  must  live  only in
Detroit?”  This  was  said  by a black  person.

By the  same  token  we  are  not  going  to  put a ring around  the
black  middle  class.  I believe  now,  as  I believed  on  April 7, that ina
heterogeneous  society  integrated  education  adds  a  dimension  to
quality  education  which  is lacking  without  it. I do feel  SES  has an
impact  on  it.  I do  believe,  however,  that  a  certain  number  of
people,  although  a  certain  number  of people  will  accept  a judge’s
order  and  comply  with  it that  there  will  also  be  those  that  unless
the services  are  enlarged  and  expanded  and  approved  that  they will
undoubtedly,  both  black  and  white,  turn  to  private  education  or
leave  the city.

[157]  and  parochial  education.  In  the  very  last  bill  the  governor
had  he had  one  section,  an  indorsement  providing  aid  for  parochial
schools  which  are  very  small  enrollment  schools  and  can  not
operate  efficiently  and  in another  part  of his bill  he had  a proposal
for  consolidation.  The  21  schools  that  were  closed  in Detroit  that
were  private  schools  have  a  total  enrollment  of  5100  students.

makes  efforts  for consolidation.

in the  Citizens  Committee—a  6-3-3  plan  was  the  northwest  district
committee.  All  the  others  wanted  6-3-3.  The  Citizens  Committee
proposed  6-3-3.  The  school  district  after  World  War  II  built  K
through  8s,  I believe  for  economic  reasons.  Later  on  parents,  I
think,  wanted  to retain  them  as  K through  9 for other reasons.

is a much  wider  one  than  is the  elementary  one  so  you  keep  more
of your  own  in one  building  when  you  have  a  K through  9 than
when  you  have  a  separate  junior  high  school  which  extends  over
four or  five elementary  schools.

tracking  and  since  there  seems  to be difference  of opinion  about it
from  the High School  Study  Commission  report  and others,  would
you  indorse  or  have  any  opposition,  as  an  administrator,  to a
requirement in a court  order  that  there  be no  tracking,  and I say to
you  the  court  in San  Francisco  put  that  in as a requirement  with
out  dealing  in any  great detail  of the issue.  As an  order  would you
support  such  a thing?

A.  I certainly  would  support  it.  It  is  not  an  educationally

sound  procedure.

{160]  Q.  That  is really  what  I wanted  an  answer  to, the other

gets into  a lawyer’s  argument.

A.  Whether  the  court  should  do  it  or  not  I don’t  want  it

because  it is not  educationally  sound.

Q.  Doctor,  assuming  that  the  court  does  order  full
desegregation  of  the  Detroit  School  System,  would  the  task  of
desegregation  in  achieving  and  achieving  some  of  the  other
measures  such  as  good  education  as  you  describe  be  assisted  in
terms,  particularly  in  providing  population  groups  by  the
requirement  that  the  metropolitanization  extend  only  to  those
school  districts  presently  contiguous  to  the  boundaries  of  the
Detroit  School  District?

MR.  BUSHNELL:  Doctor,  I ask  you  to  look  at  the  map in

your  mind’s  eye.

A.  Yes,  I am  doing  that.
Q.  Let  me  ask  the  question  in two  parts;  1, limited  to Wayne
County  and,  2, not  limited  to  Wayne  County,  perhaps  that  would
simplify  it.

Val97

A.  Well,  I don’t  believe  that  the  contiguity  alone  should  be
the  criteria.  Obviously  you  know  we  have  in  Detroit,  Highland
Park and Hamtramck  which  are  surrounded  by us.

Q.  They  are  inner-contiguity,  I am  assuming  that,  Doctor.
A.  Yes,  but  seriously  I think  it ought  to be done  on  the  basis
of a  master  plan  which  needs  to go beyond  the  immediate  areas
[161]  because  if it is to  survive  I don’t  want  Eight  and  Nine  Mile
Road  to  become  what  Six to Eight  Mile  Road  has been  in the  past
five years.

Q. A little  longer  reach?
A.  I want  a  longer  reach,  yes,  and  a decision  or  at  least  an
acceptance  on  the  part  of our  people  that,““Look,  we  have  to stop
running.  We  are  past  the  stage  where  we  were  always  seeking  for
new  frontiers”  and  resolve  our  problems  where  we  are  rather  than
think  that  we  can  resolve  them  by  moving  from  the  East  Coast
past  the  Alleghenys  and  past  the  Mississippi  and  finally  we  end  up
in the  craziest  spot  of all  -  in California.  So  I want a longer reach
by all means.

Q.  Doctor,  would  you  say  that  the  State  of  Michigan  is
essential  to  solving  the  problems  of the  school  district  of the  City
of Detroit?  Let  me  say  it another  way:  Can  it be solved  alone?

A.  No,  definitely  not  in the  context  of your  former  question.
Let alone  that,  even  if it were  to be  done  within  the  city  the  state
would,  the  State  of  Michigan  would  have  to  provide  various
resources  to make  it possible.

Q.  If you  did  not  have  the  present  state  funds  that  you  get

every  year  could  the  school  district  be operated  at all?

A.  No.

HAROLD  WAGNER

having  first  been  sworn  by the  notary  public  to tell  the  truth,  the
whole  truth  and  nothing  but  the  truth,  testified  as  follows:

EXAMINATION  BY  PAUL  DIAMOND,  Esq.:

Q.  Mr.  Wagner,  could  you  state  your  full  name,  please?

A.  Harold  Wagner.

Vail98

Q.  And  what  is your  position?

A.  Supervisor  of  the  Pupil  Transportation  Section,  Michigan

Department  of Education.

Q.  How  long have  you  held  that  position?
A.  With increasing  responsibilities,  some  12 years.
Q.  What  do you  mean  by “increasing  responsibilities”?

A.  In  the  beginning,  I was  an  Assistant  to a Supervisor,  but in

the  last  five  years  I have  held  the role.

Q.  What  are  the  responsibilities  of your  office?

A.  Other?

Q.  Of your  office,  what  are  they,  basically?
A.  My  responsibility  is to  establish  the  rules  for  the  District
distribution  of  State  School  Aid  for  transportation,  assist  School
Districts  in  their  interpretation  of  these  [5]  rules,  to  make
whatever  decisions  are  necessary  to  protect  the  interests  of the
State,  the  public  in-pupil  transportation.  It would  include  the area
of  safety,  as  well  as  the  area  of  the  economics  of  pupil
transportation.

Q.  Are  you  involved  at  all  in  assisting  in  the  purchase  of
transportation,  design  transportation  systems  or  of advice  where
routes  should  be drawn,  how  they  should  be drawn?

A.  In  the  sense  that  we  finance  approximately  75 percent of
the  cost  of transportation,  that  we  write  the  specifications  under
which  school  buses  are  purchased,  in  the  sense  that  we  havea
responsibility  for  the  —  the  school  bus  route  and  the adequacy  of
the  transportation,  we  do have  this responsibility.

Q.  Could  you  explain  a little  more  fully  what  you  mean  by
specif ying or setting specifications for purchase,  and  also  for setting
routes?

A.  We  —  we  prescribe  the  manner  in  which  school  buses are
to  be  manufactured  for  use  in  Michigan.  We have  the  authority to
overrule  the  local  School  District  on  its  selection  of  school  bus
reoutes  and  on  the  manner  in  which  school  bus  routes  ar
established.

Q.  Doyou  then  review  every  school  system’s  transportation

system?

Vail99

A.  We  do  not.  We  —  wherever  there  are  problems  that  are

referred  to us, we  would  look  into  these  problems.

Q.  Who  refers  the problems  to you?
A.  The  problem  might  be  referred  by  a  school  official.  It

might be referred  by a dissatisfied  citizen.

Q.  What  did  you  do  before  you  arrived  12  years  ago  to

handle  transportation?

A.  I have  had  varying  responsibilities  in  the  Department  of
Education,  including  that  of  Special  Education,  of  the  area  of
school  financing,  distribution  of  State  Aid.  I think  that  would
cover  it.

Q.  And  when  did  you  first  arrive  with  the  State  Department?

A.  In  1947.

Q.  And  what  did  you  do before  then?
A.  I have  been  variously  a  teacher,  and  a  Director  of  —  of
Education  in  the  civilian  Conservation  Corps  camps  and  at  one
time  an  employee  of  the  Dow  Chemical  Company  in  an
administrative  responsibility.

Q.  What  is your  educational  background?

A.  I  have  a  Master’s  degree  in  Education,  in  Educational

Administration  and  have  some  education  beyond  that  point.

Q.  In  the  12  years  since  you  have  been  involved  in
transportation,  are  there  any  types  of  courses  or  seminars  or
nationwide  conferences  that  you  attend  to  keep  up  with  [7]
current  trends  in the  country?

see

to  ae
THE  WITNESS:  Which  provides  for  distribution  for the  most
part  to  School  Districts  located  outside  of  a  city  limits  and  for
children  who  lived  more  than  a  mile  and-a-half  from  the  school
they  attend.  This  State  Aid  Act  makes  certain  exceptions  to this,
‘the  exceptions  being  to  former  rural-agricultural  School  Districts,
to former  township  School  Districts,  to children  who  live  within  a
city  but  are  transported  over  routes  which  extend  outside  of a city
or into township  territory  and return  to the  city or  —  or  to schools
outside  of the  city, to other  situations  in which  a transportation  of

Va  200

municipality  is  contained  in  a  School  District  which  has  no
facility,  no  educational  facility  [11]  within  that  fraction  or
municipality.  The  children  are  transported  into  a  second
municipality  for education.

Q.  By way  of example  on  Exhibit  1, is Pontiac  a city?

A.  Yes.
Q.  And  yet  Pontiac  is  reimbursed  some  $58,000.00.  How

would  that  —  how  would  they get reimbursed?

A.  The  School  District  of Pontiac  contains  an  area  larger than
the  City  of  Pontiac  and  for  the  children  who  live  outside  of the
City,  their  transportation  is financed  in part  by State  Aid.

Q.  But  if the  child  lived  within  the  City  of Pontiac  and  was
bused  the  same  distance,  the  transportation  would  not  be  paid
unless  he happened  to go outside  the  City  limits?

A.  That  is right.
Q.  Now,  you  mentioned  that  you  also  got  reimbursement  if
the  child  lived  in  the  city,  crossed  a  city  line  and  then  came  back
to the  city?

A.  Yes.
Q.  Could  you  give  me  some  examples  of how  this happens or

where  it happens?

A.  May | illustrate  it on  the  blackboard?

Q.  Yes.
MR.  KRASICKY:  You  might  describe  what  you  ar

illustrating  so  the  record  will  show  what  you  are  [12]  doing.
THE  WITNESS:  (Illustrating)  We have  a pupil living  —
Q.  (MR.  DIAMOND)  Could  you  describe  what  you  ar

drawing  on  the  board?

A.  Yes,  I have  drawn  an  L-shaped  diagram,  indicating  this
block  L as  a city.  A child  who  lives  at the inner  corner  of this Lis
transported  on  a  road  to  a  School  District  located  at  the  second
portion  of  the  L,  and  his  route,  the  situation  being  that  the
shortest  available  route  to  him  requires  that  he  leave  the city and
then  return  to  the  city  in  which  his school  is located.  He becomes
eligible  by having  gone  through  township  territory.

Va201

Q.  Do  you  have a limit  that  it has  to be, the  shortest  route  to

avoid  —

A.  The  shortest  available  route,  yes.
Q.  Could  you  give  some  examples  of where  that  happens  in
the  State  or  how  often  it  happens?  How  many  pupils  become
eligible  under  that  type  of in-and-out  situation?

A.  I  can’t  tell  you  how  often  it  happens.  It  is  not  of
considerable  importance,  I believe,  although  we have  situations in
Flint,  in  Howell,  Michigan,  Cadillac,  Michigan.  There  may  be
situations  of  minor  nature  wherein  it so  happens  that  the  school
bus  route  follows  a  city  [13]  boundary  and  where  the bus  travels
on  the  township  side  of the  road  on  one  part  of the  trip  and  on
the  municipal  part  of  the  route.  Their  eligibility  is  provided  by
having been  in the  township.

side-by-side,  we  would  —  we  would  consider  that  they  were  still

Q.  (MR.  DIAMOND)  Mr.  Wagner,  follwing  along  on  the  —

border  between  the  City  of  Detroit  and  some  of  the  suburban
cities  and  towns,  would  that  child  be eligible  for  reimbursement?
A.  The  fact  that  he  rode  along  that  road  wouldn’t  make  him
[16]  eligible.  The  question  would  be,  is  it  the  shortest  route
available  to  him,  that  he  takes  a  route  that  takes  him  into  a
Township.

(Whereupon  there  was  a short  off-the-record  discussion.)
Q. (MR.  DIAMOND)  So it is either  going  or  coming?

A.  That  is right.

Q.  Thank  you.  In  Exhibit  1  it  speaks  about  bus  capacity.

Could  you  explain  what  that  figure  means?

A.  Bus capacity?

Q.  Yes.

A.  What  we  have  done  here,  buses  are  typically  66-passenger
buses.  What  we  have  done  here  is mulitply  the  number  of buses  in
the first  column  by 66 and  arrived  at a capacity  for that  system.

The  second,  the  last  column,  we  provided  for  your

information  to indicate  —

Q.  Let  me  interrupt  there,  so  what  you  have  done  is basically
[17]  multiply  the  number  of  buses  by  66  to  get  a  bus-capacity
figure?

A.  That  is right.

Q.  Okay.  Thank  you.

A.  And  to  indicate  the  use  of  the  bus  by  that  particular
system  we  —
we  divided  this  capacity  by  the  number  of
passengers  which  were  transported  and  arrived  at  a  —  the bus use,
so  as  you  will  see  in  this  column,  there  are  many  School  Districts
which  were  sending  the buses out a  second  or  third  time in the

Va  204

transportation  of children  and  this  —  this  procedure  provides  for a
reduction  of the  cost  of pupil  transportation.

Q.  Now,  I take  it,  then,  what  you  are  —  you  have  another
column  labeled  “Passengers”.  You  divided,  then,  passengers  by -

A.  Capacity  by passengers,  yes.

Q.  And  that gives you  your  bus  use?

A.  That  is right.

Q.  Now,  are  the  numbers  listed  under  Passengers  in  that
column  —  do they  reflect  only  eligible  passengers  or  all passengers?

A.  All  passengers.

Q.  All  passengers?

A.  All passengers  reported  to  us,  yes.

[18]  Q. Now, do School  Districts  not report to you  some  of the

people  they  are  transporting?

A.  It  is  conceivable  a  School  District  could  have  two  bus
fleets,  one  bus  fleet  which  is  used  to  transport  ineligible  pupils,
pupils  for  whom  no  State  Aid  may  be  granted,  and  another  fleet
for the  transportation  of eligible  pupils.

Q.  What  if a  School  District  charters  buses  for eligible  pupils

rather  than  purchases  them?

A.  We have  very  little  of this  practice  in Michigan  and...

Q.  Would  that  number  be included  in the  passengers?

A.  Yes,  they  would  be  in  the  number  of buses.  If the  buses
are  chartered  or  leased  by  School  Districts  to  carry  on  this
function,  they  would  be in the  list  of buses  here.

Q.  I see.  I notice  that  the  School  District  of  the  City  of
Detroit  is not  listed  here,  yet  they  do  transport  some  children  by
chartered  buses  in the City,  is that  right?

A.  Yes,  largely  handicapped  pupils.

Q.  Why  aren’t  they  listed  in this  Tri-County  list?
A.  We  haven’t  —  first  of all, we  don’t  accumulate  information
relating  to  transportation  of  handicapped  pupils  in  these  terms,
because  of  their  varieties  of  services  that  are  often  given  to the
transportation  of the  handicapped  pupil.  For  an  example,  a pupil -

7

V a 205

living  in  School  District  A  may  be  picked  up  on  a regular  bus
route,  mentally  handicapped,  [19]  but  his  sister  is with  him  and
he can  be  transported  in a regular  school  bus route,  but  he attends
the  School  District  at  some  distance  and  he  may ride  in  a  special
vehicle  to —  to a second  location  or  even  a  third  location  where
they  are  in a  shuttling  operation  or  in a gathering  operation  and  it
has  not  been  necessary  or  even  feasible  to  try  to  assemble
information  relating  to  these  small  numbers  of  people  in  these
unique  kinds  of situations.

Q.  (Mr.  DIAMOND)  Now,  on  Exhibit  1,  on  the  bus  use
figure,  do  you  make  any  independent  investigation  of  whether
these  buses  are  being  fully  utilized  or  not,  or  whether,  in  fact,
there  is a little  slippage  going  on?

A.  The  bus  use  column  here?

Q.  Yes.

A.  Would  indicate  the  use  of the  vehicle.

Q.  The  actual  use  of the  vehicle?

A.  That  is right.

[23]Q.  Do  you  ever  make  any  investigation  to  suggest  to  a
local  School  District,  in  light  of  your  supervisory  authority,  to
suggest  to  them,  “You  ought  to  use  your  buses a little  better;
therefore,  instead  of  buying  ten  new  buses  you  can  use  present
buses  and  change  your  routes  around  or  stagger  your  school
hours”  or  whatever?

A.  There  are  many  things  involved  in  making  multiple  use  of
vehicles.  Sometimes  contracts  with  teachers  are  involved.  One
doesn’t  stagger  the  opening  and  closing  of schools  daily  unless  the
contract  with  teachers  will  permit  it.

Q.  Are  there  schools  in  Michigan  that  have  staggered  starting

times?

A.  Yes, yes.

Q.  To take  advantage  of transportation?
A.  That  is right.  It is part  of our  counsel  to  School  Districts
that  they  do  provide  for  multiple  use  of  the  vehicles  and  —  but

Va208

-

under  no  situation,  in  no  situation  have  we  directed  a  School
District  to  take  such  action,  the  employment  of personnel  being
purely  a local  responsibility.

|

Q.  But  in any,  of these  studies  have  you  suggested  to  School
Districts  that  they  reorganize  their  routes  or  stagger  times  or
perhaps  even  take  other  means  simply  to  lower  the  per-pupil  cost
of transportation?

[24] A. There  is  no  situation  in  which  we  have  gone to a
School  District  and  said  to  them,  “Your  costs  are  too  high.  You
should  do this.”

School  Districts,  themselves,  are  much  concerned  about  the
diversion  of education  funds  to transportation  and  —  and  such, and
our  part  is —  it is not  necessary.  They  come  to us  for  counsel  and
we  advise  them  as  to  the  kinds  of  things  that  they  may  do  to
reduce  the  cost.

:

Q.  So  have  you,  on  the  request  of  local  School  Districts  -
have  you  ever,  for  example,  done  a  transportation  study  which
would  suggest  to  the  local  School  District  how  they  could  lower
their  cost?

A.  Yes,  we  do this  on  occasion.

Q.  How  often?

A.  Perhaps  once  in  three,  four  months  we  do  this  fora

School  District.

Q.  What  kind  of factors  do  you  consider  in  terms  of —

such  an  analysis?

A.  Distance,  road  conditions,  numbers  of pupils  would  be the
controlling  factors,  the  concentration  of pupils  being an  important
factor.  Say  you  have  children  located  ‘in a suburban  area  arounda
municipality.  We  would  point  out  to  that  School  District  that they
could  gather  half  [25]  a  busload  Of a busload  of  children  ata
gathering  point  and  rapidly  move  children  from  the  schools  they
attend  and  --  to  the  schools  they  attend  and  repeat  the
performance  one,  two,  three,  four, even  five  times.  This  is possible
in some  situations.  Where  children  are  dispersed,  of course,  thisis
not  possible.

;

Q.  Are  you  familiar  with  -  I think  it  is  Mr.  Kusey's  (sic)

study  for  Flint  and  Genesee?  Is that  —

Va  209

A.  Tam  not.

A.  I am  not  acquainted  with  the  study  itself.  I can  only  say
that  some  gentlemen  from  the  Genesee  County  area,  with  some
outside  consultant  services,  did  come  to  me  and  ask  for  certain
basic  information  and  we  made  available  to  them  some  of our  own

Q.  Do  you  favor  such  a  consolidation  of  transportation

systems?

A.  We  favor  any  consolidation  in  transportation  that  would
reduce  the  costs  provided  —  provided  that  no  abuse  of  children
would  -  would  be involved  in this  reduction.

Q.  Are  you  familiar  with  Ohio’s  new  system  of  purchasing

[26]  buses?

A.  Yes.

*

Q.  Could  you  explain  what  that  is?
A.  Ohio,  and  several  other  states  as  well  —
Q.  Could  you  give  us  the  names,  for  example,  of some  other

states  that  do it?

A.  Uh  —  Ohio  —  I would  prefer  to give you  the information  at

a later date,  if I may,  as  to just  what  these  other  states  are.

Q.  Yes.
A.  Ohio  provides.a  mandatory  purchase  of  school  buses
through  its own  purchasing  agency  and a certain  criteria  must  be —
must  be established  by  the  School  District  before  a — a criteria  of
need,  of deterioration  of presently  acquired  vehicles  is established
and  then  the  bus  is  ordered  to  meet  some  State  specifications
through  the  State  purchasing  agency,  the  local  District  having  the
privilege,  as  I recall  it, of adding  certain  additional  specifications
and equipment  to meet  its own  local  needs.

;

Q.  Does  this  tend  to  lower  the  cost  in  terms  of  purchase

order by making  a larger order possible?

Va2l0

A.  Initially  the  cost  may  very  well  be  —  be  reduced  through
mass  purchasing.  There  is some  question  as  to —  as  to  whether th.
element  of  value  is  acquired  through  this  [27]  procedur
in the mag
inasmuch  as  the  local  dealer  is  eliminated  in  —
asing procedure  and  the  function  of the local  dealer,  whichis

to provide  an  equal  service.

assistants  in  Ohio  that  they  consider  it a satisfactory  arrangement,
yes.

Q.  Have  you  considered  adopting  such  a  procedure for this

A.  We  have  —  we  have  considered  it.  Our  lack  of staff and -

on  the  State  level  makes  it  —  makes  it  inappropriate  for us

Q.  Do  local  Districts,  within  the  State  of Michigan,  ever ge

together  to  cooperate  on  transportation  either  in terms  of training

A.  Yes,  there  are  have  —  there  have  been  School  Districts
which  have  pooled  their  purchases.  Specifically,  Oakland  County
has  done  this  over  a  period  of years.  There  has  been  a  —  some of

not  meet  unanimous  [28]  approval  on  the  part  of administraton.

A.  This  is  one  of  the  things  that  happens  when  we  har

School  District  consolidation.

Q.  You mean  consolidation?
A.  Consolidation  of  Districts.  Not  only  are  economis
secured  through  larger transportation  operations,  but  better used
educational  facilities  as well.

change  the  city boundaries  or governmental  districts  but  they take

transportation  in  an  intermediate  School  District  be  consolidated

A.  No.  There’s  no  procedure  whereby  one  School  District  can
-  one  High  School  District  can  provide  transportation  for another

which  prevent  the  operation  of one  bus  system  inside  of another,
one  High  School  District  transportation  system  inside  of another
High School  District  transportation  system.

Q.  Those  are  administrative  rules?

A.  Yes,  that  is right.
